assertion.title: Assertion
assertion.summary: Assertion is supported on compute capability 2.x and higher. The assert() function stops kernel execution if the expression is zero, triggering a debugger breakpoint or printing a formatted failure message to stderr. Subsequent synchronization calls return cudaErrorAssert until cudaDeviceReset() is called. Assertions can be disabled at compile time via NDEBUG but should not contain side effects.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence covers Section 10.32, which details the `assert` macro for CUDA device code. It is grouped with other debugging utilities like `__trap()`, `__brkpt()`, and `printf()` in the CUDA C Programming Guide, serving as a runtime validation tool for kernel development.

facts.claim: The `assert` macro halts kernel execution if the provided integer expression evaluates to zero. Its signature is `void assert(int expression);`.
facts.note: Supported on devices with compute capability 2.x or higher.

facts.claim: When executed inside a debugger, `assert` triggers a breakpoint allowing device state inspection. Outside a debugger, it queues a failure message to stderr, which is flushed upon host synchronization via `cudaDeviceSynchronize()`, `cudaStreamSynchronize()`, or `cudaEventSynchronize()`.
facts.note: If the expression is non-zero, kernel execution continues unaffected.

facts.claim:
The stderr failure message follows the format: `<filename>:<line number>:<function>:
block: [blockId.x,blockId.x,blockIdx.z],
thread: [threadIdx.x,threadIdx.y,threadIdx.z]
Assertion <expression> failed.`
facts.note: Note: The source contains a typographical repetition in the block ID fields (`blockId.x,blockId.x`).

facts.claim: An assertion failure places the device in a persistent error state. All subsequent host-side synchronization calls return `cudaErrorAssert`, and no new commands can be issued until `cudaDeviceReset()` is called to reinitialize the device.
facts.note: Intended strictly for debugging; can negatively impact performance.

facts.claim: Assertions should be disabled in production code by defining the `NDEBUG` preprocessor macro before including `assert.h`. Expressions should avoid side effects (e.g., `++i > 0`), as disabling assertions will bypass the side effect and alter program functionality.
facts.note: None

caveats: The source text contains minor typographical errors (e.g., `blockId.x,blockId.x,blockIdx.z`, `should_be_ one`, `unafected`, `afect`, `diferent`). These should be corrected in the final wiki page.
The `assert` macro is a host-side preprocessor macro that expands to device code; it is not a standalone device function.
contradictions: None detected. The behavior aligns with standard CUDA debugging utilities.

breakpoint-function.title: Breakpoint Function
breakpoint-function.summary: The __brkpt() function can be called from any device thread to suspend the execution of a kernel function.

It sits between the Trap function (10.33) and Formatted Output (10.35), grouping it with other debugging/control-flow primitives. The source provides only the function signature and a one-line behavioral description, with no additional details on debugger integration, hardware requirements, or synchronization behavior.

facts.claim: The __brkpt() function suspends execution of a kernel function when called from any device thread.
facts.note: The source provides only the function signature and a one-line behavioral description, with no additional details on debugger integration, hardware requirements, or synchronization behavior.

facts.claim: The function signature is void __brkpt();
facts.note: The source provides only the function signature and a one-line behavioral description, with no additional details on debugger integration, hardware requirements, or synchronization behavior.

facts.note: The source provides only the function signature and a one-line behavioral description, with no additional details on debugger integration, hardware requirements, or synchronization behavior.

concept/64-bit-compatibility.title: 64-Bit Compatibility
concept/64-bit-compatibility.summary: Explains that the 64-bit nvcc compiles device code in 64-bit mode, which requires host code to also be compiled in 64-bit mode.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence appears in Chapter 6 (Programming Interface), specifically under section 6.1.6 'Compilation with NVCC'. It is positioned immediately after sections covering binary, PTX, application, and C++ compatibility, serving as the final compatibility constraint in the compilation workflow overview.

facts.claim: The 64-bit version of nvcc compiles device code in 64-bit mode, which means pointers are 64-bit.
facts.note: Device code compiled in 64-bit mode is strictly coupled with host code: it is only supported when the host code is also compiled in 64-bit mode.

caveats: No additional 64-bit compatibility details (e.g., address space limits, pointer size variations across architectures, or migration paths) are present in the provided context window.
recommended_structure: Definition of 64-bit compilation mode in nvcc
Placement within CUDA compilation compatibility framework

concept/access-rights.title: Controlling Access Rights
concept/access-rights.summary: Explains cuMemSetAccess for explicit device-level access control on mapped VA ranges, contrasting with cudaEnablePeerAccess.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence (Section 14.7) sits within the CUDA Virtual Memory Management (VMM) chapter. It follows address reservation (`cuMemAddressReserve`) and memory mapping (`cuMemMap`), explaining how mapped memory is actually made accessible to devices before advancing to Fabric Memory and Multicast features.

facts.claim: VMM APIs enable explicit protection of virtual address (VA) ranges. Mapping an allocation via `cuMemMap` does not automatically grant access; attempting to access it without explicit rights causes a program crash.
facts.note: Critical distinction: mapping ≠ accessible.

facts.claim: Access is controlled via `cuMemSetAccess`, which allows or restricts specific devices from accessing a mapped address range. Configuration uses the `CUmemAccessDesc` struct to define location type, device ID, and flags (e.g., `CU_MEM_ACCESS_FLAGS_PROT_READWRITE`).
facts.note: API-level mechanism.

facts.claim: VMM's allocation-granularity access control provides a performance-optimized alternative to `cudaEnablePeerAccess`. While `cudaEnablePeerAccess` automatically maps all prior/future `cudaMalloc` allocations to a peer device (convenient but overhead-heavy), VMM allows explicit, minimal-overhead peer mappings.
facts.note: Performance/legacy comparison.

facts.claim: The `vectorAddMMAP` sample code demonstrates practical usage of these VMM access control APIs.
facts.note: Reference implementation.

caveats: Access control is device-specific within the VMM framework. Cross-device visibility still requires explicit mapping and access configuration per device.

concept/address-operator.title: Address Operator
concept/address-operator.summary: Prohibition on taking the address of built-in variables.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence is a single-sentence restriction located in section 18.5.5.2 'Address Operator' of the CUDA C++ language reference. It is grouped with other operator limitations (e.g., Assignment Operator) and pointer restrictions (18.5.4), which collectively define valid memory and metadata access patterns across host and device execution spaces.

facts.claim: The address-of operator (`&`) is explicitly prohibited when applied to built-in variables in CUDA C++.
facts.note: This restriction applies to all variables categorized under Built-in Variables (e.g., thread, block, and grid metadata), as they represent hardware execution state rather than standard addressable memory locations.

caveats: The source text does not detail alternative ways to access or manipulate built-in metadata, only the prohibition on taking their addresses.
The restriction is presented as a hard language rule without conditional exceptions in this section.
contradictions: None detected. The rule aligns with CUDA's execution model where built-in variables are compiler/runtime intrinsics, not host/device addressable objects.

concept/address-space-conversion.title: 10.16 Address Space Conversion Functions (Overview)
concept/address-space-conversion.summary: Overview of CUDA C++ address space conversion functions. Enables converting pointers from/to other representations for PTX interoperability and performance optimizations (e.g., 32-bit truncation for shared/local/const spaces). Roundtrip guarantees equivalence.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence covers the conceptual overview and usage examples for Section 10.16 (Address Space Conversion Functions). It sits between Section 10.15 (Address Space Predicate Functions) and Section 10.17 (Alloca Function). The surrounding context (L8358-L8420) lists the actual function signatures, but the assigned evidence focuses on the rationale, interoperability use cases, and memory optimization patterns.

facts.claim: The functions convert CUDA C++ generic pointers (T*) to and from specific address space representations (global, shared, constant, local) to enable PTX interoperability and memory optimization.
facts.note: PTX Interoperability: Specific PTX instructions (e.g., ld.shared.u32) expect addresses formatted for a particular memory space rather than generic pointers. Conversion functions like __cvta_generic_to_shared() adapt generic pointers to meet these requirements.

facts.claim: Memory Optimization: The address ranges for shared, local, and constant spaces are smaller than 32 bits. Programs can truncate 64-bit generic pointers to 32-bit integers to reduce data structure size, then zero-extend them back to 64-bit integers before calling the corresponding _to_generic conversion functions.
facts.note: Guarantees: A roundtrip conversion (generic pointer → 32-bit representation → generic pointer) is guaranteed to yield an equivalent output pointer for shared, local, and constant spaces.

facts.claim: Contextual Fit: This overview logically follows the Address Space Predicate Functions (Section 10.15) and precedes the alloca function (Section 10.17). The assigned evidence provides the conceptual rationale and usage examples, while the surrounding document context (L8358-L8420) enumerates the specific function signatures (__cvta_generic_to_* and __cvta_*_to_generic). No contradictions or scope clashes were detected.
facts.note: All facts are strictly derived from the assigned evidence.

recommended_structure: Related Functions (refer to surrounding context for signatures)

concept/adl-lookup-issues.title: ADL Lookup Issues
concept/adl-lookup-issues.summary: Potential Argument-Dependent Lookup (ADL) ambiguities caused by the CUDA compiler's placeholder type substitution for extended lambdas, which introduces enclosing function namespaces into lookup.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence is located in Section 18.7.5 'Additional Notes' of the CUDA C Programming Guide. It specifically addresses a quirk in how the CUDA compiler handles extended `__device__` lambdas when they are passed to host code. The surrounding context (lines 18922-19040) discusses `*this` capture modes for lambdas, while the subsequent section (lines 19075+) discusses relaxed constexpr rules. The ADL issue is a specific host-compiler interaction problem arising from the CUDA compiler's internal representation of extended lambdas.

facts.claim: The CUDA compiler replaces an extended lambda expression with an instance of a placeholder type before invoking the host compiler.
facts.note: This replacement is the root cause of the ADL issue.

facts.claim: One template argument of the placeholder type uses the address of the function enclosing the original lambda expression.
facts.note: This inclusion of the enclosing function's address introduces associated namespaces into the type system.

facts.claim: This placeholder type causes additional namespaces to participate in Argument Dependent Lookup (ADL) for any host function call whose argument types involve the closure type of the extended lambda expression.
facts.note: This happens during the host compilation phase.

facts.claim: ADL participation by these additional namespaces may cause an incorrect function to be selected by the host compiler, potentially leading to ambiguity.
facts.note: The host compiler may find overload candidates it would not have found otherwise.

facts.claim: Example scenario: A host function `bar` takes an argument from namespace `N1`. An extended `__device__` lambda is defined inside `bar` and passed to a host template function `N2::doit`. Inside `doit`, an unqualified call to `foo` is made. Because the lambda's placeholder type involves `N1`, ADL finds `N1::foo` alongside the intended `N2::foo`, causing compilation failure due to ambiguity.
facts.note: This demonstrates how the namespace of the enclosing function's parameters leaks into the ADL of the called host function.

caveats: This issue specifically affects extended `__device__` lambdas used in host code.
The problem occurs during the host compilation phase, after the CUDA compiler has generated the placeholder type.
The issue is triggered by host function calls where the argument types involve the closure type of the extended lambda.
recommended_structure: Overview of the ADL issue with extended lambdas
Mechanism: Placeholder type generation and enclosing function address
Impact: Unexpected namespace participation in ADL

concept/application-compatibility.title: Application Compatibility
concept/application-compatibility.summary: Describes how applications load compatible binary or PTX code, compiler options (-arch, -code, -gencode), runtime code selection, and macros for differentiating code paths.

research_reports.context_summary: Section 6.1.4 falls under Chapter 6 (Programming Interface) and follows discussions on offline compilation, just-in-time (JIT) compilation, binary compatibility, and PTX compatibility. It details how applications manage code embedding and runtime selection across varying GPU compute capabilities.

facts.claim: Application compatibility relies on loading binary or PTX code that matches the target device's compute capability. To support future architectures, applications must embed PTX code for just-in-time compilation at runtime.
facts.note: This bridges the gap between compile-time generation and runtime hardware discovery.

facts.claim: Embedding of PTX and binary code is controlled by the `-arch`, `-code`, or `-gencode` compiler options.
facts.note: Example provided shows `-gencode arch=compute_50,code=sm_50` for binary-only, and `-gencode arch=compute_70,code="compute_70,sm_70"` for both PTX and binary.

facts.claim: The compiled host code automatically selects the most appropriate embedded code at runtime based on the detected compute capability (e.g., CC 5.0/5.2 uses 5.0 binary; CC >7.5 uses PTX for JIT).
facts.note: This abstraction allows a single application binary to run across multiple GPU generations.

facts.claim: The `__CUDA_ARCH__` macro enables conditional compilation of device code paths based on compute capability (e.g., equals 800 when compiling with `-arch=compute_80`).
facts.note: Useful for enabling architecture-specific optimizations like warp reduction.

facts.claim: Family-specific targets (e.g., `sm_100f` / `compute_100f`) restrict execution to a specific GPU family and define `__CUDA_ARCH_FAMILY_SPECIFIC__`.
facts.note: Architecture-specific targets (e.g., `sm_100a` / `compute_100a`) are stricter, restricting execution to an exact compute capability, and define `__CUDA_ARCH_SPECIFIC__`. Architecture-specific is a superset of family-specific.

facts.claim: Applications using the CUDA Driver API must compile code into separate files and explicitly load/execute the appropriate file at runtime, unlike the Runtime API which handles selection automatically.
facts.note: Highlights a key workflow difference between the two APIs.

facts.claim: The Volta architecture's Independent Thread Scheduling changed SIMT behavior, potentially breaking code relying on previous scheduling. Volta developers can opt into Pascal's scheduling via `-arch=compute_60 -code=sm_70`.
facts.note: Serves as a migration aid for legacy SIMT-dependent code.

facts.claim: nvcc provides shorthand compiler options, such as `-arch=sm_70` expanding to `-arch=compute_70 -code=compute_70,sm_70`.
facts.note: Simplifies command-line usage for common compatibility configurations.

caveats: The document is marked as legacy (replaced by CUDA Programming Guide as of CUDA 13.0), but the compatibility mechanisms described remain foundational.

Specific compute capability numbers (e.g., 5.0, 7.0, 10.0) are used as examples and may be outdated depending on the current CUDA version, though the compatibility model (binary vs PTX, family vs architecture specific) is structural.
recommended_structure: Overview of compatibility model (binary vs PTX/JIT)
Driver API vs Runtime API handling

concept/assignment-operator.title: Assignment Operator
concept/assignment-operator.summary: Restrictions on assigning to __constant__, __shared__, and built-in variables.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence is located in Section 18.5.5 ('Operators'), specifically subsection 18.5.5.1. It sits between general operator rules and the subsequent 'Address Operator' section (18.5.5.2), detailing CUDA-specific constraints on the assignment operator for special memory qualifiers and built-in variables.

facts.claim: __constant__ variables cannot be assigned from device code; host-side assignment is only permitted through runtime memory management functions.
facts.note: Inline initialization is also restricted for __shared__ variables, and direct assignment to built-in variables is strictly forbidden.

caveats: No contradictions with standard C++ assignment semantics found; restrictions are specific to CUDA's execution and memory model.

concept/asynchronous-barrier.title: Asynchronous Barrier (cuda::barrier / std::barrier)
concept/asynchronous-barrier.summary: GPU implementation of std::barrier for thread synchronization. Supports hardware acceleration on CC 8.0+, temporal splitting (arrive/wait), bootstrap initialization, phase management, spatial partitioning (warp specialization), early exit (arrive_and_drop), completion functions, and C-like __mbarrier_* primitives.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned context (Section 10.26) is the primary documentation for the Asynchronous Barrier feature in the CUDA C Programming Guide. It details the transition from traditional synchronous barriers (`__syncthreads`) to the temporally-split `cuda::barrier`, covering initialization, phase management, spatial partitioning, early exit, completion functions, and a legacy C-primitives API.

facts.claim: cuda::barrier is the GPU implementation of std::barrier in the NVIDIA C++ standard library, allowing explicit scope specification. It is hardware-accelerated on compute capability 8.0+ and integrates with memcpy_async, while remaining available on CC 7.0+ without acceleration. The legacy nvcuda::experimental::awbarrier is deprecated.
facts.note: Replaces traditional __syncthreads()/group.sync() which block all threads until all arrive, guaranteeing memory visibility in a 3-stage pattern (pre-sync, sync, post-sync).

facts.claim: cuda::barrier splits synchronization into an asynchronous arrive() and blocking wait() pattern, creating a 5-stage iterative loop: pre-arrive code, arrive point (implicit memory fence), code between arrive/wait, wait point, and post-wait code. arrive() does not block, allowing threads to proceed with independent work, while wait() blocks until the expected arrival count is met.
facts.note: Arrive() acts as an implicit memory fence equivalent to atomic_thread_fence(memory_order_seq_cst, thread_scope_block).

facts.claim: Barriers must be initialized via init() with an expected arrival count before any thread calls arrive(). This creates a bootstrapping challenge requiring an initial sync (e.g., block.sync()) to initialize the barrier before using it. Unlike __syncthreads() which is strictly block-wide, cuda::barrier supports flexible participation and partial thread subsets.
facts.note: Expected arrival count dictates how many arrive() calls are needed before wait() unblocks.

facts.claim: A barrier counts down from the expected arrival count to zero. Upon reaching zero, the phase completes and automatically resets to the expected count for the next phase. arrive() returns an arrival_token tied to the current phase. wait() blocks if the token's phase matches the barrier's phase, and unblocks if the phase advances. Rules dictate arrive() must occur in the current phase, and wait() must use a token from the current or immediately preceding phase.
facts.note: Undefined behavior occurs if wait() is called with a token from any other phase.

facts.claim: Producer/consumer spatial partitioning patterns use two one-sided synchronizations (ready/filled buffers). Double buffering requires four barriers. arrive_and_wait() is a convenience wrapper for wait(arrive()).
facts.note: Enables warp specialization where disjoint thread subsets operate concurrently.

facts.claim: arrive_and_drop() allows a thread to exit a synchronization sequence early by fulfilling the current phase obligation and decrementing the expected arrival count for subsequent phases, allowing remaining threads to proceed normally.
facts.note: Prevents deadlocks when threads conditionally diverge from a synchronization loop.

facts.claim: An optional CompletionFunction template parameter is executed once per phase after the last thread arrives and before any thread unblocks from wait(). It provides strict memory visibility guarantees between arriving threads and the completion function.
facts.note: Useful for in-barrier reductions or post-synchronization housekeeping.

facts.claim: A C-compatible API is available via <cuda_awbarrier_primitives.h>, defining __mbarrier_t and __mbarrier_token_t. Key functions include __mbarrier_maximum_count(), __mbarrier_init(), __mbarrier_inval(), __mbarrier_arrive(), __mbarrier_arrive_and_drop(), and __mbarrier_test_wait(). The deprecated __mbarrier_pending_count() was removed in CUDA 11.1.
facts.note: Provides low-level C-style access to barrier functionality for legacy or mixed-language codebases.

caveats: The guide explicitly recommends using __syncthreads() and __syncwarp(mask) for full block or full warp synchronization due to performance reasons, reserving cuda::barrier for flexible or partial participation patterns.
Initialization requires a bootstrap sync, meaning the first barrier usage cannot be truly asynchronous without an initial synchronous setup phase.
recommended_structure: Advanced Patterns (Spatial Partitioning, Early Exit, Completion Function)

concept/asynchronous-concurrent-executio.title: Asynchronous Concurrent Execution
concept/asynchronous-concurrent-executio.summary: CUDA capability to execute host/device computations, memory transfers, and kernel launches concurrently. Covers host-device concurrency, concurrent kernel execution limits, and overlapping data transfers with kernel execution.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence covers Section 6.2.8 and its first four subsections, detailing CUDA's hardware/driver-level concurrency primitives. It sits between memory mapping/synchronization domains (L2004-L2116) and stream management (L2173-L2292), establishing the foundational capabilities that streams later orchestrate.

facts.claim: CUDA exposes host computation, device computation, and memory transfers (host↔device, intra-device, inter-device) as independent, concurrently executable tasks. Achievable concurrency depends on device feature set and compute capability.
facts.note: Foundational concurrency model definition.

facts.claim: Asynchronous library functions return control to the host before device completion. Asynchronous operations include kernel launches, intra-device copies, host-to-device copies ≤64 KB, `Async`-suffixed functions, and memory sets.
facts.note: Host-device asynchronicity mechanics.

facts.claim: Caveats: `CUDA_LAUNCH_BLOCKING=1` globally disables kernel launch asynchronicity (debugging only). Profiling with Nsight Compute forces synchronous kernel launches unless concurrent profiling is enabled. Host-to-device copies may become synchronous if host memory is not page-locked.
facts.note: Synchronization edge cases and profiling overhead.

facts.claim: Concurrent kernel execution is supported on compute capability 2.x+. Queryable via `concurrentKernels` device property. Max concurrent kernels varies by compute capability. Kernels from different CUDA contexts cannot run concurrently (GPU time-slices them; MPS required for multi-process SM execution). High texture usage or large local memory reduces concurrency likelihood.
facts.note: Hardware limits and context isolation rules.

facts.claim: Transfer-Kernel Overlap: Supported if `asyncEngineCount > 0`. Requires page-locked host memory. Intra-device copies can also overlap with kernels and other transfers.
facts.note: Data transfer and compute overlap capability.

facts.claim: Concurrent Data Transfers: Supported if `asyncEngineCount == 2`. Requires page-locked host memory for any involved transfers.
facts.note: Host-device transfer overlap capability.

caveats: Asynchronicity is not guaranteed across all operations; specific size limits (≤64 KB) and memory types (page-locked) dictate behavior.
Context isolation prevents cross-context kernel concurrency; MPS is required for multi-process overlap.
Profiling tools can inadvertently serialize execution unless explicitly configured for concurrent profiling.

concept/asynchronous-data-copies.title: Asynchronous Data Copies (memcpy_async)
concept/asynchronous-data-copies.summary: CUDA 11+ API for overlapping computation with data movement. Enables asynchronous global-to-shared memory copies via cuda::barrier, cuda::pipeline, or cooperative_groups::wait. Includes performance guidance on alignment, trivially copyable types, and warp entanglement effects on commit/wait/arrive operations.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence covers the introduction, API headers, synchronization models (cooperative_groups, barrier, pipeline), compute capability requirements, and performance/warp-entanglement guidelines for the memcpy_async feature in CUDA 11+.

facts.claim: memcpy_async was introduced in CUDA 11 to explicitly manage asynchronous data copying, enabling overlap of computation with data movement in the standard copy-and-compute pattern.
facts.note: The feature replaces synchronous shared memory assignments (shared[local_idx] = global[global_idx]) which require block-wide syncs before and after computation.

facts.claim: APIs are provided in cuda/barrier, cuda/pipeline, and cooperative_groups/memcpy_async.h headers.
facts.note: cuda::memcpy_async pairs with cuda::barrier or cuda::pipeline, while cooperative_groups::memcpy_async pairs with cooperative_groups::wait.

facts.claim: Synchronization is handled via three models: cooperative_groups::wait, cuda::barrier::arrive_and_wait(), or cuda::pipeline stage management (producer_acquire, producer_commit, consumer_wait, consumer_release).
facts.note: The pipeline model supports single-stage and multi-stage queues to overlap memory transfers with computation across iterations.

facts.claim: cuda::barrier and cuda::pipeline variants require compute capability 7.0 or higher.
facts.note: Compute capability 8.0+ enables hardware acceleration for global-to-shared memory transfers, bypassing intermediate registers.

facts.claim: Performance on CC 8.x relies on the cp.async instruction family, which supports 4, 8, and 16-byte transfers.
facts.note: Best performance requires 128-byte alignment for both source and destination pointers. Use cuda::aligned_size_t<Align>(size) to statically prove alignment and avoid runtime overhead.

facts.claim: Data types passed to memcpy_async must be TriviallyCopyable to utilize cp.async acceleration.
facts.note: Non-trivially copyable types require invoking copy constructors, preventing hardware acceleration.

facts.claim: Warp divergence causes 'warp entanglement', leading to over-waiting and excessive barrier updates due to mismatched perceived vs. actual batch sequences.
facts.note: Recommendation: Keep commit and arrive-on operations converged. Use __syncwarp to re-converge the warp if divergent code precedes these operations.

caveats: The source text contains minor formatting artifacts in the aligned_size_t example (e.g., ',<sub>→</sub>pipeline);'), but the semantic requirement for compile-time alignment proofs is clear.
Warp entanglement behavior is specific to CC 8.x pipeline mechanisms and does not apply to older architectures or non-pipeline synchronization models.

concept/binary-compatibility.title: Binary Compatibility
concept/binary-compatibility.summary: Details architecture-specific binary code generation, cubin objects, and compatibility guarantees across minor and major compute capability revisions.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence is located in Chapter 6 (Programming Interface), under section 6.1 (Compilation with NVCC). It directly addresses how `nvcc` translates high-level code into device-executable formats, specifically detailing the rules for binary (cubin) and PTX compatibility, JIT compilation, and architecture-specific targeting options.

facts.claim: Binary code (cubin objects) is architecture-specific and generated using the `-code` compiler option (e.g., `-code=sm_80` for compute capability 8.0).
facts.note: Defines the generation mechanism and hardware dependency of CUDA binaries.

facts.claim: Binary compatibility is guaranteed only forward within the same major revision's minor revisions (compute capability X.y runs on X.z where z ≥ y). Backward compatibility and cross-major revision compatibility are not supported.
facts.note: Establishes the strict forward-only compatibility boundary for pre-compiled binaries.

facts.claim: Binary compatibility is restricted to desktop GPUs. It is explicitly unsupported on Tegra devices and between desktop and Tegra platforms.
facts.note: Highlights platform-specific limitations that constrain binary portability.

facts.claim: PTX code can be just-in-time (JIT) compiled to binary at runtime, allowing applications to run on newer architectures not present at compile time.
facts.note: Explains how PTX bridges the gap when strict binary compatibility is unavailable.

facts.claim: PTX targeting 'Architecture-Specific Features' (e.g., `sm_90a`) is neither forward nor backward compatible.
facts.note: Clarifies the strict isolation of architecture-specific PTX targets.

facts.claim: PTX targeting 'Family-Specific Features' (e.g., `sm_100f`) is forward compatible within the same architecture family but not backward compatible.
facts.note: Details the scope of family-specific PTX compatibility.

caveats: Binary compatibility guarantees do not extend to Tegra or cross-platform (desktop/Tegra) scenarios.
Using older PTX to compile binaries for newer architectures may result in suboptimal performance if newer hardware features (e.g., Tensor Cores) are not utilized.
contradictions: None detected. The documentation consistently frames binary compatibility as a strict forward-only guarantee within minor revisions, contrasting it with the more flexible but performance-variable PTX JIT model.

concept/c++-compatibility.title: C++ Compatibility
concept/c++-compatibility.summary: States that CUDA source files follow C++ syntax rules, with full C++ support for host code and a subset for device code.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence (L1204-L1206) is located in Chapter 6 (Programming Interface), section 6.1.5. It sits between sections on binary/PTX/application compatibility and 64-bit compatibility, positioning C++ compatibility as a compiler-level constraint rather than a hardware or runtime one.

facts.claim: The compiler front end processes CUDA source files according to C++ syntax rules.
facts.note: Establishes the baseline parsing behavior for CUDA files.

facts.claim: Full C++ is supported for host code.
facts.note: Defines the scope of host-side language features.

facts.claim: Device code supports only a subset of C++.
facts.note: Highlights the primary limitation; detailed restrictions are deferred to the 'C++ Language Support' section.

caveats: The evidence is highly concise and explicitly cross-references another section ('C++ Language Support') for the actual subset limitations. The lead writer should check if that referenced section is available in the source context or if it requires a separate document pull.

concept/call-stack.title: Call Stack
concept/call-stack.summary: Call stack size can be queried and set on compute capability 2.x+ devices using cudaDeviceGetLimit() and cudaDeviceSetLimit(). Stack overflow causes kernel failure or warnings, especially for recursive functions. Manual stack size configuration is required when the compiler cannot determine it statically.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence (Section 6.2.13) is embedded between Error Checking (6.2.12) and Texture/Surface Memory (6.2.14) in the CUDA C Programming Guide. It specifically addresses device-side call stack management, configuration limits, and overflow handling for compute capability 2.x+ architectures.

facts.claim: The call stack is supported on CUDA devices with compute capability 2.x and higher.
facts.note: Applies to device-side execution context.

facts.claim: Stack size can be queried using cudaDeviceGetLimit() and configured using cudaDeviceSetLimit().
facts.note: Runtime API functions for limit management.

facts.claim: Stack overflow behavior differs based on execution environment: debuggers (CUDA-GDB, Nsight) report a specific stack overflow error, while standard execution yields an unspecified launch error.
facts.note: Error reporting varies by toolchain.

facts.claim: The compiler issues a 'Stack size cannot be statically determined' warning, typically for recursive functions, requiring manual stack size configuration if the default limit is insufficient.
facts.note: Recursion prevents static analysis of stack depth.

caveats: Scope is strictly limited to compute capability 2.x and newer devices.
Default stack limits may be insufficient for recursive kernels, necessitating explicit API configuration.

concept/child-graph-memory-nodes.title: Child Graph Memory Nodes
concept/child-graph-memory-nodes.summary: Moving child graph ownership to parent graphs, allowing memory nodes while imposing restrictions on independent instantiation.

research_reports.context_summary: Falls under Section 16.8 (Memory Nodes in Child Graphs) within the broader CUDA Graphs memory management chapter. It follows discussions on physical memory sharing, performance/remapping, and peer access, concluding the graph memory topic before Chapter 17 (Mathematical Functions).

facts.claim: Introduced in CUDA 12.9, the feature allows transferring ownership of a child graph to a parent graph, enabling the child to contain memory allocation and free nodes.
facts.note: Facilitates independent construction of child graphs with memory operations before parent integration.

facts.claim: Post-transfer restrictions: child graphs cannot be independently instantiated or destroyed, attached to a different parent, used with cuGraphExecUpdate, or have additional alloc/free nodes added.
facts.note: These constraints enforce strict lifecycle and structural immutability once ownership is moved.

facts.claim: Ownership transfer is performed via cudaGraphAddNode using cudaGraphNodeParams, specifying cudaGraphNodeTypeGraph and setting graph.ownership to cudaGraphChildGraphOwnershipMove.
facts.note: Code example demonstrates creating a child graph with alloc/free nodes, then moving it into a parent graph.

caveats: The documentation is marked as legacy (replaced by CUDA Programming Guide as of CUDA 13.0), but the API behavior remains valid for historical/reference purposes.
No contradictions found; the restrictions are absolute and clearly scoped to post-ownership-transfer states.

concept/choosing-page-size.title: Choosing the Right Page Size
concept/choosing-page-size.summary: Analyzes the trade-offs between small and large virtual page sizes regarding memory fragmentation, TLB misses, and migration latency. Highlights that GPU TLB misses are particularly costly.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence is extracted from the 'Performance Tuning' section of the CUDA C Programming Guide, specifically subsection 24.2.2.1.1. It provides the foundational trade-offs and architectural considerations for developers optimizing Unified Memory access patterns.

facts.claim: Small virtual page sizes reduce memory fragmentation but increase TLB misses, while larger virtual page sizes increase fragmentation but reduce TLB misses.
facts.note: Directly addresses the core trade-off for page size selection.

facts.claim: TLB misses are significantly more expensive on GPUs than on CPUs. Frequent random GPU accesses to Unified Memory mapped with small page sizes can cause severe slowdowns compared to larger page sizes.
facts.note: Highlights the GPU-specific penalty driving the recommendation toward larger pages for random access patterns.

facts.claim: CPU threads accessing large memory areas with small page sizes also suffer slowdowns, but to a lesser extent. Applications may accept this CPU slowdown to benefit from reduced memory fragmentation.
facts.note: Provides the explicit trade-off logic for CPU-bound or mixed workloads.

facts.claim: Larger page sizes increase memory migration costs because full pages are migrated, potentially causing larger latency spikes.
facts.note: Introduces the migration overhead factor that counterbalances the TLB miss benefit.

facts.claim: Performance tuning advice applies strictly to virtual page sizes, not physical page sizes, as physical sizes vary across hardware and are subject to change.
facts.note: Clarifies the scope of the guidance to prevent hardware-specific tuning mistakes.

caveats: No contradictions or scope clashes detected. The evidence is self-contained and directly supports the target concept.

concept/cluster-launch-control.title: Cluster Launch Control
concept/cluster-launch-control.summary: Chapter header and introduction to Cluster Launch Control, a Compute Capability 10.0 feature for advanced thread block scheduling and work-stealing.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned document introduces Cluster Launch Control as a Compute Capability 10.0 feature that enables asynchronous thread block cancellation for work-stealing. It details the API workflow using cooperative groups and PTX memory barriers, outlines strict constraints around failed requests, and extends the mechanism to thread block clusters with multicast semantics and cluster-scoped synchronization.

facts.claim: Cluster Launch Control was introduced in Compute Capability 10.0 to provide developers with advanced control over thread block scheduling by allowing cancellation of pending thread blocks or clusters.
facts.note: Bridges the trade-off between fixed work per block (high overhead, good load balancing/preemption) and fixed number of blocks (low overhead, poor load balancing/preemption), achieving reduced overhead, preemption, and load balancing simultaneously.

facts.claim: The mechanism enables work-stealing: a running block cancels a pending block's launch and processes the cancelled block's assigned data index.
facts.note: Cancellations fail if no indices remain or if a higher-priority kernel preempts the scheduler.

facts.claim: The API is asynchronous and relies on memory barriers for synchronization, accessible via libcu++ (cuda::ptx namespace).
facts.note: Standard workflow: declare shared result/barrier/phase variables -> initialize barrier -> submit async request via single thread using cg::invoke_one -> synchronize with mbarrier_try_wait_parity -> decode success/index -> apply async proxy fences.

facts.claim: Submitting a new cancellation request after observing a previous failure is Undefined Behavior (UB), as is retrieving the block index from a failed request.
facts.note: Multi-thread submission within a block is discouraged; if used, each thread must provide unique shared result pointers and adjust barrier transaction counts.

facts.claim: For thread block clusters (declared with __cluster_dims__), cancellation is submitted by a single cluster thread, and the result is multicasted to all blocks in the cluster.
facts.note: Requires cg::cluster_group::sync() to guarantee all blocks exist, uses ptx::scope_cluster for barriers, and uses ptx::clusterlaunchcontrol_try_cancel_multicast. Thread blocks must add their local cluster block index to the stolen global index.

caveats: The document is marked as legacy (replaced by a new CUDA Programming Guide as of CUDA 13.0), but the technical specifications for Compute Capability 10.0 remain valid for implementation reference.

concept/compiler-optimization-hint-restr.title: 10.18.6 Compiler Optimization Hint Restrictions
concept/compiler-optimization-hint-restr.summary: Restrictions for compiler optimization hint functions. __assume() only supported with cl.exe host compiler. Other functions require invocation from __device__/__global__ functions or when __CUDA_ARCH__ macro is defined, unless host compiler supports them.

research_reports.context_summary: Section 10.18 covers CUDA C compiler optimization hint functions. Subsection 10.18.6 specifically details platform and invocation restrictions for these hints.

facts.claim: __assume() is restricted to the cl.exe host compiler.
facts.note: Other hint functions (e.g., __builtin_assume_aligned, __builtin_expect) are supported on all platforms.

facts.claim: Invocation scope depends on host compiler support.
facts.note: If the host compiler supports the function, it can be called from anywhere in the translation unit. If not, it must be called inside a __device__/__global__ function body or guarded by the __CUDA_ARCH__ macro.

caveats: The restriction applies specifically to host compiler support; device-side execution rules are handled via __CUDA_ARCH__ or __device__/__global__ scoping.

concept/compressible-memory.title: Compressible Memory
concept/compressible-memory.summary: Details on allocating and querying compressible memory using CU_MEM_ALLOCATION_COMP_GENERIC and CU_DEVICE_ATTRIBUTE_GENERIC_COMPRESSION_SUPPORTED.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence falls under Chapter 14 (Virtual Memory Management), specifically section 14.3.2.1 'Compressible Memory'. It details how to query, allocate, and verify memory with hardware compression capabilities using the VMM API suite.

facts.claim: Purpose and Performance Benefits
facts.note: Accelerates accesses to data with unstructured sparsity and other compressible data patterns. Can save DRAM bandwidth, L2 read bandwidth, and L2 capacity depending on the data.

facts.claim: Hardware Requirement
facts.note: Requires devices that support Compute Data Compression. Support is queried using `cuDeviceGetAttribute` with `CU_DEVICE_ATTRIBUTE_GENERIC_COMPRESSION_SUPPORTED`.

facts.claim: Allocation Mechanism
facts.note: Allocated via the VMM API `cuMemCreate`. Developers opt-in at allocation time by setting `CUmemAllocationProp::allocFlags::compressionType` to `CU_MEM_ALLOCATION_COMP_GENERIC`.

facts.claim: Verification and Hardware Caveat
facts.note: Due to limited hardware resources, a requested compressible allocation may not actually receive compression attributes. Developers must verify the actual allocation by calling `cuMemGetAllocationPropertiesFromHandle` and checking if `allocationProp.allocFlags.compressionType == CU_MEM_ALLOCATION_COMP_GENERIC`.

caveats: The source document is marked as legacy (replaced as of CUDA 13.0), but the VMM API behavior for compressible memory remains the authoritative reference for this specific feature.

concept/concurrent-access-pre6x.title: Coherency and Concurrency Constraints on Pre-6.x
concept/concurrent-access-pre6x.summary: Explains that simultaneous CPU/GPU access is not possible on pre-6.x GPUs due to coherency guarantees, the role of the concurrentManagedAccess property, and the requirement for explicit synchronization.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence (L21848-L21897) forms the core of the 'Coherency and Concurrency' constraints for older GPU architectures. It sits within the broader pre-6.x Unified Memory limitations section (24.3.2), directly preceding the `cudaStreamAttachMemAsync` workaround (24.3.2.4.3) and following discussions on data migration and oversubscription limits.

facts.claim: Pre-6.x architectures enforce strict GPU-exclusive access to managed memory. The GPU holds exclusive rights to all managed data while any kernel is active, regardless of whether the kernel actually touches that specific data.
facts.note: This is a hardware coherency limitation; CC 6.x+ lifts this via GPU page faulting.

facts.claim: On devices where the `concurrentManagedAccess` device property is 0, any concurrent CPU access to managed memory while the GPU is active triggers a segmentation fault, as the page is marked inaccessible to the CPU.
facts.note: Applies even if CPU and GPU access different managed allocations.

facts.claim: Explicit synchronization is mandatory. A CPU thread cannot access any managed data between a kernel launch and a subsequent synchronization call. The mere potential for concurrent access is sufficient to raise a process-level exception.
facts.note: Synchronization functions include `cudaDeviceSynchronize`, `cudaStreamSynchronize`, `cudaEventSynchronize`, and synchronous `cudaMemcpy`/`cudaMemset`.

facts.claim: Dynamically allocating managed memory (`cudaMallocManaged`/`cuMemAllocManaged`) while the GPU is active yields unspecified behavior until the GPU is synchronized or additional work is launched. Accessing it on the CPU during this window may cause a segfault.
facts.note: Exception: Memory allocated with `cudaMemAttachHost` or `CU_MEM_ATTACH_HOST` is not subject to this restriction.

caveats: The constraints apply globally to the GPU; they are not data-specific. Even if a kernel only touches variable `x`, the CPU cannot safely touch variable `y` until synchronization.
The `cudaMemcpy*()` and `cudaMemset*()` operations on managed memory can also impose temporary concurrency constraints depending on whether the system chooses host or device access paths.
These restrictions are architectural. CC 6.x+ and modern hardware coherency models remove these limits via on-demand page faulting.

concept/conditional-graph-nodes.title: Conditional Graph Nodes Overview
concept/conditional-graph-nodes.summary: Covers conditional nodes (IF, WHILE, SWITCH) that enable dynamic control flow and looping within graphs. Evaluation happens on-device after dependencies are met. Uses condition handles created before the node, with values set via `cudaGraphSetConditional()` or defaults.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence sits within the CUDA Graphs control-flow section, immediately following discussions on Tail Launch, Tail Self-launch, and Sibling Launch. It introduces on-device branching and looping capabilities, shifting control flow management from the host to the device.

facts.claim: Conditional nodes enable on-device conditional execution and looping, allowing dynamic and iterative workflows to be defined entirely within a graph. This frees the host CPU to perform parallel work.
facts.note: Establishes the primary architectural purpose of the feature.

facts.claim: Condition values are evaluated on the device only after the conditional node's dependencies have been satisfied.
facts.note: Clarifies execution timing relative to the graph dependency graph.

facts.claim: Three node types are supported: IF (executes body once if condition is non-zero, with an optional zero-body), WHILE (repeats body until condition is zero), and SWITCH (executes the nth body graph if condition equals n, or skips if no match).
facts.note: Details the branching/looping semantics.

facts.claim: Condition values are accessed via a cudaGraphConditionalHandle created before the node. Device code sets the value using cudaGraphSetConditional(), and a default value can be specified at handle creation to apply on each launch.
facts.note: Describes the API contract for state management.

facts.claim: Instantiating a conditional node automatically creates an empty body graph and returns its handle. The body can be populated using standard graph APIs or cudaStreamBeginCaptureToGraph().
facts.note: Explains the graph population workflow.

facts.claim: Conditional nodes support nesting.
facts.note: Defines structural limits/capabilities.

caveats: The assigned evidence covers the high-level overview and API concepts. Detailed constraints (e.g., allowed node types inside body graphs, memory access rules, and specific API signatures) are deferred to subsequent subsections (6.2.8.7.8.1–6.2.8.7.8.3) and are outside the assigned evidence range.

concept/conditional-node-body-graph-requ.title: Conditional Node Body Graph Requirements
concept/conditional-node-body-graph-requ.summary: Covers constraints on the body graphs of conditional nodes: single-device nodes, allowed node types (kernel, empty, memcpy, memset, child graph, conditional), restrictions on Dynamic Parallelism/Device Graph Launch, MPS limits, and memory access rules matching device graph requirements.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence is the dedicated requirements subsection (6.2.8.7.8.2) within the broader 'Conditional Graph Nodes' chapter. It immediately follows the introduction to conditional handles and precedes the specific implementation details for IF, WHILE, and SWITCH conditional nodes.

facts.claim: All nodes within a conditional body graph must reside on a single device.
facts.note: General requirement for single-device residency.

facts.claim: Permitted node types are strictly limited to kernel, empty, memcpy, memset, child graph, and conditional nodes.
facts.note: Defines allowed graph composition.

facts.claim: Kernels inside the body graph cannot use CUDA Dynamic Parallelism or launch device graphs.
facts.note: Kernel node restriction.

facts.claim: Cooperative launches are allowed provided Multi-Process Service (MPS) is not active.
facts.note: Kernel node exception/condition.

facts.claim: Only device memory and pinned device-mapped host memory are permitted for memcpy/memset operations.
facts.note: Memory transfer scope.

facts.claim: Transfers involving CUDA arrays are explicitly forbidden.
facts.note: Memory transfer restriction.

facts.claim: Both source and destination operands must be accessible from the current device at the time of graph instantiation.
facts.note: Instantiation accessibility rule.

facts.claim: Copy operations are executed from the device where the graph resides, even if the target memory belongs to a different device.
facts.note: Execution device vs target memory device distinction.

caveats: No contradictions or scope clashes detected. The requirements are strictly scoped to the body graphs of conditional nodes (IF, WHILE, SWITCH).

concept/conditional-switch-nodes.title: Conditional SWITCH Nodes
concept/conditional-switch-nodes.summary: SWITCH nodes, introduced in CUDA 12.8, allow execution of one of n different graphs within a conditional node based on an evaluated condition value. If the condition value is greater than or equal to n, no graph is executed. The section provides code examples for creating graphs with SWITCH conditional nodes using the CUDA Graph API.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence is section 6.2.8.7.8.5, positioned immediately after IF and WHILE conditional nodes in the CUDA Graphs documentation. It follows the same structural pattern: conceptual explanation, code example, and diagram reference. The surrounding context establishes that conditional nodes share a common handle-based API (`cudaGraphConditionalHandle`) and execution lifecycle (`cudaGraphInstantiate`/`cudaGraphLaunch`), with SWITCH being the newest variant (CUDA 12.8).

facts.claim: Conditional SWITCH nodes were introduced in CUDA 12.8 to enable dynamic branching within a single conditional node.
facts.note: Directly from source text.

facts.claim: A SWITCH node executes exactly one of `n` body graphs based on a runtime condition value. If the value equals `n`, the corresponding `n`th graph runs. If the value is `>= n`, no body graph executes.
facts.note: 1-indexed logic implied by 'nth graph... if condition value is n'.

facts.claim: Configuration uses `cudaGraphNodeTypeConditional` with `cParams.conditional.type = cudaGraphCondTypeSwitch`. The number of body graphs is set via `cParams.conditional.size`.
facts.note: API struct fields from example.

facts.claim: Body graphs are accessed via `cParams.conditional.phGraph_out`, which is an array of `cudaGraph_t` pointers. Bodies are populated by adding nodes to indices `0` through `n-1`.
facts.note: Array indexing confirmed by example accessing `[0]` and `[4]` for size 5.

facts.claim: The condition value is set in an upstream kernel using `cudaGraphSetConditional(handle, value)`, where `handle` is created via `cudaGraphConditionalHandleCreate`.
facts.note: Handle lifecycle matches surrounding IF/WHILE examples.

facts.claim: The documentation references a diagram (Figure 25) depicting a 3-node graph where the middle node is the conditional SWITCH node.
facts.note: Visual reference only.

caveats: The source code block is mislabeled as `javascript` but contains standard C/CUDA code. This is a formatting artifact in the original guide.
The condition logic is 1-indexed: value `n` triggers the `n`th graph, and values `>= n` trigger no execution. This differs from typical 0-indexed array semantics and should be explicitly documented.
No information is provided on performance characteristics, graph capture limitations, or error handling for invalid condition values.
recommended_structure: Usage Example (handle creation, upstream kernel, body population)

concept/cooperative-groups.title: Cooperative Groups Introduction
concept/cooperative-groups.summary: Introduction to Cooperative Groups, an extension to the CUDA programming model for organizing communicating threads and expressing parallel decomposition granularity.

research_reports.context_summary: The provided context covers the introduction, programming model, group types (implicit/explicit), partitioning, collectives, usage requirements, and version history for CUDA Cooperative Groups.

facts.claim: Cooperative Groups is an extension to the CUDA programming model introduced in CUDA 9 that organizes communicating threads and expresses synchronization granularity.
facts.note: Replaces brittle, ad-hoc warp/block synchronization primitives with a safe, future-proof mechanism that improves software composition and compiler optimization compatibility.

facts.claim: The programming model centers on first-class group objects that explicitly name participating threads, enabling richer parallel decompositions and collective group-wide function interfaces.
facts.note: Supports producer-consumer, opportunistic, and global grid synchronization patterns.

facts.claim: Implicit groups represent the kernel launch configuration and must be created upfront before any branching to avoid deadlocks.
facts.note: Includes thread_block (replaces __syncthreads), cluster_group (CC 9.0+), and grid_group (requires cooperative launch API for sync).

facts.claim: Explicit groups are created via partitioning or discovery, including thread_block_tile (templated, HW-optimized up to 1024 threads), coalesced_group (opportunistic warp-active threads), and single-thread groups.
facts.note: thread_block_tile requires block_tile_memory on CC 7.5 or lower for sizes >32, but not on CC 8.0+.

facts.claim: Groups can be partitioned using tiled_partition (row-major tiling), labeled_partition (by integral label), or binary_partition (by predicate).
facts.note: tiled_partition requires parent size to be evenly divisible by tile size and supports native hardware sizes plus larger templated sizes.

facts.claim: Collective operations like sync() and barrier_arrive/barrier_wait require all threads in the group to participate; otherwise, behavior is undefined.
facts.note: barrier_arrive/wait allows latency hiding by decoupling arrival from wait phases.

facts.claim: Usage requires CUDA 9.0+, includes <cooperative_groups.h> (and optional algorithm headers), and mandates C++11 for collective algorithms.
facts.note: Groups should be passed by reference to specialized types to preserve compile-time optimizations.

facts.claim: Recent versions removed multi_grid_group (CUDA 13.0), added barrier_arrive/wait for grid/block (12.2), invoke_one APIs (12.1), and moved experimental APIs to main namespace (12.0).
facts.note: CUDA 12.0 also relaxed block_tile_memory requirements for CC 8.0+.

caveats: Implicit group creation is a collective operation; instantiating it inside divergent conditional branches causes deadlocks or data corruption.
All threads in a group must participate in every collective call with matching arguments; divergent participation yields undefined behavior.
The documentation notes that the CUDA C++ Programming Guide has been replaced by the CUDA Programming Guide as of CUDA 13.0, marking this content as legacy.

concept/cooperative-groups-barrier-arriv.title: barrier_arrive and barrier_wait
concept/cooperative-groups-barrier-arriv.summary: Synchronization API functions that provide a barrier mechanism for cooperative groups. barrier_arrive returns an arrival_token, and barrier_wait consumes it to synchronize threads. Allows independent work between arrive and wait to hide synchronization latency.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence is a subsection (11.6.1.1) under 'Group Collectives' and 'Synchronization' in the Cooperative Groups library documentation. It details the two-phase barrier synchronization API, followed immediately by a simpler `sync()` wrapper (11.6.1.2). The parent section establishes that collective operations require full group participation, which directly governs the strict phase constraints of `barrier_arrive`/`barrier_wait`.

facts.claim: API Signatures and Purpose
facts.note: Provides `T::arrival_token T::barrier_arrive();` and `void T::barrier_wait(T::arrival_token&&);`. Functions as a synchronization API similar to `cuda::barrier`.

facts.claim: Automatic Initialization and Phase Constraints
facts.note: Cooperative Groups automatically initializes the group barrier. All threads must call both `barrier_arrive` and `barrier_wait` exactly once per phase.

facts.claim: Undefined Behavior and Release Conditions
facts.note: Calling any collective operation or another barrier arrival after `barrier_arrive` is undefined until `barrier_wait` completes the phase. Blocked threads are released only after all group members call `barrier_arrive`, but may be released before others call `barrier_wait`.

facts.claim: Latency Hiding and Token Consumption
facts.note: The two-step model allows independent work between arriving and waiting to hide synchronization latency. `barrier_arrive` returns an `arrival_token` that must be passed to `barrier_wait`; the token is consumed and cannot be reused.

facts.claim: Cross-Block Cluster Synchronization Example
facts.note: Demonstrates initializing shared memory across a cluster: `cluster.barrier_arrive()` after local init, local processing, mapping shared memory from another block, then `cluster.barrier_wait(std::move(token))` before consuming data, followed by `cluster.sync()`.

caveats: The strict 'all threads must arrive and wait once per phase' rule means this API cannot be used for dynamic or partial group participation within a single phase.
Token consumption is mandatory; attempting to reuse an `arrival_token` or passing it by value instead of rvalue reference (`std::move`) will violate the API contract.

concept/cooperative-groups-binary-partit.title: binary_partition
concept/cooperative-groups-binary-partit.summary: A collective operation that partitions the parent group into two subgroups based on a boolean predicate. It is a specialized form of labeled_partition where the label is restricted to 0 or 1.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence falls under Section 11.5 Group Partitioning, specifically following `labeled_partition` (11.5.2) and preceding the Group Collectives section (11.6). It documents the `binary_partition` collective API within the Cooperative Groups library.

facts.claim: Definition & Purpose
facts.note: A collective operation that partitions a parent group into one-dimensional, coalesced subgroups based on a boolean predicate. It is a specialized form of `labeled_partition()` restricted to labels 0 or 1.

facts.claim: API Signatures
facts.note: Two overloads are provided: one accepting a general `coalesced_group`, and a templated version accepting a `thread_block_tile<Size>`.

facts.claim: Synchronization Behavior
facts.note: As a collective operation, it may block the calling thread until all members of the parent group have invoked the function.

facts.claim: Requirements & Caveats
facts.note: Requires Compute Capability 7.0 minimum and C++11. The documentation explicitly notes the functionality is still under evaluation and may change.

facts.claim: Example Usage
facts.note: Demonstrates splitting a 32-thread tile into two subgroups (odd vs. even) by evaluating `(elem & 1)` on each thread's input element.

caveats: The documentation warns that the API is still under evaluation and subject to change.
No scope clashes detected; aligns perfectly with the Cooperative Groups partitioning hierarchy.

concept/cooperative-groups-execution-con.title: Cooperative Groups Execution Control (invoke_one / invoke_one_broadcast)
concept/cooperative-groups-execution-con.summary: Covers the cooperative groups execution control functions invoke_one and invoke_one_broadcast, including function signatures, parameter requirements, codegen constraints, and usage examples for dynamic buffer allocation and aggregated atomics.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence covers section 11.6.4.1 of the CUDA C++ Programming Guide, detailing the `invoke_one` and `invoke_one_broadcast` collective execution control functions within the Cooperative Groups library. It provides function signatures, behavioral constraints, group type limitations, return type requirements, hardware compute capability notes, and a practical atomic operation example.

facts.claim: Function signatures differ in return type: `invoke_one` returns `void`, while `invoke_one_broadcast` returns `decltype(fn(args...))`.
facts.note: Core behavior of `invoke_one` is to select an arbitrary thread from the calling group to execute the provided invocable `fn` with arguments `args`.

facts.claim: `invoke_one_broadcast` extends this by distributing the return value of `fn` to all threads in the group and returning it from the collective.
facts.note: Implicit synchronization occurs between the calling group and the selected thread before and/or after the invocation.

facts.claim: Communication within the calling group inside the invocable body is prohibited to guarantee forward progress, but communication with threads outside the group is permitted.
facts.note: Thread selection is non-deterministic.

facts.claim: All cooperative group types are valid for `invoke_one`, but only `coalesced_group` and `thread_block_tile` are valid for `invoke_one_broadcast`.
facts.note: Return type constraints for `invoke_one_broadcast` require the type to be trivially copyable (`is_trivially_copyable<T>::value == true`).

facts.claim: Size limits apply to the return type: `sizeof(T) <= 32` for `coalesced_group` and tiles ≤ 32 threads, and `sizeof(T) <= 8` for larger tiles.
facts.note: Hardware acceleration for thread selection may be utilized on Compute Capability 9.0+ devices when called with explicit group types.

facts.claim: Minimum compute capability is 5.0, with C++11 required. CC 9.0 enables hardware acceleration.
facts.note: Example usage demonstrates `invoke_one_broadcast` with `cuda::atomic` to perform a relaxed atomic add across a coalesced group, returning the previous value to all threads.

facts.claim: Example usage demonstrates `invoke_one_broadcast` with `cuda::atomic` to perform a relaxed atomic add across a coalesced group, returning the previous value to all threads.
facts.note: No contradictions or scope clashes detected. The evidence directly maps to the target page slug and title.

caveats: The source explicitly restricts `invoke_one_broadcast` to `coalesced_group` and `thread_block_tile` types, unlike `invoke_one` which accepts all group types.
Return value size limits are strictly tied to group size (≤32 vs >32 threads) and group type.
Forward progress is only guaranteed if no intra-group communication occurs inside the invoked function.

concept/cooperative-groups-group-collect.title: Group Collectives
concept/cooperative-groups-group-collect.summary: Overview of collective operations provided by the Cooperative Groups library. These operations require participation of all threads in the specified group to complete, and all threads must pass the same values for corresponding arguments unless explicitly allowed otherwise.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence (L12561-L12564) is the introductory paragraph for Section 11.6 'Group Collectives'. It sits between 'Group Partitioning' (11.5) and specific collective operation subsections like 'Synchronization' (11.6.1) and 'Data Transfer' (11.6.2). The surrounding context shows how collective operations replace manual atomic/shuffle patterns and outlines the mandatory participation model.

facts.claim: Group Collectives are a set of collective operations provided by the Cooperative Groups library that require participation from all threads in the specified group to complete.
facts.note: This defines the core concept and its mandatory participation requirement.

facts.claim: All threads in the group must pass identical values for corresponding arguments to each collective call, unless the argument description explicitly allows different values.
facts.note: This establishes the strict argument consistency rule for collective calls.

facts.claim: Violating the argument consistency rule results in undefined behavior.
facts.note: Highlights the consequence of non-compliance.

caveats: The assigned evidence is purely definitional. Specific collective operations (e.g., `barrier_arrive`, `memcpy_async`) are detailed in subsequent subsections (11.6.1, 11.6.2) and are not covered by the assigned evidence range.

concept/cooperative-groups-inclusive-sca.title: inclusive_scan and exclusive_scan
concept/cooperative-groups-inclusive-sca.summary: Collective operations that perform prefix sum (scan) on data provided by each thread. inclusive_scan includes the calling thread's value, while exclusive_scan does not. Supports atomic update variants.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence covers section 11.6.3.3 of the CUDA C++ Programming Guide, detailing the `inclusive_scan` and `exclusive_scan` collective operations within the Cooperative Groups library. It provides function prototypes, behavioral definitions, parameter constraints, operator support, atomic update variants, codegen requirements, and practical examples like stream compaction.

facts.claim: Two overloads are provided for each scan type: one accepting a binary operator `op` and one without it.
facts.note: Overloads without `op` default to `cg::plus<TyVal>()`.

facts.claim: `exclusive_scan` returns the reduction of values from threads with a lower `thread_rank` than the calling thread.
facts.note: `inclusive_scan` extends this by including the calling thread's own value in the reduction.

facts.claim: Valid group types for these collectives are `coalesced_group` and `thread_block_tile`.
facts.note: Different threads in the group may pass different values for the `val` argument.

facts.claim: The `val` argument must be trivially copyable, support suitable arithmetic/comparative operators, and adhere to size limits: `sizeof(T) <= 32` for coalesced groups and tiles ≤ 32, or `sizeof(T) <= 8` for larger tiles.
facts.note: Built-in function objects include `plus()`, `less()`, `greater()`, `bit_and()`, `bit_xor()`, and `bit_or()`. Lambdas and custom function objects are also supported.

facts.claim: `inclusive_scan_update` and `exclusive_scan_update` variants accept an additional `atomic` argument (`cuda::atomic` or `cuda::atomic_ref`).
facts.note: They atomically update the atomic with the sum of all thread inputs using `op`, then combine the previous atomic value with each thread's scan result. Updates use relaxed memory ordering.

facts.claim: Codegen requires a minimum Compute Capability of 5.0 and C++11. The `cooperative_groups/scan.h` header must be included.
facts.note: Examples demonstrate computing cumulative sums of thread ranks and calculating per-thread output indices for stream compaction using `exclusive_scan`.

caveats: The provided evidence ends before the dynamic buffer space allocation example using `exclusive_scan_update` (lines 13107-13152), so that specific use case is excluded.
Size constraints for `val` are strict and depend on group size, which may impact custom type usage.

concept/cooperative-groups-labeled-parti.title: labeled_partition
concept/cooperative-groups-labeled-parti.summary: A collective operation that partitions the parent group into one-dimensional subgroups based on a label value. Threads with the same label value are assigned to the same subgroup.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence falls under section 11.5.2 (labeled_partition) within the broader 'Group Partitioning' (11.5) and 'Group Collectives' (11.6) chapters of the CUDA C Programming Guide. It is positioned between `tiled_partition` and `binary_partition`.

facts.claim: labeled_partition is a collective operation that partitions a parent group into one-dimensional subgroups where threads are coalesced.
facts.note: Threads sharing the same `label` value are assigned to the same subgroup.

facts.claim: Two template overloads are provided: one for `coalesced_group` and one for `thread_block_tile<Size>`.
facts.note: Both take the parent group and a `Label` parameter.

facts.claim: The `Label` parameter must be an integral type.
facts.note: Used to evaluate the partitioning condition.

facts.claim: The operation is synchronous and may block until all parent group members invoke it.
facts.note: Standard collective behavior for group partitioning.

facts.claim: Requires Compute Capability 7.0+ and C++11.
facts.note: Codegen requirements for the function.

facts.claim: Marked as experimental; functionality may change.
facts.note: Explicit caveat in the documentation.

facts.claim: binary_partition is a specialized form of labeled_partition where the label is restricted to boolean values (0 or 1).
facts.note: Contextual relationship to adjacent partitioning functions.

caveats: Explicitly noted as still being evaluated and subject to change.
Synchronous collective: all threads in the parent group must invoke the operation.

concept/cooperative-groups-memcpy-async.title: memcpy_async
concept/cooperative-groups-memcpy-async.summary: A group-wide collective memcpy that utilizes hardware acceleration for nonblocking memory transactions from global to shared memory. Requires 16-byte alignment and should be paired with wait/wait_prior for completion.

research_reports.context_summary: Section 11.6.2.1 of the CUDA C Programming Guide details the `memcpy_async` collective operation within the Cooperative Groups library, covering its purpose, API signatures, alignment requirements, synchronization patterns, and codegen constraints.

facts.claim: Definition & Purpose
facts.note: `memcpy_async` is a group-wide collective operation that leverages hardware-accelerated, nonblocking memory transactions to copy data from global memory to shared memory. It moves data through a single pipeline stage.

facts.claim: Asynchronous Behavior Conditions
facts.note: The operation is strictly asynchronous only when the source is global memory, the destination is shared memory, and both are aligned to 16, 8, or 4 bytes. Asynchronously copied data must only be read after a `wait` or `wait_prior` collective call confirms completion.

facts.claim: API Signature 1 (Byte-based)
facts.note: `memcpy_async(group, _dst, _src, shape)` copies `shape` bytes.

facts.claim: API Signature 2 (Layout-based)
facts.note: `memcpy_async(group, dst, dstLayout, src, srcLayout)` copies `min(dstLayout, srcLayout)` elements. If layouts use `cuda::aligned_size_t<N>`, both must specify the same alignment.

facts.claim: Alignment & Layout Constraints
facts.note: Best performance requires 16-byte alignment for both shared and global memory. When using `cuda::aligned_size_t<N>`, the total bytes must be a multiple of N, and pointers must be aligned to N bytes. Alignment is guaranteed at least `min(16, N)`.

facts.claim: Errata / Layout Unit Clarification
facts.note: The CUDA 11.1 layout-based API expects layout values in elements, not bytes. The element type is inferred from `TyElem`. Using `std::byte` or `char` is recommended when specifying alignments.

facts.claim: Synchronization & Overlap Strategy
facts.note: To overlap data transfer with execution, developers can kick off an N+1 request while waiting on request N using the stage-based `wait_prior` API.

facts.claim: Codegen Requirements
facts.note: Requires Compute Capability 5.0 minimum, C++11, and Compute Capability 8.0 for true asynchronicity. The `cooperative_groups/memcpy_async.h` header must be included.

facts.claim: Example Usage Pattern
facts.note: Demonstrates streaming data from global to a limited shared memory buffer by looping `memcpy_async` calls interspersed with `wait` calls to process chunks sequentially.

caveats: The layout-based API signature (Usage 2) was introduced in CUDA 11.1 and has specific unit expectations (elements vs bytes) that differ from naive byte-counting assumptions.
Asynchronicity is hardware-dependent and strictly requires CC 8.0+; on older hardware (CC 5.0-7.5), it may behave synchronously or lack pipeline overlap benefits.

concept/cooperative-groups-reduce.title: reduce
concept/cooperative-groups-reduce.summary: Performs a reduction operation on data provided by each thread in the group. Supports hardware acceleration on CC 8.0+ for arithmetic and logical operations. Includes async variants for atomic updates or pointer stores.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence covers section 11.6.3.1 'reduce' within the CUDA Cooperative Groups API. It details the synchronous `reduce` function, its parameters, hardware acceleration capabilities, asynchronous variants (`reduce_update_async`, `reduce_store_async`), and provides code examples for standard deviation calculation and block-wide atomic reductions.

facts.claim: The `reduce` function signature is `template <typename TyGroup, typename TyArg, typename TyOp> auto reduce(const TyGroup& group, TyArg&& val, TyOp&& op) -> decltype(op(val, val));` and performs a reduction operation across all threads in the specified group.
facts.note: Hardware acceleration is available on Compute Capability 8.0 and higher for arithmetic add, min, max, and logical AND, OR, XOR operations on 4-byte types. Older hardware uses a software fallback.

facts.claim: Valid group types are `coalesced_group` and `thread_block_tile`.
facts.note: The `val` argument must be trivially copyable. Size constraints are ≤32 bytes for `coalesced_group` and tiles ≤32, and ≤8 bytes for larger tiles. Different threads may pass different values.

facts.claim: The `op` argument accepts constructed function objects like `cg::plus<int>()`, `cg::less<int>()`, `cg::greater<int>()`, `cg::bit_and<int>()`, `cg::bit_xor<int>()`, and `cg::bit_or<int>()` for hardware acceleration. Lambdas and other callable objects are also supported.
facts.note: Asynchronous variants include `reduce_update_async`, `reduce_store_async` (with atomic), and `reduce_store_async` (with pointer). These compute results asynchronously to a destination rather than returning to each thread, requiring explicit synchronization (e.g., `group.sync()`) to observe results.

facts.claim: Atomic variants use `cuda::atomic` or `cuda::atomic_ref` with relaxed memory ordering. The pointer store variant weakly stores the result.
facts.note: Codegen requirements: Compute Capability 5.0 minimum, Compute Capability 8.0 for hardware acceleration, C++11. Requires including `cooperative_groups/reduce.h`.

facts.claim: Examples demonstrate calculating approximate standard deviation using `cg::reduce` with `cg::plus<int>()`, and performing block-wide reductions using `cg::reduce_update_async` with an atomic variable followed by `block.sync()`.
facts.note: None.

caveats: Hardware acceleration is strictly limited to 4-byte types on CC 8.0+.
Asynchronous variants require explicit synchronization to ensure results are committed before downstream operations.

concept/cooperative-groups-reduce-operat.title: Reduce Operators
concept/cooperative-groups-reduce-operat.summary: Function objects mirroring hardware intrinsics for reduction operations. Includes plus, less, greater, bit_and, bit_xor, and bit_or. Designed to match hardware behavior rather than strict STL semantics.

research_reports.context_summary: Section 11.6.3.2 defines the built-in function objects used with `cg::reduce` to enable hardware-accelerated reductions on CC 8.0+ devices, detailing their signatures, functional behavior, and fallback mechanisms for non-accelerated types.

facts.claim: The `cooperative_groups` namespace exposes six function objects (`cg::plus`, `cg::less`, `cg::greater`, `cg::bit_and`, `cg::bit_xor`, `cg::bit_or`) designed to mirror hardware intrinsics for use with `cg::reduce`.
facts.note: These objects differ from C++ STL equivalents in that `cg::less` and `cg::greater` return the actual value (using `operator<` and `operator>`) rather than a boolean result.

facts.claim: Hardware acceleration is type-dependent: integral types (e.g., `cg::plus<int>`) on CC 8.0+ trigger intrinsics like `__reduce_add_sync(...)`, while floating-point types (e.g., `cg::plus<float>`) and vector types (e.g., `int4`) fall back to shuffle-based reductions.
facts.note: Vector types require custom operator definitions to work with `reduce`.

facts.claim: Lambdas and other custom function objects cannot be inspected by the compiler for hardware dispatch and will always perform shuffle-based reductions.
facts.note: This applies regardless of the operation performed inside the lambda.

caveats: The source text contains a typo stating `cg::greater` uses `operator<`; the functional description clarifies it returns the greater value.
Hardware acceleration is strictly limited to CC 8.0+ devices and 4B integral types for the listed operators.

concept/cooperative-groups-sync.title: sync
