concept/cooperative-groups-sync.summary: Synchronizes all threads named in a group. Available as a member function or free function. Equivalent to calling barrier_arrive followed by barrier_wait.

research_reports.context_summary: The `sync` function is documented in section 11.6.1.2 under 'Group Collectives' and 'Synchronization'. It sits between the `barrier_arrive`/`barrier_wait` API (11.6.1.1) and the data transfer collective `memcpy_async` (11.6.2.1).

facts.claim: Purpose and Behavior
facts.note: Synchronizes all threads named in the group. Halts execution until every thread in the group reaches the synchronization point.

facts.claim: Function Signatures
facts.note: Available as a static member function `static void T::sync();` and a free function template `template <typename T> void sync(T& group);`.

facts.claim: Group Type Support
facts.note: Works with any existing Cooperative Groups type, as all group types support synchronization.

facts.claim: Grid Group Launch Requirement
facts.note: If the group is a `grid_group`, the kernel must have been launched using the appropriate cooperative launch APIs.

facts.claim: Implementation Equivalence
facts.note: Functionally equivalent to calling `T.barrier_wait(T.barrier_arrive())`.

caveats: No specific compute capability or C++ standard requirements are listed for `sync` itself, though it relies on the underlying group type's collective synchronization mechanics.
recommended_structure: Example usage (if available in broader context, otherwise note absence)

concept/cooperative-groups-wait-and-wait.title: wait and wait_prior
concept/cooperative-groups-wait-and-wait.summary: Collective functions to wait for memcpy_async copies to complete. wait blocks until all previous copies finish, while wait_prior allows overlapping by waiting only on requests older than NumStages.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence is an excerpt from Section 11.6.2.2 of the CUDA C++ Programming Guide, which documents data transfer collectives. It directly follows the `memcpy_async` specification and precedes the `reduce` collective, providing the necessary synchronization primitives to safely consume asynchronously transferred data.

facts.claim: Purpose and Functionality
facts.note: `wait` and `wait_prior` are collective synchronization primitives designed to wait for `memcpy_async` global-to-shared memory transfers to complete. Both functions implicitly synchronize the named group.

facts.claim: API Signatures
facts.note: Two template functions are provided: `template <typename TyGroup> void wait(TyGroup & group);` and `template <unsigned int NumStages, typename TyGroup> void wait_prior(TyGroup & group);`.

facts.claim: Behavioral Differences
facts.note: `wait` blocks all calling threads until every previously issued `memcpy_async` request has finished. `wait_prior<NumStages>` implements stage-based waiting: it waits for all previous requests to complete while allowing the most recent `NumStages` to remain in-flight. For N total copies, it ensures the first N-NumStages are done.

facts.claim: Compilation & Hardware Requirements
facts.note: Requires C++11, a minimum Compute Capability of 5.0, and Compute Capability 8.0 for asynchronous execution. The `cooperative_groups/memcpy_async.h` header must be included.

facts.claim: Typical Pipelining Pattern
facts.note: Used to overlap data transfer with computation. A standard pattern involves kicking off an initial `memcpy_async`, then looping where the next request is launched while `wait_prior<1>` is called on the previous stage's buffer. After the loop, `wait` is called to safely process the final stage.

caveats: The evidence explicitly notes that `wait_prior` is intended to improve flexibility over `wait` by allowing the latest N stages to remain in progress, enabling computation on stage N-1 while stage N is still transferring.

concept/cuda-16-bit-floating-point-textu.title: 16-Bit Floating-Point Textures
concept/cuda-16-bit-floating-point-textu.summary: Details the IEEE 754-2008 binary2 half format support in CUDA arrays, host/device conversion intrinsics, texture fetching promotion to 32-bit, and channel description creation.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence is located in section 6.2.14.1.2 of the CUDA C Programming Guide. It is positioned between a comprehensive code example demonstrating texture object creation and usage (lines 3691-3810) and subsequent sections detailing layered and cubemap texture types (lines 3821-3861). The section functions as a specific format reference within the broader texture memory chapter.

facts.claim: The 16-bit floating-point (half) format supported by CUDA arrays is identical to the IEEE 754-2008 binary2 specification.
facts.note: Direct data type support is absent in CUDA C++, requiring conversion intrinsics for device code and external libraries for host code.

facts.claim: Device code utilizes the intrinsic functions __float2half_rn(float) and __half2float(unsigned short) to convert between 32-bit floats and 16-bit half values via the unsigned short type.
facts.note: These intrinsics are restricted to device code execution.

facts.claim: Host code equivalents for half-precision conversion are not natively provided by CUDA C++ and must be sourced from external libraries, such as OpenEXR.
facts.note: None

facts.claim: During texture fetch operations, 16-bit floating-point components are automatically promoted to 32-bit floats before any filtering is performed.
facts.note: None

facts.claim: A channel description for 16-bit floating-point textures is created by calling one of the cudaCreateChannelDescHalf*() functions.
facts.note: None

caveats: The documentation explicitly restricts the provided conversion intrinsics to device code, which may impact host-side data preparation workflows.
Automatic promotion to 32-bit floats during texture fetches means that filtering operations will inherently operate at 32-bit precision, regardless of the underlying 16-bit storage format.
recommended_structure: Data Type & Conversion (Device intrinsics vs Host libraries)
Texture Fetching Behavior (Automatic promotion to 32-bit float)

concept/cuda-arrays.title: CUDA Arrays
concept/cuda-arrays.summary: Defines CUDA arrays as opaque, optimized memory layouts for texture fetching (1D, 2D, 3D) with various component types. Notes they are only accessible via texture fetching or surface read/write operations.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence provides the foundational definition of CUDA arrays within Section 6.2.14 (Memory). It is positioned between subsections on Surface Memory (L3873-L3990) and Graphics Interoperability (L3992-L4107), establishing CUDA arrays as the underlying opaque device memory structure that both texture and surface APIs operate upon.

facts.claim: CUDA arrays are opaque memory layouts specifically optimized for texture fetching.
facts.note: Core definition.

facts.claim: CUDA arrays support 1D, 2D, and 3D memory layouts.
facts.note: Dimensional flexibility.

facts.claim: Elements within a CUDA array contain 1, 2, or 4 components.
facts.note: Structural composition.

facts.claim: Supported element data types include signed/unsigned 8-, 16-, or 32-bit integers, 16-bit floats, and 32-bit floats.
facts.note: Type constraints.

facts.claim: Kernels cannot directly pointer-access CUDA arrays; they must be accessed exclusively through texture fetching or surface read/write operations.
facts.note: Access restrictions.

caveats: The opaque nature means developers cannot use standard pointer arithmetic or `cudaMemcpy` directly on the array; they must use texture/surface APIs or array-specific copy functions (e.g., `cudaMemcpy2DToArray`).

Surface read/write and texture gather require specific allocation flags (`cudaArraySurfaceLoadStore`, `cudaArrayTextureGather`) and compute capability limits, which are detailed in the surrounding context but outside the core definition.

concept/cuda-atomic-functions.title: Atomic Functions
concept/cuda-atomic-functions.summary: Overview of CUDA atomic read-modify-write operations on global or shared memory, including scope definitions (device, block, system), memory ordering, and CUDA 12.8+ builtin function signatures.

research_reports.context_summary: Section 10.14 defines CUDA atomic functions, covering their read-modify-write behavior, thread scopes, hardware support exceptions, CUDA 12.8+ builtin signatures with memory ordering, and strict usage restrictions.

facts.claim: Atomic functions perform read-modify-write operations on 32-bit, 64-bit, or 128-bit words in global or shared memory. For vector types like float2/float4, operations are applied element-wise. They are restricted to device code.
facts.note: Definition & Scope

facts.claim: Default memory ordering is cuda::memory_order_relaxed. Thread scope is determined by suffixes: _system maps to cuda::thread_scope_system, no suffix maps to cuda::thread_scope_device, and _block maps to cuda::thread_scope_block.
facts.note: Scopes & Ordering

facts.claim: Any atomic operation can be implemented using atomicCAS(). A fallback implementation for double-precision atomicAdd on compute capability < 6.0 is provided.
facts.note: Custom Implementation

facts.claim: Hardware support exceptions: Compute capability < 6.0 only supports device-wide operations. Tegra devices with CC < 7.2 lack system-wide support.
facts.note: Hardware Support

facts.claim: CUDA 12.8+ introduces compiler builtins with explicit memory order and thread scope arguments, following GNU's atomic built-in signature.
facts.note: CUDA 12.8+ Builtins

facts.claim: Supported memory orders include __NV_ATOMIC_RELAXED, __NV_ATOMIC_CONSUME, __NV_ATOMIC_ACQUIRE, __NV_ATOMIC_RELEASE, __NV_ATOMIC_ACQ_REL, and __NV_ATOMIC_SEQ_CST. Supported scopes include __NV_THREAD_SCOPE_THREAD, __NV_THREAD_SCOPE_BLOCK, __NV_THREAD_SCOPE_CLUSTER, __NV_THREAD_SCOPE_DEVICE, and __NV_THREAD_SCOPE_SYSTEM.
facts.note: Enums

facts.claim: Atomic functions cannot operate on local memory, must be used within the block scope of a __device__ function, and their function addresses cannot be taken (e.g., in host functions, template default arguments, or constructor initialization lists).
facts.note: Restrictions

facts.claim: Implementation notes: __NV_ATOMIC_CONSUME is implemented using __NV_ATOMIC_ACQUIRE, and __NV_THREAD_SCOPE_THREAD is implemented using __NV_THREAD_SCOPE_BLOCK.
facts.note: Implementation Notes

caveats: The source explicitly notes that supported data types for specific operations (like atomicAdd, atomicMin, etc.) are detailed in their respective subsections, which are not included in this context window.
The _system suffix scope is conditional ('if they meet particular conditions'), though the exact conditions are not detailed in this excerpt.

concept/cuda-c17-features.title: C++17 Features (constexpr host/device rules)
concept/cuda-c17-features.summary: Details on how constexpr __host__ and __device__ functions are handled during device and host code generation, including ODR-use restrictions and unsupported patterns like exceptions and RTTI.

research_reports.context_summary: Falls under the 'Relaxed Constexpr' section, detailing cross-execution space calls for constexpr functions when relaxed rules are active.

facts.claim: When a __host__-only constexpr function is called from device code, the compiler generates device code for its body unless the function is only used in a constexpr context.
facts.note: Device restrictions apply to host constexpr functions called from device code, including prohibitions on ODR-use of host variables/functions and exceptions/RTTI. The compiler may not emit diagnostics for these violations, leading to silent runtime failures.

facts.claim: When a __device__-only constexpr function is called from host code, its body is preserved for the host compiler. If it ODR-uses device variables or __device__-only non-constexpr functions, the call is unsupported and may fail silently at runtime.
facts.note: Calling constexpr __host__ functions from standard C++ headers in device code is hazardous. Platform-specific implementations may ODR-use host variables/functions, causing silent breakage across host environments.

caveats: Cross-execution constexpr calls rely on relaxed rules; standard strict constexpr evaluation does not apply across execution spaces.
Lack of compiler diagnostics for ODR-use and RTTI/exception violations in host constexpr bodies called from device code requires manual code review.
recommended_structure: Overview of relaxed constexpr rules for cross-execution space calls
Host constexpr in device code: generation rules and device restrictions
Device constexpr in host code: preservation rules and ODR limitations
Caveats: silent failures and standard library portability risks

concept/cuda-code-samples.title: CUDA C++ Code Samples
concept/cuda-code-samples.summary: Example code snippets demonstrating Data Aggregation Classes, Derived Classes, Class Templates, Function Templates, and Functor Classes in CUDA.

research_reports.context_summary: Section 18.9 of the CUDA C++ Programming Guide provides five code samples demonstrating C++ features supported in device and global code: data aggregation classes, derived classes, class templates, function templates, and functor classes.

facts.claim: The 'Data Aggregation Class' sample demonstrates a `PixelRGBA` struct with `__device__` constructors and a friend `__device__` operator+ overload, showing how custom types can be instantiated and operated on in device code.
facts.note: C++ features like operator overloading and friend functions are supported in device code.

facts.claim: The 'Derived Class' sample illustrates inheritance, virtual functions, and destructors marked with `__device__`. It also shows custom placement `new` and `delete` operators using a `MemoryPool`, and instantiation of a derived class (`Point`) from a base class (`Shape`) via placement new.
facts.note: Polymorphism and custom memory management are supported on the device.

facts.claim: The 'Class Template' sample shows a template class `myValues<T>` with `__device__` methods, which is instantiated and used inside a `__global__` kernel function `useValues<T>`, demonstrating template classes in kernels.
facts.note: Template classes can be fully utilized within GPU kernels.

facts.claim: The 'Function Template' sample provides a `__device__` function template `func<T>`, an explicit specialization for `int`, and examples of both explicit argument specification and implicit argument deduction when calling the function.
facts.note: Function templates and explicit specializations are valid in device code.

facts.claim: The 'Functor Class' sample defines functor classes (`Add`, `Sub`) with `__device__` `operator()`. It demonstrates passing a functor as a template parameter to a `__global__` kernel (`VectorOperation`) to perform element-wise vector operations, showing how functors can be used for flexible kernel logic.
facts.note: Functors enable parameterized operations in CUDA kernels.

caveats: The samples are legacy (CUDA 13.0+ replaced by a new guide), but the C++ feature support they demonstrate remains relevant for understanding device-side C++ syntax.
All code snippets use `__device__` or `__global__` qualifiers to indicate execution space.
The functor sample is split across a page break, requiring concatenation of the host and device code sections.

concept/cuda-compute-capabilities.title: Compute Capabilities

facts.claim: Compute capability defines the general specifications and feature set of a CUDA compute device.
facts.note: Baseline features introduced with an architecture are generally available on all subsequent architectures.

facts.note: Architecture-specific features (introduced with CC 9.0) target specialized operations (e.g., Tensor Cores) and are not guaranteed on future architectures. They require the `a` suffix in the compilation target (e.g., `compute_100a`).

facts.claim: Feature support varies by CC. facts.note: Technical specifications define hardware limits per CC, including maximum resident grids (128), grid/block dimensions, warp size (32), registers per SM/thread, shared memory limits, local/constant memory sizes, and texture/surface dimension limits.

facts.note: Devices follow IEEE 754-2008 with specific deviations: no dynamic rounding mode, all exceptions are masked, NaN propagation rules differ (quiet NaN 0x7ffff for single-precision), and float-to-int out-of-range conversions clamp to the supported range. Global atomic FP adds use flush-to-zero, while shared memory atomics support denormals.

facts.claim: Devices follow IEEE 754-2008 with specific deviations: no dynamic rounding mode, all exceptions are masked, NaN propagation rules differ (quiet NaN 0x7ffff for single-precision), and float-to-int out-of-range conversions clamp to the supported range. Global atomic FP adds use flush-to-zero, while shared memory atomics support denormals.

facts.note: CC 6.x SM contains 64 (6.0) or 128 (6.1/6.2) cores, 16/32 SFUs, 2/4 schedulers, 24/48 KB L1/texture cache, and 64/96 KB shared memory.

facts.claim: CC 6.x SM contains 64 (6.0) or 128 (6.1/6.2) cores, 16/32 SFUs, 2/4 schedulers, 24/48 KB L1/texture cache, and 64/96 KB shared memory.
facts.note: The assigned evidence is extracted from Chapter 20 of the *CUDA C Programming Guide*. It serves as the definitive reference for compute capability definitions, feature matrices, compiler target suffixes, and architectural breakdowns. The document is explicitly marked as legacy as of CUDA 13.0 and has been superseded by the new *CUDA Programming Guide*.

caveats: The source document is explicitly marked as legacy as of CUDA 13.0 and has been superseded by the new *CUDA Programming Guide*. `CUDA_C_Programming_Guide:L19440`

concept/cuda-compute-modes.title: CUDA Compute Modes
concept/cuda-compute-modes.summary: Describes the three CUDA compute modes configurable via nvidia-smi: Default (multi-thread/process), Exclusive-Process (single context per system), and Prohibited (no contexts). Explains how `cudaSetDevice()` and `cudaSetValidDevices()` interact with these modes. Introduces Compute Preemption (Pascal+), which allows instruction-level task preemption to prevent monopolization or timeouts, and notes the associated context switch overheads.

research_reports.scope: Section 6.4 (Compute Modes) within Chapter 6 (CUDA Driver API), positioned between Versioning/Compatibility (6.3) and Mode Switches (6.5).
research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence details how GPU compute contexts are managed at the OS/driver level, specifically focusing on access control and preemption behaviors that affect multi-process and multi-threaded CUDA applications.

facts.claim: Compute modes are configured using NVIDIA's System Management Interface (nvidia-smi) on Tesla solutions running Windows Server 2008+ or Linux.
facts.note: Legacy 'Tesla' terminology used in source; applies to modern NVIDIA GPUs.

facts.claim: Three compute modes exist: Default (multiple host threads can use the device simultaneously), Exclusive-process (only one CUDA context allowed across all system processes, though multiple threads within that process can use it), and Prohibited (no CUDA context can be created).
facts.note: Defines the core access control semantics.

facts.claim: If a device is in Prohibited or Exclusive-process mode and occupied by another process, a host thread using the runtime API without an explicit cudaSetDevice() call may be automatically associated with a different device. cudaSetValidDevices() can define a prioritized device list.
facts.note: Describes fallback behavior and device selection APIs.

facts.claim: Compute Preemption is supported on Pascal architecture and newer (compute capability major revision 6+), enabling instruction-level task preemption instead of thread-block granularity.
facts.note: Prevents long-running kernels from monopolizing the GPU or timing out.

facts.claim: Compute Preemption incurs context switch overheads and is automatically enabled on supported devices. It can be queried via cudaDeviceGetAttribute() using cudaDevAttrComputePreemptionSupported.
facts.note: Performance and query details.

facts.claim: To avoid context switch overheads from multiple processes, users can select Exclusive-process mode to ensure only one process is active on the GPU.
facts.note: Optimization recommendation.

facts.claim: The current compute mode of a device can be queried by checking the cudaDevAttrComputeMode attribute.
facts.note: Runtime query method.

caveats: Source uses legacy 'Tesla' branding for GPU solutions; modern documentation may use 'NVIDIA GPU'.

Mode switches (Section 6.5) can invalidate CUDA contexts by reallocating display primary surface memory, causing subsequent runtime calls to fail with an invalid context error. This is related but distinct from compute modes.

concept/cuda-context.title: CUDA Context
concept/cuda-context.summary: A CUDA context is analogous to a CPU process, encapsulating all driver API resources and actions. It maintains a distinct address space, manages current context per host thread via a stack, and tracks usage counts for interoperability.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence defines the CUDA context as a core driver API abstraction, detailing its lifecycle, thread association, memory isolation, and interoperability mechanisms. No scope clashes or contradictions were detected.

facts.claim: A CUDA context is analogous to a CPU process, encapsulating all resources and actions performed within the driver API. Resources are automatically cleaned up upon destruction.
facts.note: Definition & Analogy

facts.claim: Each context maintains a distinct address space, meaning CUdeviceptr values from different contexts reference different memory locations.
facts.note: Address Space Isolation

facts.claim: A host thread can only have one device context current at a time. cuCtxCreate() automatically makes the new context current to the calling thread. Driver functions requiring an active context return CUDA_ERROR_INVALID_CONTEXT if none is current.
facts.note: Thread Association & Current Context

facts.claim: Each host thread maintains a stack of current contexts. cuCtxCreate() pushes a context onto the stack, while cuCtxPopCurrent() detaches it, making it floating for any thread to use, and restores the previous context.
facts.note: Context Stack & Floating Contexts

facts.claim: Contexts are managed via a usage count initialized to 1 by cuCtxCreate(). cuCtxAttach() increments the count, and cuCtxDetach() decrements it. A context is destroyed when the count reaches 0 via cuCtxDetach() or cuCtxDestroy().
facts.note: Lifecycle & Usage Count

facts.claim: The driver API is interoperable with the runtime, allowing access to the runtime's primary context via cuDevicePrimaryCtxRetain().
facts.note: Runtime Interoperability

facts.claim: The usage count mechanism facilitates third-party library integration. Libraries can safely share a context by incrementing/decrementing the count, or manage their own isolated contexts using cuCtxPushCurrent() and cuCtxPopCurrent().
facts.note: Library Interoperability

concept/cuda-cpp-c17-features.title: C++17 Features
concept/cuda-cpp-c17-features.summary: Overview of C++17 features supported by nvcc, including inline variables and structured bindings, with specific restrictions for device/managed memory spaces and whole-program compilation.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence covers the introduction to C++17 support in nvcc, followed by specific restrictions on inline variables and structured bindings. It sits between C++14 and C++20 feature sections in the programming guide.

facts.claim: C++17 features are enabled by passing the nvcc -std=c++17 flag, which also configures the host preprocessor, compiler, and linker to use the C++17 dialect.
facts.note: Inline variables with __device__, __constant__, or __managed__ specifiers must have internal linkage (e.g., static or anonymous namespace) when compiled in whole program mode. inline __shared__ variables are exempt.

facts.claim: When using the g++ host compiler, inline variables declared with the __managed__ specifier may not be visible to the debugger.
facts.note: Structured bindings cannot be declared with a variable memory space specifier (e.g., __device__ auto [a, b] = ...; is an error).

caveats: The restriction on inline variables applies specifically to whole program compilation mode; separate compilation mode allows external linkage.
Debugger visibility issues for __managed__ inline variables are specific to the g++ host compiler.
recommended_structure: Overview of C++17 support and compilation flag
Inline Variables (linkage rules, __shared__ exception, debugger caveat)

concept/cuda-cpp-c20-features.title: C++20 Features
concept/cuda-cpp-c20-features.summary: Overview of C++20 features supported by nvcc, including module support, coroutine support, three-way comparison operator, and consteval functions, with device/host execution space restrictions.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence corresponds to Section 18.5.25 of the CUDA C++ Programming Guide, positioned between C++17 feature restrictions (Section 18.5.24) and Polymorphic Function Wrappers (Section 18.6). It details nvcc's handling of the C++20 dialect and outlines specific compiler restrictions or workarounds for four key C++20 features.

facts.claim: nvcc supports C++20 features enabled by default in the host compiler. Passing the `-std=c++20` flag enables all C++20 features and forwards the corresponding dialect option to the host preprocessor, compiler, and linker.
facts.note: Modules are not supported in CUDA C++ for either host or device code. Using `module`, `export`, or `import` keywords will be diagnosed as compilation errors.

facts.claim: Coroutines are not supported in device code. Using `co_await`, `co_yield`, or `co_return` within a device function scope results in a compilation error.
facts.note: The three-way comparison operator (`<=>`) is supported in both host and device code. However, some uses rely on host-provided STL functionality, which may require the `--expt-relaxed-constexpr` flag to silence warnings and must satisfy device code requirements.

facts.claim: Consteval functions relax cross-execution-space call restrictions. A `__device__` or `__global__` function can call a `__host__ consteval` function, and a `__host__` function can call a `__device__ consteval` function.
facts.note: None. The provided evidence aligns with standard CUDA C++ dialect restrictions and does not contradict existing target meaning.

caveats: Module and coroutine support is explicitly blocked in device code, with modules blocked entirely across both host and device.
Three-way comparison operator usage may trigger host-STL dependencies that require experimental constexpr relaxation flags.
Consteval functions are the primary mechanism for bypassing standard cross-execution-space call restrictions.
recommended_structure: Overview of C++20 dialect support in nvcc

concept/cuda-cpp-language-restrictions.title: CUDA C++ Language Restrictions and Features
concept/cuda-cpp-language-restrictions.summary: This section details the support and restrictions for various C++ language features within CUDA, including class members, templates, const-qualified variables, attributes (deprecated, noreturn, likely/unlikely, const/pure), and specific C++11 and C++14 features such as lambda expressions, constexpr, inline namespaces, and variable templates. It also covers platform-specific constraints like those for the Microsoft and Intel host compilers.
aliases: CUDA C++ Attributes and Type Restrictions

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence covers Section 18.5.10.8 through 18.5.23.2 of the CUDA C++ Programming Guide. It details language-level restrictions and feature support for C++ classes, templates, execution space specifiers, and C++11/C++14 language features within the CUDA compilation pipeline.

facts.claim: Operator functions and allocation/deallocation functions cannot be __global__, and user-defined operators cannot replace compiler builtins for __host__ or __device__ code.
facts.note: C++ operator overloading and memory management are restricted to prevent undefined execution space transitions.

facts.claim: Class static data members are unsupported unless const-qualified. Static member functions cannot be __global__.
facts.note: Aligns with device memory model constraints where static storage typically maps to __device__ or __constant__ space.

facts.claim: Virtual function overrides require matching execution space specifiers (__host__/__device__). Passing objects with virtual functions or virtual base classes to __global__ functions is prohibited. Cross-execution space virtual calls (host-created in device, device-created in host) yield undefined behavior.
facts.note: Vtable layout and object lifetime across memory domains are not safely managed by the CUDA runtime.

facts.claim: Namespace-scope anonymous union members cannot be referenced in __global__ or __device__ functions.
facts.note: Restriction prevents host-only symbol resolution in device compilation.

facts.claim: Template arguments for __global__ instantiations or __device__/__constant__ variable instantiations cannot be types defined in __host__ or __host__ __device__ scopes, private/protected class members (unless parent is __device__/__global__), unnamed types, or compounds thereof.
facts.note: Ensures template instantiation occurs in a valid device compilation context.

facts.claim: Trigraphs are unsupported on all platforms; digraphs are unsupported on Windows.
facts.note: Standard C++ compatibility note.

facts.claim: Host const-qualified variables can be used in device code if initialized with constant expressions, non-volatile, and of built-in float/int types (except MSVC float). Taking their address or reference in device code is forbidden.
facts.note: Allows compile-time constant propagation without requiring explicit __constant__ memory allocation.

facts.claim: The long double type is not supported in device code.
facts.note: Hardware/ABI limitation on GPU architectures.

facts.claim: Deprecation attributes ([[deprecated]], __deprecated__, deprecated declspec) are supported. CUDA frontend handles device deprecation diagnostics; host compiler handles host. Host compiler pragmas do not affect CUDA frontend warnings.
facts.note: Dual-compiler pipeline requires separate warning suppression mechanisms.

facts.claim: [[likely]] and [[unlikely]] attributes are supported for optimizer hints. May trigger unknown attribute warnings on older host compilers.
facts.note: Standard C++17 feature adapted for device code optimization.

facts.claim: GNU const and pure attributes are supported for device functions to hint the optimizer about mutable state access.
facts.note: Enables aggressive inlining and dead code elimination in device code.

facts.claim: __nv_pure__ attribute is supported for host/device, translating to pure GNU or MSVC noalias on host.
facts.note: NVIDIA-specific cross-platform attribute for stateless function hints.

facts.claim: Intel host compiler support relies on __INTEL_COMPILER_USE_INTRINSIC_PROTOTYPES macro to expose intrinsic function declarations to nvcc.
facts.note: Preprocessing workaround for Intel-specific intrinsics.

facts.claim: Lambda execution space specifiers are derived from the enclosing function scope. Lambda closure types cannot be used in __global__ template arguments unless defined inside __device__/__global__ functions or are extended lambdas.
facts.note: Closure type serialization across execution spaces is restricted.

facts.claim: std::initializer_list member functions are implicitly __host__ __device__ by default. std::move/std::forward are similarly implicit. Both behaviors can be disabled via --no-host-device-initializer-list and --no-host-device-move-forward.
facts.note: Default device compatibility for C++11 standard library utilities.

facts.claim: constexpr functions cannot be called across incompatible execution spaces by default. The --expt-relaxed-constexpr flag removes this restriction.
facts.note: Experimental flag enables cross-domain constexpr evaluation.

facts.claim: Host constexpr variables can be used in device code if scalar, non-volatile, and not long double. Address/reference taking is forbidden.
facts.note: Extends const-variable propagation rules to C++11 constexpr.

facts.claim: Inline namespaces cause host compiler ambiguity when CUDA injects references to __global__/__device__/__constant__/texture/surface entities. Inline unnamed namespaces cannot declare __managed__, __device__, __shared__, __constant__, __global__, or texture/surface variables.
facts.note: Namespace resolution conflicts between CUDA frontend and host compiler pipelines.

facts.claim: thread_local storage specifier is prohibited in device code.
facts.note: GPU thread model does not support host-style thread-local storage.

facts.claim: __global__ functions cannot be constexpr, cannot accept std::initializer_list, va_list, or rvalue reference parameters. Variadic __global__ templates allow only one pack parameter, which must be last.
facts.note: Kernel launch semantics and parameter passing limitations.

facts.claim: __managed__ and __shared__ variables cannot be constexpr. Execution space specifiers on non-virtual functions explicitly defaulted on first declaration are ignored (inferred instead).
facts.note: Memory space and function generation rules override explicit annotations in defaulted cases.

facts.claim: __global__ functions cannot have deduced return types. __device__ functions with deduced return types cause compilation errors if referenced outside device bodies. __device__/__constant__ variable templates cannot have const-qualified types on Windows.
facts.note: C++14 feature restrictions tied to device compilation and Windows ABI constraints.

caveats: Windows-specific class layout mismatches (IA64 ABI vs MSVC) cause undefined behavior when passing complex types (virtual functions, virtual bases, multiple inheritance with empty bases) between host and device.
Deprecation and warning suppression mechanisms differ between the CUDA frontend compiler and host compilers; host pragmas do not suppress device warnings.
C++11/14 constexpr and deduced return type features require careful scoping to avoid host-device introspection failures.

concept/cuda-cpp-language-support.title: CUDA C++ Language Support
concept/cuda-cpp-language-support.summary: Support matrix and restrictions for C++11, C++14, C++17, and C++20 features in CUDA device code, including __CUDA_ARCH__ constraints.

research_reports.context_summary: Chapter 18 of the CUDA C Programming Guide detailing C++ language support in nvcc, including version-specific feature matrices, host extension restrictions, and strict preprocessor constraints around the __CUDA_ARCH__ macro.

facts.claim: nvcc mixes host and device code and emulates host compiler behavior for C++ input, processing C++ ISO/IEC 14882:2003, 2011, 2014, and 2017 specifications while extending them with CUDA constructs.
facts.note: This guide is marked as legacy as of CUDA 13.0 and replaced by the new CUDA Programming Guide.

facts.claim: C++11 features (e.g., rvalue references, variadic templates, lambdas, auto, initializer lists) are supported in device code starting from nvcc 7.0. Concurrency features (atomics, memory model, thread-local storage) are explicitly unsupported in device code.
facts.note: Table 23 lists C++11 features and their nvcc device code availability.

facts.claim: C++14 features (e.g., binary literals, generalized lambda capture, variable templates, [[deprecated]] attribute) are supported in device code starting from nvcc 9.0.
facts.note: Table 24 lists C++14 features and their nvcc device code availability.

facts.claim: C++17 features are supported in nvcc 11.0 and later, and C++20 features are supported in nvcc 12.0 and later.
facts.note: Both sections note that support is subject to restrictions described elsewhere in the guide.

facts.claim: Host compiler-specific language extensions are unsupported in device code. __Complex types are host-only. __int128 is supported in device code only if the host compiler supports it. __float128 is supported for compute capability 10.0+ with host compiler support, though constant expressions may lose precision.
facts.note: Section 18.5.1 covers host compiler extensions and type restrictions.

facts.claim: The type signatures of __global__ functions/templates, __device__/__constant__ variables, and textures/surfaces cannot depend on the definition or value of __CUDA_ARCH__.
facts.note: Section 18.5.2.1 outlines __CUDA_ARCH__ restrictions.

facts.claim: __global__ function templates launched from the host must instantiate with identical template arguments regardless of whether __CUDA_ARCH__ is defined.
facts.note: Example demonstrates error when instantiation depends on __CUDA_ARCH__.

facts.claim: In separate compilation, definitions of functions/variables with external linkage cannot depend on __CUDA_ARCH__. Using __CUDA_ARCH__ in headers to alter behavior across objects compiled for different compute architectures can cause link-time conflicts.
facts.note: Compiler does not guarantee diagnostics for unsupported __CUDA_ARCH__ usage patterns.

caveats: The source document is explicitly marked as legacy as of CUDA 13.0 and has been replaced by the new CUDA Programming Guide. Feature support matrices and restrictions may have evolved in newer releases.

Concurrency-related C++11 features (atomics, memory model, thread-local storage) are listed in the source table without nvcc device code version numbers, indicating they are unsupported in device code.
__float128 support is strictly limited to compute capability 10.0+ and requires host compiler support; constant expressions may be downgraded in precision.
contradictions: None detected within the assigned evidence. The document is self-consistent regarding version requirements and preprocessor constraints.

concept/cuda-cubemap-layered-surfaces.title: Cubemap Layered Surfaces
concept/cuda-cubemap-layered-surfaces.summary: Describes cubemap layered surface access using surfCubemapLayeredread() and surfCubemapLayeredwrite() as a two-dimensional layered surface, explaining index calculation for faces across multiple cubemaps.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence sits within the 'Surface Memory' section (6.2.14.2) of the CUDA C Programming Guide. It directly follows the single-cubemap surface subsection (6.2.14.2.2) and precedes the general CUDA Arrays and Read/Write Coherency sections. The surrounding text establishes that surface memory requires compute capability 2.0+, relies on the `cudaArraySurfaceLoadStore` flag, uses byte addressing, and lacks intra-kernel cache coherency with global memory.

facts.claim: Cubemap layered surfaces are accessed using the `surfCubemapLayeredread()` and `surfCubemapLayeredwrite()` functions, treating the data as a two-dimensional layered surface.
facts.note: Citations use the exact format requested.

facts.claim: Addressing a cubemap layered surface requires an integer index to specify a face within a cubemap layer, plus two floating-point texture coordinates to target a texel within that face.
facts.note: Citations use the exact format requested.

facts.claim: The integer index follows a specific face ordering (referenced as Table 6 in the guide). The index is calculated as `(cubemap_index * 6) + face_index`. For example, an index of `((2 * 6) + 3)` targets the fourth face (index 3) of the third cubemap (index 2).
facts.note: Citations use the exact format requested.

facts.claim: This feature is part of the broader Surface Memory subsystem, requiring compute capability 2.0+ and CUDA arrays created with the `cudaArraySurfaceLoadStore` flag.
facts.note: Citations use the exact format requested.

facts.claim: Surface memory uses byte addressing. When comparing surface access to texture access, the x-coordinate must be multiplied by the element's byte size.
facts.note: Citations use the exact format requested.

facts.claim: Read/Write coherency rules apply: texture and surface memory caches are not kept coherent with global memory writes or surface writes within the same kernel call. Accessing a location written to by the same kernel call returns undefined data.
facts.note: Citations use the exact format requested.

caveats: The guide references 'Table 6' for face ordering but does not include the table itself in the provided context window.
The text notes that surface memory uses byte addressing, which is a key distinction from texture memory's texel indexing.

concept/cuda-cubemap-layered-textures.title: Cubemap Layered Textures
concept/cuda-cubemap-layered-textures.summary: Defines cubemap layered textures as layered textures where layers are cubemaps. Covers addressing with integer cubemap indices and three texture coordinates, CUDA array creation with cudaArrayLayered and cudaArrayCubemap flags, texCubemapLayered() fetch function, filtering scope, and compute capability 2.0+ requirement.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence (Section 6.2.14.1.5) defines cubemap layered textures within the broader CUDA texture memory hierarchy. It sits between standard layered textures (1D/2D) and single cubemap textures, extending the layering concept to cubemap geometry. The surrounding context also notes that corresponding surface memory operations exist for cubemap layered data (L3980-L3982), though the assigned evidence focuses strictly on the texture fetch API.

facts.claim: Definition and Structure
facts.note: A cubemap layered texture is a layered texture where each layer is a cubemap of the same dimensions.

facts.claim: Addressing
facts.note: Addressing requires an integer index to select the cubemap within the sequence, and three floating-point texture coordinates to locate a texel within that specific cubemap.

facts.claim: Allocation
facts.note: Must be allocated as a CUDA array using cudaMalloc3DArray() with both the cudaArrayLayered and cudaArrayCubemap flags.

facts.claim: Fetching
facts.note: Fetched using the texCubemapLayered() device function.

facts.claim: Filtering Behavior
facts.note: Texture filtering is applied only within a single layer (cubemap) and does not interpolate across different cubemap layers.

facts.claim: Hardware Support
facts.note: Supported on devices with compute capability 2.0 and higher.

caveats: While the assigned evidence focuses on the texture read API, the surrounding context (L3980-L3982) indicates that cubemap layered data can also be accessed via surface memory using surfCubemapLayeredread() and surfCubemapLayeredwrite().

concept/cuda-cubemap-surfaces.title: Cubemap Surfaces
concept/cuda-cubemap-surfaces.summary: Describes cubemap surface access using surfCubemapread() and surfCubemapwrite() as a two-dimensional layered surface, referencing face ordering from Table 6.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence defines the base cubemap surface access pattern within the broader Surface Memory API. It sits between the general Surface Object API (byte-addressed CUDA arrays) and Cubemap Layered Surfaces (multi-cubemap arrays).

facts.claim: Cubemap surfaces are accessed via the device functions surfCubemapread() and surfCubemapwrite().
facts.note: The source text contains a typographical repetition of the function names in parentheses; this should be cleaned up in the final page.

facts.claim: They are abstracted as a two-dimensional layered surface.
facts.note: This aligns with the general surface API structure where 2D surfaces use (x, y) coordinates.

facts.claim: Access requires an integer index to denote the cubemap face, followed by two floating-point texture coordinates to address a texel within that face's layer.
facts.note: This differs from standard 2D surfaces which only use two coordinates, and from layered cubemaps which add a layer index for multiple cubemaps.

facts.claim: The ordering of the six cubemap faces follows the convention specified in Table 6 of the guide.
facts.note: The lead writer should locate Table 6 in the source to document the exact face order (e.g., +X, -X, +Y, -Y, +Z, -Z).

caveats: No contradictions found. The scope is strictly limited to single-cubemap surfaces, distinct from the immediately following Cubemap Layered Surfaces section.
All surface operations implicitly operate on byte-addressed CUDA arrays, as established in the parent Surface Object API section.

concept/cuda-cubemap-textures.title: Cubemap Textures
concept/cuda-cubemap-textures.summary: Describes cubemap textures as a special two-dimensional layered texture with six faces. Details addressing via direction vectors, face selection logic, coordinate mapping (Table 6), CUDA array creation with cudaArray-Cubemap, texCubemap() fetch function, and compute capability 2.0+ requirement.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence covers section 6.2.14.1.4, which defines cubemap textures, their addressing scheme via direction vectors, creation flags, fetch functions, and compute capability requirements. It sits between layered textures (6.2.14.1.3) and cubemap layered textures (6.2.14.1.5), establishing the base cubemap concept before extending to arrays of cubemaps.

facts.claim: A cubemap texture is a specialized two-dimensional layered texture consisting of six layers, each representing a face of a cube. Each layer must have equal width and height.
facts.note: Directly defines structure and layer count.

facts.claim: Cubemaps are addressed using three texture coordinates (x, y, z) treated as a direction vector from the cube's center. The target face is determined by the coordinate with the largest absolute magnitude `m`. The 2D coordinates within that face are calculated as `(s/m+1)/2` and `(t/m+1)/2`, with `s` and `t` values dictated by the dominant axis and its sign.
facts.note: Summarizes the directional addressing logic and coordinate transformation table.

facts.claim: Cubemap textures must be created as CUDA arrays using `cudaMalloc3DArray()` with the `cudaArrayCubemap` flag. Fetching is performed via the `texCubemap()` device function.
facts.note: Covers API requirements for allocation and sampling.

facts.claim: Cubemap textures are only supported on devices with compute capability 2.0 or higher.
facts.note: Hardware constraint.

caveats: The source text contains a typographical artifact in Table 6 (`x □ 0` instead of `x > 0`), but the logical structure for face selection remains clear and is accurately captured in the summary.
contradictions: None detected. The scope is strictly limited to base cubemap textures, distinct from cubemap layered textures (L3851-L3861) and surface memory (L3873+).

concept/cuda-device-memory.title: CUDA Device Memory
concept/cuda-device-memory.summary: Explains device memory allocation (linear memory vs CUDA arrays), address space sizes, cudaMalloc/cudaFree/cudaMemcpy usage, pitch/3D allocations, and global/constant symbol access.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence corresponds to Section 6.2.2 'Device Memory' within the CUDA Runtime chapter. It serves as the core reference for device memory management, bridging runtime initialization (6.2.1) and advanced L2 cache access management (6.2.3). The text establishes foundational APIs, address space limits, and allocation strategies required before discussing performance optimizations.

facts.claim: The CUDA programming model assumes separate host and device memory, with kernels operating exclusively on device memory.
facts.note: Device memory is allocated as either linear memory (unified address space allowing cross-referencing via pointers) or CUDA arrays (opaque layouts optimized for texture fetching). CUDA arrays are detailed in the Texture and Surface Memory documentation.

facts.claim: Address space limits depend on the host system and GPU compute capability. For CC ≤ 5.3, the space is 40-bit across x86_64, POWER, and ARM64, with an uncommitted 40-bit virtual address reservation. For CC ≥ 6.0, it expands to up to 47-bit (x86_64), 49-bit (POWER), and 48-bit (ARM64).
facts.note: Standard linear memory uses cudaMalloc() and cudaFree(). 2D/3D arrays should use cudaMallocPitch() and cudaMalloc3D() to ensure hardware alignment padding. These return a pitch/stride that must be used for element access and paired with cudaMemcpy2D()/cudaMemcpy3D() for transfers.

facts.claim: Host-to-device and device-to-host transfers are handled by cudaMemcpy().
facts.note: Host code can manipulate __device__ and __constant__ variables via runtime APIs: cudaMemcpyToSymbol(), cudaMemcpyFromSymbol(), cudaGetSymbolAddress(), and cudaGetSymbolSize().

facts.claim: Allocation requests should be based on problem size to avoid impacting system-wide performance. Failures should trigger fallbacks to slower memory (e.g., cudaMallocHost) or explicit error reporting. cudaMallocManaged() is recommended when exact allocation parameters cannot be determined upfront.
facts.note: All facts derived from the assigned evidence block.

caveats: The source text uses 'cudaMallocPitch()' and 'cudaMalloc3D()' for 2D/3D allocations, emphasizing pitch/stride usage. This differs from modern unified memory approaches but is the documented standard for this guide section.
Address space limits are explicitly tied to compute capability and host architecture, which may vary for newer architectures not covered in this specific excerpt.
contradictions: None detected. The evidence aligns with standard CUDA runtime memory management concepts.

concept/cuda-direct3d-12-interoperabilit.title: CUDA-Direct3D 12 Interoperability
concept/cuda-direct3d-12-interoperabilit.summary: Covers Direct3D 12 interoperability with CUDA, including device LUID matching, importing memory objects, mapping buffers and mipmapped arrays, and importing/signaling synchronization objects.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned source covers Section 6.2.16.3 (Direct3D 12 Interoperability) of the CUDA C Programming Guide, detailing device matching, memory/synchronization object import, mapping, and async signaling/waiting workflows.

facts.claim: CUDA and D3D12 objects must be imported and mapped on the same physical device. Match them by comparing `cudaDeviceProp.luid` with `ID3D12Device::GetAdapterLuid()`. The D3D12 device must not be a linked node adapter (`GetNodeCount() == 1`).
facts.note: Handle ownership and resource references must be managed by the application; NT handles hold references and must be closed via `CloseHandle()` after import.

facts.claim: D3D12 heaps (`D3D12_HEAP_FLAG_SHARED` on `CreateHeap`) and committed resources (`D3D12_HEAP_FLAG_SHARED` on `CreateCommittedResource`) are imported via `cudaImportExternalMemory()`. Committed resources require the `cudaExternalMemoryDedicated` flag. Both support NT and named handles.
facts.note: Mapped buffers are created with `cudaExternalMemoryGetMappedBuffer()` and freed with `cudaFree()`. Mipmapped arrays use `cudaExternalMemoryGetMappedMipmappedArray()` and are freed with `cudaFreeMipmappedArray()`.

facts.claim: Mapping mipmapped arrays requires matching D3D12 offset, dimensions, format, and mip levels. If the resource is a D3D12 render target, set `cudaArrayColorAttachment`. Helper functions map DXGI formats to `cudaChannelFormatDesc`, `D3D12_SRV_DIMENSION` to `cudaExtent`, and D3D12 resource flags to CUDA array flags (e.g., `cudaArrayCubemap`, `cudaArrayLayered`, `cudaArraySurfaceLoadStore`).
facts.note: D3D12 fences (`D3D12_FENCE_FLAG_SHARED` on `CreateFence`) are imported via `cudaImportExternalSemaphore()` using NT or named handles (`cudaExternalSemaphoreHandleTypeD3D12Fence`).

facts.claim: Synchronization uses `cudaSignalExternalSemaphoresAsync()` and `cudaWaitExternalSemaphoresAsync()`. Signaling sets the D3D12 fence value; waiting blocks until the fence value is >= the specified value. Cross-API ordering must be maintained (D3D12 signal before CUDA wait, etc.).
facts.note: All async operations are tied to a `cudaStream_t`.

caveats: The source explicitly notes that the D3D12 device must not be created on a linked node adapter.
Handle ownership rules differ slightly between handle types; NT handles hold references and require explicit `CloseHandle()` calls, while named handles rely on system naming conventions.
Format and extent conversion helpers are provided as reference code; actual implementation must align with the specific DXGI formats and resource dimensions used.

concept/cuda-direct3d-9-interoperability.title: Direct3D 9 Version
concept/cuda-direct3d-9-interoperability.summary: Provides a complete code sample for Direct3D 9 interoperability. Covers D3D device creation, CUDA device selection, vertex buffer registration, mapping/unmapping, kernel execution for vertex modification, and resource cleanup.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence covers section 6.2.15.2.1, providing a complete C/C++ code walkthrough for registering, mapping, modifying, and releasing a Direct3D 9 vertex buffer via CUDA graphics interop. It sits within the broader Direct3D interoperability section (6.2.15.2) that also covers D3D 10 and 11.

facts.claim: Adapter Discovery
facts.note: Initialize Direct3D 9 using `Direct3DCreate9Ex`, then iterate through adapters via `GetAdapterIdentifier` to locate a CUDA-compatible device using `cudaD3D9GetDevice`.

facts.claim: Device Creation Requirements
facts.note: Create the Direct3D device with `CreateDeviceEx`, explicitly requiring `D3DDEVTYPE_HAL` and the `D3DCREATE_HARDWARE_VERTEXPROCESSING` flag to ensure hardware acceleration and successful interop.

facts.claim: Resource Registration
facts.note: Allocate a vertex buffer via `CreateVertexBuffer`, register it with the CUDA interop layer using `cudaGraphicsD3D9RegisterResource`, and configure access behavior (e.g., `cudaGraphicsMapFlagsWriteDiscard`) via `cudaGraphicsResourceSetMapFlags`.

facts.claim: Mapping & Execution Workflow
facts.note: In the rendering loop, map the resource with `cudaGraphicsMapResources`, retrieve the device pointer via `cudaGraphicsResourceGetMappedPointer`, launch the CUDA kernel, and unmap it with `cudaGraphicsUnmapResources`.

facts.claim: Kernel Implementation
facts.note: The example kernel (`createVertices`) computes normalized UV coordinates, applies a time-varying sine wave displacement, and writes `float4` vertex data directly to the mapped buffer.

facts.claim: Cleanup Procedure
facts.note: Properly release resources by calling `cudaGraphicsUnregisterResource` followed by the Direct3D object's `Release()` method.

caveats: The code assumes a pre-existing window handle (`hWnd`) and rendering loop structure, focusing solely on the interop lifecycle.
Error handling and COM reference counting beyond the initial creation/release are omitted in the snippet.
The example uses `D3DPOOL_DEFAULT` for the vertex buffer, which is standard for GPU-resident resources but requires explicit mapping for CUDA access.

concept/cuda-direct3d-interoperability.title: Direct3D Interoperability
concept/cuda-direct3d-interoperability.summary: Covers Direct3D 9Ex, 10, and 11 interoperability. Specifies device creation requirements (HAL, hardware vertex processing, D3D_DRIVER_TYPE_HARDWARE). Lists registered resources (buffers, textures, surfaces) and registration functions (cudaGraphicsD3D9/10/11RegisterResource).

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence is the header/introductory block for section 6.2.15.2 'Direct3D Interoperability'. It sits between the general CUDA graphics interoperability workflow (lines 3997-4002) and the OpenGL interoperability section (6.2.15.1), serving as the direct bridge to the Direct3D 9 code sample that follows (lines 4128-4247).

facts.claim: Direct3D interoperability is supported for Direct3D 9Ex, Direct3D 10, and Direct3D 11.
facts.note: Supported API versions.

facts.claim: Direct3D 9Ex devices must be created with DeviceType set to D3DDEVTYPE_HAL and BehaviorFlags including D3DCREATE_HARDWARE_VERTEXPROCESSING.
facts.note: D3D9Ex hardware requirement.

facts.claim: Direct3D 10 and 11 devices must be created with DriverType set to D3_DRIVER_TYPE_HARDWARE.
facts.note: D3D10/11 hardware requirement.

facts.claim: Direct3D buffers, textures, and surfaces can be mapped into the CUDA address space.
facts.note: Mappable resource types.

facts.claim: Resources are registered using cudaGraphicsD3D9RegisterResource(), cudaGraphicsD3D10RegisterResource(), and cudaGraphicsD3D11RegisterResource().
facts.note: Registration API functions.

caveats: The assigned evidence covers only the registration and device setup phase. Mapping, unmapping, and kernel access follow the general interoperability workflow outlined earlier in the document (e.g., cudaGraphicsMapResources, cudaGraphicsUnmapResources, cudaGraphicsResourceGetMappedPointer).
No contradictions or scope clashes detected. Content aligns precisely with the target page's scope.
recommended_structure: Mapping & Access Workflow (referencing general interop section)

concept/cuda-driver-api.title: CUDA Driver API
concept/cuda-driver-api.summary: Overview of the low-level handle-based imperative API (cu* prefix), object model (devices, contexts, modules, kernels, memory, streams, events), initialization requirements, PTX vs binary loading, and a complete host code example for kernel execution.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence covers Chapter 21 (Driver API) of the CUDA C Programming Guide. It details the API's implementation, core objects, initialization flow, context management, module loading, and kernel execution, accompanied by a complete C code sample demonstrating the standard workflow.

facts.claim: The CUDA Driver API is a low-level, handle-based, imperative interface implemented in the `cuda` dynamic library (`cuda.dll` or `cuda.so`), with all entry points prefixed by `cu`.
facts.note: Legacy Status: The source explicitly warns that this documentation is legacy as of CUDA 13.0 and has been replaced by the new CUDA Programming Guide.

facts.claim: The API manages several core objects, each referenced by an opaque handle: Device (`CUdevice`), Context (`CUcontext`), Module (`CUmodule`), Function (`CUfunction`), Heap memory (`CUdeviceptr`), CUDA array (`CUarray`), Texture object (`CUtexref`), Surface reference (`CUsurfref`), Stream (`CUstream`), and Event (`CUevent`).
facts.note: Contexts encapsulate resources, maintain distinct address spaces, and are analogous to CPU processes.

facts.claim: Initialization requires calling `cuInit()` first, followed by `cuCtxCreate()` to attach a context to a specific device and make it current to the calling host thread.
facts.note: Contexts maintain a usage count and can be pushed/popped per thread for interoperability.

facts.claim: Kernels are loaded as PTX or binary objects via modules. PTX is recommended over binary code to ensure forward compatibility, as the driver compiles PTX to architecture-specific binary at load time.
facts.note: Modules maintain symbol scope, allowing third-party code to interoperate within the same context.

facts.claim: Kernel execution is handled by `cuLaunchKernel()`, which takes execution configuration (grid/block dimensions), parameters, and optional launch parameters.
facts.note: A complete C code sample demonstrates the standard workflow: `cuInit` → `cuDeviceGet` → `cuCtxCreate` → `cuModuleLoad` → `cuMemAlloc` → `cuMemcpyHtoD` → `cuModuleGetFunction` → `cuLaunchKernel`.

caveats: The source explicitly marks this API documentation as legacy (CUDA 13.0+) and directs users to the new CUDA Programming Guide. This should be noted in the target page's introduction or deprecation notice.

The provided code sample uses legacy C-style casting and error handling; modern examples typically use the CUDA Runtime API or C++ wrappers, but the Driver API workflow remains structurally relevant for low-level control.

concept/cuda-dynamic-parallelism.title: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism
concept/cuda-dynamic-parallelism.summary: Comprehensive guide to CUDA Dynamic Parallelism, including overview, glossary, execution environment, memory model (global, zero-copy, constant, shared/local, texture), programming interface, device-side kernel launch syntax, asynchronous behavior, and launch environment configuration inheritance.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence covers Chapter 13 of the CUDA C Programming Guide, detailing the introduction, execution environment, memory model, and programming interface for CUDA Dynamic Parallelism. It defines parent/child grid relationships, memory coherence rules, device-side launch syntax, and stream/event handling, while noting deprecations like device-side `cudaDeviceSynchronize()`.

facts.claim: Dynamic Parallelism extends the CUDA programming model to allow kernels to launch and synchronize with new work directly on the GPU, reducing host-device transfers and enabling runtime, data-dependent parallelism.
facts.note: Supported on compute capability 3.5+ devices.

facts.claim: A grid that launches another is the 'Parent'; the newly created grid is the 'Child'. Execution is properly nested, meaning the parent is not complete until all children finish, guaranteeing implicit synchronization.
facts.note: None

facts.claim: CUDA primitives (streams, events, launches) are visible to all threads within a grid, but host-created streams/events have undefined behavior when used in device code.
facts.note: None

facts.claim: Parent and child grids share global and constant memory but have distinct local and shared memory. Global memory is coherent with weak consistency; a child sees all parent writes prior to its invocation.
facts.note: None

facts.claim: Parent grids cannot directly read modifications made by child grids. Accessing child modifications before the parent exits requires launching a kernel into the `cudaStreamTailLaunch` stream.
facts.note: None

facts.claim: Shared and local memory are private to their thread blocks/threads and are not coherent between parent and child. Passing pointers to local/shared memory to a child kernel is illegal and causes undefined behavior.
facts.note: The `__is-Global()` intrinsic can verify pointer validity before launch.

facts.claim: The Device Runtime provides APIs similar to the host-side CUDA Runtime API, enabling per-thread dynamic decision-making without deadlock risks.
facts.note: None

facts.claim: Device-side kernel launches use the standard `<<< >>>` syntax, are asynchronous, and inherit global device configurations (cache size, limits) from the parent.
facts.note: None

facts.claim: Explicit synchronization with child kernels via `cudaDeviceSynchronize()` in device code is deprecated in CUDA 11.6 and removed for compute capability 9.0+.
facts.note: Compile-time opt-in required for older architectures.

facts.claim: Device-side events must be created with the `cudaEventDisableTiming` flag, and only inter-stream synchronization (`cudaStreamWaitEvent`) is supported.
facts.note: None

facts.claim: The `cudaStreamFireAndForget` and `cudaStreamTailLaunch` streams provide specialized scheduling: fire-and-forget launches run independently without tracking overhead, while tail launches guarantee execution after all parent work completes.
facts.note: None

caveats: The source explicitly notes that `cudaDeviceSynchronize()` in device code is deprecated and slated for removal, shifting reliance to implicit synchronization and the `cudaStreamTailLaunch` stream for parent-child coordination.
Concurrency between different thread blocks or between parent and child grids is not guaranteed; programs must not depend on it.
Device runtime lacks multi-GPU support and cannot modify host-created streams/events.

concept/cuda-dynamic-parallelism-api-ref.title: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism API Reference
concept/cuda-dynamic-parallelism-api-ref.summary: Overview table of CUDA Runtime API functions supported in the device runtime.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence is the core reference table for the device runtime API subset used in dynamic parallelism. It follows conceptual sections on device management, memory, and error handling, and precedes PTX-level launch details. The table explicitly lists supported host runtime functions and notes semantic deviations when running from device code.

facts.claim: The device runtime exposes a functional subset of the CUDA Runtime API with identical syntax to the host version; semantics match unless explicitly noted.
facts.note: Error state is tracked per-thread, not per-block, for `cudaGetLastError()` and `cudaPeekAtLastError()`.

facts.claim: `cudaGetDevice()` always returns the current device ID as seen from the host. `cudaDeviceGetAttribute()` can query attributes for any device.
facts.note: Other device management functions like `cudaDeviceGetCacheConfig()`, `cudaDeviceGetLimit()`, and `cudaGetDeviceCount()` are supported without behavioral changes.

facts.claim: Stream and event creation require specific flags: `cudaStreamCreateWithFlags()` must pass `cudaStreamNonBlocking`, and `cudaEventCreateWithFlags()` must pass `cudaEventDisableTiming`.
facts.note: Synchronization and lifecycle functions (`cudaStreamWaitEvent()`, `cudaEventRecord()`, `cudaStreamDestroy()`, `cudaEventDestroy()`) are supported.

facts.claim: Only asynchronous memory functions are supported: `cudaMemcpyAsync`, `cudaMemcpy2DAsync`, `cudaMemcpy3DAsync`, `cudaMemsetAsync`, `cudaMemset2DAsync`, and `cudaMemset3DAsync`.
facts.note: These functions are restricted to device-to-device transfers and cannot accept local or shared memory pointers.

facts.claim: `cudaMalloc()` and `cudaFree()` are supported, but host-allocated pointers cannot be freed on the device, and device-allocated pointers cannot be freed on the host.
facts.note: Occupancy query functions (`cudaOccupancyMaxActiveBlocksPerMulti-processor`, `cudaOccupancyMaxPotentialBlockSize`, `cudaOccupancyMaxPotentialBlockSize-VariableSMem()`) and `cudaFuncGetAttributes()` are supported.

caveats: The table explicitly notes that semantics are identical to the host API unless stated otherwise. Any function not listed here is implicitly unsupported in the device runtime context.
The per-thread error tracking for `cudaGetLastError()` differs from the host's per-block tracking, which is critical for debugging concurrent device-side launches.
contradictions: None detected. The evidence aligns with the broader dynamic parallelism context, which restricts device-side capabilities to a safe, asynchronous subset of the host runtime.
recommended_structure: Overview of Device Runtime API Subset

concept/cuda-dynamic-parallelism-cdp1.title: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism Legacy (CDP1)
concept/cuda-dynamic-parallelism-cdp1.summary: Legacy CUDA Dynamic Parallelism (CDP1) execution environment, memory model, synchronization, and programming interface.

research_reports.context_summary: The provided context covers the legacy CUDA Dynamic Parallelism (CDP1) interface, detailing its execution model, memory semantics, programming interface, toolkit requirements, implementation limits, and its relationship with the newer CDP2 interface. It heavily emphasizes the deprecation of explicit device-side synchronization (`cudaDeviceSynchronize()`).

facts.claim: CDP1 is the legacy interface for CUDA Dynamic Parallelism. Explicit device-side synchronization via `cudaDeviceSynchronize()` is deprecated in CUDA 11.6, removed for compute capability 9.0+ compilation, and slated for full removal.
facts.note: This deprecation warning is repeated throughout the CDP1 sections.

facts.claim: CDP1 uses properly nested parent/child grids. A parent grid is not considered complete until all its child grids finish, guaranteeing implicit synchronization between them.
facts.note: Implicit sync occurs even without explicit `cudaDeviceSynchronize()` calls.

facts.claim: CUDA primitives (streams, events, kernel launches) are scoped to the thread block. They are visible to all threads within the block but cannot be shared across blocks or passed between parent/child kernels.
facts.note: Using a stream handle in a block that did not allocate it results in undefined behavior.

facts.claim: `cudaDeviceSynchronize()` waits for all work launched by any thread in the calling block. It does not perform intra-block synchronization, so `__syncthreads()` is required if multiple threads launch work that needs to be synchronized together.
facts.note: Simultaneous calls to `cudaDeviceSynchronize()` by multiple threads may drain work in the first call, leaving subsequent calls with no effect.

facts.claim: Device streams must be created with `cudaStreamCreateWithFlags()` using the `cudaStreamNonBlocking` flag. The implicit NULL stream does not have cross-stream barrier semantics. Events can only be used for inter-stream synchronization (`cudaStreamWaitEvent()`); timing events are unsupported and must be created with `cudaEventDisableTiming`.
facts.note: `cudaStreamSynchronize()` and `cudaStreamQuery()` are unsupported on the device; `cudaDeviceSynchronize()` must be used instead.

facts.claim: Parent and child grids share global and constant memory, but have distinct local and shared memory. Global memory operations before a child launch are visible to the child, and child operations become visible to the parent after synchronization.
facts.note: Shared and local memory pointers cannot be passed to child kernels or async memory functions.

facts.claim: Kernels are launched from the device using the standard `<<<>>>` syntax, which is asynchronous. The device runtime maps to the host runtime API where possible, inheriting launch environment configurations from the parent.
facts.note: Per-kernel configurations set from the host take precedence and cannot be reconfigured from the device.

facts.claim: Low-level device-side launches use `cudaLaunchDevice()` and `cudaGetParameterBuffer()`. The parameter buffer has a maximum size of 4KB and must be 64-byte aligned.
facts.note: Parameter reordering in the buffer is prohibited.

facts.claim: Programs using CDP1 must link against the static device runtime library (`libcudadevrt` or `cudadevrt.lib`) and require separate compilation (`-rdc=true`).
facts.note: Prototypes for the device runtime API are included automatically.

facts.claim: Maximum nesting depth is 24. Default synchronization depth is 2, configurable via `cudaLimitDevRuntimeSyncDepth`. Reserving memory for parent-grid state during synchronization can consume up to 860MB per generation.
facts.note: If a parent kernel never calls `cudaDeviceSynchronize()`, the system may optimize and require less backing store.

facts.claim: Pending launches are managed via a fixed-size pool (default 2048) and a virtualized pool. Configurable via `cudaLimitDevRuntimePendingLaunchCount`. Device `cudaMalloc()` maps to device-side `malloc()`, limited by `cudaLimitMallocHeapSize`.
facts.note: Host-allocated memory cannot be freed from the device, and vice versa.

facts.claim: CDP2 is the default. Applications can opt-out of CDP2 on devices < CC 9.0 using `-DCUDA_FORCE_CDP1_IF_SUPPORTED`. CDP1 and CDP2 functions can run in the same context but cannot launch each other; attempting to do so results in `cudaErrorCdpVersionMismatch`.
facts.note: CDP1 functions can use CDP1-specific features (e.g., `cudaDeviceSynchronize`), while CDP2 functions use CDP2-specific features (e.g., tail launch).

caveats: The source repeatedly flags `cudaDeviceSynchronize()` usage in device code as deprecated (since CUDA 11.6) and removed for compute capability 9.0+. This is a critical scope/clash point with modern CUDA development and must be highlighted in the target page.
CDP1 and CDP2 are mutually exclusive in the call graph; a CDP1 function cannot launch a CDP2 function and vice versa.
Device-side memory allocation (`cudaMalloc` on device) is constrained by `cudaLimitMallocHeapSize` and is distinct from host-side device memory allocation.

concept/cuda-dynamic-parallelism-cdp2-vs.title: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism CDP2 vs CDP1
concept/cuda-dynamic-parallelism-cdp2-vs.summary: Differences, compatibility, and interoperability between the new (CDP2) and legacy (CDP1) CUDA Dynamic Parallelism interfaces.

research_reports.context_summary: Section 13.5 of the CUDA C Programming Guide explicitly contrasts the legacy CDP1 interface with the newer CDP2 interface. It details architectural shifts (synchronization model, stream scoping, resource pooling), introduces new stream types, and outlines strict compilation flags and interoperability rules between the two versions.

facts.claim: Explicit device-side synchronization (cudaDeviceSynchronize) is removed in CDP2 and on compute capability 9.0+. Implicit synchronization (e.g., tail launches) must be used instead.
facts.note: Attempting to query or set cudaLimitDevRuntimeSyncDepth yields cudaErrorUnsupportedLimit.

facts.claim: CDP2 removes the virtualized pool for pending launches. Developers must explicitly configure cudaLimitDevRuntimePendingLaunchCount to avoid cudaErrorLaunchOutOfBoundsException.
facts.note: The maximum number of concurrent events is strictly capped at twice the pending launch count.

facts.claim: Stream scoping changed from per-thread-block (CDP1) to per-grid (CDP2/CC 9.0+). This enables cross-thread-block stream usage, whereas CDP1 restricts streams to thread-block scope and throws cudaErrorInvalidValue otherwise.
facts.note: None

facts.claim: CDP2 introduces cudaStreamTailLaunch and cudaStreamFireAndForget named streams.
facts.note: None

facts.claim: CDP2 is 64-bit compilation only. It is the default in CUDA 12.0+.
facts.note: Legacy CDP1 can be forced on devices with compute capability < 9.0 using the -DCUDA_FORCE_CDP1_IF_SUPPORTED compiler flag.

facts.claim: Compilation behavior differs by mode: Default compilation fails if cudaDeviceSynchronize is used. Legacy compilation fails on cudaStreamTailLaunch/cudaStreamFireAndForget usage, and fails on cudaDeviceSynchronize for sm_90+.
facts.note: On sm_90+, legacy code referencing cudaDeviceSynchronize will fail to load with cudaErrorSymbolNotFound.

facts.claim: CDP1 and CDP2 functions can coexist in the same context, but cannot launch each other. A mismatch in the call graph results in cudaErrorCdpVersionMismatch at load time.
facts.note: None

caveats: The source treats CDP2 as the modern default and CDP1 as legacy/deprecated. Writers should frame CDP1 as a backward-compatibility path rather than a recommended architecture.
The strict 64-bit-only requirement for CDP2 is a hard constraint that may impact legacy 32-bit codebases.
Event limits are mathematically tied to the pending launch count limit, requiring careful resource budgeting in deep launch trees.
contradictions: None detected. The source is internally consistent and aligns with standard CUDA versioning practices.

concept/cuda-dynamic-parallelism-device-.title: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism Device Management
concept/cuda-dynamic-parallelism-device-.summary: Device management API restrictions and capabilities when running from the device runtime.

research_reports.context_summary: Falls under Section 13.3.1 (Device Runtime API), which details the subset of the CUDA Runtime API available for execution directly from device code, alongside constraints on streams, events, synchronization, and memory.

facts.claim: Scope of Control
facts.note: Device-side kernels can only manage the GPU they are currently executing on; cross-device control from the device is restricted.

facts.claim: Unsupported APIs
facts.note: cudaSetDevice() is explicitly unsupported within the device runtime.

facts.claim: Device Identification
facts.note: cudaGetDevice() returns the exact same device ID as observed from the host system.

facts.claim: Attribute Queries
facts.note: cudaDeviceGetAttribute() is supported and allows querying attributes for any device by specifying a device ID parameter.

facts.claim: Property Queries
facts.note: The legacy cudaGetDeviceProperties() API is not offered by the device runtime; properties must instead be queried individually.

caveats: No contradictions or scope clashes detected. The evidence strictly defines the boundaries of device-side device management APIs.

concept/cuda-dynamic-parallelism-errors.title: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism Errors
concept/cuda-dynamic-parallelism-errors.summary: Error handling, API error codes, and launch failure reporting in the device runtime.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence covers error handling and low-level launch mechanisms within the broader 'Device Runtime' section. The surrounding context (L13894-L14147) details other device runtime constraints and features, such as tail launch streams, event limitations, synchronization rules, and memory declarations, providing the operational environment where these error handling rules apply.

facts.claim: Errors from device-side launches are recorded per-thread and retrieved via cudaGetLastError().
facts.note: Directly supports the target page's focus on error tracking mechanics.

facts.claim: Device-side launches may fail due to invalid arguments or other issues, requiring explicit error checking.
facts.note: Clarifies that launch failures are possible and must be manually checked.

facts.claim: Lack of an immediate error after a launch does not guarantee the child kernel completed successfully.
facts.note: Highlights the asynchronous nature of dynamic parallelism execution.

facts.claim: Device-side exceptions in child grids (e.g., invalid memory access) propagate back to the host.
facts.note: Explains error propagation pathways between device and host.

facts.claim: Kernel launches are exposed via cudaGetParameterBuffer() and cudaLaunchDevice() APIs, which can be called directly from PTX or CUDA.
facts.note: Details the underlying system-level mechanisms for launch setup.

facts.claim: The device-side <<<>>> launch configuration syntax maps directly to these underlying launch APIs.
facts.note: Connects high-level syntax to low-level implementation.

caveats: The source does not specify exact error codes returned by cudaGetLastError() for dynamic parallelism, only the general mechanism and propagation rules.
Error handling is tightly coupled with the device runtime library; host-side error checking does not automatically capture device-side exceptions without explicit propagation.

concept/cuda-dynamic-parallelism-events.title: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism Events
concept/cuda-dynamic-parallelism-events.summary: Inter-stream synchronization capabilities of CUDA events within the device runtime.

research_reports.context_summary: The evidence is extracted from Section 13.3.1 (Device-Side Kernel Launch), specifically subsection 13.3.1.3 Events. It sits alongside discussions on device-side streams (implicit, fire-and-forget, tail launch) and synchronization, detailing how CUDA's event model is constrained when executed from within a device grid. The restrictions mirror the device runtime's broader limitations on explicit host-side synchronization and timing APIs.

facts.claim: Only inter-stream synchronization is supported for device-launched events.
facts.note: cudaStreamWaitEvent() is explicitly supported, while explicit synchronization (cudaEventSynchronize), timing (cudaEventElapsedTime), and querying (cudaEventQuery) are unsupported.

facts.claim: Event creation requires the cudaEventDisableTiming flag.
