facts.note: Because cudaEventElapsedTime() is unsupported, timing must be disabled at creation via cudaEventCreateWithFlags().

facts.claim: Events are grid-local and cannot be passed between kernels.
facts.note: Event objects can be shared among all threads within the creating grid but cannot be passed to parent or child kernels.

facts.claim: Event handles lack cross-grid uniqueness.
facts.note: Handles are not guaranteed to be unique between grids; using a handle in a grid that did not create it results in undefined behavior.

caveats: Device-side events cannot be used for timing measurements or explicit thread synchronization, limiting their use strictly to stream ordering.
Scope is strictly confined to the creating grid, preventing cross-kernel event signaling without additional synchronization mechanisms.
recommended_structure: Scope, visibility, and handle uniqueness rules

concept/cuda-dynamic-parallelism-guideli.title: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism Programming Guidelines
concept/cuda-dynamic-parallelism-guideli.summary: Programming guidelines, syntax, and a Hello World example for dynamic parallelism.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence covers Section 13.4 (Programming Guidelines) and Section 13.4.1 (Basics), providing a high-level overview of the device runtime and a complete 'Hello World' example demonstrating parent-child-tail kernel execution, implicit synchronization, and compilation flags. This sits within a broader document section (L14146-L14327) that also details low-level PTX launch APIs, toolkit linking requirements, configuration limits (e.g., pending launch counts, stack size), and the architectural shift from legacy CDP1 to the modern CDP2 interface.

facts.claim: The device runtime is a functional subset of the host runtime, exposing API-level device management, kernel launching, device memcpy, stream management, and event management.
facts.note: Device runtime syntax and semantics closely mirror the host API, making it familiar to experienced CUDA developers.

facts.claim: Dynamic parallelism programs typically follow a parent-child-tail structure. A parent kernel launches a child kernel, and error states are checked via `cudaGetLastError()`.
facts.note: Implicit synchronization is achieved by launching a tail kernel into the `cudaStreamTailLaunch` named stream, which automatically waits for the child kernel to complete before proceeding.

facts.claim: Host-side synchronization with the parent grid is handled using `cudaDeviceSynchronize()`.
facts.note: Compilation requires the `-rdc=true` flag for relocatable device code and linking against the device runtime library via `-lcudadevrt`.

caveats: The provided guidelines and example reflect the modern CDP2 model, which relies on implicit synchronization (e.g., `cudaStreamTailLaunch`) rather than explicit device-side synchronization (`cudaDeviceSynchronize()`), which is deprecated/removed on newer compute capabilities.

concept/cuda-dynamic-parallelism-launch-.title: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism Launch Setup APIs
concept/cuda-dynamic-parallelism-launch-.summary: Low-level kernel launch APIs (`cudaGetParameterBuffer`, `cudaLaunchDevice`) exposed by the device runtime.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence (L14028-L14041) sits within the broader Dynamic Parallelism device runtime documentation. It specifically addresses the low-level launch setup mechanisms that replace or underpin the standard `<<<>>>` kernel launch syntax when executing from device code. The surrounding text clarifies that these are device-only functions, distinct from host-side runtime APIs, and are critical for PTX-level implementation and direct device-side invocation.

facts.claim: cudaGetParameterBuffer and cudaLaunchDevice are device-only launch implementation functions that differ from host-side CUDA Runtime API equivalents.
facts.note: These functions are automatically generated from the `<<<>>>` launch syntax but can also be invoked directly by users or compiler front-ends targeting PTX. Backwards compatibility is guaranteed for the underlying data structures.

facts.claim: cudaGetParameterBuffer(void **params) generates the parameter buffer required for device-side kernel launches.
facts.note: It is generated automatically from `<<<>>>` and differs from the host equivalent.

facts.claim: cudaLaunchDevice(void *kernel, void *params, dim3 gridDim, dim3 blockDim, unsigned int sharedMemSize = 0, cudaStream_t stream = 0) executes the kernel launch with the specified configuration.
facts.note: Like the parameter buffer API, it is generated automatically from `<<<>>>` and has a different signature than the host runtime equivalent.

caveats: The APIs are explicitly device-side (`__device__` or `extern device`) and are not part of the standard host CUDA Runtime API.
Direct usage requires manual population of data structures in the correct format, though the `<<<>>>` syntax handles this automatically.
Backwards compatibility is guaranteed for the data structures used by these APIs.

concept/cuda-dynamic-parallelism-limitat.title: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism Limitations
concept/cuda-dynamic-parallelism-limitat.summary: Implementation restrictions including memory footprint, pending launch limits, configuration options, and SM/Warp ID volatility.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence covers Section 13.4.3 'Implementation Restrictions and Limitations' of the CUDA C Programming Guide, specifically detailing runtime constraints, configuration limits, memory allocation semantics, and hardware register volatility relevant to CUDA Dynamic Parallelism (CDP).

facts.claim: The device runtime reserves memory for management tasks, primarily tracking pending grid launches in a fixed-size system-managed launch pool. This reservation can be reduced via configuration controls, but at the cost of launch limitations.
facts.note: Fits under 'Memory Footprint' and 'Pending Kernel Launches' subsections.

facts.claim: The launch pool size is configurable via cudaDeviceSetLimit() with cudaLimitDevRuntimePendingLaunchCount. Limits must be set from the host before any kernel launch and cannot be modified while the GPU is active. The default launch slot count is 2048, with event slots set to twice this value. If the buffer is full, launch allocation fails with cudaErrorLaunchOutOfBoundsException and event allocation fails with cudaMemoryAllocation.
facts.note: Covers 'Configuration Options' and 'Pending Kernel Launches' details.

facts.claim: Per-thread stack size is controlled by cudaLimitStackSize via cudaDeviceSetLimit(). The driver automatically increases stack size per launch as needed but does not reset it. Changing this limit resizes the stack immediately and may block the device until preceding tasks complete.
facts.note: Covers 'Configuration Options' stack limit behavior.

facts.claim: cudaMalloc() and cudaFree() on the device map to standard malloc() and free(), meaning device-side allocations are limited by the device malloc() heap size (cudaLimitMallocHeapSize), which may be smaller than total free device memory. It is an error to free a device-allocated pointer on the host, or vice versa.
facts.note: Covers 'Memory Allocation and Lifetime' cross-environment restrictions.

facts.claim: PTX %smid and %warpid are volatile. The device runtime may reschedule thread blocks across different SMs for efficiency, making it unsafe to rely on these IDs remaining constant across a thread or block's lifetime.
facts.note: Covers 'SM Id and Warp Id' volatility warning.

caveats: The source treats these as hard implementation limits tied to the device runtime system software. No contradictions with other CDP sections were found in the assigned window.
Configuration limits must be applied from the host before any kernel launch; attempting to modify them during active GPU execution is unsupported.

concept/cuda-dynamic-parallelism-memory.title: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism Memory Declarations
concept/cuda-dynamic-parallelism-memory.summary: Memory space handling in CDP including device, constant, texture, surface, shared, and symbol addresses.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence falls under Section 13.3.1.6 'Memory Declarations' within the broader CUDA Dynamic Parallelism chapter (13.3). It details how memory spaces, objects, and symbols behave when kernels are launched from device code, explicitly noting device-runtime limitations and inheritance rules relative to host-runtime behavior.

facts.claim: File-scope __device__ and __constant__ variables are accessible to all kernels regardless of launch origin, and all kernels share a single view of module-scope __constant__ memory.
facts.note: Constant data is strictly read-only from device code and cannot be modified, even ahead of child launches. Direct symbol addressing via the & operator is supported, making host-side symbol APIs (cudaMemcpyToSymbol, cudaGetSymbolAddress) redundant and unsupported in the device runtime.

facts.claim: Host-created dynamic texture and surface objects can be freely passed to and used by parent and child kernels, but device code cannot create or destroy them.
facts.note: Legacy module-scope (Fermi-style) textures and surfaces are restricted to the top-level host-launched kernel and are unsupported in any device-launched kernels.

facts.claim: Both static (file/function scope) and dynamic (extern __shared__) shared memory declarations are valid under the device runtime.
facts.note: Shared memory is block-local and cannot be passed to child kernels; data must be written back to global memory before launching children. Dynamic shared memory size can be specified at launch time.

caveats: Device runtime does not support legacy module-scope textures/surfaces in device-launched kernels.
Constant memory is read-only from device code; runtime symbol APIs are unsupported.
Shared memory cannot be directly inherited or passed to child grids.
recommended_structure: Overview of memory inheritance in device-launched kernels
Device and Constant Memory (read-only constraint, symbol addressing)
Textures and Surfaces (host creation only, legacy restrictions)
Shared Memory (static vs dynamic, global memory write-back requirement)

concept/cuda-dynamic-parallelism-perform.title: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism Performance
concept/cuda-dynamic-parallelism-perform.summary: Performance overheads associated with dynamic parallelism-enabled kernels and system software.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence concludes a basic dynamic parallelism code example (showing the `nvcc` compilation command with `-rdc=true` and `-lcudadevrt`) and introduces Section 13.4.2. It serves as a transitional performance caveat before the guide details implementation restrictions (memory footprint, pending launch limits) and the architectural shift to CDP2.

facts.claim: Dynamic parallelism introduces performance overhead from system software that controls dynamic launches. This overhead affects any kernel running at the time, whether or not it invokes kernel launches itself.
facts.note: Overhead is tied to the device runtime's execution tracking and management software, impacting applications linking against `libcudadevrt`.

caveats: The performance impact is directly tied to linking the device runtime library.
No contradictions detected. The performance note aligns with the subsequent discussion on configurable limits (`cudaLimitDevRuntimePendingLaunchCount`) and CDP2 architectural changes.
recommended_structure: Overview of Dynamic Parallelism Performance Overhead
Source of Overhead (System Software & Execution Tracking)

concept/cuda-dynamic-parallelism-ptx-lau.title: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism PTX Launch
concept/cuda-dynamic-parallelism-ptx-lau.summary: Device-side kernel launch implementation details at the PTX level, including parameter buffer layout.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence sits between the device runtime API reference table (L14042-L14049) and the toolkit compilation/linking section (L14119-L14145). It serves as the implementation bridge for compiler and language developers, detailing the exact PTX-level mechanisms required to execute dynamic kernel launches, contrasting with the higher-level C++/CUDA API usage covered in surrounding sections like Launch Setup APIs (L14021-L14041) and Programming Guidelines (L14146-L14238).

facts.claim: The section targets language and compiler implementers needing low-level PTX details to support Dynamic Parallelism kernel launches.
facts.note: Targets language and compiler implementers needing low-level PTX details to support Dynamic Parallelism kernel launches.

facts.claim: Device-side kernel launches are implemented using two PTX-accessible APIs: cudaLaunchDevice() and cudaGetParameterBuffer().
facts.note: Device-side kernel launches are implemented using two PTX-accessible APIs: cudaLaunchDevice() and cudaGetParameterBuffer().

facts.claim: cudaLaunchDevice() requires a PTX-level declaration before use, with a 64-bit address size example provided.
facts.note: cudaLaunchDevice() requires a PTX-level declaration before use, with a 64-bit address size example provided.

facts.claim: cudaLaunchDevice() maps to a CUDA-level declaration in cuda_device_runtime_api.h and is defined in the cudadevrt system library.
facts.note: cudaLaunchDevice() maps to a CUDA-level declaration in cuda_device_runtime_api.h and is defined in the cudadevrt system library.

facts.claim: cudaLaunchDevice() parameters include the kernel pointer, parameter buffer, grid/block dimensions, shared memory size, and stream.
facts.note: cudaLaunchDevice() parameters include the kernel pointer, parameter buffer, grid/block dimensions, shared memory size, and stream.

facts.claim: cudaGetParameterBuffer() requires a PTX-level declaration before use, with a 64-bit address size example provided.
facts.note: cudaGetParameterBuffer() requires a PTX-level declaration before use, with a 64-bit address size example provided.

facts.claim: cudaGetParameterBuffer() maps to a CUDA-level declaration taking alignment and size arguments.
facts.note: cudaGetParameterBuffer() maps to a CUDA-level declaration taking alignment and size arguments.

facts.claim: cudaGetParameterBuffer() returns a buffer guaranteed to be 64-byte aligned; the alignment argument is currently ignored but recommended for future portability.
facts.note: cudaGetParameterBuffer() returns a buffer guaranteed to be 64-byte aligned; the alignment argument is currently ignored but recommended for future portability.

facts.claim: Parameter reordering in the parameter buffer is prohibited.
facts.note: Parameter reordering in the parameter buffer is prohibited.

facts.claim: Each parameter must be aligned to its size, placed at the smallest multiple of its size greater than the preceding parameter's offset.
facts.note: Each parameter must be aligned to its size, placed at the smallest multiple of its size greater than the preceding parameter's offset.

facts.claim: The maximum size of the parameter buffer is 4KB.
facts.note: The maximum size of the parameter buffer is 4KB.

facts.claim: Detailed compiler-generated PTX code references the PTX-3.5 specification.
facts.note: Detailed compiler-generated PTX code references the PTX-3.5 specification.

caveats: The alignment parameter for cudaGetParameterBuffer() is currently ignored by the implementation but should be passed correctly for future portability.
Parameter buffer layout strictly enforces alignment rules and prohibits reordering; compilers must adhere to these constraints when generating PTX.

concept/cuda-dynamic-parallelism-streams.title: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism Streams
concept/cuda-dynamic-parallelism-streams.summary: Streams in CUDA Dynamic Parallelism (CDP) including named, unnamed (NULL), fire-and-forget, and tail launch streams.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence covers Section 13.3.1.2 (Streams) within the CUDA C++ Device Runtime API documentation. It details how asynchronous execution, synchronization, and stream semantics are adapted for device-side kernel launches, contrasting them with host-side behaviors. This section sits between device-side launch syntax (13.3.1.1) and event/synchronization APIs (13.3.1.3/13.3.1.4).

facts.claim: Device-side streams (named and unnamed) are private to the grid where they are created; stream handles cannot be passed between parent and child kernels.
facts.note: Concurrency between child kernels is not guaranteed; depending on it yields undefined behavior.

facts.claim: All device streams must be created using cudaStreamCreateWithFlags() with the cudaStreamNonBlocking flag. The host-only cudaStreamCreate() will fail to compile on the device.
facts.note: cudaStreamSynchronize() and cudaStreamQuery() are unsupported on the device; completion must be waited on via the tail launch stream.

facts.claim: The implicit (NULL) stream is shared across all threads in a thread block but lacks the host-side cross-stream barrier semantics due to the non-blocking flag.
facts.note: Work in the NULL stream will not implicitly depend on pending work in other streams.

facts.claim: The fire-and-forget stream (cudaStreamFireAndForget) schedules work immediately without dependencies on prior grids.
facts.note: No other grid can depend on its completion except via the parent grid's implicit end synchronization. It cannot record or wait on events, is unsupported under CUDA_FORCE_CDP1_IF_SUPPORTED, and requires 64-bit compilation.

facts.claim: The tail launch stream (cudaStreamTailLaunch) acts as a device-side replacement for cudaDeviceSynchronize().
facts.note: Each grid has one tail launch stream that only executes after all non-tail work (ordinary, per-thread, and fire-and-forget) from the parent completes. Multiple tail launches to the same grid queue sequentially. It also blocks the next grid in the parent's stream. To launch concurrent grids via tail launch, a parent grid must be tail-launched that then fires-and-forgets the children. Like fire-and-forget, it cannot record/wait on events, is unsupported under CUDA_FORCE_CDP1_IF_SUPPORTED, and requires 64-bit compilation.

caveats: Device streams cannot be used to record or wait on events; attempting so returns cudaErrorInvalidValue.
Fire-and-forget and tail launch streams require 64-bit compilation and are incompatible with the CUDA_FORCE_CDP1_IF_SUPPORTED macro.

concept/cuda-dynamic-parallelism-synchro.title: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism Synchronization
concept/cuda-dynamic-parallelism-synchro.summary: Inter-thread and inter-grid synchronization mechanisms and limitations in the device runtime.

research_reports.context_summary: Section 13.3.1.4 sits between discussions on device-side Events (13.3.1.3) and Device Management (13.3.1.5). It addresses the fundamental synchronization constraints of the device runtime, contrasting with host-side capabilities and aligning with the asynchronous launch model (13.3.1.1.1) and the absence of explicit sync APIs like `cudaStreamSynchronize` (13866).

facts.claim: Programmer-Managed Inter-Thread Synchronization
facts.note: If a calling thread needs to synchronize with child grids launched by other threads, the program must explicitly handle this inter-thread synchronization, typically using CUDA Events.

facts.claim: No Explicit Parent-to-Child Synchronization
facts.note: The device runtime does not allow a parent thread to explicitly synchronize with its child work.

facts.claim: Unpredictable Memory Visibility
facts.note: Due to the lack of explicit synchronization mechanisms, there is no guarantee that memory changes occurring in child grids will be visible to threads within the parent grid.

caveats: Explicit synchronization APIs available on the host (e.g., `cudaStreamSynchronize`, `cudaEventSynchronize`) are unsupported on the device, reinforcing the reliance on implicit barriers like the Tail Launch stream (L13866, L13890) and manual event-based coordination.

The assigned evidence strictly covers the conceptual limitation; practical workarounds rely on the Tail Launch stream (L13890) or fire-and-forget streams (L13874) to enforce execution ordering without explicit parent-side waits.

concept/cuda-dynamic-parallelism-toolkit.title: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism Toolkit Support
concept/cuda-dynamic-parallelism-toolkit.summary: Compiler and linker support for dynamic parallelism, including `nvcc` flags and library linking.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence (Section 13.3.3) details the compiler and linker support required to use the CUDA device runtime for dynamic parallelism. It sits between the low-level PTX launch mechanics (Section 13.3.2) and the high-level programming guidelines and limitations (Section 13.4).

facts.claim: Device runtime API prototypes are automatically included during compilation, so explicitly including cuda_device_runtime_api.h is unnecessary.
facts.note: The device runtime is provided as a static library (libcudadevrt.a on Linux, cudadevrt.lib on Windows). When compiling dynamic parallelism programs with nvcc, the program automatically links against this library. Linking can be accomplished via nvcc and/or nvlink.

facts.claim: Compilation examples are provided for both single-step and two-stage (separate) compilation using the -rdc=true and -dc flags respectively.
facts.note: For separate compilation, developers should refer to the 'Using Separate Compilation' section of the NVCC guide.

caveats: The text states that nvcc 'automatically links' against the device runtime library, but the provided command-line examples explicitly pass the -lcudadevrt flag.

concept/cuda-events.title: CUDA Events
concept/cuda-events.summary: Events provide a mechanism to monitor device progress and perform accurate timing by asynchronously recording events at any program point. Events complete when all preceding tasks or commands in a stream finish. The section covers event creation, destruction, and measuring elapsed time between events.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence introduces the CUDA Event API as a core runtime synchronization and timing primitive. It is positioned between Graph/Conditional Node documentation (L3283-L3402) and sections on synchronous calls/multi-device behavior (L3449+), establishing events as the standard mechanism for host-side monitoring and accurate device timing.

facts.claim: Purpose: Provides a runtime mechanism to monitor device progress and perform accurate timing by asynchronously recording events at arbitrary program points and querying their completion.
facts.note: Completion Semantics: An event completes when all preceding tasks or commands in its associated stream have finished. Events in stream zero complete only after all preceding tasks and commands in *all* streams have completed.

facts.claim: Lifecycle Management: Events are created via `cudaEventCreate()` and destroyed via `cudaEventDestroy()`.
facts.note: Recording: Events are recorded into a specific stream using `cudaEventRecord(event, stream)`.

facts.claim: Synchronization: `cudaEventSynchronize(event)` blocks the host thread until the specified event is recorded.
facts.note: Timing Calculation: `cudaEventElapsedTime(&elapsedTime, start, stop)` computes the time difference between two recorded events.

facts.claim: Typical Usage Pattern: Events are typically recorded before and after sequences of asynchronous operations (e.g., memory transfers and kernel launches) to accurately measure execution duration.
facts.note: No contradictions or scope clashes detected. All facts are strictly derived from the assigned evidence.

concept/cuda-extended-lambdas.title: Extended Lambdas
concept/cuda-extended-lambdas.summary: Documentation for extended lambdas in CUDA C++, including execution space annotations, type traits for compile-time detection, and restrictions on definition scope and enclosing functions.

research_reports.context_summary: The provided context covers the definition, syntax, type traits, and strict scoping restrictions for CUDA extended lambdas, detailing how the compiler handles their closure types and enclosing function addresses.

facts.claim: Extended lambdas are enabled via the nvcc '--extended-lambda' flag, which defines the __CUDACC_EXTENDED_LAMBDA__ macro.
facts.note: Execution space annotations (e.g., __device__ or __host__ __device__) must be placed after the lambda-introducer and before the optional lambda-declarator.

facts.claim: An extended __device__ lambda requires explicit __device__ annotation and must be defined within the immediate or nested block scope of a __host__ or __host__ __device__ function.
facts.note: An extended __host__ __device__ lambda requires explicit __host__ __device__ annotation and the same scoping requirement.

facts.claim: If execution space annotations are omitted, they are inferred from the scopes enclosing the lambda's closure class.
facts.note: Extended lambdas can be used as type arguments in __global__ function template instantiations.

facts.claim: The compiler provides __nv_is_extended_device_lambda_closure_type(type) to detect closure types for extended __device__ lambdas.
facts.note: The compiler provides __nv_is_extended_host_device_lambda_closure_type(type) to detect closure types for extended __host__ __device__ lambdas.

facts.claim: The compiler provides __nv_is_extended_device_lambda_with_preserved_return_type(type), which is true if the extended __device__ lambda uses a trailing return type that does not reference the lambda's parameter names.
facts.note: These type traits are available in all compilation modes.

facts.claim: The CUDA compiler replaces extended lambdas with a namespace-scope placeholder type before invoking the host compiler.
facts.note: The placeholder's template argument requires taking the address of an 'enclosing function'.

facts.claim: The enclosing function is the __host__ or __host__ __device__ function containing the lambda, unless the lambda is nested inside other lambda operator()s, in which case it is the outermost function containing those lambdas.
facts.note: If no such function exists, the extended lambda is invalid.

facts.claim: An extended lambda cannot be defined inside another extended lambda.
facts.note: An extended lambda cannot be defined inside a generic lambda.

facts.claim: If an extended lambda is nested within other lambda expressions, the outermost such lambda must be defined inside a function's block scope.
facts.note: The enclosing function must be named and its address must be takable. For member functions, all enclosing classes must be named and have public access.

facts.claim: The address of the enclosing routine must be unambiguous at the point of definition (e.g., typedefs cannot shadow template type arguments).
facts.note: Extended lambdas cannot be defined in a class that is local to a function.

facts.claim: The enclosing function cannot have a deduced return type.
facts.note: __host__ __device__ extended lambdas cannot be generic lambdas.

facts.claim: If the enclosing function is a template instantiation or member of a class template, it must have at most one variadic parameter (listed last), all parameters must be named, and instantiation arguments cannot involve local types or private/protected members.
facts.note: None

caveats: The compiler's placeholder mechanism fundamentally changes how extended lambdas are represented to the host compiler, requiring strict scoping rules to guarantee the enclosing function's address can be resolved.
Type traits are strictly compile-time checks and do not enable runtime polymorphism for extended lambdas.

concept/cuda-external-memory-mapping.title: Mapping Buffers onto Imported Memory Objects
concept/cuda-external-memory-mapping.summary: Covers mapping device pointers and mipmapped arrays onto imported NvSciBuf memory objects using `cudaExternalMemoryGetMappedBuffer` and `cudaExternalMemoryGetMappedMipmappedArray`. Includes code examples for setting offsets, sizes, formats, and extents, and notes that mapped resources must be freed with `cudaFree()` or `cudaFreeMipmappedArray()`.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence covers the final steps of the NvSciBuf external memory workflow: mapping imported memory objects to CUDA device pointers and mipmapped arrays. This follows the initial object creation, attribute querying, and import via `cudaImportExternalMemory` (L5879-L5933).

facts.claim: Device pointers are mapped to imported memory objects using `cudaExternalMemoryGetMappedBuffer`.
facts.note: Requires a `cudaExternalMemoryBufferDesc` structure specifying `offset` and `size`. Mapped pointers must be freed with `cudaFree()`.

facts.claim: Mipmapped arrays are mapped to imported memory objects using `cudaExternalMemoryGetMappedMipmappedArray`.
facts.note: Requires a `cudaExternalMemoryMipmappedArrayDesc` structure specifying `offset`, `formatDesc`, `extent`, `flags`, and `numLevels`. Mapped arrays must be freed with `cudaFreeMipmappedArray()`.

facts.claim: Mipmapped array mapping is restricted to a single mip level.
facts.note: The `numLevels` parameter must be set to 1.

facts.claim: Mapping parameters are derived from the original `NvSciBufObj` attributes.
facts.note: Offset, size, dimensions, and format must align with the attributes of the allocated NvSciBuf object.

caveats: The source text contains a typo in the section header ('Mapping Bufers...'), but the functionality clearly refers to buffer/device pointer mapping.
No contradictions found; the evidence cleanly extends the external memory import workflow.
recommended_structure: Overview of mapping imported memory to CUDA resources

concept/cuda-external-semaphore.title: Importing and Signaling/Waiting on Imported Synchronization Objects
concept/cuda-external-semaphore.summary: Covers importing NvSciSync objects into CUDA as external semaphores using `cudaImportExternalSemaphore`, and signaling/waiting on them using `cudaSignalExternalSemaphoresAsync` and `cudaWaitExternalSemaphoresAsync`. Details attribute reconciliation, fence parameters, and the `cudaExternalSemaphoreSignalSkipNvSciBufMemSync`/`cudaExternalSemaphoreWaitSkipNvSciBufMemSync` flags for cache coherency control.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence covers sections 6.2.16.5.4 and 6.2.16.5.5 of the CUDA C Programming Guide, detailing the lifecycle of NvSciSync-based external semaphores. It outlines the creation, reconciliation, and import of synchronization objects, followed by the asynchronous signaling and waiting mechanisms, including specific flags for cache coherency management.

facts.claim: Importing a synchronization object requires generating device-compatible NvSciSync attributes via cudaDeviceGetNvSciSyncAttributes(), reconciling signaler and waiter attribute lists with NvSciSyncAttrListReconcile(), and allocating the object with NvSciSyncObjAlloc().
facts.note: Application retains ownership of the nvSciSyncObj handle post-import; freeing it causes undefined behavior.

facts.claim: Importing is finalized by calling cudaImportExternalSemaphore() with a cudaExternalSemaphoreHandleDesc struct, where type is set to cudaExternalSemaphoreHandleTypeNvSciSync and handle.nvSciSyncObj is populated.
facts.note: Signaling uses cudaSignalExternalSemaphoresAsync() with cudaExternalSemaphoreSignalParams. The fence is initialized via params.nvSciSync.fence. The cudaExternalSemaphoreSignalSkipNvSciBufMemSync flag skips default memory synchronization operations and should be used when NvSciBufGeneralAttrKey_GpuSoftwareNeedCacheCoherency is FALSE.

facts.claim: Waiting uses cudaWaitExternalSemaphoresAsync() with cudaExternalSemaphoreWaitParams. The fence is initialized via params.nvSciSync.fence. The cudaExternalSemaphoreWaitSkipNvSciBufMemSync flag skips default memory synchronization operations and should be used when NvSciBufGeneralAttrKey_GpuSoftwareNeedCacheCoherency is FALSE.
facts.note: A wait operation must be issued after the corresponding signal operation. The wait blocks until the input fence is signaled by the corresponding signaler.

caveats: The source document is marked as legacy (replaced by the CUDA Programming Guide as of CUDA 13.0), but the assigned evidence contains the complete procedural details for this specific concept.

concept/cuda-floating-point-standard.title: Floating-Point Standard
concept/cuda-floating-point-standard.summary: IEEE 754-2008 compliance details, deviations, rounding modes, NaN handling, flush-to-zero behavior, compiler flags, and atomic operation specifics for NVIDIA GPUs.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence is a standalone subsection within Chapter 20 (Compute Capabilities) of the CUDA C Programming Guide. It details NVIDIA GPU deviations from the IEEE 754-2008 standard, covering rounding, exception masking, NaN handling, flush-to-zero behavior, and integer conversion rules. The surrounding context covers texture filtering, compute capability feature tables, and architectural specs, but does not overlap with floating-point semantics.

facts.claim: All compute devices adhere to the IEEE 754-2008 standard for binary floating-point arithmetic, with specific architectural deviations.
facts.note: Deviations are explicitly enumerated in the source.

facts.claim: There is no dynamically configurable rounding mode at the hardware level, but most operations support multiple IEEE rounding modes exposed via device intrinsics.
facts.note: Intrinsics are required to select rounding modes.

facts.claim: Floating-point exceptions cannot be detected; all operations behave as if IEEE-754 exceptions are permanently masked, returning the masked response defined by the standard.
facts.note: Exception masking is implicit and non-configurable.

facts.claim: Signaling NaNs (SNaN) are supported but are not signaling; they are treated and handled as quiet NaNs.
facts.note: SNaNs lose their signaling property on compute devices.

facts.claim: Single-precision floating-point operations involving one or more input NaNs always return the quiet NaN with bit pattern 0x7ffff.
facts.note: This is a fixed propagation rule for FP32 NaNs.

facts.claim: Double-precision absolute value and negation operations are not IEEE-754 compliant regarding NaNs; they pass NaNs through unchanged.
facts.note: Standard IEEE behavior would typically convert NaNs to quiet NaNs or propagate specific payloads.

facts.claim: IEEE compliance requires compilation with -ftz=false, -prec-div=true, and -prec-sqrt=true, which are the default settings.
facts.note: Deviations from IEEE compliance occur if these flags are altered.

facts.claim: Atomic single-precision floating-point adds on global memory always operate in flush-to-zero (FTZ) mode, equivalent to FADD.F32.FTZ.RN.
facts.note: FTZ behavior is forced regardless of the -ftz compiler flag.

facts.claim: Atomic single-precision floating-point adds on shared memory always operate with denormal support, equivalent to FADD.F32.RN.
facts.note: Denormals are preserved in shared memory atomics.

facts.claim: For fminf(), fmin(), fmaxf(), and fmax(), if one argument is NaN and the other is not, the result is the non-NaN argument (per IEEE-754R).
facts.note: This follows the IEEE-754R min/max specification.

facts.claim: Converting a floating-point value to an integer when the value falls outside the integer range clamps to the end of the supported range.
facts.note: IEEE-754 leaves this undefined; this behavior differs from x86.

facts.claim: Integer division by zero and integer overflow are undefined by IEEE-754 and lack detection mechanisms on compute devices; division by zero yields an unspecified, machine-specific value.
facts.note: No hardware traps or exceptions are raised for these integer edge cases.

caveats: The source document is marked as legacy (replaced by the CUDA Programming Guide as of CUDA 13.0), but the floating-point standard section remains the authoritative reference for this topic within the provided context.
All floating-point exception handling is implicit and non-configurable; developers must rely on masked responses and intrinsics rather than exception flags.

concept/cuda-graphics-interoperability.title: Graphics Interoperability
concept/cuda-graphics-interoperability.summary: Overview of mapping OpenGL and Direct3D resources into CUDA address space. Covers registration (cudaGraphicsRegisterResource), mapping/unmapping, usage hints, accessing mapped pointers/arrays, and restrictions on concurrent access.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence is Section 6.2.15, which acts as the architectural overview for CUDA's Graphics Interoperability feature. It bridges the preceding memory management concepts (Surfaces, CUDA Arrays, Coherency) with the subsequent API-specific implementation guides (OpenGL, Direct3D, SLI).

facts.claim: Purpose and Bidirectional Flow
facts.note: Enables mapping OpenGL/Direct3D resources into CUDA's address space to either read data written by graphics APIs or write data for graphics APIs to consume.

facts.claim: Registration Lifecycle
facts.note: High-overhead operation returning a `struct cudaGraphicsResource` pointer. Typically called once per resource. Each CUDA context must register the resource independently. Unregistered via `cudaGraphicsUnregisterResource()`.

facts.claim: Mapping and Access
facts.note: Mapped/unmapped repeatedly via `cudaGraphicsMapResources()`/`cudaGraphicsUnmapResources()`. Usage hints (read-only/write-only) are set via `cudaGraphicsResourceSetMapFlags()`. Kernels access mapped data via `cudaGraphicsResourceGetMappedPointer()` (buffers) or `cudaGraphicsSubResourceGetMappedArray()` (CUDA arrays).

facts.claim: Concurrency Constraint
facts.note: Accessing a mapped resource through its originating graphics API, another CUDA context, or any other method while mapped produces undefined results.

caveats: The section explicitly defers API-specific registration functions, supported formats, and code samples to the subsequent 'OpenGL Interoperability' and 'Direct3D Interoperability' subsections.
No scope clashes or contradictions detected; the text functions strictly as a high-level procedural overview.

concept/cuda-graphs.title: CUDA Graphs
concept/cuda-graphs.summary: A model for work submission separating graph definition, instantiation, and execution to reduce CPU overhead and enable optimizations. Covers node types, edge data, creation via APIs or stream capture, cross-stream dependencies, user objects, and graph update mechanisms.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence constitutes the primary reference section for CUDA Graphs in the CUDA C Programming Guide. It systematically covers the conceptual model, lifecycle stages, structural components (nodes/edges), creation mechanisms (explicit API vs. stream capture), resource management via user objects, and update/optimization workflows. It serves as the definitive technical specification for the target concept page.

facts.claim: CUDA Graphs decouple workflow definition from execution, reducing CPU launch overhead and enabling optimizations unavailable with piecewise stream submission.
facts.note: Lifecycle consists of Definition, Instantiation (creating an executable graph with pre-allocated setup), and Execution (repeated stream launches).

facts.claim: Graph nodes represent operations (kernels, CPU calls, memcpy, memset, events, semaphores, conditionals, child graphs) and edges represent dependencies that constrain execution order.
facts.note: Scheduling is deferred to the CUDA system once dependencies are satisfied.

facts.claim: Edge Data (CUDA 12.3+) modifies dependencies via outgoing/incoming ports and types. Zero-initialized data implies full task dependency with memory synchronization.
facts.note: Supports cudaGraphDependencyTypeProgrammatic for programmatic dependent launch between kernel nodes.

facts.claim: Graphs can be created via explicit Graph APIs (manual node/edge construction) or Stream Capture (bracketing existing stream code with begin/end capture calls).
facts.note: Stream capture works on any stream except the legacy stream and can target existing graphs via cudaStreamBeginCaptureToGraph().

facts.claim: Cross-stream dependencies are handled via captured events. Forked streams must rejoin the origin stream before cudaStreamEndCapture() is called.
facts.note: Invalid operations (e.g., synchronous queries, legacy stream usage during capture) invalidate the capture graph.

facts.claim: CUDA User Objects manage asynchronous resource lifetimes using reference counting, similar to shared_ptr.
facts.note: Created via cudaUserObjectCreate and retained by graphs via cudaGraphRetainUserObject. Destructors cannot call CUDA APIs.

facts.claim: Graph Update modifies node parameters in-place without topology changes, amortizing instantiation overhead.
facts.note: Whole Graph Update (cudaGraphExecUpdate) requires identical topology and consistent dependency/sink node ordering. Individual Node Update skips topology checks for efficiency.

facts.claim: Nodes can be enabled/disabled via cudaGraphNodeSetEnabled() without altering parameters.
facts.note: Update limitations include fixed kernel context/DP usage, fixed device/context for memcpy/memset (1D only), fixed external semaphore counts, and fixed conditional node parameters/handle order.

caveats: Stream capture strictly prohibits synchronous API calls and legacy stream usage while active. Invalid operations immediately invalidate the capture graph.
Graph updates cannot change topology, kernel contexts, or dynamic parallelism usage. Memcpy/memset nodes are restricted to 1D transfers on the original device/context.
CUDA User Object destructors are restricted from calling CUDA APIs to prevent blocking internal threads.
recommended_structure: Updating & Optimization (Graph Update, Node Update, Enable/Disable)

concept/cuda-kernel-execution.title: CUDA Kernel Execution
concept/cuda-kernel-execution.summary: Details the cuLaunchKernel API, parameter passing mechanisms (array of pointers vs extra options buffer), and strict alignment requirements for device code types, structures, and CUdeviceptr.

research_reports.context_summary: Section 21.3 details the driver API mechanism for launching kernels (`cuLaunchKernel`), focusing on parameter passing strategies, device-side alignment requirements, and host/device structure padding discrepancies.

facts.claim: `cuLaunchKernel()` is the driver API function used to launch a kernel with a specified execution configuration (grid and block dimensions).
facts.note: Core launch entry point.

facts.claim: Kernel arguments can be passed either as an array of pointers (next-to-last parameter) referencing host memory, or as a single contiguous buffer via the `CU_LAUNCH_PARAM_BUFFER_POINTER` extra option (last parameter).
facts.note: Two supported parameter passing methods.

facts.claim: When using the buffer method, parameters must be laid out sequentially according to device-side alignment rules. Built-in vector types have specific alignments, while other basic types generally match host alignment (`__alignof()`).
facts.note: Buffer layout alignment rules.

facts.claim: `double` and `long long` are strictly aligned on a two-word boundary in device code, overriding host compiler flags like `-mno-align-double`. `CUdeviceptr` aligns to `__alignof(void*)`.
facts.note: Specific type alignment exceptions.

facts.claim: Structs containing vector types, `CUdeviceptr`, or non-aligned doubles/long longs may be padded differently on the device versus the host. For example, a struct with a `float` followed by a `float4` is unpadded on the host but receives 12 bytes of padding after the `float` on the device to satisfy the `float4`'s 16-byte alignment.
facts.note: Host/device structure padding discrepancy.

facts.claim: Developers typically use alignment macros (e.g., `ALIGN_UP`) and copy macros (e.g., `ADD_TO_PARAM_BUFFER`) to sequentially pack parameters into a single buffer before passing it to `cuLaunchKernel()`.
facts.note: Common implementation pattern for buffer parameters.

caveats: Alignment requirements for structures differ between host and device compilation, which can cause misaligned parameter buffers if host-side struct sizes/offsets are assumed for device-side buffer packing.
The `CU_LAUNCH_PARAM_BUFFER_POINTER` method requires manual offset calculation and padding to match device-side alignment, unlike the pointer array method which handles copying automatically.
recommended_structure: Parameter Passing Strategies (Pointer Array vs. Contiguous Buffer)

concept/cuda-layered-textures.title: Layered Textures
concept/cuda-layered-textures.summary: Explains one-dimensional and two-dimensional layered textures (texture arrays), addressing using integer layer indices and floating-point coordinates, CUDA array creation with cudaArray-Layered, fetch functions, and compute capability 2.0+ requirement.

research_reports.context_summary: Section 6.2.14.1.3 defines layered textures within the CUDA C Programming Guide, detailing their structure, addressing, creation, fetching, and hardware requirements.

facts.claim: A layered texture is a sequence of layers consisting of regular textures that share the same dimensionality, size, and data type. It is referred to as a 'texture array' in Direct3D and an 'array texture' in OpenGL.
facts.note: Direct mapping of terminology and structural definition.

facts.claim: Addressing uses an integer index to select the layer, combined with floating-point coordinates to select a texel within that layer (one coordinate for 1D, two for 2D).
facts.note: Clarifies the coordinate system specific to layered textures.

facts.claim: Layered textures must be created as CUDA arrays using `cudaMalloc3DArray()` with the `cudaArrayLayered` flag. For 1D layered textures, the height parameter must be set to zero.
facts.note: Specifies the exact API and flag required for allocation.

facts.claim: Data is fetched using `tex1DLayered()` and `tex2DLayered()`. Texture filtering is applied strictly within a single layer and does not interpolate across layer boundaries.
facts.note: Highlights the isolation of filtering operations per layer.

facts.claim: Layered textures are only supported on GPU devices with compute capability 2.0 or higher.
facts.note: Establishes the minimum hardware architecture requirement.

caveats: The source explicitly restricts layered texture creation to CUDA arrays via `cudaMalloc3DArray()`; it does not mention support for linear memory or other allocation methods in this section.
Filtering behavior is strictly intra-layer, which may impact applications expecting cross-layer interpolation.

concept/cuda-maximize-utilization.title: Maximizing CUDA Utilization
concept/cuda-maximize-utilization.summary: Covers strategies to maximize parallel execution across host, device, and multiprocessor levels. Explains latency hiding via warp residency, the impact of register/shared memory usage on occupancy, branch divergence costs, and synchronization overheads. Details how to control register usage via compiler flags and launch bounds, and recommends choosing thread block sizes as multiples of warp size.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence covers Section 8.2 (Maximize Utilization) and related subsections from the CUDA C Programming Guide. It details a three-tiered approach (Application, Device, Multiprocessor) for exposing parallelism and hiding latency. The surrounding context notes this chapter is legacy (superseded by the new CUDA Programming Guide as of CUDA 13.0), but the architectural concepts remain standard.

facts.claim: Maximizing utilization is the primary of four core performance strategies, alongside optimizing memory/instruction throughput and minimizing memory thrashing.
facts.note: Optimization efforts should target the specific bottleneck (e.g., memory vs. compute) identified via profiling.

facts.claim: At the application level, parallelism across host, device, and PCIe bus is maximized using asynchronous calls and streams. Inter-block synchronization should be minimized by keeping data-sharing computations within a single block using __syncthreads() and shared memory, avoiding expensive global memory round-trips.
facts.note: Serial work is assigned to the host; parallel work to the device.

facts.claim: At the device level, maximum utilization is achieved by running multiple kernels concurrently across multiprocessors using streams.
facts.note: None

facts.claim: At the multiprocessor level, utilization relies on thread-level parallelism (resident warps). Warp schedulers issue ready instructions to hide latency.
facts.note: Full utilization occurs when schedulers always have an instruction to issue.

facts.claim: Latency-hiding instruction count formulas: 4L for CC 5.x, 6.1, 6.2, 7.x, and 8.x (4 warps issued/cycle); 2L for CC 6.0 (2 warps issued/cycle). L = latency in clock cycles.
facts.note: None

facts.claim: Latency sources include register dependencies (~4 cycles on CC 7.x, requiring ~16 warps to hide), off-chip memory accesses (hundreds of cycles, requiring more warps based on arithmetic intensity), and synchronization points.
facts.note: Multiple resident blocks mitigate idle cycles caused by intra-block synchronization.

facts.claim: Resident warps/blocks are constrained by on-chip registers and shared memory. Register usage heavily impacts occupancy; a single extra register can halve resident blocks.
facts.note: Register usage can be controlled via maxrregcount, __launch_bounds__(), or __maxnreg__().

facts.claim: Thread block size should be a multiple of the warp size (32) to prevent under-populated warps. Compiler resource usage is reported with --ptxas-options=-v.
facts.note: None

facts.claim: Occupancy Calculator APIs (cudaOccupancyMaxActiveBlocksPerMultiprocessor, cudaOccupancyMaxPotentialBlockSize, cudaOccupancyMaxActiveClusters) predict occupancy and calculate optimal launch configurations based on kernel resource requirements.
facts.note: None

caveats: The source document is marked as legacy (replaced by the new CUDA Programming Guide as of CUDA 13.0), though the architectural principles remain valid.
Specific latency formulas and warp-scheduler counts are architecture-dependent (e.g., CC 6.0 vs 7.x+); developers should verify against current Compute Capability documentation.

concept/cuda-mode-switches.title: CUDA Mode Switches
concept/cuda-mode-switches.summary: Explains how display mode switches (resolution/bit depth changes, fullscreen app launches, task switches) alter the memory required for the GPU's primary surface. Notes that if mode switches increase primary surface memory, CUDA may cannibalize allocations, causing subsequent CUDA runtime calls to fail with an invalid context error.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence (Section 6.5) is situated between sections on Compute Modes (6.4) and Tesla Compute Cluster Mode (6.6). While the surrounding context discusses GPU compute modes (Default, Exclusive-process, Prohibited) and versioning, Section 6.5 specifically addresses display mode switches and their impact on CUDA contexts.

facts.claim: Display mode switches occur when the display resolution or bit depth is changed, typically via the NVIDIA control panel or the Windows Display control panel.
facts.note: Changing the display mode alters the amount of DRAM dedicated to the GPU's 'primary surface' used to refresh the display. For example, increasing resolution from 1280x1024x32-bit to 1600x1200x32-bit increases the required memory from 5.24 MB to 7.68 MB. Full-screen graphics applications with anti-aliasing may require significantly more memory for the primary surface.

facts.claim: On Windows, specific events can initiate display mode switches, including launching a full-screen DirectX application, hitting Alt+Tab to task switch away from a full-screen DirectX application, or pressing Ctrl+Alt+Del to lock the computer.
facts.note: These events trigger the system to adjust the primary surface memory allocation.

facts.claim: If a mode switch increases the memory required for the primary surface, the system may cannibalize (reclaim) memory allocations that were dedicated to CUDA applications.
facts.note: This memory reclamation directly impacts the validity of existing CUDA contexts.

facts.claim: A mode switch that results in memory cannibalization causes any subsequent call to the CUDA runtime to fail, returning an 'invalid context error'.
facts.note: Developers must handle this error state, as the CUDA context is no longer valid after such a switch.

caveats: The source document is marked as legacy (replaced by the new CUDA Programming Guide as of CUDA 13.0), though the specific behavior regarding display mode switches remains a fundamental hardware/driver interaction.
recommended_structure: Definition of Mode Switches (Display resolution/bit depth changes)
Triggers (Windows events like Alt+Tab, DirectX launch)

concept/cuda-module.title: CUDA Module
concept/cuda-module.summary: Modules are dynamically loadable packages of device code and data (akin to DLLs) output by nvcc. They maintain symbol scope for interoperability and can be loaded from PTX files or compiled/linked dynamically at runtime.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence falls under Chapter 21 (Driver API), specifically Section 21.2 (Module). It defines the CUDA Module as a Driver API object used to load, compile, and link device code (PTX or binary) at runtime. The surrounding context (L20052-L20376) establishes that modules are loaded within a CUDA context, act as containers for kernels, and support interoperability between third-party code.

facts.claim: A CUDA Module is a dynamically loadable package of device code and data, analogous to a Windows DLL, and is output by nvcc.
facts.note: In the Driver API, the module is represented by the CUmodule handle, which is roughly equivalent to a dynamic library (CUDA_C_Programming_Guide:L20073).

facts.claim: Symbol names (functions, global variables, texture/surface references) are maintained at module scope, allowing modules from independent third parties to interoperate within the same CUDA context.
facts.note: Modules are explicitly loaded within a CUDA context, which encapsulates the resources and actions performed by the module (CUDA_C_Programming_Guide:L20077-L20078, L20157).

facts.claim: Modules can be loaded from a file (e.g., PTX) using the cuModuleLoad function.
facts.note: After loading, kernels are retrieved from the module using cuModuleGetFunction before being launched.

facts.claim: Modules can be compiled and loaded directly from a PTX string at runtime using cuModuleLoadDataEx, which supports parsing compilation errors.
facts.note: This process allows applications to load PTX code without pre-compiling it to a binary file.

facts.claim: Multiple PTX codes can be linked together into a single module (Cubin) using the linker API (cuLinkCreate, cuLinkAddData, cuLinkComplete) and then loaded using cuModuleLoadData.
facts.note: The linker API also supports parsing link/compilation errors and retrieving wall time for the linking process.

caveats: The evidence focuses exclusively on the Driver API (`cuModule*` functions). It does not cover the Runtime API equivalents (e.g., `cuModuleLoad` vs `cudaModuleLoad`), though the context notes that the Driver API is interoperable with the Runtime API (CUDA_C_Programming_Guide:L20325-L20352).
recommended_structure: Definition and Nature (DLL analogy, nvcc output)
Loading and Compilation (File loading, PTX string loading, Linking)

concept/cuda-nvstd-function.title: Polymorphic Function Wrappers (nvstd::function)
concept/cuda-nvstd-function.summary: Documentation for the nvstd::function class template, which provides polymorphic function wrapper capabilities in both host and device code, including usage rules, restrictions, and API signature.

research_reports.context_summary: Section 18.6 of the CUDA C++ Programming Guide details the `nvstd::function` polymorphic function wrapper, its usage constraints across execution spaces, and its C++ API signature.

facts.claim: nvstd::function is a polymorphic function wrapper class template provided in the nvfunctional header.
facts.note: It can store, copy, and invoke any callable target, such as lambda expressions, and is usable in both host and device code.

facts.claim: Execution space initialization restrictions apply: host code nvstd::function instances cannot be initialized with __device__ functions or __device__ functors, and device code instances cannot be initialized with __host__ functions or __host__ functors.
facts.note: This ensures type and execution space safety at compile time.

facts.claim: nvstd::function instances cannot be passed between host and device code at runtime, and cannot be used as parameters for __global__ functions launched from host code.
facts.note: Attempting to pass them across execution boundaries or use them in kernel parameters results in compiler errors.

facts.claim: The nvstd::function API includes constructors, assignment operators, swap, and null pointer comparisons, all marked __device__ __host__.
facts.note: Constructors support default, nullptr_t, copy, move, and generic callable initialization.

facts.claim: The function invocation operator() is marked __device__ only, while capacity checks (explicit operator bool) and swap are __device__ __host__.
facts.note: This design allows checking validity on the host but requires device execution for invocation.

caveats: The API signature provided in the documentation uses placeholder template parameters (_RetType, _ArgTypes, _F) rather than standard C++ library names, reflecting nvcc's internal implementation details.
No support for passing nvstd::function across execution space boundaries at runtime is explicitly enforced by the compiler.

concept/cuda-occupancy-calculator.title: CUDA Occupancy Calculator
concept/cuda-occupancy-calculator.summary: Describes the Occupancy Calculator APIs (`cudaOccupancyMaxActiveBlocksPerMultiprocessor`, `cudaOccupancyMaxPotentialBlockSize`, etc.) used to predict and optimize thread block and cluster sizes based on register and shared memory constraints. Includes a code example demonstrating how to query device properties and calculate occupancy percentage.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence falls under Section 8.2.3.1 'Occupancy Calculator' in the legacy CUDA C Programming Guide. It follows discussions on multiprocessor resource limits (registers/shared memory), latency hiding, and hardware multithreading, serving as the practical implementation guide for calculating and optimizing occupancy.

facts.claim: The CUDA Occupancy Calculator provides APIs to assist programmers in selecting thread block and cluster sizes based on register and shared memory constraints to maximize multiprocessor utilization.
facts.note: Purpose & Core APIs

facts.claim: cudaOccupancyMaxActiveBlocksPerMultiprocessor predicts occupancy based on block size and shared memory, returning concurrent thread blocks per multiprocessor.
facts.note: API: Block-level prediction

facts.claim: cudaOccupancyMaxPotentialBlockSize and cudaOccupancyMaxPotentialBlockSizeVariableSMem heuristically calculate execution configurations for maximum multiprocessor-level occupancy.
facts.note: API: Launch configurators

facts.claim: cudaOccupancyMaxActiveClusters predicts occupancy based on cluster size, block size, and shared memory, returning the maximum number of active clusters.
facts.note: API: Cluster-level prediction

facts.claim: Occupancy returned by the API (concurrent blocks per multiprocessor) can be converted to concurrent warps or occupancy percentage (concurrent warps / max warps per multiprocessor).
facts.note: Metric conversion

facts.claim: A standalone implementation of the occupancy calculator and launch configurator is available in <CUDA_Toolkit_Path>/include/cuda_occupancy.h via the Nsight Compute User Interface, useful for environments without the CUDA software stack and as a learning/visualization tool.
facts.note: Standalone tool

facts.claim: The source document is explicitly marked as legacy (replaced by the new CUDA Programming Guide as of CUDA 13.0).
facts.note: Legacy status

caveats: The assigned evidence range (L6241-L6296) covers the primary API descriptions and code examples. Additional cluster-specific APIs (e.g., cudaOccupancyMaxPotentialClusterSize) appear later in the full context window but fall outside the assigned evidence range.
The guide is legacy; developers should verify API availability and behavior in the current CUDA Programming Guide.

concept/cuda-opengl-interoperability.title: OpenGL Interoperability
concept/cuda-opengl-interoperability.summary: Details mapping OpenGL buffers, textures, and renderbuffers to CUDA. Covers registration functions (cudaGraphicsGLRegisterBuffer/Image), usage as device pointers or CUDA arrays, surface load/store flags, context requirements, bindless texture limitations, and provides a complete code sample for dynamic vertex buffer modification.

research_reports.context_summary: The first evidence block covers the primary OpenGL-CUDA interoperability workflow (Section 6.2.15.1), detailing resource registration, mapping, and kernel access. The second block (Section 6.2.16.2) describes an alternative Vulkan-mediated interoperability path where Vulkan exports resources consumed by both OpenGL and CUDA.

facts.claim: CUDA maps OpenGL buffer, texture, and renderbuffer objects. Buffers appear as device pointers accessible via kernels or cudaMemcpy(), while textures/renderbuffers appear as CUDA arrays accessible via texture/surface references or cudaMemcpy2D().
facts.note: Buffers use cudaGraphicsGLRegisterBuffer(); textures/renderbuffers use cudaGraphicsGLRegisterImage().

facts.claim: Interop requires explicit per-frame mapping and unmapping via cudaGraphicsMapResources() and cudaGraphicsUnmapResources(). The OpenGL context sharing resources must be current on the host thread making API calls.
facts.note: Mapped buffers yield device pointers via cudaGraphicsResourceGetMappedPointer().

facts.claim: cudaGraphicsGLRegisterImage() supports 1, 2, or 4-component float, normalized integer, and unnormalized integer formats. Unnormalized integers require OpenGL 3.0 and are shader-write-only. Surface writes to textures/renderbuffers require the cudaGraphicsRegisterFlagsSurfaceLoadStore flag.
facts.note: Bindless textures (created via glGetTextureHandle*/glGetImageHandle*) cannot be registered; registration must occur before handle creation.

facts.claim: On Windows/Quadro systems, cudaWGLGetDevice() links the CUDA device to the OpenGL GPU. Quadro GPUs provide optimized performance in multi-GPU setups where rendering and compute are split across different GPUs.
facts.note: None

facts.claim: An alternative interoperability path exists where Vulkan exports memory and synchronization objects imported into both OpenGL and CUDA to coordinate access, relying on GL_EXT_memory_object and GL_EXT_semaphore extensions (and their FD/Win32 variants).
facts.note: None

caveats: Traditional direct OpenGL-CUDA interop consumes OpenGL handles natively. The Vulkan-mediated path is presented as an alternative for cross-API synchronization and memory sharing.
Surface write access to OpenGL textures/renderbuffers is conditional on the cudaGraphicsRegisterFlagsSurfaceLoadStore registration flag.
Bindless texture handles invalidate CUDA registration; the application must register before requesting handles.

concept/cuda-overview.title: CUDA Overview & Scalable Programming Model
concept/cuda-overview.summary: Covers Chapter 1-3: Introduction to CUDA, GPU benefits, and the scalable parallel programming model.

research_reports.context_summary: The provided context covers the foundational overview of CUDA, its architectural rationale compared to CPUs, and the core abstractions of its scalable programming model. It also contains explicit legacy warnings regarding the document's status as of CUDA 13.0.

facts.claim: CUDA is a general-purpose parallel computing platform and programming model introduced by NVIDIA in November 2006 to accelerate compute-intensive applications using C, C++, and Fortran, widely used in deep learning, scientific computing, and HPC.
facts.note: Foundational definition and ecosystem adoption.

facts.claim: The programming model leverages GPU architecture, which prioritizes massive parallelism (thousands of threads) and data processing transistors over CPU-style single-thread speed and large caches. This design hides memory latency through computation rather than caching.
facts.note: Architectural rationale for the programming model.

facts.claim: The scalable programming model uses three core abstractions: a hierarchy of thread groups, shared memory, and barrier synchronization. It decomposes problems into coarse-grained data/task parallelism (independent thread blocks) nested within fine-grained data/thread parallelism (cooperative threads within a block).
facts.note: Core abstractions and problem decomposition strategy.

facts.claim: Automatic scalability is achieved because thread blocks are independent and can be scheduled on any Streaming Multiprocessor (SM) in any order or concurrently. The runtime handles physical core counts, allowing the same code to scale across the entire GPU market range (GeForce, Quadro, Tesla) by simply scaling multiprocessor and memory partition counts.
facts.note: Mechanism for hardware-agnostic scaling.

facts.claim: The assigned source document is explicitly marked as legacy, having been replaced by the new CUDA Programming Guide as of CUDA 13.0. While the conceptual overview remains valid, the guide itself is no longer updated.
facts.note: Scope and maintenance status caveat.

caveats: The source document contains repeated legacy warnings indicating it is no longer updated as of CUDA 13.0. The lead writer should verify if the target page requires a 'legacy' notice or if the content should be cross-referenced with the newer CUDA Programming Guide.

The context focuses heavily on the conceptual and architectural foundations rather than API specifics or modern compute capability details, which are covered in later chapters not included in this window.
recommended_structure: 3. Core Abstractions (Thread Hierarchy, Shared Memory, Synchronization)

concept/cuda-pipeline.title: cuda::pipeline API
concept/cuda-pipeline.summary: Introduction to the cuda::pipeline synchronization object, describing its double-ended FIFO queue structure and core member functions (producer_acquire, producer_commit, consumer_wait, consumer_release). Includes the header and initial setup for the single-stage asynchronous copy example.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned document introduces `cuda::pipeline` as a C++ synchronization object for overlapping asynchronous data transfers (`cuda::memcpy_async`) with computation. It details the API's FIFO queue model, core member functions, thread role flexibility, and usage patterns (single/multi-stage). It also specifies compilation requirements and contrasts the C++ API with legacy C primitives.

facts.claim: Purpose & Core Concept
facts.note: `cuda::pipeline` is a synchronization object that manages and overlaps asynchronous data movement with computation. It operates as a double-ended, N-stage FIFO queue where producer threads commit work to the head and consumer threads pull from the tail.

facts.claim: Core Member Functions
facts.note: The API provides four collective operations: `producer_acquire()` (acquires an available stage/head), `producer_commit()` (commits async operations on the acquired stage), `consumer_wait()` (waits for completion on the oldest stage/tail), and `consumer_release()` (releases the oldest stage for reuse).

facts.claim: Pipeline Shared State
facts.note: Finite resources are encapsulated in `cuda::pipeline_shared_state<scope, count>`, allowing up to `count` concurrent stages. If all stages are in use, `producer_acquire()` blocks producers until consumers release stages.

facts.claim: Thread Roles & Scopes
facts.note: The API supports flexible thread participation. `pipeline<thread_scope_block>` allows arbitrary subsets of threads to act as producers, consumers, or both (via `cuda::pipeline_role`), but incurs overhead (e.g., shared memory barriers). For full-block participation, `pipeline<thread_scope_thread>` combined with `__syncthreads()` or `__syncwarp()` is more efficient.

facts.claim: Usage Patterns
facts.note: Common implementations use a prolog (kickstart), a pipelined loop (overlapping the next `memcpy_async` with the previous batch's computation), and an epilog (drain). Multi-stage pipelines effectively hide `memcpy_async` latency.

facts.claim: Requirements & Headers
facts.note: Requires CUDA 11.0+, ISO C++ 2011 compatibility, and `#include <cuda/pipeline>`. A legacy C-like interface is available via `cuda_pipeline.h`.

caveats: The C++ `cuda::pipeline` API is distinct from the older C-like `__pipeline_*` primitives; the guide recommends the C++ API for modern codebases.
Warp entanglement (divergence) can impact performance for `commit`, `wait`, and `arrive` operations; the guide advises keeping these operations converged or using `__syncwarp()` before them.

Using `pipeline<thread_scope_block>` with mixed producer/consumer roles incurs synchronization overhead (shared memory barriers) that can be avoided with `thread_scope_thread` when all threads participate.

concept/cuda-pipeline-primitives.title: CUDA Pipeline Primitives Interface
concept/cuda-pipeline-primitives.summary: C-like Pipeline Primitives Interface (__pipeline_memcpy_async, __pipeline_commit, __pipeline_wait_prior, __pipeline_arrive_on). Covers function signatures, usage requirements, race condition warnings, and barrier synchronization details.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence details the C-like Pipeline Primitives API, positioned as an alternative to the C++ `cuda::pipeline` interface for environments lacking C++11 support. It covers four core primitives for asynchronous memory transfer, batch management, synchronization, and barrier integration, along with strict alignment and race-condition constraints.

facts.claim: Purpose & Headers
facts.note: Provides a C-like interface for `cuda::memcpy_async` functionality. Requires `<cuda_pipeline.h>` for standard compilation, or `<cuda_pipeline_primitives.h>` when compiling without ISO C++ 2011 compatibility.

facts.claim: __pipeline_memcpy_async
facts.note: Submits an asynchronous copy from global memory (`src_global`) to shared memory (`dst_shared`). Automatically zero-fills the destination up to `size_and_align` if `zfill` is provided.

facts.claim: __pipeline_commit
facts.note: Commits the pending `memcpy_async` operation to the pipeline as the current batch.

facts.claim: __pipeline_wait_prior
facts.note: Blocks execution until all pipeline batches up to index `L-N` have completed, where `L` is the sequence index of the last `__pipeline_commit()` call by that thread.

facts.claim: __pipeline_arrive_on
facts.note: Attaches async operations to a shared memory barrier (`__mbarrier_t*`). Increments the barrier's arrival count upon submission and decrements it upon completion, yielding a net zero change. Users must ensure the count does not exceed `__mbarrier_maximum_count()`.

facts.claim: Alignment & Size Constraints
facts.note: `size_and_align` must be exactly 4, 8, or 16 bytes and must match the memory alignment of both `dst_shared` and `src_global`.

facts.claim: Race Condition Safety
facts.note: Strict prohibition on reading, writing, storing, or applying atomic updates to source/destination memory between submission and completion wait. Violating this introduces a race condition.

caveats: The C-like primitives are explicitly framed as a fallback for non-C++11 compilation environments, contrasting with the C++ `cuda::pipeline` object model shown in the surrounding context.
Barrier integration relies on user-managed counts; exceeding `__mbarrier_maximum_count()` is undefined behavior.
Zero-fill (`zfill`) is an optional extension to the copy operation, padding up to the alignment boundary.

concept/cuda-pointer-attributes.title: CUDA Pointer Attributes
concept/cuda-pointer-attributes.summary: Details the use of cudaPointerGetAttributes() to identify pointer types (cudaMemoryTypeManaged, cudaMemoryTypeHost, cudaMemoryTypeUnregistered) and clarifies that attributes indicate allocation/registration method, not physical location. Includes a runtime detection code example.

research_reports.context_summary: Section 24.1.2.5 sits within the Unified Memory chapter, bridging allocation APIs (`cudaMallocManaged`, `__managed__`) and runtime support detection. It provides the definitive API for querying pointer allocation/registration types at runtime.

facts.claim: API Entry Point
facts.note: Programs query pointer metadata via `cudaPointerGetAttributes()`, which populates a `cudaPointerAttributes` struct.

facts.claim: Memory Type Enum Values
facts.note: The `type` field returns: `cudaMemoryTypeManaged` (CUDA Managed Memory), `cudaMemoryTypeHost` (System-Allocated Memory registered via `cudaHostRegister()`), `cudaMemoryTypeDevice` (standard device memory), and `cudaMemoryTypeUnregistered` (System-Allocated Memory unknown to CUDA).

facts.claim: Allocation Method vs. Physical Location
facts.note: Attributes explicitly state how memory was allocated or registered, not where it physically resides. This is critical for Unified Memory workflows where data migrates transparently.

facts.claim: Runtime Detection Pattern
facts.note: Typical usage pairs `cudaPointerGetAttributes()` with device attribute queries (`cudaDevAttrPageableMemoryAccess`, `cudaDevAttrConcurrentManagedAccess`) to determine if a pointer's memory type is fully supported by the current hardware (Unified vs. Not Unified).

facts.claim: Supported Allocation Types in Example
facts.note: The provided example demonstrates runtime detection across five pointer sources: `malloc`, `cudaMallocManaged`, `cudaMallocHost`, `cudaMalloc`, and `__managed__` global variables.

caveats: Pointer attributes are static metadata about the allocation/registration path, not dynamic residency trackers. Applications relying on physical location must use migration APIs (e.g., `cudaMemPrefetchAsync`) instead of relying solely on attributes.
The `cudaMemoryTypeHost` and `cudaMemoryTypeUnregistered` distinctions only apply to System-Allocated Memory; they do not indicate CPU residency.

concept/cuda-programming-interface.title: CUDA Programming Interface & NVCC Compilation
concept/cuda-programming-interface.summary: Covers Chapter 6: Programming interface overview, C++ extensions, runtime library, and nvcc compilation workflow.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence covers Chapter 6 (Programming Interface) and Section 6.1 (Compilation with NVCC), detailing the structure of the CUDA C++ programming interface, the relationship between the runtime and driver APIs, and the offline compilation workflow using nvcc. The surrounding context (L983-L1260) provides additional details on newer features like cluster launches, compute capabilities, JIT compilation, and memory hierarchy, but the assigned evidence strictly covers the core interface and nvcc offline compilation workflow.

facts.claim: The CUDA C++ programming interface consists of minimal C++ language extensions and a runtime library.
facts.note: Extensions allow defining kernels as C++ functions and specifying grid/block dimensions. The runtime provides host-side C/C++ functions for device memory management, data transfers, and multi-device handling.

facts.claim: The CUDA Runtime API is built on the lower-level CUDA Driver API.
facts.note: The Driver API exposes lower-level concepts like contexts and modules. Most applications use the Runtime API for conciseness, falling back to the Driver API only when specific control is needed.

facts.claim: nvcc is the compiler driver used to compile CUDA C++ or PTX code into device-executable binary.
facts.note: It simplifies the compilation process by invoking various tools and providing familiar command-line options.

facts.claim: Offline compilation workflow separates host and device code.
facts.note: Device code is compiled into PTX and/or cubin. Host code is modified to replace the <<<...>>> launch syntax with CUDA runtime function calls. The output is either C++ or object code, which applications typically link to, or applications can use the Driver API directly to load PTX/cubin.

facts.claim: Legacy Warning
facts.note: The source document explicitly warns that it has been replaced by a new CUDA Programming Guide as of CUDA 13.0 and is no longer updated.

caveats: The source document is marked as legacy (CUDA 13.0+). Modern workflows may rely on updated guides or JIT compilation (covered in surrounding context L1141-L1149 but outside assigned evidence).
The assigned evidence focuses strictly on the conceptual overview and offline compilation; it does not cover JIT compilation, binary/PTX compatibility flags, or the CUDA Runtime API reference.
contradictions: None detected within the assigned evidence scope.
recommended_structure: Overview of CUDA C++ Interface (Language Extensions + Runtime)

concept/cuda-programming-model.title: CUDA Programming Model
concept/cuda-programming-model.summary: Covers Chapter 5: Kernels, thread hierarchy, thread block clusters, memory hierarchy, heterogeneous programming, asynchronous SIMT, and compute capabilities.

research_reports.context_summary: Chapter 5 of the CUDA C Programming Guide outlines the core programming model, covering kernel execution, thread hierarchy (blocks, grids, clusters), memory spaces, heterogeneous host-device execution, asynchronous SIMT operations, and compute capability versioning.

facts.claim: Kernels are C++ functions marked with __global__ that execute in parallel across multiple threads. Execution configuration (grid and block dimensions) is specified using the <<<...>>> syntax, and threads access unique IDs via built-in variables like threadIdx and blockIdx.
facts.note: I will fix the JSON formatting in the actual output to be valid markdown/text as requested by the system prompt style, but keep the structure.

facts.claim: Threads are grouped into thread blocks (max 1024 threads) and organized into grids. Blocks execute independently and can be scheduled in any order across cores. Threads within a block cooperate via shared memory and synchronize using __syncthreads() or the Cooperative Groups API.
facts.note: Same fix needed.

facts.claim: Thread Block Clusters (introduced in Compute Capability 9.0) group blocks so they are guaranteed to be co-scheduled on a single GPU Processing Cluster (GPC). Clusters support hardware synchronization via cluster.sync() and access Distributed Shared Memory. They can be configured via __cluster_dims__ or cudaLaunchKernelEx.
facts.note: Same fix needed.

facts.claim: The __block_size__ attribute allows explicit compile-time configuration of the number of clusters and blocks per cluster, treating the grid launch argument as cluster count rather than block count.
facts.note: Same fix needed.

facts.claim: CUDA provides multiple memory spaces: private local memory (per thread), shared memory (per block, low-latency), global memory (all threads), and read-only constant/texture memory. Global, constant, and texture memory persist across kernel launches.
facts.note: Same fix needed.
