facts.claim: The model assumes separate host (CPU) and device (GPU) memory spaces. Unified Memory bridges these spaces, providing a coherent, common address space accessible by both CPUs and GPUs, enabling memory oversubscription.
facts.note: Same fix needed.

facts.claim: The Asynchronous SIMT Programming Model (Ampere+) allows memory operations to be initiated by a thread and executed asynchronously ('as-if' by another). Synchronization uses objects like cuda::barrier or cuda::pipeline across thread scopes: thread, block, device, and system.
facts.note: Same fix needed.

facts.claim: Compute Capability (X.Y) identifies GPU hardware features and supported instructions. Major revision indicates the core architecture (e.g., 9=Hopper, 8=Ampere, 7=Volta, 6=Pascal, 5=Maxwell, 3=Kepler), while minor revision indicates incremental updates. It is distinct from the CUDA software platform version.
facts.note: Same fix needed.

caveats: The source document is marked as legacy (replaced by a new CUDA Programming Guide as of CUDA 13.0), but the programming model concepts remain foundational.
Thread block size limit mentioned as 'up to 1024 threads' reflects the legacy guide's phrasing; actual limits may vary by architecture, but the source explicitly states this constraint.
contradictions: None detected within the assigned scope. The guide consistently presents the model as a scalable abstraction independent of physical core count.

concept/cuda-read-write-coherency.title: Read/Write Coherency
concept/cuda-read-write-coherency.summary: Explains that texture and surface memory is cached and not kept coherent with global/surface writes within the same kernel call. Reads return undefined data if written to in the same kernel call, requiring updates from previous kernel calls or memory copies.

research_reports.context_summary: The evidence is located in Section 6.2.14.4, which sits between the descriptions of CUDA Arrays (6.2.14.3) and Graphics Interoperability (6.2.15). It specifically addresses the caching behavior of texture and surface memory relative to global and surface writes.

facts.claim: Texture and surface memory are cached.
facts.note: Caching behavior is referenced, with a cross-reference to the 'Device Memory Accesses' section for details.

facts.claim: Cache coherency is not maintained within the same kernel call between cached texture/surface memory and global memory or surface memory writes.
facts.note: Writes to global memory or surface memory do not automatically invalidate or update the texture/surface cache during a single kernel execution.

facts.claim: Reading a texture or surface location that has been written to via a global write or surface write in the same kernel call yields undefined data.
facts.note: This applies regardless of whether the write was performed by the same thread or a different thread within the same kernel launch.

facts.claim: Safe reading of a texture or surface memory location requires the data to have been updated by a previous kernel call or a memory copy operation.
facts.note: This establishes a strict synchronization boundary: kernel calls and memory copies are the only safe mechanisms to guarantee coherency for subsequent texture/surface reads.

caveats: The source explicitly states that coherency is only guaranteed across kernel call boundaries or explicit memory copies. No hardware or software cache invalidation mechanisms are mentioned within this specific excerpt.
The text references 'Device Memory Accesses' for deeper caching details, but those details are outside the provided evidence window.
recommended_structure: Definition of Read/Write Coherency in CUDA
Caching Behavior of Texture and Surface Memory

concept/cuda-runtime.title: CUDA Runtime
concept/cuda-runtime.summary: Introduces the cudart library, its linking methods, symbol prefixing, and provides an overview of runtime features like device memory, shared memory, async execution, and graphics interoperability.

research_reports.context_summary: Section 6.2 serves as the introductory overview for the CUDA Runtime API within Chapter 6 (Programming Interface). It outlines the runtime's implementation, linking requirements, naming conventions, and the broad functional areas it covers before diving into detailed subsections.

facts.claim: The CUDA Runtime is implemented in the cudart library, linkable either statically (cudart.lib/libcudart.a) or dynamically (cudart.dll/libcudart.so).
facts.note: Dynamic linking typically requires bundling the shared library with the application installation package.

facts.claim: Passing the address of a CUDA runtime symbol between components is only safe if they link to the same instance of the cudart library.
facts.note: None

facts.claim: All CUDA Runtime API entry points are prefixed with 'cuda'.
facts.note: None

facts.claim: The runtime operates on the assumption of separate host and device memory spaces.
facts.note: None

facts.claim: The runtime API covers device memory management, shared memory, page-locked host memory, asynchronous execution, multi-device systems, error checking, call stack management, texture/surface memory, and graphics interoperability (OpenGL, Direct3D).
facts.note: None

caveats: This evidence is strictly an introductory overview. Detailed mechanics (e.g., context creation, JIT compilation, specific API signatures) are covered in subsequent subsections (6.2.1 onwards) and are outside the assigned evidence range.

concept/cuda-runtime-driver-interoperabi.title: CUDA Runtime and Driver API Interoperability
concept/cuda-runtime-driver-interoperabi.summary: Explains how runtime and driver APIs can be mixed. Driver-created contexts are picked up by runtime calls, and vice versa. Memory allocated via one API can be cast and used by the other.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence is extracted from Section 21.4 ('Interoperability between Runtime and Driver APIs'). It sits between Section 21.3 (Kernel Execution parameter alignment) and Section 21.5 (Driver Entry Point Access), serving as a dedicated bridge explaining how the two CUDA APIs can coexist and share resources within a single application.

facts.claim: Runtime and Driver APIs can be mixed in the same application.
facts.note: Context management is bidirectional: driver-created contexts are automatically picked up by subsequent runtime calls, while runtime-initialized contexts can be retrieved via `cuCtxGetCurrent()` for driver use.

facts.claim: The implicitly created runtime context is termed the 'primary context' and is fully manageable via the Driver API's Primary Context Management functions.
facts.note: None

facts.claim: Device memory allocated with one API can be safely used or freed by the other. Pointers are interchangeable via casting (`CUdeviceptr` ↔ `void*`/`float*`).
facts.note: Code example demonstrates `cuMemAlloc`/`cudaMalloc` and pointer casting.

facts.claim: Driver API applications can directly invoke libraries built on the Runtime API (e.g., cuFFT, cuBLAS).
facts.note: None

facts.claim: Device and version management functions from the reference manual are fully interchangeable between both APIs.
facts.note: None

caveats: None detected. The scope is strictly limited to API interoperability and does not conflict with surrounding sections on PTX JIT linking or entry point access.

concept/cuda-runtime-initialization.title: CUDA Runtime Initialization
concept/cuda-runtime-initialization.summary: Details runtime initialization behavior in CUDA 12.0+, context creation, cudaSetDevice() changes, and warnings about global state during program initiation/termination.

research_reports.scope: Section 6.2.1 Initialization within the CUDA Runtime chapter
research_reports.context_summary: The evidence details how the CUDA Runtime API (`cudart`) bootstraps device contexts, manages implicit vs. explicit initialization, and handles context lifecycle. It highlights significant behavioral shifts introduced in CUDA 12.0 regarding `cudaSetDevice()` and notes constraints around global state and non-initializing API functions.

facts.claim: Explicit initialization is performed via cudaInitDevice() and cudaSetDevice() (CUDA 12.0+), which initialize the runtime and the primary context for the specified device.
facts.note: Before CUDA 12.0, cudaSetDevice() was lazy; developers used cudaFree(0) as a no-op to isolate initialization timing and error handling.

facts.claim: If explicit initialization calls are absent, the runtime implicitly defaults to device 0 and self-initializes on demand when processing the first API request requiring an active context.
facts.note: Context creation is shared across all host threads and transparently triggers JIT compilation of device code and loading it into device memory.

facts.claim: cudaDeviceReset() destroys the primary context of the currently active device; subsequent runtime calls on that device will recreate a new primary context.
facts.note: Error handling and version management runtime functions do not trigger initialization.

facts.claim: CUDA interfaces rely on global state initialized at program start and destroyed at termination. Using runtime/driver APIs during program startup or teardown (after main) causes undefined behavior.
facts.note: None

caveats: CUDA 12.0+ changed cudaSetDevice() from lazy to explicit initialization, requiring developers to check its return value for initialization errors.
Pre-12.0 codebases often relied on cudaFree(0) for timing/error isolation; this pattern is now obsolete but may still appear in legacy code.

concept/cuda-stream-ordered-memory-adden.title: CUDA Stream-Ordered Memory Addendums
concept/cuda-stream-ordered-memory-adden.summary: Covers specific behaviors and limitations for async memcpy, pointer attributes, graph memset nodes, and CPU virtual memory limits when using stream-ordered allocators.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence (L15863-L15883) comprises Section 15.13 'Addendums' from the CUDA C Programming Guide. It sits between the IPC memory pool sections (15.11) and the Graph Memory Nodes chapter (16), serving as a dedicated caveat and debugging reference for the stream-ordered memory allocator introduced in Chapter 15.

facts.claim: cudaMemcpyAsync Context Requirement
facts.note: Async memcpy operations involving `cudaMallocAsync` memory require the calling thread's current context to match the specified stream's context. This constraint does not apply to `cudaMemcpyPeerAsync`.

facts.claim: Post-Free Attribute Queries
facts.note: Invoking `cuPointerGetAttribute` after `cudaFreeAsync` on an allocation results in undefined behavior, regardless of whether the allocation remains accessible from a given stream.

facts.claim: Graph Memset Node Incompatibility
facts.note: `cuGraphAddMemsetNode` is incompatible with stream-ordered allocations. However, memset operations targeting these allocations can still be successfully captured via stream capture.

facts.claim: Pointer Attribute Behavior
facts.note: `cuPointerGetAttributes` works on stream-ordered memory. `CU_POINTER_ATTRIBUTE_CONTEXT` returns NULL due to lack of context association. `CU_POINTER_ATTRIBUTE_DEVICE_ORDINAL` identifies the allocation's location (useful for `cudaMemcpyPeerAsync`), and `CU_POINTER_ATTRIBUTE_MEMPOOL_HANDLE` (CUDA 11.3+) aids debugging and IPC verification.

facts.claim: CPU Virtual Memory Limits
facts.note: Using `ulimit -v` to set VRAM limitations is unsupported and should be avoided when utilizing stream-ordered memory allocator APIs.

caveats: The guide explicitly notes these are driver-level behaviors and constraints specific to the stream-ordered allocator, distinct from traditional `cudaMalloc` semantics.

concept/cuda-streams.title: CUDA Streams
concept/cuda-streams.summary: Ordered sequences of commands (kernels, copies) that enable concurrent execution across multiple streams. Covers stream lifecycle, dependency management, and resource cleanup behavior.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence sits within Section 6.2.8 (Asynchronous Concurrent Execution). It acts as the conceptual bridge between the preceding enumeration of GPU concurrency capabilities (L2117-L2172) and the subsequent detailed sections on synchronization, overlapping behavior, and stream attributes (L2219-L2338). It establishes the fundamental abstraction (streams) that enables the concurrency features described earlier.

facts.claim: A stream is a sequence of commands (kernel launches and host-device memory copies) that execute in strict order.
facts.note: Different streams may execute out of order or concurrently, but this is not guaranteed and must not be relied upon for correctness (e.g., inter-kernel communication is undefined).

facts.claim: Commands execute when all dependencies are met, which can include previously launched commands on the same stream or dependencies from other streams.
facts.note: A successful synchronize call guarantees that all commands launched in the stream have completed.

facts.claim: Streams are created using cudaStreamCreate and destroyed using cudaStreamDestroy.
facts.note: cudaStreamDestroy returns immediately if the device is still processing work in the stream, automatically releasing resources once the device completes all pending tasks.

facts.claim: Streams are used to issue asynchronous operations concurrently, but overlapping data transfers require page-locked host memory.
facts.note: The guide provides a code example demonstrating concurrent host-to-device copies, kernel launches, and device-to-host copies across multiple streams.

caveats: The text explicitly warns that concurrent execution across different streams is not guaranteed and should not be used for inter-kernel communication correctness.
Page-locked host memory is a strict requirement for achieving overlap between stream operations.

concept/cuda-surface-memory.title: Surface Memory
concept/cuda-surface-memory.summary: Introduces surface memory for compute capability 2.0+ devices, allowing read/write access to CUDA arrays via surface objects. References Table 27 for maximum surface dimensions.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence covers the core definition and requirements of Surface Memory. The surrounding context (L3879-L3990) provides detailed API usage, byte addressing rules, and coherency caveats, but these specific details fall outside the assigned evidence range.

facts.claim: Surface memory enables reading and writing to a CUDA array via a surface object.
facts.note: Core functionality of surface memory.

facts.claim: Surface memory requires compute capability 2.0 or higher.
facts.note: Hardware requirement.

facts.claim: The underlying CUDA array must be created with the cudaArraySurfaceLoadStore flag.
facts.note: Memory allocation requirement.

facts.claim: Maximum surface width, height, and depth depend on the compute capability of the device.
facts.note: Referenced in Table 27 within the documentation.

caveats: The assigned evidence is limited to the high-level definition and requirements. Detailed implementation specifics such as byte addressing, the Surface Object API, and read/write coherency rules are present in the surrounding context (L3879-L3990) but are excluded from these findings as they fall outside the assigned evidence range.
recommended_structure: Note on API and Addressing (referencing surrounding context for lead writer)

concept/cuda-surface-object-api.title: Surface Object API
concept/cuda-surface-object-api.summary: Explains surface object creation via cudaCreateSurfaceObject(), byte-addressing differences compared to texture memory, and provides a complete code sample for a simple copy kernel using surf2Dread/surf2Dwrite, including host setup, array allocation, surface creation, kernel launch, and cleanup.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence details the creation, addressing model, and usage of surface objects in CUDA, contrasting them with texture memory and providing a complete host/device code example for a 2D copy kernel.

facts.claim: Surface objects are created using cudaCreateSurfaceObject() from a struct cudaResourceDesc (typically cudaResourceTypeArray) and destroyed with cudaDestroySurfaceObject().
facts.note: Byte addressing is the core differentiator from texture memory; X-coordinates must be multiplied by the element's byte size (e.g., 4*x for 32-bit data), while Y-coordinate offsets are handled internally via line pitch.

facts.claim: Underlying CUDA arrays must be allocated with the cudaArraySurfaceLoadStore flag to enable read/write access via surfaces.
facts.note: Device kernels access surfaces using functions like surf2Dread() and surf2Dwrite().

facts.claim: The provided code example demonstrates the full lifecycle: host data allocation, 2D array transfer, surface object creation, kernel launch, and cleanup.
facts.note: None.

caveats: Surface memory is cached and not kept coherent with global memory writes within the same kernel call (L3990), meaning reads/writes across kernels or memory copies are required for consistency.
contradictions: None detected.

concept/cuda-tcc-mode.title: Tesla Compute Cluster (TCC) Mode
concept/cuda-tcc-mode.summary: Describes TCC mode, a configuration for Tesla and Quadro GPUs on Windows via nvidia-smi. TCC mode disables all graphics functionality, optimizing the device for pure compute workloads.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence sits within Section 6.4 (Compute Modes) and Section 6.5 (Mode Switches), which detail how GPUs can be restricted for compute-only usage or how display changes can invalidate CUDA contexts. The TCC mode snippet serves as the specific Windows implementation of a compute-restricted GPU state.

facts.claim: TCC (Tesla Compute Cluster) mode is a Windows device driver configuration accessible for Tesla and Quadro Series GPUs.
facts.note: Managed via NVIDIA's System Management Interface (`nvidia-smi`).

facts.claim: Enabling TCC mode completely removes support for any graphics functionality on the device.
facts.note: Positions TCC as a compute-exclusive mode within the broader Compute Modes section.

caveats: The source document is marked as legacy (replaced by the new CUDA Programming Guide as of CUDA 13.0), so TCC mode details may be outdated or relocated in current documentation.

concept/cuda-texture-fetching.title: Texture Fetching
concept/cuda-texture-fetching.summary: Explains texture coordinate systems, nearest-point sampling, linear filtering formulas for 1D/2D/3D textures, and table lookup implementations.

research_reports.context_summary: Chapter 19 of the legacy CUDA C Programming Guide detailing the mathematical formulas and constraints for texture fetching, including nearest-point sampling, linear filtering, and table lookup implementations.

facts.claim: The source document is explicitly marked as legacy and no longer updated as of CUDA 13.0.
facts.note: Important context for lead writer regarding documentation currency.

facts.claim: A bound texture is represented as an array T of texels: N for 1D, N×M for 2D, and N×M×L for 3D.
facts.note: Defines the underlying data structure for texture coordinates.

facts.claim: Texture coordinates can be non-normalized (x, y, z) or normalized (x/N, y/M, z/L). Out-of-range coordinates are remapped based on the addressing mode.
facts.note: Clarifies coordinate space and boundary handling.

facts.claim: Nearest-point sampling returns the texel at the floor of the coordinate: T[floor(x)] for 1D, T[floor(x), floor(y)] for 2D, and T[floor(x), floor(y), floor(z)] for 3D.
facts.note: Core formula for point sampling.

facts.claim: For integer textures, the fetched value can optionally be remapped to the [0.0, 1.0] range.
facts.note: Special case for integer texture formats.

facts.claim: Linear filtering is only available for floating-point textures.
facts.note: Critical hardware/format constraint.

facts.claim: Linear filtering uses coordinate-shifted fractional parts: xB = x - 0.5, α = frac(xB), i = floor(xB) (and similarly for y, z).
facts.note: Explains the 0.5 offset used in interpolation calculations.

facts.claim: Interpolation weights (α, β, γ) are stored in a 9-bit fixed-point format with 8 fractional bits, ensuring 1.0 is exactly represented.
facts.note: Details hardware implementation precision for filtering weights.

facts.claim: A table lookup TL(x) over an interval [0, R] can be implemented via texture filtering using the mapping TL(x) = tex(((N-1)/R)x + 0.5).
facts.note: Shows how texture hardware can emulate lookup tables.

facts.claim: The table lookup mapping guarantees boundary conditions: TL(0) = T[0] and TL(R) = T[N-1].
facts.note: Ensures correct edge behavior for table lookups.

caveats: The entire chapter is legacy (CUDA 13.0+). Lead writer should verify if modern CUDA Programming Guide has superseded these formulas or constraints.
Linear filtering formulas provided are specific to the legacy guide's coordinate system (xB = x - 0.5 offset). Modern guides may use different normalization conventions.
contradictions: None detected within the assigned context. The formulas are consistent with standard bilinear/trilinear interpolation but include a legacy-specific 0.5 coordinate shift.

concept/cuda-texture-gather.title: Texture Gather
concept/cuda-texture-gather.summary: Details the tex2Dgather() function for 2D textures, which returns four 32-bit values for a specific component from the four texels used in bilinear filtering. Covers 8-bit fractional precision limitations, cudaArrayTextureGather flag requirements, size limits, and compute capability 2.0+ requirement.

research_reports.context_summary: Falls within the CUDA texture memory subsystem (Section 6.2.14), positioned immediately after layered/cubemap texture descriptions and before surface memory. Serves as a component-specific gathering alternative to full bilinear interpolation.

facts.claim: Texture gather is a specialized fetch operation available exclusively for two-dimensional textures, implemented via the tex2Dgather() function.
facts.note: Parameters match tex2D() plus an additional comp argument (0–3).

facts.claim: Returns four 32-bit values representing the specified component (comp) from the four texels that would normally participate in bilinear filtering.
facts.note: Example provided: comp=2 extracts the third channel from the four filter texels.

facts.claim: Internal texture coordinate computation uses only 8 bits of fractional precision, which can cause unexpected index selection near boundaries where regular filtering weights approach 1.0.
facts.note: Example: x=2.49805 rounds to indices 2 and 3 instead of 1 and 2.

facts.claim: Requires CUDA arrays allocated with the cudaArrayTextureGather flag.
facts.note: Maximum width/height is defined in Table 27 and is strictly smaller than the limit for standard texture fetches.

facts.claim: Hardware support requires compute capability 2.0 or higher.
facts.note: None

caveats: Coordinate precision limitation may cause off-by-one index behavior near texture boundaries; developers should account for 8-bit fractional rounding when using gather near 1.0 filter weights.
Array creation must explicitly include cudaArrayTextureGather; standard arrays will not support this operation.

concept/cuda-time-function.title: Time Function
concept/cuda-time-function.summary: Device functions that return the value of a per-multiprocessor counter incremented every clock cycle, used to measure thread execution time in clock cycles.

research_reports.context_summary: The Time Function is documented in Section 10.13, situated between cache load/store functions (10.10-10.12) and atomic functions (10.14).

facts.claim: The Time Function provides two device-side functions: `clock_t clock();` and `long long int clock64();`.
facts.note: Execution is restricted to device code.

facts.claim: The functions return the value of a per-multiprocessor hardware counter that increments once per clock cycle.
facts.note: Sampling the counter at a kernel's start and end, computing the difference, and recording it per thread measures the total clock cycles the device took to finish executing that thread.

facts.claim: This measurement does not reflect the actual number of clock cycles spent executing the thread's instructions.
facts.note: The measured value is strictly greater than the actual instruction execution time because threads are time-sliced by the hardware.

caveats: The documentation explicitly warns that the returned value includes scheduling overhead and time-slicing delays, making it a wall-clock metric per thread rather than a pure instruction-cycle metric.
contradictions: None detected within the assigned evidence.
recommended_structure: Important Caveats (Time Slicing vs. Actual Execution Cycles)

concept/cuda-versioning-compatibility.title: CUDA Versioning and Compatibility
concept/cuda-versioning-compatibility.summary: Explains the two key version numbers in CUDA development: compute capability (device features) and driver API version. Details backward compatibility (applications compiled against older API versions work on newer drivers) and lack of forward compatibility. Outlines rules for mixing driver/runtime versions and static linking of CUDA Toolkit libraries.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence (Section 6.3) serves as a foundational conceptual overview of CUDA versioning. It is situated between detailed external memory and synchronization APIs (Section 6.2.16.5) and device-specific operational modes (Section 6.4), providing necessary prerequisites before the document transitions into hardware implementation (Chapter 7).

facts.claim: Developers must track two version numbers: Compute Capability (device specs/features) and the CUDA Driver API version (features supported by the driver/runtime).
facts.note: The Driver API version is defined as `CUDA_VERSION` in the driver header file.

facts.claim: The Driver API is backward compatible (applications compiled against a specific version work on subsequent driver releases) but not forward compatible (applications will not work on previous driver versions).
facts.note: Applications, plug-ins, and libraries (including the CUDA runtime) follow this compatibility rule.

facts.claim: Only one version of the CUDA Driver can be installed at a time on a system.
facts.note: The installed driver must be of the same or higher version than the maximum Driver API version against which any application, plug-in, or library was built.

facts.claim: Plug-ins and libraries must use the same version of the CUDA Runtime unless they statically link to it, in which case multiple runtime versions can coexist in the same process space.
facts.note: If `nvcc` is used to link the application, the static version of the CUDA Runtime library is used by default.

facts.claim: All CUDA Toolkit libraries are statically linked against the CUDA Runtime.
facts.note: Plug-ins and libraries must also use the same version of any runtime-dependent libraries (such as cuFFT, cuBLAS) unless statically linking to those libraries.

facts.claim: For Tesla GPU products, CUDA 10 introduced a forward-compatible upgrade path for the usermode components of the CUDA Driver.
facts.note: The version requirements described in this section apply to the version of the user-mode components.

caveats: The source document is marked as legacy as of CUDA 13.0, replaced by the new CUDA Programming Guide.
recommended_structure: Overview of Version Numbers (Compute Capability vs. Driver API)

concept/cuda-vulkan-interoperability.title: CUDA-Vulkan Interoperability
concept/cuda-vulkan-interoperability.summary: Covers the interoperability between CUDA and Vulkan, including extent conversion, memory object importing, synchronization object importing, and signaling/waiting operations.

research_reports.context_summary: The source covers CUDA-Vulkan interoperability workflows, focusing on importing Vulkan memory and synchronization objects via various handle types, mapping them to CUDA buffers/mipmapped arrays, converting Vulkan formats/extents to CUDA equivalents, and managing async signaling/waiting semantics. It also briefly notes indirect OpenGL interop via Vulkan exports.

facts.claim: Vulkan memory objects are imported into CUDA using `cudaImportExternalMemory` with handle descriptors supporting `VK_EXTERNAL_MEMORY_HANDLE_TYPE_OPAQUE_WIN32_BIT` (NT/named handles) and `VK_EXTERNAL_MEMORY_HANDLE_TYPE_OPAQUE_WIN32_KMT_BIT` (D3DKMT handles).
facts.note: Handle ownership semantics differ: KMT handles do not hold a reference and are auto-destroyed when other references vanish. NT handles hold a reference and must be explicitly closed/freed by the application.

facts.claim: Imported memory is mapped to CUDA using `cudaExternalMemoryGetMappedBuffer` (for buffers) or `cudaExternalMemoryGetMappedMipmappedArray` (for arrays). Offset, size, dimensions, format, and mip levels must exactly match Vulkan creation parameters. Mapped buffers are freed with `cudaFree()`, mipmapped arrays with `cudaFreeMipmappedArray()`.
facts.note: If a mipmapped array is bound as a color target in Vulkan, the `cudaArrayColorAttachment` flag must be set during CUDA mapping.

facts.claim: Vulkan formats and extents map directly to CUDA equivalents: e.g., `VK_FORMAT_R8_UINT` → `cudaChannelFormatKindUnsigned`, `VK_FORMAT_R32_SFLOAT` → `cudaChannelFormatKindFloat`. Image view types map to CUDA array flags (e.g., `VK_IMAGE_VIEW_TYPE_CUBE` → `cudaArrayCubemap`, `VK_IMAGE_USAGE_COLOR_ATTACHMENT_BIT` → `cudaArrayColorAttachment`).
facts.note: Extent mapping adjusts `cudaExtent` fields based on `VkImageViewType` (e.g., 2D arrays map `arrayLayers` to `cudaExtent.depth`).

facts.claim: Vulkan semaphores are imported via `cudaImportExternalSemaphore` using file descriptors (`VK_EXTERNAL_SEMAPHORE_HANDLE_TYPE_OPAQUE_FD_BIT`), NT handles (`VK_EXTERNAL_SEMAPHORE_HANDLE_TYPE_OPAQUE_WIN32_BIT`), or D3DKMT handles (`VK_EXTERNAL_SEMAPHORE_HANDLE_TYPE_OPAQUE_WIN32_KMT_BIT`).
facts.note: CUDA assumes ownership of imported file descriptors (post-import usage causes undefined behavior). NT handles are not owned by CUDA and must be closed by the app. KMT handles do not hold a reference and are auto-destroyed.

facts.claim: Signaling and waiting on imported semaphores use `cudaSignalExternalSemaphoresAsync` and `cudaWaitExternalSemaphoresAsync`. Signaling sets the semaphore to signaled; the corresponding wait must be issued in Vulkan afterward. Waiting blocks until signaled, then resets the semaphore to unsignaled; the corresponding signal must be issued in Vulkan before the wait.
facts.note: Both operations are stream-ordered and asynchronous.

facts.claim: Indirect OpenGL-CUDA interoperability can be achieved by exporting Vulkan memory and synchronization objects to both OpenGL and CUDA, leveraging extensions like `GL_EXT_memory_object` and `GL_EXT_semaphore`.
facts.note: This bypasses traditional direct OpenGL-CUDA handle consumption by routing through Vulkan exports.

caveats: Handle ownership and lifetime management are critical: file descriptors are consumed by CUDA, NT handles require manual closure, and KMT handles rely on external reference counting.
Mapping parameters (offset, size, format, extent, flags) must strictly match the Vulkan-side creation/mapping to avoid undefined behavior or validation errors.
Signaling/waiting ordering is strict: Vulkan signals must precede CUDA waits, and CUDA signals must precede Vulkan waits.

concept/custom-abi-pragmas.title: 10.43. Custom ABI Pragmas
concept/custom-abi-pragmas.summary: Covers the #pragma nv_abi directive for optimizing separate compilation performance, its arguments for register preservation, and placement rules for direct vs indirect calls.

research_reports.context_summary: Section 10.43 sits between Diagnostic Pragmas (10.42) and CUDA C++ Memory Model (10.44) in the CUDA C Programming Guide. It focuses on compiler directives for optimizing device function call ABIs, specifically for separate compilation workflows.

facts.claim: Purpose
facts.note: The #pragma nv_abi directive enables applications compiled in separate compilation mode to achieve performance similar to whole program compilation by modifying custom ABI properties.

facts.claim: Syntax & Arguments
facts.note: Syntax is #pragma nv_abi preserve_n_data(ICE) preserve_n_control(ICE), where ICE is an integral constant expression. Arguments are optional but at least one is required, and order does not matter.

facts.claim: Register Limits
facts.note: preserve_n_data(ICE) limits preserved data registers during a function call, while preserve_n_control(ICE) limits preserved control registers.

facts.claim: Placement Rules (Direct Calls)
facts.note: For direct function calls, the pragma must be placed immediately before the device function's declaration or definition. Placing it before a direct call site has no effect.

facts.claim: Placement Rules (Indirect Calls)
facts.note: For indirect function calls, the pragma must be placed directly before the call site within a C++ expression statement inside a device function. Only indirect calls to free functions are supported; calls through function argument references or class member functions are not.

facts.claim: Constraints
facts.note: A program is ill-formed if the pragma arguments for a function's declaration and its corresponding definition do not match.

caveats: No contradictions or scope clashes detected. The section is self-contained and strictly defines compiler directive behavior for device code.

concept/data-migration-coherency-pre6x.title: Data Migration and Coherency on Pre-6.x Architectures
concept/data-migration-coherency-pre6x.summary: Explains the lack of fine-grained data movement on pre-6.x GPUs, the introduction of the GPU page faulting mechanism in CC 6.x, and system-wide virtual address space benefits.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence details the data migration mechanics for GPU architectures with compute capability lower than 6.0, contrasting them with the page faulting model introduced in 6.x. The surrounding context (L21849-L21856) explains that this migration behavior is tied to strict coherency constraints where the GPU requires exclusive access to managed data while active, preventing simultaneous CPU access without explicit synchronization.

facts.claim: Pre-6.0 GPU architectures do not support fine-grained, on-demand movement of managed data.
facts.note: To avoid memory access faults, all managed memory must be transferred to GPU memory before a kernel launch.

facts.claim: Compute capability 6.x introduced a GPU page faulting mechanism that enables on-demand migration.
facts.note: This eliminates the need for the CUDA system software to synchronize all managed memory allocations to the GPU before each kernel launch.

facts.claim: Unified Memory operates system-wide, allowing both CPUs and GPUs to fault on and migrate pages.
facts.note: Pages can be migrated from CPU memory or from the memory of other GPUs in the system.

facts.claim: Beyond migration, pages can be mapped into the GPU address space for access over PCIe or NVLink.
facts.note: Mapping on access can sometimes be faster than migrating the page to GPU memory.

caveats: The assigned evidence focuses on migration mechanics. The coherency constraints that necessitate this pre-6.x model (such as the inability to guarantee coherency during simultaneous CPU/GPU access) are detailed in the surrounding context (L21849-L21856).

concept/data-prefetching-cuda.title: Data Prefetching in CUDA
concept/data-prefetching-cuda.summary: Describes cudaMemPrefetchAsync, an asynchronous stream-ordered API to migrate data closer to a specified processor (GPU or CPU). Includes examples for System Allocator and Managed memory prefetching workflows.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence is Section 24.1.2.8.1 'Data Prefetching', positioned within the Unified Memory 'Performance Hints' chapter. It introduces the explicit prefetch API and demonstrates standard CPU↔GPU migration workflows, sitting between sections on memory discarding and data usage hints.

facts.claim: cudaMemPrefetchAsync is the primary asynchronous, stream-ordered API for migrating a memory region [devPtr, devPtr + count) closer to a specified processor.
facts.note: Target location is determined by cudaMemLocation.type (cudaMemLocationTypeDevice for GPU, cudaMemLocationTypeHost for CPU).

facts.claim: Prefetch operations respect strict stream ordering and allow concurrent access: migration begins only after prior stream operations complete, finishes before subsequent ones, and does not block the target processor from reading/writing the data during migration.
facts.note: None

facts.claim: Standard usage involves prefetching data to the GPU before kernel execution, and prefetching it back to the CPU after kernel completion, followed by stream synchronization.
facts.note: Demonstrated for both system-allocated (malloc) and managed (cudaMallocManaged) memory.

facts.claim: Prefetching is a performance hint that does not alter application semantics or results.
facts.note: Part of the broader Unified Memory performance optimization suite.

caveats: The API operates on Unified Memory allocations. While it can be used with system-allocated memory on systems with full Unified Memory support, its primary design target is cudaMallocManaged and __managed__ variables.
Prefetching is asynchronous and stream-ordered; improper stream synchronization or mixing with non-stream operations can lead to unexpected migration timing.
recommended_structure: Interaction with Unified Memory & Performance Hints

concept/data-usage-hints-cuda.title: Data Usage Hints in CUDA
concept/data-usage-hints-cuda.summary: Explains cudaMemAdvise and its values: cudaMemAdviseSetReadMostly, cudaMemAdviseSetPreferredLocation, and cudaMemAdviseSetAccessedBy. Provides code examples and explains how these hints guide the Unified Memory driver for optimal data placement and migration.

research_reports.context_summary: Section 24.1.2.8.3 details `cudaMemAdvise`, a performance-only API for hinting access patterns of managed memory regions when multiple processors concurrently access them. It covers three primary hint types, their unset counterparts, and notes that hints can be overridden by other migration APIs.

facts.claim: The `cudaMemAdvise` API allows applications to hint how a memory region `[devPtr, devPtr + count)` will be accessed when multiple processors simultaneously access it.
facts.note: The API signature is `cudaError_t cudaMemAdvise(const void *devPtr, size_t count, enum cudaMemoryAdvise advice, struct cudaMemLocation location);`

facts.claim: `cudaMemAdviseSetReadMostly` indicates data is mostly read and occasionally written, allowing the system to trade read bandwidth for write bandwidth on the region.
facts.note: Example usage shows combining this with prefetching to multiple GPUs to trigger read duplication rather than data migration.

facts.claim: `cudaMemAdviseSetPreferredLocation` tells the system that migrating the memory region away from its specified location is undesired. Setting `location.type` to `cudaMemLocationTypeHost` designates CPU memory as the preferred location.
facts.note: This hint can be overridden by other hints like `cudaMemPrefetchAsync`, which may still migrate the memory.

facts.claim: `cudaMemAdviseSetAccessedBy` signals that data will be frequently accessed by a specific device (`location.id` when `location.type` is `cudaMemLocationTypeDevice`), enabling the system to pre-establish memory mappings.
facts.note: This hint does not dictate data residency but can be combined with `cudaMemAdviseSetPreferredLocation`.

facts.claim: Each advice can be removed using corresponding unset values: `cudaMemAdviseUnsetReadMostly`, `cudaMemAdviseUnsetPreferredLocation`, and `cudaMemAdviseUnsetAccessedBy`.
facts.note: None of these hints alter application semantics or results; they are strictly performance hints that should only be applied when they improve performance.

caveats: Hints are performance-only and do not impact application semantics or correctness.
`cudaMemAdviseSetPreferredLocation` can be overridden by explicit prefetch operations.
Unset values must be used to remove previously applied advice.

concept/default-stream.title: Default Stream
concept/default-stream.summary: Kernel launches and memory copies without a stream parameter use the default stream, executing in order. Compilation flags like --default-stream per-thread or legacy alter whether the default stream is per-thread or a shared NULL stream.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence (L2219-L2230) defines the default stream's behavior, its relationship to the NULL stream, and how compilation flags dictate whether the default stream is shared across threads or isolated per thread.

facts.claim: Operations that omit the stream parameter or explicitly set it to zero are issued to the default stream and execute in sequential order.
facts.note: This establishes the baseline execution guarantee for unqualified CUDA API calls.

facts.claim: The default compilation mode (`--default-stream legacy`, assumed if unspecified) treats the default stream as the NULL stream. This is a single, device-wide stream shared by all host threads that enforces implicit synchronization.
facts.note: Highlights the legacy behavior and its synchronization implications.

facts.claim: Using the `--default-stream per-thread` compilation flag (or the `CUDA_API_PER_THREAD_DEFAULT_STREAM` macro) changes the default stream into a regular stream, granting each host thread its own independent default stream.
facts.note: Contrasts with the legacy NULL stream behavior.

facts.claim: When compiling with `nvcc`, simply using `#define CUDA_API_PER_THREAD_DEFAULT_STREAM 1` is insufficient because `nvcc` implicitly includes `cuda_runtime.h` at the top of the translation unit. The macro must be passed via the `-DCUDA_API_PER_THREAD_DEFAULT_STREAM=1` compiler flag instead.
facts.note: Provides a critical implementation caveat for the per-thread mode.

caveats: The distinction between the legacy NULL stream and the per-thread default stream is strictly tied to the compilation flag used. Writers should emphasize that `nvcc` requires the `-D` flag variant for the macro to take effect.

concept/device-enumeration.title: Device Enumeration
concept/device-enumeration.summary: Host systems can contain multiple devices. This section demonstrates how to enumerate CUDA-enabled devices, query their properties, and determine the total device count using cudaGetDeviceCount() and cudaGetDeviceProperties().

research_reports.context_summary: Falls under the 'Multi-Device System' section, serving as the initial step before device selection and stream/event management.

facts.claim: Host systems can contain multiple CUDA-enabled devices, requiring enumeration to identify and query them.
facts.note: Foundational premise for multi-device programming.

facts.claim: cudaGetDeviceCount(&deviceCount) is used to determine the total number of available devices.
facts.note: Entry point for enumeration.

facts.claim: cudaGetDeviceProperties(&deviceProp, device) queries specific attributes for a given device index.
facts.note: Retrieves per-device metadata.

facts.claim: The cudaDeviceProp structure stores device attributes, including compute capability (major and minor fields).
facts.note: Standard structure for device introspection.

facts.claim: Enumeration typically follows a sequential loop from index 0 to deviceCount - 1.
facts.note: Standard iteration pattern shown in the code sample.

caveats: The provided code sample uses a pseudo-code/text block format rather than strict C syntax.
This enumeration step is strictly preparatory; actual device targeting requires a subsequent cudaSetDevice() call.

concept/device-graph-launch.title: Device Graph Launch Overview
concept/device-graph-launch.summary: Covers the concept of device graphs, which allow dynamic control flow and decision-making directly on the GPU without host round-trips. Requires unified addressing. Distinguishes between device graphs (launchable from host/device) and host graphs (host-only). Notes concurrency restrictions: device graphs cannot be launched twice from the device simultaneously, and simultaneous host/device launches cause undefined behavior.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence is the introductory section (6.2.8.7.7) of the CUDA C Programming Guide. It establishes the high-level rationale, terminology, and concurrency constraints for device graph launch, which are subsequently detailed in the surrounding context through subsections on creation, structural requirements, upload mechanisms, update procedures, and specific launch modes (e.g., fire-and-forget, tail launch).

facts.claim: Device graph launch enables dynamic, data-dependent control flow directly on the GPU, eliminating the need for host round-trips for runtime decisions.
facts.note: Covers the primary use case and performance motivation.

facts.claim: CUDA distinguishes between 'device graphs' (capable of device-side launch) and 'host graphs' (host-only).
facts.note: Establishes core terminology used throughout the guide.

facts.claim: Device graph launch functionality is restricted to systems that support unified addressing.
facts.note: Hardware/architecture prerequisite.

facts.claim: Device graphs may be launched from either the host or the device, whereas host graphs are host-only.
facts.note: Defines cross-platform launch flexibility.

facts.claim: Concurrent execution from the device is prohibited; attempting to launch a device graph from the device while a previous instance is running returns cudaErrorInvalidValue.
facts.note: Critical concurrency constraint for device-side launchers.

facts.claim: Simultaneous launching of a device graph from both the host and device results in undefined behavior.
facts.note: Defines host-device launch boundary conditions.

caveats: No contradictions or scope clashes detected. The assigned evidence strictly defines the conceptual overview and launch constraints, while the surrounding context windows (L2864-L2983) provide the procedural mechanics (creation, upload, update, and specific launch modes) that implement this overview.

concept/device-graph-requirements.title: Device Graph Requirements
concept/device-graph-requirements.summary: Covers constraints for device graphs: single-device nodes, allowed node types (kernel, memcpy, memset, child graph), restrictions on CUDA Dynamic Parallelism, MPS cooperative launch limits, memory access rules for memcpy/memset (device/pinned host memory only, no CUDA arrays), and operand accessibility at instantiation.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence (L2868-L2887) is a specific subsection detailing the constraints a CUDA graph must satisfy to be instantiated with the `cudaGraphInstantiateFlagDeviceLaunch` flag. It is situated between the introduction of device graph creation (L2864-L2866) and the procedures for uploading device graphs (L2889-L2921). The context establishes that these requirements are mandatory prerequisites for enabling a graph to be launched directly from the GPU.

facts.claim: Device Graphs must reside on a single device, and are restricted to specific node types.
facts.note: All nodes must reside on a single device. The graph can only contain kernel, memcpy, memset, and child graph nodes.

facts.claim: Kernel nodes in a device graph cannot use CUDA Dynamic Parallelism.
facts.note: Use of CUDA Dynamic Parallelism by kernels in the graph is not permitted.

facts.claim: Cooperative launches are permitted in device graphs only if Multi-Process Service (MPS) is not in use.
facts.note: Cooperative launches are permitted so long as MPS is not in use.

facts.claim: Memcpy nodes in a device graph are restricted to specific memory types and accessibility rules.
facts.note: Only copies involving device memory and/or pinned device-mapped host memory are permitted. Copies involving CUDA arrays are not permitted. Both operands must be accessible from the current device at the time of instantiation. The copy operation will be performed from the device on which the graph resides, even if it is targeting memory on another device.

recommended_structure: Overview of Device Graph instantiation requirements
Memcpy node restrictions (memory types, accessibility, execution location)

concept/device-launch-modes.title: Device Launch Modes
concept/device-launch-modes.summary: Covers the three distinct named streams for device graph launch modes: Fire and Forget, Tail Launch, and Fire and Forget As Sibling. Device graphs cannot be launched into regular CUDA streams.

research_reports.context_summary: The provided context details device-side graph launch mechanics, specifically focusing on the three named stream modes (Fire and Forget, Tail, Sibling), their execution environments, and operational limits.

facts.claim: Device graphs cannot be launched into standard CUDA streams; they require distinct named streams to define their launch behavior.
facts.note: This is a strict prerequisite for device graph execution.

facts.claim: Fire and Forget Launch (`cudaStreamGraphFireAndForget`) submits the graph immediately to run independently of the parent graph, establishing a parent-child relationship.
facts.note: Encapsulated within the parent's execution environment.

facts.claim: A parent graph can spawn up to 120 fire-and-forget graphs during a single execution cycle; this counter resets between parent launches.
facts.note: Hard limit per launch instance.

facts.claim: Tail Launch (`cudaStreamGraphTailLaunch`) enables GPU-side serial dependencies and synchronization, as traditional host sync APIs (e.g., `cudaDeviceSynchronize()`) are unavailable for device graphs.
facts.note: Replaces host-side synchronization for device-side control flow.

facts.claim: A tail-launched graph executes only after the launching graph's execution environment is fully complete (i.e., the graph and all its fire-and-forget children have finished).
facts.note: Ensures strict sequential ordering on the device.

facts.claim: Tail launches execute sequentially in FIFO order. If a tail graph enqueues its own tail launches, those execute before tail launches from earlier graphs in the chain.
facts.note: Defines the queueing and execution priority model.

facts.claim: A graph can maintain up to 255 pending tail launches.
facts.note: Hard limit for the tail launch queue.

facts.claim: Sibling Launch (`cudaStreamGraphFireAndForgetAsSibling`) is listed as a valid mode but lacks detailed behavioral documentation in the provided source context.
facts.note: Requires external verification or future documentation updates.

facts.claim: All device graph launches operate within hierarchical execution environments that encapsulate the graph's work and any generated fire-and-forget work, completing only when all nested child work finishes.
facts.note: Fundamental to understanding synchronization and completion states.

caveats: Sibling launch mode is mentioned in the API table but has no accompanying explanatory text or code examples in the provided context.
Device graphs require explicit instantiation with `cudaGraphInstantiateFlagDeviceLaunch` and must be uploaded to the device before device-side execution.
Device graph launches are per-thread; users must coordinate single-threaded launch selection to avoid undefined behavior or errors.
recommended_structure: Overview & Prerequisites (Named streams vs regular streams)
Fire and Forget Launch (Behavior, Parent-Child model, 120 limit)
Tail Launch (Sequential execution, Environment completion, 255 limit, FIFO ordering)
Sibling Launch (Mentioned, note lack of detail)
Execution Environment Model (Hierarchical encapsulation, completion criteria)

concept/device-memory-accesses.title: Device Memory Accesses
concept/device-memory-accesses.summary: Explains how memory accesses (global, local, shared, constant, texture/surface) are handled, coalesced, and cached. Covers alignment requirements, bank conflicts, and optimal access patterns for different compute capabilities.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence corresponds to Section 8.3.2 'Device Memory Accesses' within the CUDA C Programming Guide's performance guidelines. It details how warp-level memory access distributions impact instruction throughput across different addressable memory spaces, serving as a foundational reference for optimizing memory bandwidth and latency by explaining coalescing, alignment, bank conflicts, and caching behaviors.

facts.claim: Memory access instructions (global, local, shared, constant, texture) may be re-issued multiple times depending on how memory addresses are distributed across threads in a warp. Scattered addresses generally reduce throughput, particularly for global memory.
facts.note: General Principle

facts.claim: Global memory is accessed via 32-, 64-, or 128-byte naturally aligned transactions. Warps coalesce thread accesses into these transactions; more transactions mean more unused words transferred, reducing throughput.
facts.note: Global Memory Transactions

facts.claim: Global memory supports 1, 2, 4, 8, or 16-byte words. Accesses must be naturally aligned (address is a multiple of size) to compile into a single instruction. Built-in vector types auto-fulfill alignment; structures require __align__(8) or __align__(16). Misaligned 8/16-byte reads yield incorrect results.
facts.note: Global Memory Alignment

facts.claim: For 2D array access pattern BaseAddress + width * ty + tx, full coalescing requires both block width and array width to be multiples of the warp size. cudaMallocPitch()/cuMemAllocPitch() are recommended for padding.
facts.note: Global Memory 2D Arrays

facts.claim: Local memory is used for automatic variables (non-constant indexed arrays, large structures, or register spilling). It resides in device memory with high latency/low bandwidth, subject to coalescing rules. Consecutive 32-bit words map to consecutive thread IDs; fully coalesced if threads access the same relative address. Cached in L2 on compute capability 5.x+.
facts.note: Local Memory

facts.claim: Shared memory is on-chip with high bandwidth and low latency. It is divided into equally-sized banks. Simultaneous access to distinct banks yields multiplied bandwidth. Bank conflicts occur when multiple threads access the same bank, serializing access and reducing throughput by the conflict factor (n-way conflict).
facts.note: Shared Memory

facts.claim: Constant memory resides in device memory and is cached in the constant cache. Requests are split into separate requests for each unique address, reducing throughput by the number of unique addresses. Serviced at constant cache throughput on hits, or device memory throughput otherwise.
facts.note: Constant Memory

facts.claim: Texture and surface memory reside in device memory and are cached in the texture cache. Optimized for 2D spatial locality; best performance when warp threads read nearby 2D addresses. Designed for streaming fetches with constant latency; cache hits reduce DRAM bandwidth but not fetch latency. Benefits include off-kernel addressing, packed data broadcasting, and optional 8/16-bit integer to 32-bit float conversion.
facts.note: Texture and Surface Memory

caveats: The source document is marked as legacy (replaced by the new CUDA Programming Guide as of CUDA 13.0), but the memory access principles remain technically accurate for the target concept.
Specific compute capability details (5.x through 12.0) are referenced in the source but deferred to other sections; this summary focuses on the universal access mechanics described here.
recommended_structure: Texture & Surface Memory (Spatial Locality, Latency, Benefits)

concept/device-memory-space-specifiers.title: Device Memory Space Specifiers
concept/device-memory-space-specifiers.summary: Restrictions and rules for device memory space specifiers, including constructor/destructor constraints and compilation mode differences.

research_reports.context_summary: Falls under section 18.5.3 (Qualifiers) and 18.5 (Restrictions) of the CUDA C++ Programming Guide. Details compiler-enforced limitations on GPU memory space declarations (`__device__`, `__shared__`, `__managed__`, `__constant__`), including placement rules, class type constraints, and linkage behavior across compilation modes.

facts.claim: The specifiers `__device__`, `__shared__`, `__managed__`, and `__constant__` are prohibited on class, struct, and union data members, formal parameters, and non-`extern` variable declarations within host-executing functions.
facts.note: Directly maps to the first restriction block in the assigned evidence.

facts.claim: `__device__`, `__constant__`, and `__managed__` are additionally forbidden on variable declarations that are neither `extern` nor `static` inside device-executing functions.
facts.note: Second restriction block in the assigned evidence.

facts.claim: Variable definitions using these specifiers cannot use class types with non-empty constructors or destructors. An 'empty' constructor/destructor is defined as trivial, or strictly meeting conditions: defined, no parameters/empty body, no virtuals/virtual bases, and all base/member constructors/destructors also considered empty.
facts.note: Covers the class type constraint and the detailed logical conditions for 'empty' special member functions.

facts.claim: In whole program compilation mode, these variables cannot be defined as `extern` (except for dynamically allocated `__shared__` variables).
facts.note: First compilation mode restriction.

facts.claim: In separate compilation mode, `extern` definitions are allowed, but `nvlink` will generate an error if a definition is missing (except for dynamically allocated `__shared__` variables).
facts.note: Second compilation mode restriction.

caveats: The assigned evidence strictly covers placement, class-type, and linkage restrictions. It does not cover `__managed__`-specific runtime behaviors (e.g., static initialization limits, `const`/reference type bans, or multi-GPU allocation), which are detailed in the adjacent section 18.5.3.2 (L16682+).
recommended_structure: Prohibited locations (host/device functions, class members, parameters)
Linkage & compilation mode behavior (whole program vs. separate compilation)

concept/device-selection.title: Device Selection
concept/device-selection.summary: Host threads can set the active device using cudaSetDevice(). All memory allocations and kernel launches occur on the currently set device. If unset, device 0 is used by default.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence falls under section 6.2.9 (Multi-Device System) of the CUDA C Programming Guide, specifically subsection 6.2.9.2. It follows device enumeration and precedes stream/event behavior and peer-to-peer access discussions.

facts.claim: Device selection is performed via the `cudaSetDevice()` runtime API call.
facts.note: A host thread can invoke `cudaSetDevice()` at any time to change the active device for subsequent operations.

facts.claim: The default active device is device 0 if `cudaSetDevice()` is never called.
facts.note: No explicit initialization call is required to use the first GPU.

facts.claim: All device memory allocations, kernel launches, stream creations, and event creations are bound to the currently selected device.
facts.note: Switching the active device changes the target context for all subsequent CUDA runtime calls until changed again.

caveats: Device selection is a per-thread concept; different host threads can maintain different active devices independently.
The provided evidence focuses on the basic selection mechanism and does not cover advanced multi-GPU scheduling or explicit device affinity constraints.
recommended_structure: Impact on allocations and kernel launches

concept/device-tensor-map-encoding.title: Device-Side Tensor Map Encoding and Modification
concept/device-tensor-map-encoding.summary: Details the process of creating, copying, and modifying tiled-type tensor maps on the device using `cuda::ptx::tensormap_replace` functions. Covers the release-acquire synchronization pattern for using modified tensor maps across thread blocks.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence covers Section 10.30 (Encoding a Tensor Map on Device) and its subsections. It details the workflow, API functions, synchronization requirements, and architectural constraints for dynamically creating and modifying tiled-type tensor maps on the GPU at runtime.

facts.claim: Purpose & Use Case
facts.note: Enables encoding a tiled-type tensor map directly on the device, bypassing the typical `const __grid_constant__` kernel parameter transfer. This is particularly useful for dynamic scenarios like processing a batch of tensors with varying sizes in a single kernel launch.

facts.claim: Recommended Workflow
facts.note: 1. Host: Create a static tensor map template using the CUDA Driver API. 2. Device Kernel: Copy the template to shared memory, modify the desired fields, write the result to global memory, and apply a fence. 3. Consumer: Use the modified tensor map from global memory with appropriate synchronization.

facts.claim: Device-Side Modification API
facts.note: Modifications are performed in shared memory using `cuda::ptx::tensormap_replace` functions, which wrap the `tensormap.replace` PTX instruction. Supported field replacements include: `global_address`, `rank`, `box_dim`, `global_dim`, `global_stride`, `element_stride`, `elemtype`, `interleave_layout`, `swizzle_mode`, and `fill_mode`. After modification, the updated tensor map is copied to global memory and released using `cuda::ptx::tensormap_cp_fenceproxy`.

facts.claim: Synchronization & Usage Constraints
facts.note: Using a tensor map stored in global memory requires explicit proxy fencing. The modifier calls the release fence (`tensormap_cp_fenceproxy`), and the consumer calls the acquire fence (`cuda::ptx::fence_proxy_tensormap_generic`). Use `.gpu` scope for threads on the same device, and `.sys` scope for cross-device communication. The thread modifying the tensor map and the thread acquiring/using it must reside in the same thread block. Standard cross-block synchronization APIs do not establish the necessary ordering for tensor map updates; threads in other blocks must still acquire the proxy. If no intermediate modifications occur, the release fence does not need to be repeated before every `cp.async.bulk.tensor` instruction.

facts.claim: Implementation Notes & Limitations
facts.note: The `tensormap_replace` functions and corresponding PTX instructions are specific to `sm_90a` and require `-arch sm_90a` compilation. On-device modification is strictly limited to tiled-type tensor maps. On `sm_90a`, a zero-initialized shared memory buffer can serve as the initial tensor map value, enabling purely device-side encoding without a host-side template. A minimal tiled-type template can be created on the host using `cuTensorMapEncodeTiled` with dummy dimensions/strides, which is then passed to the device for runtime modification.

caveats: The source code snippets contain minor OCR/formatting artifacts (e.g., `ptr::` instead of `ptx::`, `fence_proxy_tensorsgammaGeneric` instead of `fence_proxy_tensormap_generic`), but the surrounding prose accurately describes the intended API and behavior.
The zero-initialization trick for purely device-side encoding is explicitly noted as an `sm_90a` feature.

Cross-block synchronization via cooperative groups or stream ordering is explicitly stated as insufficient for tensor map proxy ordering; explicit `.acquire` fencing is mandatory for any consumer outside the modifying block.

concept/diagnostic-pragmas.title: 10.42. Diagnostic Pragmas
concept/diagnostic-pragmas.summary: Covers pragmas for controlling compiler diagnostic severity, state management (push/pop), and deprecation notices for non-nv_ prefixed pragmas in CUDA 12.0+.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence covers section 10.42 of the CUDA C++ Programming Guide, which details compiler diagnostic pragmas. It is embedded within a chapter on CUDA C++ programming interfaces, flanked by SIMD video instruction assembly directives (10.41) and custom ABI pragmas for separate compilation (10.43). The section is part of a broader set of compiler control mechanisms aimed at low-level performance tuning and code quality management.

facts.claim: Section 10.42 provides a set of pragmas to control the severity of compiler diagnostic messages.
facts.note: The pragmas are `#pragma nv_diag_suppress`, `#pragma nv_diag_warning`, `#pragma nv_diag_error`, `#pragma nv_diag_default`, and `#pragma nv_diag_once`.

facts.claim: Syntax follows `#pragma nv_diag_xxx error_number, error_number ...`, targeting diagnostics by the numeric code shown in warning messages.
facts.note: Any diagnostic can be promoted to an error. Only warnings can be suppressed or restored to a warning after promotion.

facts.claim: `#pragma nv_diag_default` reverts a diagnostic's severity to its original state, respecting command-line options.
facts.note: Example provided for suppressing warning 177 ("declared but never referenced").

facts.claim: State management is supported via `#pragma nv_diagnostic push` and `#pragma nv_diagnostic pop`.
facts.note: Allows saving and restoring the current diagnostic pragma configuration across code blocks.

facts.claim: Pragmas exclusively affect the `nvcc` CUDA frontend compiler and do not impact the host compiler.
facts.note: None.

facts.claim: Support for diagnostic pragmas without the `nv_` prefix was removed in CUDA 12.0.
facts.note: Device code using non-prefixed pragmas triggers an "unrecognized #pragma" warning. Otherwise, they are passed to the host compiler. Users must use `nv_`-prefixed variants for CUDA code.

caveats: The source text contains minor typographical errors (e.g., "afected", "efect", "diference") which should be corrected in the final wiki page.
The `#pragma nv_diag_once` pragma is listed in the syntax block but lacks explicit behavioral description in the provided excerpt.
contradictions: No contradictions detected. The section is self-contained and aligns with standard compiler pragma documentation practices.

concept/direct-unified-memory-access-hos.title: Direct Unified Memory Access from Host
concept/direct-unified-memory-access-hos.summary: Describes cudaDevAttrDirectManagedMemAccessFromHost, which enables hardware-coherent direct reads/stores/atomics from host to GPU-resident memory without page faults. Notes the requirement for cudaMemAdviseSetAccessedBy with cudaMemLocationTypeHost.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence falls under section 24.2.2.2 'Direct Unified Memory Access from host'. It defines the hardware capability for coherent host-to-GPU memory access, the required device attribute, and the software hint needed to enable it. The surrounding context (L21681-L21749) provides code examples (System Allocator vs. Managed Memory) and detailed behavioral comparisons between enabled and disabled states.

facts.claim: Hardware-accelerated coherent reads, stores, and atomic accesses from the host to GPU-resident unified memory are supported on specific devices.
facts.note: Device attribute `cudaDevAttrDirectManagedMemAccessFromHost` must be set to 1. NVLink-connected devices on hardware-coherent systems typically have this enabled.

facts.claim: Software configuration is required to enable this behavior for CUDA Managed Memory.
facts.note: The `cudaMemAdviseSetAccessedBy` hint with `cudaMemLocationTypeHost` must be set to allow direct host access without page faults.

facts.claim: Performance and migration behavior differ based on the attribute state.
facts.note: When enabled (`=1`), host CPU accesses GPU-resident memory directly without page faults or data migration. When disabled (`=0`), host accesses trigger page faults and initiate device-to-host migrations, with subsequent GPU accesses potentially triggering host-to-device migrations.

caveats: The source text contains a line-break typo in the attribute name (`cudaDevAttrDirectManaged-MemAccessFromHost`); the canonical CUDA API name is `cudaDevAttrDirectManagedMemAccessFromHost`.
This feature is specific to hardware-coherent systems and NVLink-connected devices; it does not apply to software-coherent systems with separate CPU/GPU page tables.

concept/direct3d-10-interoperability.title: Direct3D 10 Interoperability
concept/direct3d-10-interoperability.summary: Demonstrates how to create a Direct3D 10 device, register a vertex buffer with CUDA, map it for GPU writing, launch a kernel to generate vertex data, and unmap it for rendering.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence details the complete workflow for interoperating CUDA with Direct3D 10, covering adapter selection, resource registration, mapping for compute, kernel execution, unmapping, and cleanup. It provides a full code example demonstrating how a D3D10 vertex buffer can be written to by a CUDA kernel and then read by the D3D10 device.

facts.claim: Adapter and device initialization requires enumerating DXGI adapters to find a CUDA-compatible one using cudaD3D10GetDevice, followed by creating the D3D10 device and selecting the matching CUDA device.
facts.note: Establishes the prerequisite pairing between the D3D10 adapter and the CUDA device before any graphics interop operations.

facts.claim: D3D10 buffers are registered with CUDA via cudaGraphicsD3D10RegisterResource, and can be configured with map flags like cudaGraphicsMapFlagsWriteDiscard using cudaGraphicsResourceSetMapFlags.
facts.note: Highlights the specific registration API for D3D10 and the option to optimize mapping behavior.

facts.claim: CUDA kernels access D3D10 resources by first mapping them with cudaGraphicsMapResources, retrieving the device pointer via cudaGraphicsResourceGetMappedPointer, and then launching the kernel.
facts.note: Demonstrates the standard interop synchronization pattern: map -> get pointer -> compute.

facts.claim: After compute, the resource must be unmapped with cudaGraphicsUnmapResources to make it available to D3D10, and eventually unregistered with cudaGraphicsUnregisterResource before releasing the D3D10 object.
facts.note: Covers the teardown and synchronization steps required to prevent race conditions and resource leaks.

caveats: The example uses `cudaGraphicsMapFlagsWriteDiscard`, which implies the buffer is overwritten entirely each frame; different access patterns may require different flags.
The code assumes a single-device setup; multi-GPU or multi-adapter scenarios would require explicit device management and potentially different adapter enumeration logic.
D3D10 is a legacy API; modern implementations typically use D3D11 or D3D12, though the interop workflow remains structurally similar.

concept/direct3d-11-buffer-mapping.title: Direct3D 11 Buffer Mapping
concept/direct3d-11-buffer-mapping.summary: Maps device pointers onto imported Direct3D 11 memory objects using cudaExternalMemoryGetMappedBuffer.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence covers the specific CUDA API calls and constraints required to map Direct3D 11 resources (buffers and mipmapped arrays) onto imported external memory objects. The surrounding context (L5334-L5620) establishes that these mapping operations are part of the broader Direct3D 11 Interoperability workflow, which first requires matching device LUIDs and importing shareable D3D11 resources (textures/buffers) into CUDA using `cudaImportExternalMemory`.

facts.claim: To map a device pointer onto an imported memory object, use the `cudaExternalMemoryGetMappedBuffer` function.
facts.note: Requires an `cudaExternalMemory_t` object, an offset, and a size.

facts.claim: To map a CUDA mipmapped array onto an imported memory object, use the `cudaExternalMemoryGetMappedMipmappedArray` function.
facts.note: Requires an `cudaExternalMemory_t` object, an offset, a format description, an extent (dimensions), flags, and the number of mip levels.

facts.claim: The offset, size, dimensions, format, and number of mip levels provided to the CUDA mapping functions must exactly match the parameters specified when creating the mapping in the corresponding Direct3D 11 API.
facts.note: Applies to both buffer and mipmapped array mapping.

facts.claim: If the mipmapped array can be bound as a render target in Direct3D 12, the `cudaArrayColorAttachment` flag must be set during the mapping process.
facts.note: Specific to mipmapped array mapping.

facts.claim: Mapped device pointers must be freed using `cudaFree()`, and mapped mipmapped arrays must be freed using `cudaFreeMipmappedArray()`.
facts.note: Applies to both buffer and mipmapped array mapping.

caveats: The source text contains typos such as 'Mapping Bufers' and 'ofset' which should be corrected to 'Buffers' and 'offset' in the final page.
Mapping operations are only possible after the D3D11 resource has been successfully imported into CUDA as an external memory object (as detailed in the surrounding context L5381-L5453).
recommended_structure: Overview of mapping imported D3D11 memory to CUDA

concept/direct3d-11-device-luid-matching.title: Direct3D 11 Device LUID Matching
concept/direct3d-11-device-luid-matching.summary: Matches CUDA devices to Direct3D 11 devices by comparing their Local Unique Identifiers (LUIDs).

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence covers section 6.2.16.4.1 'Matching Device LUIDs', which is the foundational step for Direct3D 11/CUDA interoperability. It precedes the actual import and mapping procedures for D3D11 memory objects, buffers, and mipmapped arrays (L5381-L5499). The surrounding context (L5217-L5336) details D3D12 synchronization object handling, highlighting that LUID matching is specific to D3D11 resource sharing.

facts.claim: Purpose & Requirement
facts.note: Imported D3D11 memory and synchronization objects must be imported and mapped on the exact same CUDA device where they were originally created. LUID comparison is the mechanism to identify this matching device.

facts.claim: D3D11 LUID Extraction
facts.note: The D3D11 device's LUID is obtained by querying the `ID3D11Device` for an `IDXGIDevice`, retrieving the `IDXGIAdapter`, and calling `GetDesc` to access the `DXGI_ADAPTER_DESC.AdapterLuid`.

facts.claim: CUDA Device Enumeration
facts.note: The code iterates through all available CUDA devices using `cudaGetDeviceCount` and `cudaGetDeviceProperties`, extracting the `luid` field from `cudaDeviceProp` for each device.

facts.claim: LUID Comparison Logic
facts.note: Matching is performed by comparing the `LowPart` and `HighPart` of the D3D11 LUID against the CUDA device's LUID structure using `memcmp`.

facts.claim: Return Values
facts.note: The function returns the index of the matching CUDA device upon success, or `cudaInvalidDeviceId` if no corresponding device is found.

caveats: This procedure is strictly scoped to Direct3D 11 interoperability. D3D12 interoperability (covered in adjacent sections) uses different handle types and synchronization primitives, though the underlying principle of device affinity remains relevant.
The provided code sample assumes standard Windows/DXGI headers are included and handles the full enumeration loop required before proceeding to resource import.
recommended_structure: Overview of D3D11/CUDA device affinity requirement
CUDA device enumeration and property retrieval

concept/direct3d-11-interoperability.title: Direct3D 11 Interoperability
concept/direct3d-11-interoperability.summary: Shows the equivalent workflow for Direct3D 11, including device creation, buffer registration via cudaGraphicsD3D11RegisterResource, mapping, kernel execution, and resource unregistration.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence corresponds to section 6.2.15.2.3, providing a complete C/C++ code example that demonstrates the standard workflow for Direct3D 11 and CUDA interoperability, following a similar D3D10 example in the preceding section.

facts.claim: Device Initialization: The example shows iterating through DXGI adapters to find a CUDA-compatible one using cudaD3D11GetDevice, then binding the CUDA runtime to that device via cudaSetDevice.
facts.note: The example shows iterating through DXGI adapters to find a CUDA-compatible one using cudaD3D11GetDevice, then binding the CUDA runtime to that device via cudaSetDevice.

facts.claim: Resource Registration: A Direct3D 11 vertex buffer (ID3D11Buffer) is created and registered with the CUDA graphics interop layer using cudaGraphicsD3D11RegisterResource. Optional map flags, such as cudaGraphicsMapFlagsWriteDiscard, can be set via cudaGraphicsResourceSetMapFlags.
facts.note: A Direct3D 11 vertex buffer (ID3D11Buffer) is created and registered with the CUDA graphics interop layer using cudaGraphicsD3D11RegisterResource. Optional map flags, such as cudaGraphicsMapFlagsWriteDiscard, can be set via cudaGraphicsResourceSetMapFlags.

facts.claim: Interoperability Workflow: Within a rendering loop, the resource is mapped for CUDA access using cudaGraphicsMapResources. The device pointer is retrieved via cudaGraphicsResourceGetMappedPointer, a CUDA kernel is launched to process the data, and the resource is subsequently unmapped using cudaGraphicsUnmapResources.
facts.note: Within a rendering loop, the resource is mapped for CUDA access using cudaGraphicsMapResources. The device pointer is retrieved via cudaGraphicsResourceGetMappedPointer, a CUDA kernel is launched to process the data, and the resource is subsequently unmapped using cudaGraphicsUnmapResources.

facts.claim: Cleanup: Proper teardown involves unregistering the resource with cudaGraphicsUnregisterResource and releasing the native D3D11 object.
facts.note: Proper teardown involves unregistering the resource with cudaGraphicsUnregisterResource and releasing the native D3D11 object.

facts.claim: Kernel Execution: The example includes a sample __global__ kernel that writes to the mapped buffer, demonstrating that CUDA can directly read/write to the interoperated D3D11 memory.
facts.note: The example includes a sample __global__ kernel that writes to the mapped buffer, demonstrating that CUDA can directly read/write to the interoperated D3D11 memory.

caveats: The evidence is purely a code example demonstrating the API workflow. It does not cover error handling, multi-GPU/SLI considerations, or advanced synchronization mechanisms, which are discussed in surrounding sections (e.g., SLI Interoperability at L4508-L4518).
The example uses `cudaGraphicsMapFlagsWriteDiscard`, implying a pattern where the buffer is overwritten each frame rather than incrementally updated.
contradictions: None detected. The evidence aligns with standard CUDA-D3D11 interop documentation.

concept/direct3d-11-memory-import.title: Direct3D 11 Memory Object Import
concept/direct3d-11-memory-import.summary: Imports shareable Direct3D 11 textures and buffers into CUDA using NT handles, named handles, or KMT handles.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence (Section 6.2.16.4.2) sits between device LUID matching (lines 5337-5379) and buffer/mipmapped array mapping (lines 5455-5573). It exclusively covers the `cudaImportExternalMemory` phase for Direct3D 11 resources.
