facts.claim: D3D11 textures (ID3D11Texture1D/2D/3D) and buffers (ID3D11Buffer) must be created with specific misc flags: D3D11_RESOURCE_MISC_SHARED, D3D11_RESOURCE_MISC_SHARED_KEYEDMUTEX (Windows 7), or D3D11_RESOURCE_MISC_SHARED_NTHANDLE (Windows 10) during ID3D11Device::CreateTexture or CreateBuffer calls.
facts.note: Handle Types & Import APIs: Three distinct handle types are supported via cudaImportExternalMemory: 1. cudaExternalMemoryHandleTypeD3D11Resource (NT Handle): Requires manual CloseHandle() post-import, as the handle retains a reference to the resource. 2. cudaExternalMemoryHandleTypeD3D11Resource (Named NT Handle): Accepts a LPCWSTR name string. 3. cudaExternalMemoryHandleTypeD3D11ResourceKmt (D3DKMT Handle): Used for SHARED/SHARED_KEYEDMUTEX resources. Does not hold a reference and is automatically destroyed when external references drop.

facts.claim: Mandatory Configuration: The cudaExternalMemoryDedicated flag must be explicitly set in the cudaExternalMemoryHandleDesc.flags field during the import call.
facts.note: Scope Alignment: The evidence aligns perfectly with the target page slug and type. No contradictions or scope clashes were detected. All API signatures, flag names, and lifecycle rules are explicitly documented.

caveats: NT handles require explicit application-level CloseHandle() calls, whereas D3DKMT handles are automatically destroyed.
The cudaExternalMemoryDedicated flag is strictly required for D3D11 imports.
recommended_structure: Import Methods (NT Handle, Named NT Handle, D3DKMT Handle)

concept/direct3d-11-mipmapped-array-mapp.title: Direct3D 11 Mipmapped Array Mapping
concept/direct3d-11-mipmapped-array-mapp.summary: Maps CUDA mipmapped arrays onto imported memory objects, including DXGI format conversion and extent/mip-level flag handling.

research_reports.context_summary: Section 6.2.16.4.4 details the process of mapping a CUDA mipmapped array onto an imported Direct3D 11 memory object. It provides the core mapping API call, cleanup requirements, and a suite of helper functions to translate D3D11/DXGI parameters into their CUDA equivalents.

facts.claim: A CUDA mipmapped array is mapped to an imported D3D11 resource using `cudaExternalMemoryGetMappedMipmappedArray`. The offset, dimensions, format, and number of mip levels must exactly match the D3D11 API creation parameters. If the resource can be bound as a render target in D3D12, the `cudaArrayColorAttachment` flag must be set. Mapped arrays are freed with `cudaFreeMipmappedArray()`.
facts.note: Helper functions for parameter conversion are provided to bridge D3D11/DXGI types to CUDA types.

facts.claim: `getCudaChannelFormatDescForDxgiFormat` maps DXGI formats (R8, R8G8, R8G8B8A8, R16, R16G16, R16G16B16A16, R32, R32G32, R32G32B32A32 variants of UINT, SINT, and FLOAT) to `cudaChannelFormatDesc`, setting the correct bit depths (x, y, z, w) and `cudaChannelFormatKind`.
facts.note: Covers all standard 8-bit, 16-bit, and 32-bit channel configurations.

facts.claim: `getCudaExtentForD3D11Extent` translates `D3D11_SRV_DIMENSION` enumeration values (TEXTURE1D, TEXTURE2D, TEXTURE3D, CUBE, TEXTURE1DARRAY, TEXTURE2DARRAY, TEXTURECUBEARRAY) into `cudaExtent` structures, correctly assigning width, height, and depth/arraySize fields.
facts.note: Handles both standard textures and array/cube variants.

facts.claim: `getCudaMipmappedArrayFlagsForD3D12Resource` converts D3D11 service view dimensions and bind flags into CUDA array flags. `D3D11_SRV_DIMENSION_TEXTURECUBE` maps to `cudaArrayCubemap`. Array types (1D, 2D, CubeArray) map to `cudaArrayLayered`. `D3D11_BIND_RENDER_TARGET` sets `cudaArrayColorAttachment`. A boolean `allowSurfaceLoadStore` parameter enables `cudaArraySurfaceLoadStore`.
facts.note: Ensures CUDA array properties align with D3D11 resource capabilities.

caveats: The helper functions assume standard DXGI/D3D11 enumerations and may require updates for non-standard or vendor-specific formats.
Memory management is explicit: the application must close NT handles after import and free mapped arrays/buffers using `cudaFreeMipmappedArray()` and `cudaFree()` respectively.
recommended_structure: Overview of D3D11 to CUDA Mipmapped Array Mapping

concept/direct3d-11-synchronization-impo.title: Direct3D 11 Synchronization Object Import
concept/direct3d-11-synchronization-impo.summary: Imports shareable Direct3D 11 fence and keyed mutex synchronization objects into CUDA using NT or KMT handles.

research_reports.context_summary: Section 6.2.16.4.5 details the import of shareable Direct3D 11 synchronization objects (fences and keyed mutexes) into CUDA using various Windows handle types.

facts.claim: CUDA supports importing shareable Direct3D 11 fence objects and keyed mutex objects for cross-API synchronization.
facts.note: Fences require the D3D11_FENCE_FLAG_SHARED flag during creation via ID3D11Device5::CreateFence. Keyed mutexes (IDXGIKeyedMutex) require the D3D11_RESOURCE_MISC_SHARED_KEYEDMUTEX flag during resource creation.

facts.claim: Import is performed using cudaImportExternalSemaphore with a cudaExternalSemaphoreHandleDesc structure.
facts.note: The handle type field in the descriptor determines the import method and supported handle types.

facts.claim: Supported handle types include standard NT handles, named NT handles, and globally shared D3DKMT handles.
facts.note: Handle type enumerations include cudaExternalSemaphoreHandleTypeD3D11Fence, cudaExternalSemaphoreHandleTypeKeyedMutex, and cudaExternalSemaphoreHandleTypeKeyedMutexKmt.

facts.claim: NT handles hold a reference to the underlying resource, requiring explicit cleanup.
facts.note: Applications must call CloseHandle after import and explicitly free the handle before the underlying semaphore can be destroyed. D3DKMT handles do not hold a reference and are automatically destroyed when other resource references are released.

caveats: The source only covers the import phase (section 6.2.16.4.5). Signaling and waiting on imported objects are covered in the subsequent section (6.2.16.4.6) and are out of scope for this target page.

Handle management rules differ significantly between standard NT handles (reference-counted, require explicit CloseHandle) and D3DKMT handles (non-referencing, auto-cleanup). This distinction is critical for correct resource lifecycle management.
recommended_structure: Overview of Direct3D 11 synchronization import into CUDA
Supported Synchronization Objects & D3D11 Creation Flags
Import Mechanisms & Handle Types (NT, Named, D3DKMT)

concept/direct3d-11-synchronization-sign.title: Direct3D 11 Synchronization Signaling and Waiting
concept/direct3d-11-synchronization-sign.summary: Signals and waits on imported Direct3D 11 fence and keyed mutex objects using CUDA asynchronous semaphore APIs.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence corresponds to Section 6.2.16.4.6, which immediately follows the synchronization object import procedures (6.2.16.4.5) and precedes the NVSCI interoperability section (6.2.16.5). It provides the operational CUDA API calls for managing imported Direct3D 11 fences and keyed mutexes.

facts.claim: CUDA signals imported D3D11 fence objects using `cudaSignalExternalSemaphoresAsync` with a value parameter, which sets the fence to that exact value.
facts.note: The corresponding wait must be issued in Direct3D 11 after the CUDA signal.

facts.claim: CUDA waits on imported D3D11 fence objects using `cudaWaitExternalSemaphoresAsync`, blocking until the fence value becomes greater than or equal to the specified value.
facts.note: The corresponding signal must have been issued in Direct3D 11 before the CUDA wait.

facts.claim: Signaling an imported D3D11 keyed mutex releases it for a specified key value using `cudaSignalExternalSemaphoresAsync`.
facts.note: The corresponding Direct3D 11 wait must use the same key and be issued after the CUDA signal.

facts.claim: Waiting on an imported D3D11 keyed mutex uses `cudaWaitExternalSemaphoresAsync` with a key and a timeout in milliseconds, blocking until the key matches or the timeout elapses.
facts.note: The Windows `INFINITE` macro can be used for an infinite timeout. The corresponding Direct3D 11 signal must be issued before the CUDA wait.

facts.claim: Both signaling and waiting operations populate `cudaExternalSemaphoreSignalParams` or `cudaExternalSemaphoreWaitParams` structs (initialized with `memset`) before passing them to the async CUDA functions.
facts.note: Parameters are assigned to `.params.fence.value` or `.params.keyedmutex.key`/`.timeoutMs`.

caveats: Strict cross-API ordering is required: CUDA signals must precede Direct3D 11 waits, and Direct3D 11 signals must precede CUDA waits.
Keyed mutex waits require explicit timeout handling, whereas fence waits rely purely on value thresholds.
recommended_structure: Overview of async signaling/waiting for imported D3D11 primitives
Code examples for params struct population and async calls

concept/distributed-shared-memory.title: Distributed Shared Memory
concept/distributed-shared-memory.summary: Shared memory partitioned across thread blocks within a cluster (compute capability 9.0+), enabling cross-block read/write/atomic operations via cooperative_groups API.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence (Section 6.2.5) bridges standard per-block shared memory examples (like the preceding matrix multiplication kernel) and advanced host memory management sections. It introduces a cluster-level memory abstraction that extends traditional shared memory beyond block boundaries, fitting into the broader CUDA memory hierarchy discussion.

facts.claim: Distributed Shared Memory (DSM) is a partitioned shared memory address space introduced in compute capability 9.0 via thread block clusters, allowing threads within a cluster to read, write, or perform atomics on shared memory belonging to any participating block (local or remote).
facts.note: DSM size specifications (static/dynamic) remain per-thread-block, but total DSM capacity equals the number of blocks per cluster multiplied by the shared memory size per block.

facts.claim: Accessing DSM requires all participating thread blocks to exist concurrently. Developers must use `cluster.sync()` from the Cooperative Groups API to guarantee block startup and to ensure all DSM operations complete before any block exits, preventing race conditions when remote blocks read local block memory.
facts.note: DSM provides an intermediate optimization step between local shared memory and global memory atomics, particularly useful for workloads like histogram computation where the total bin size exceeds a single block's shared memory capacity but fits within the cluster's aggregate shared memory.

facts.claim: Access is facilitated via the Cooperative Groups API (`cooperative_groups.h`). Blocks use `cg::this_cluster()` to obtain a cluster group, `cluster.map_shared_rank()` to get pointers to remote blocks' shared memory, and standard atomic operations (e.g., `atomicAdd`) to update shared data across the cluster.
facts.note: DSM-enabled kernels are launched using the extensible launch API (`cudaLaunchKernelEx`). The cluster dimensions are set via `cudaLaunchAttributeClusterDimension`, and dynamic shared memory is configured per block, with the total distributed size implicitly scaling with the cluster size.

caveats: DSM operations must strictly adhere to lifecycle rules: `cluster.sync()` is mandatory before accessing remote shared memory and after completing writes to prevent premature block exit.
Shared memory allocation syntax remains per-block; the distributed aspect is purely a runtime/cluster-level addressing feature, not a change to the `__shared__` declaration syntax.
contradictions: None detected. The section cleanly extends the existing shared memory model without conflicting with prior matrix multiplication examples or subsequent host memory sections.
recommended_structure: Launch Configuration (Extensible launch, cluster dimensions, dynamic smem)
Use Cases & Performance (Histogram example, bridging local smem and global atomics)

concept/driver-entry-point-access.title: CUDA Driver Entry Point Access
concept/driver-entry-point-access.summary: Provides APIs (cuGetProcAddress, cudaGetDriverEntryPointByVersion) to retrieve driver function addresses dynamically. Covers versioned typedefs, ABI compatibility, per-thread default stream variants, and accessing newer driver features without toolkit updates.

research_reports.context_summary: Section 21.5 of the CUDA C Programming Guide details the Driver Entry Point Access APIs, introduced in CUDA 11.3. It covers the purpose, typedef headers, retrieval methods via Driver and Runtime APIs, stream semantics, forward compatibility, error handling, and usage guidelines.

facts.claim: The Driver Entry Point Access APIs provide a mechanism to retrieve the memory addresses of CUDA driver functions as function pointers, available starting in CUDA 11.3. They serve a similar purpose to POSIX `dlsym` or Windows `GetProcAddress`.
facts.note: Enables dynamic loading of driver APIs without requiring static linking to specific toolkit versions.

facts.claim: The CUDA Toolkit supplies header files (e.g., `cudaTypedefs.h` for `cuda.h`) containing function pointer typedefs for all driver APIs. Driver symbols use a version-based naming scheme with a `_v*` suffix (e.g., `PFN_cuMemAlloc_v3020` for CUDA 3.2) to manage ABI changes across toolkit versions.
facts.note: Headers do not define actual function pointers, only the typedefs required to declare them safely.

facts.claim: Function pointers can be retrieved using either the Driver API (`cuGetProcAddress`) or the Runtime API (`cudaGetDriverEntryPointByVersion`). Both require passing the exact CUDA version that matches the typedef's version suffix to ensure ABI compatibility.
facts.note: Specifying a higher CUDA version than the typedef's introduction version can cause the API to return a newer, incompatible symbol, leading to undefined behavior.

facts.claim: Per-thread default stream variants of driver APIs (suffixed with `_ptsz` or `_ptds`) can be requested by passing `CU_GET_PROC_ADDRESS_PER_THREAD_DEFAULT_STREAM` (or `cudaEnablePerThreadDefaultStream`) to the retrieval API, or by compiling with `--default-stream per-thread` or defining `CUDA_API_PER_THREAD_DEFAULT_STREAM`.
facts.note: This alters synchronization behavior compared to the default stream variant.

facts.claim: The APIs enable forward compatibility, allowing applications to call new driver features introduced in a newer driver version even when compiled against an older toolkit. This requires manually defining the function prototype if the older toolkit's headers lack it.
facts.note: Useful for deploying newer features without requiring a full toolkit upgrade on the target machine.

facts.claim: Lookup failures are categorized into API/usage errors (returned as `CUresult`) and symbol-not-found errors (returned via `CUdriverProcAddressQueryResult` in the status pointer). Key status codes include `CU_GET_PROC_ADDRESS_SUCCESS`, `CU_GET_PROC_ADDRESS_VERSION_NOT_SUFFICIENT` (symbol exists but was introduced after the requested version), and `CU_GET_PROC_ADDRESS_SYMBOL_NOT_FOUND` (symbol does not exist in the current driver).
facts.note: Distinguishing between these helps developers decide whether to update the driver, adjust the requested CUDA version, or fix typos.

facts.claim: Best practices dictate verifying the installed driver version (e.g., via `cuDriverGetVersion`) before calling the retrieval API. The CUDA version argument should be hardcoded to match the specific typedef version rather than using dynamic version macros like `CUDA_VERSION`.
facts.note: Prevents unexpected symbol resolution and ensures stable ABI matching across deployments.

caveats: The source explicitly warns that using a dynamic CUDA version (e.g., from `cuDriverGetVersion`) as the argument to `cuGetProcAddress` is discouraged, as it may resolve to a newer, ABI-incompatible symbol if the driver supports it.
Typedef headers are provided for compatibility but do not contain the actual function implementations; they only define the function pointer signatures.

concept/error-checking.title: Error Checking
concept/error-checking.summary: Runtime functions return error codes, but asynchronous functions report errors via subsequent calls. cudaDeviceSynchronize() is required to catch async errors. cudaPeekAtLastError() and cudaGetLastError() manage thread-local error variables. Kernel launches require explicit error checking immediately after launch, and cudaErrorNotReady is not considered an error.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence (Section 6.2.12) is the dedicated section for error checking within the CUDA C Programming Guide. The surrounding context covers multi-device streams, peer-to-peer memory access, and IPC, highlighting that error checking is critical when managing complex device interactions and memory sharing.

facts.claim: The CUDA runtime maintains a per-host-thread error variable initialized to cudaSuccess, which is overwritten by the error code whenever an error occurs.
facts.note: This applies to both parameter validation errors and asynchronous errors.

facts.claim: cudaPeekAtLastError() returns the current value of the runtime error variable.
facts.note: None

facts.claim: cudaGetLastError() returns the current value of the runtime error variable and resets it to cudaSuccess.
facts.note: None

facts.claim: Asynchronous runtime functions only report host-side errors (e.g., parameter validation) that occur before execution; asynchronous device errors are reported by a subsequent unrelated runtime function call.
facts.note: None

facts.claim: To check for asynchronous errors immediately after an asynchronous function call, the application must synchronize (e.g., using cudaDeviceSynchronize()) and check the error code returned by the synchronization call.
facts.note: None

facts.claim: Kernel launches do not return error codes; cudaPeekAtLastError() or cudaGetLastError() must be called immediately after the launch to retrieve pre-launch errors.
facts.note: None

facts.claim: To ensure error checks do not catch errors from prior calls, cudaGetLastError() should be called immediately before the kernel launch to reset the error variable.
facts.note: None

facts.claim: To check for asynchronous errors originating from a kernel launch, the application must synchronize between the kernel launch and the call to cudaPeekAtLastError() or cudaGetLastError().
facts.note: None

facts.claim: cudaErrorNotReady, returned by cudaStreamQuery() and cudaEventQuery(), is not considered an error and is not reported by cudaPeekAtLastError() or cudaGetLastError().
facts.note: None

caveats: cudaErrorNotReady is a special case that bypasses the standard error variable mechanism.
recommended_structure: Overview of the CUDA error variable

concept/exception-handling.title: Exception Handling
concept/exception-handling.summary: Exception handling supported only in host code; not supported for __global__ functions.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence is located in Section 18.5 (C++ Language Features), which catalogs C++ language constructs and explicitly delineates which features are restricted to host execution versus device execution.

facts.claim: Exception handling is exclusively supported in host code and is unavailable in device code.
facts.note: Directly restricts the scope of exception handling to the host side of CUDA C++ applications.

facts.claim: Exception specifications are not supported for __global__ functions.
facts.note: Explicitly prohibits the use of exception specifications on device entry points.

caveats: The guide does not mention alternative error propagation mechanisms (e.g., error codes, assertions, or `cudaGetLastError()`) in this specific section, so the page should note that device-side errors must be handled via other CUDA-specific patterns.

concept/execution-configuration.title: 10.37. Execution Configuration
concept/execution-configuration.summary: Covers the execution configuration syntax for __global__ functions, including grid/block dimensions, shared memory allocation, stream association, and cluster dimensions (CC 9.0+).

research_reports.context_summary: Section 10.37 details the mandatory execution configuration syntax for `__global__` kernel launches, defining grid/block dimensions, dynamic shared memory, and stream association. It also covers validation rules and Compute Capability 9.0+ cluster hierarchy support.

facts.claim: Execution configuration is mandatory for any `__global__` function call and defines the grid/block dimensions and associated stream.
facts.note: Mandatory syntax placement

facts.claim: Configuration is specified using `<<< Dg, Db, Ns, S >>>` between the kernel name and argument list.
facts.note: Syntax structure

facts.claim: `Dg` (`dim3`) specifies grid dimensions; `Dg.x * Dg.y * Dg.z` equals total blocks launched.
facts.note: Grid parameter

facts.claim: `Db` (`dim3`) specifies block dimensions; `Db.x * Db.y * Db.z` equals threads per block.
facts.note: Block parameter

facts.claim: `Ns` (`size_t`) specifies dynamic shared memory per block (added to static allocation); defaults to 0.
facts.note: Dynamic shared memory parameter

facts.claim: `S` (`cudaStream_t`) specifies the associated stream; defaults to 0.
facts.note: Stream parameter

facts.claim: Configuration arguments are evaluated before the kernel's actual arguments.
facts.note: Evaluation order

facts.claim: Launch fails if `Dg` or `Db` exceed device maximums (per Compute Capability), or if `Ns` exceeds available shared memory minus static allocation.
facts.note: Validation/failure conditions

facts.claim: Compute Capability 9.0+ supports compile-time cluster dimensions via `__cluster_dims__(x, [y, [z]])` on the kernel definition.
facts.note: Cluster hierarchy (compile-time)

facts.claim: Default `__cluster_dims__()` designates a cluster grid launch; omitting runtime cluster dimensions causes a launch error.
facts.note: Cluster launch behavior

facts.claim: Runtime cluster dimensions can be configured using `cudaLaunchKernelEx` with `cudaLaunchConfig_t` and `cudaLaunchAttributeClusterDimension`.
facts.note: Cluster hierarchy (runtime API)

facts.claim: When using cluster launches, the grid dimension must be a multiple of the cluster size.
facts.note: Cluster constraint

caveats: Cluster dimension syntax and `cudaLaunchKernelEx` API are restricted to Compute Capability 9.0 and above.
Dynamic shared memory (`Ns`) is additive to statically allocated `__shared__` memory.

concept/explicit-synchronization.title: Explicit Synchronization
concept/explicit-synchronization.summary: Methods to explicitly synchronize streams include cudaDeviceSynchronize(), cudaStreamSynchronize(), cudaStreamWaitEvent(), and cudaStreamQuery().

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence (L2231-L2242) is a direct excerpt from Section 6.2.8.5.3 'Explicit Synchronization' within the broader 'Streams' subsection (6.2.8.5). It sits between implicit synchronization guidelines (6.2.8.5.4) and overlapping behavior explanations, serving as the authoritative reference for host-side API calls that enforce ordering or wait states across asynchronous CUDA streams.

facts.claim: Explicit synchronization provides host-side API calls to wait for or order device operations across CUDA streams.
facts.note: Framed by surrounding context as the mechanism to manage concurrency between asynchronous operations (kernel launches, memory transfers) that otherwise execute out of order across streams.

facts.claim: cudaDeviceSynchronize() blocks the host until all preceding commands in all streams of all host threads have completed.
facts.note: Global device-level wait; contrasts with stream-specific waits.

facts.claim: cudaStreamSynchronize(stream_t) blocks the host until all preceding commands in a specific stream have completed, allowing other streams to continue executing concurrently.
facts.note: Stream-scoped wait; preserves concurrency for unaffected streams.

facts.claim: cudaStreamWaitEvent(stream_t, cudaEvent_t) inserts a dependency into a stream, delaying all subsequent commands until a specified event completes.
facts.note: Used for fine-grained ordering between streams or between host/device operations via events.

facts.claim: cudaStreamQuery(stream_t) provides a non-blocking way to check if all preceding commands in a stream have completed.
facts.note: Polling alternative to blocking synchronization calls.

caveats: Explicit sync is distinct from implicit synchronization (triggered by the NULL stream or certain allocations) and programmatic dependent launch (CC 9.0+).
Commands within a single stream always execute in order; explicit sync is only required to coordinate across streams or between host and device.
recommended_structure: Usage Context (vs. Implicit Sync & Stream Ordering)

concept/explicit-synchronization-logical.title: Explicit Synchronization and Logical GPU Activity
concept/explicit-synchronization-logical.summary: Details how Unified Memory uses logical activity to determine GPU idle state, valid synchronization functions, stream dependencies, and constraints on concurrent CPU/GPU access.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence (L21898-L21943) is a subsection of 'Unified memory on Windows or devices with compute capability 5.x' under 'Coherency and Concurrency'. It explains the strict CPU/GPU access rules for managed memory on legacy architectures that lack fine-grained hardware coherency and on-demand page migration.

facts.claim: Unified Memory uses 'logical activity' to determine if the GPU is idle, meaning a kernel is considered active from launch until an explicit host synchronization call, regardless of actual execution time.
facts.note: Aligns with CUDA's asynchronous programming model where kernel completion is not guaranteed until synchronization.

facts.claim: Explicit synchronization is mandatory before CPU access to managed memory on systems where concurrentManagedAccess=0.
facts.note: Prevents coherency violations on pre-CC 6.0 hardware.

facts.claim: Valid synchronization primitives include cudaDeviceSynchronize(), cudaStreamSynchronize(), cudaStreamQuery() (if cudaSuccess and stream is the only active one), cudaEventSynchronize(), cudaEventQuery() (if no subsequent device work), and fully synchronous cudaMemcpy()/cudaMemset().
facts.note: Functions must logically guarantee GPU completion.

facts.claim: Stream dependencies (via cudaStreamWaitEvent() or the default NULL stream) are tracked to infer completion of upstream streams when synchronizing on a downstream stream or event.
facts.note: Enables transitive sync inference.

facts.claim: CPU access to managed data is permitted inside stream callbacks if no other stream accessing managed data is active. Callbacks not followed by device work can serve as sync points.
facts.note: Allows async host-device coordination without blocking the CPU thread.

facts.claim: CPU access to non-managed zero-copy data is always permitted while the GPU is active.
facts.note: Zero-copy bypasses managed memory coherency constraints.

facts.claim: The GPU is deemed active if any kernel is running, even if that kernel does not touch managed memory. CPU access to managed data is forbidden unless concurrentManagedAccess=1.
facts.note: Conservative safety model: mere potential for concurrent access triggers faults.

facts.claim: Concurrent inter-GPU access and concurrent GPU kernel access to managed memory face no restrictions, allowing potential races between GPU kernels.
facts.note: Managed memory behaves identically to non-managed memory from the GPU's perspective.

caveats: This model is specific to pre-Compute Capability 6.0 architectures and Windows platforms where fine-grained Unified Memory coherency and on-demand page migration are unavailable. Modern CC 6.0+ hardware lifts these restrictions via GPU page faulting.

The 'logical activity' constraint is the foundational rule that cudaStreamAttachMemAsync() later attempts to relax by scoping managed data visibility to specific streams rather than whole-GPU activity.
recommended_structure: Concurrency Rules (CPU vs GPU, Zero-Copy, Multi-GPU)

concept/extended-gpu-memory.title: Extended GPU Memory
concept/extended-gpu-memory.summary: CUDA feature enabling GPUs to access system memory via NVLink-C2C/NVSwitch, supporting single-node and multi-node topologies with VMM and Memory Pool APIs.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence constitutes Chapter 26 of the CUDA C Programming Guide, positioned between Chapter 25 (Lazy Loading) and Chapter 27 (Notices). It functions as a dedicated, legacy feature documentation block detailing memory management extensions for specific high-end NVIDIA architectures.

facts.claim: Extended GPU Memory (EGM) utilizes high-bandwidth NVLink-C2C to allow GPUs in integrated CPU-GPU systems to efficiently access all system memory (CPU-attached memory and HBM3) over the NVSwitch fabric at GPU-GPU NVLink or NVLink-C2C speeds.
facts.note: Legacy status: The source explicitly warns that this chapter is legacy and no longer updated as of CUDA 13.0, directing users to the new CUDA Programming Guide.

facts.claim: Supported topologies include: (1) Single-Node, Single-GPU (Arm-based CPU, CPU memory, GPU, C2C interconnect), (2) Single-Node, Multi-GPU (fully connected four single-node, single-GPU platforms), and (3) Multi-Node, Single-GPU (multiple single-node multi-socket systems). Using cgroups to limit devices blocks EGM routing; CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES should be used instead.
facts.note: Socket Identifiers & NUMA: EGM relies on OS-assigned NUMA node identifiers (numaID) rather than device ordinals. The numaID is retrieved via cuDeviceGetAttribute with the CU_DEVICE_ATTRIBUTE_HOST_NUMA_ID attribute.

facts.claim: Allocator & API Support: EGM memory is mapped using Virtual Memory Management (cuMemCreate) or Stream Ordered Memory (cudaMemPoolCreate) allocators. New CUDA property types were introduced: CU_MEM_LOCATION_TYPE_HOST_NUMA for CUmemAllocationProp and cudaMemLocationTypeHostNuma for cudaMemPoolProps. Users must explicitly specify the location type and NUMA identifier during allocation.
facts.note: Usage Patterns: Single-Node, Single-GPU uses standard host allocators. Single-Node, Multi-GPU uses VMM (cuMemCreate, cuMemAddressReserve, cuMemMap, cuMemSetAccess) or Memory Pools (cudaMemPoolCreate, cudaMemPoolSetAccess, cudaMallocAsync). EGM is mapped using 2MB pages, which may increase TLB misses for very large allocations. Multi-Node, Single-GPU follows CUDA IPC, using cuMemCreate with CU_MEM_HANDLE_TYPE_FABRIC, cuMemExportToShareableHandle (TCP/IP), cuMemImportFromShareableHandle, and local reservation/mapping/access.

caveats: Legacy documentation: Explicitly marked as legacy and unmaintained as of CUDA 13.0.
Performance note: 2MB page mapping may cause increased TLB misses for large allocations.
Configuration constraint: cgroups block EGM routing; CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES must be used instead.
contradictions: None detected within the assigned context. The document stands alone as a legacy feature chapter.

concept/extended-lambda-restrictions.title: Extended Lambda Restrictions
concept/extended-lambda-restrictions.summary: Detailed restrictions on CUDA extended lambdas, including enclosing function constraints, template parameter rules, capture variable limitations, `__CUDA_ARCH__` independence, placeholder type behavior, type trait mismatches, and diagnostic expectations.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence details restrictions 6 through 18 of the CUDA C++ extended lambda specification. It covers constraints on enclosing functions, template parameters, host compiler specifics (Visual Studio), captured variable rules, `__CUDA_ARCH__` dependency, host/device introspection limits, conversion operator availability, and type trait discrepancies between the CUDA frontend and host compiler.

facts.claim: Extended lambdas cannot be defined inside a class that is local to a function.
facts.note: Enclosing functions with deduced return types are prohibited for extended lambdas.

facts.claim: Enclosing functions with deduced return types are prohibited for extended lambdas.
facts.note: __host__ __device__ extended lambdas cannot be generic lambdas (i.e., cannot use `auto` parameters).

facts.claim: __host__ __device__ extended lambdas cannot be generic lambdas.
facts.note: If the enclosing function is a template instantiation or member of a class template, it must have at most one variadic parameter (listed last), all template parameters must be named, and instantiation arguments cannot involve types local to a function or private/protected class members.

facts.claim: Template enclosing functions must satisfy specific parameter constraints.
facts.note: On Visual Studio host compilers, the enclosing function must have external linkage, and extended lambdas cannot be defined inside `if-constexpr` blocks.

facts.claim: Visual Studio host compiler imposes external linkage and `if-constexpr` block restrictions.
facts.note: Captured variables are passed by value to helper functions before initializing closure type fields. Only value capture is allowed (no reference capture). Array dimensions > 7 cannot be captured. Array element types must be default-constructible and copy-assignable in host code. Variadic argument pack parameters cannot be captured. Captured variable types cannot involve types local to a function or private/protected class members. For `__host__ __device__` lambdas, `operator()` return/parameter types also cannot involve local/private/protected types. Init-capture is unsupported for `__host__ __device__` lambdas, but supported for `__device__` lambdas (except for array types or `std::initializer_list`). Closure types are not literal types; `constexpr`/`consteval` cannot be used. Implicit capture inside nested `if-constexpr` blocks is restricted unless captured earlier or explicitly listed.

facts.claim: Captured variable rules impose strict value-only, type, and initialization constraints.
facts.note: The definition order and count of extended lambdas within a function must not depend on the `__CUDA_ARCH__` macro's value or presence, due to compiler-assigned counter values for substituted named types. Expressions capturing variables must remain unchanged regardless of `__CUDA_ARCH__` definition/value when passed to device code, to prevent closure class layout mismatches between host and device compilation.

facts.claim: Extended lambda definitions and capture expressions must remain invariant to `__CUDA_ARCH__` macro state.
facts.note: The CUDA compiler replaces `__device__` extended lambdas in host functions with namespace-scope placeholder types. Without `__nv_is_extended_device_lambda_with_preserved_return_type()` returning true, `operator()` signature introspection (return/parameter types) fails in host code but works in device code. Placeholder types lack a pointer-to-function conversion operator in host code (supported in device code and for `__host__ __device__` lambdas).

facts.claim: Host code introspection and pointer-to-function conversion are restricted for `__device__` extended lambdas.
facts.note: Placeholder types may define special member functions, causing standard type traits (`std::is_trivially_copyable`, etc.) to differ between the CUDA frontend and host compiler. Results of these traits must not be used in `__global__` function template instantiation or `__device__`/`__constant__`/`__managed__` variable template instantiation.

facts.claim: Standard type traits may yield different results between the CUDA frontend and host compiler.
facts.note: The CUDA compiler generates diagnostics for restrictions 1-12; restrictions 13-17 may not trigger diagnostics but can cause host compiler failures.

facts.claim: Diagnostic coverage varies across restrictions.
facts.note: None

caveats: The assigned evidence covers restrictions 6-18 of the full extended lambda specification. Restrictions 1-5 (nested lambda definitions, generic lambda nesting, outermost lambda scope, named enclosing function/addressability, and unambiguous address resolution) are present in the surrounding context window (L18396-L18515) but fall outside the assigned evidence range.

Introspection and conversion limitations are explicitly tied to the compiler's placeholder type substitution strategy for `__device__` lambdas in host code, which differs from `__host__ __device__` lambdas.

concept/external-linkage.title: External Linkage
concept/external-linkage.summary: Rules for calling functions declared with extern qualifier in device code.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence is located in section 18.5.10.1 ('External Linkage') under the broader 'Functions' section (18.5.10). This section is part of a larger segment (18.5) detailing C++ language feature support and restrictions specific to CUDA device code, such as limitations on RTTI, exception handling, and standard libraries.

facts.claim: In CUDA C++, device code is only permitted to call a function declared with the `extern` qualifier if that function is defined within the same compilation unit as the device code.
facts.note: A 'compilation unit' in this context is defined as either a single file or multiple files linked together using relocatable device code (RDC) and the `nvlink` tool.

facts.claim: This restriction is specific to calls originating from device code.
facts.note: The guide frames this rule under the 'Functions' section, implying standard C++ linkage rules may apply to host code, but device code has this specific constraint.

caveats: The evidence does not specify the behavior for host code calling `extern` functions, only that device code is restricted.
The term 'extern qualifier' is used; standard C++ `extern` linkage semantics are not explicitly detailed beyond this device-code restriction.
recommended_structure: Definition of External Linkage in CUDA (focusing on the device code restriction)
Requirement for same compilation unit (single file vs. RDC + nvlink)
Context within CUDA C++ device code limitations

concept/external-resource-interoperabili.title: External Resource Interoperability
concept/external-resource-interoperabili.summary: Introduces the framework for importing external memory and synchronization objects (semaphores) from other APIs using OS-native handles or NVIDIA SWI. Covers cudaImportExternalMemory(), cudaExternalMemoryGetMappedBuffer(), cudaImportExternalSemaphore(), and their lifecycle management.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence forms the core conceptual overview of External Resource Interoperability. It is positioned in the source document between legacy Direct3D/OpenGL/SLI interoperability sections and newer, API-specific implementation guides (e.g., Vulkan), serving as the architectural foundation for cross-API resource sharing.

facts.claim: External Resource Interoperability allows CUDA to import resources explicitly exported by other APIs, typically via OS-native handles (Linux file descriptors, Windows NT handles) or unified interfaces like the NVIDIA Software Communication Interface.
facts.note: Establishes the primary purpose and transport mechanisms for cross-API resource sharing.

facts.claim: Two resource types are supported: memory objects and synchronization objects.
facts.note: Defines the categorical scope of the interoperability framework.

facts.claim: Memory objects are imported via cudaImportExternalMemory() and accessed in kernels through device pointers (cudaExternalMemoryGetMappedBuffer()) or mipmapped arrays (cudaExternalMemoryGetMappedMipmappedArray()).
facts.note: Details the import and kernel-access pathways for memory resources.

facts.claim: Memory object mappings must strictly match the exporting API's configuration; mismatches result in undefined behavior.
facts.note: Highlights a critical constraint for stable cross-API memory sharing.

facts.claim: Destroying a memory object via cudaDestroyExternalMemory() does not free its mappings. Mappings must be explicitly released using cudaFree() or cudaFreeMipmappedArray(). Accessing destroyed object mappings is illegal.
facts.note: Clarifies the decoupled lifecycle between the external object handle and its CUDA-side mappings.

facts.claim: Synchronization objects are imported via cudaImportExternalSemaphore() and managed asynchronously using cudaSignalExternalSemaphoresAsync() and cudaWaitExternalSemaphoresAsync().
facts.note: Details the import and execution-control pathways for synchronization resources.

facts.claim: Issuing a wait before the corresponding signal is illegal. Destroying a semaphore via cudaDestroyExternalSemaphore() requires all outstanding signals and waits to complete first.
facts.note: Enforces strict ordering and lifecycle completion rules for cross-API synchronization.

caveats: The source explicitly notes that additional constraints may apply depending on the specific type of imported synchronization object, which are detailed in subsequent API-specific sections.
No scope clashes detected; this section acts as the definitive conceptual anchor for the interoperability framework before diving into Vulkan-specific handle imports.

concept/fabric-memory.title: Fabric Memory
concept/fabric-memory.summary: Introduces CU_MEM_HANDLE_TYPE_FABRIC for intra-node and inter-node memory sharing via NVIDIA IMEX daemon and NVLINK fabric.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence covers Section 14.8 (Fabric Memory) and 14.8.1 (Query for Support) within the CUDA Virtual Memory Management chapter. It details the introduction, prerequisites, functionality, and API usage for the `CU_MEM_HANDLE_TYPE_FABRIC` allocation handle.

facts.claim: Fabric Memory was introduced in CUDA 12.4 as a new Virtual Memory Management (VMM) allocation handle type named `CU_MEM_HANDLE_TYPE_FABRIC`.
facts.note: Enables sharing allocations intra-node (e.g., with MPI) and inter-node, allowing GPUs in a Multi-Node NVLINK system to map memory from other GPUs across different nodes within the same NVLINK fabric, greatly scaling multi-GPU programming.

facts.claim: Requires supported hardware platforms and the NVIDIA IMEX daemon to be running.
facts.note: Device support is queried using `cuDeviceGetAttribute` with the `CU_DEVICE_ATTRIBUTE_HANDLE_TYPE_FABRIC_SUPPORTED` flag.

facts.claim: Aside from using the `CU_MEM_HANDLE_TYPE_FABRIC` handle type and eliminating the need for OS-native IPC mechanisms to exchange sharable handles, its usage is functionally identical to other VMM allocation handle types.
facts.note: No scope clashes or contradictions detected; aligns with standard VMM allocation workflows.

caveats: Requires the NVIDIA IMEX daemon to be active on supported platforms.
Inter-node sharing is contingent on GPUs belonging to the same NVLINK fabric.
contradictions: None detected.

concept/friend-functions.title: Friend Functions
concept/friend-functions.summary: Restrictions on defining __global__ functions or templates in friend declarations.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence is located in section 18.5.10.7 of the CUDA C++ language extensions. It sits between restrictions on function pointers/recursion and operator functions, specifically addressing how CUDA execution space specifiers interact with C++ friend declarations.

facts.claim: A __global__ function or function template cannot be defined inline within a friend declaration.
facts.note: Inline definition (providing a function body) in the friend declaration triggers a compilation error.

facts.claim: Declaring a __global__ function or template as a friend is permitted, provided no body is attached.
facts.note: The guide explicitly marks `friend __global__ void foo(void);` as valid, contrasting it with the invalid `friend __global__ void foo(void) { }`.

caveats: This restriction is specific to CUDA's `__global__` execution space specifier. Standard C++ friend function rules apply to `__host__` or `__device__` functions unless otherwise specified in other CUDA extension sections.

concept/function-parameters.title: Function Parameters
concept/function-parameters.summary: Limits on __global__ function parameters (32,764 bytes on Volta+, 4 KB older), restrictions on variable arguments and pass-by-reference, and ODR requirements.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence corresponds to section 18.5.10.3 "Function Parameters" within the CUDA C++ Programming Guide. It establishes baseline constraints and compilation rules for device-side function parameters, serving as a prerequisite to the subsequent section 18.5.10.3.1 which details argument processing workflows for `__global__` launches.

facts.claim: __global__ function parameters are passed to the device via constant memory.
facts.note: Size limits apply to this constant memory allocation.

facts.claim: Parameter size limits for __global__ functions are 32,764 bytes on Volta and newer architectures, and 4 KB on older architectures.
facts.note: Exceeding these limits will cause compilation or launch failures.

facts.claim: __global__ functions cannot accept variable arguments (variadic functions) or pass-by-reference parameters.
facts.note: These restrictions apply specifically to device-side function signatures.

facts.claim: In separate compilation mode, ODR-used __device__ or __global__ functions require complete parameter and return types in the translation unit.
facts.note: Using forward-declared or incomplete types for parameters results in nvlink prototype mismatch errors.

caveats: The evidence focuses strictly on `__global__` and `__device__` function parameter constraints. It does not cover host-side (`__host__`) parameter passing, which follows standard C++ semantics.

The subsequent section (18.5.10.3.1) details how non-trivially copyable arguments are handled during `__global__` launches (memcpy vs copy constructors), which is a related but distinct topic from the parameter declaration rules in this evidence.

concept/function-pointers.title: Function Pointers
concept/function-pointers.summary: Restrictions on taking addresses of __global__ and __device__ functions across host/device boundaries.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence is a subsection (18.5.10.5) within the CUDA C Programming Guide's broader section on function constraints. It explicitly defines the boundaries for using function pointers across host and device execution spaces.

facts.claim: The address of a __global__ function captured in host code cannot be used in device code, including for kernel launches.
facts.note: Directly restricts cross-space pointer usage for kernels.

facts.claim: The address of a __global__ function captured in device code cannot be used in host code.
facts.note: Enforces strict bidirectional separation for kernel addresses.

facts.claim: Taking the address of a __device__ function in host code is explicitly prohibited.
facts.note: Prevents host-side function pointer resolution for device-only code.

caveats: The guide enforces strict execution-space isolation; function pointers cannot bridge host and device memory spaces for __global__ or __device__ functions.
No examples or workarounds are provided in this section, indicating these are hard compiler/linker constraints.
recommended_structure: Overview of CUDA function pointer restrictions
Best practices for dynamic dispatch (if applicable in other sources)

research_reports.context_summary: The document contains a dedicated subsection (18.5.10.6) explicitly addressing function recursion within CUDA C++ language constraints.

facts.claim: __global__ functions do not support recursion.
facts.note: This is a strict, absolute constraint for __global__ execution space specifiers. The text does not explicitly restrict __device__ function recursion, implying it may be permitted or handled under different device compilation rules.

caveats: The provided assigned evidence block (L17235-L17236) actually pertains to function pointer restrictions (Section 18.5.10.5), not recursion. The relevant recursion constraint is located immediately after at L17240-L17242.
No contradictions or scope clashes detected. The restriction is narrowly scoped to __global__ functions only.

concept/global-function-argument-process.title: __global__ Function Argument Processing
concept/global-function-argument-process.summary: Behavior of __global__ function arguments from host code: memcpy instead of copy constructors, and potential early destructor invocation due to asynchronous execution.

research_reports.context_summary: Section 18.5.10.3.1 details how __global__ function arguments are handled during kernel launches, distinguishing between device and host launch contexts and highlighting two major divergences from standard C++ object lifecycle rules when launched from the host.

facts.claim: When a __global__ function is launched from device code, all arguments must be trivially copyable and trivially destructible.
facts.note: This is a strict requirement for device-to-device kernel launches.

facts.claim: When launched from host code, __global__ function arguments may be non-trivially copyable or non-trivially destructible, but the compiler does not follow the standard C++ object lifecycle model.
facts.note: The compiler generates host stub functions to handle argument passing.

facts.claim: Memcpy Bypasses Copy Constructors: The compiler generates host stubs that bitwise copy parameters to the device's kernel parameter memory using memcpy, completely skipping copy constructors. This breaks code relying on side effects in copy constructors (e.g., pointer aliasing or counter increments).
facts.note: Examples demonstrate failed assertions due to skipped copy construction logic.

facts.claim: Premature Destructor Invocation: Because kernel launches are asynchronous, host-side destructors for __global__ function arguments may execute before the GPU kernel finishes. This can cause use-after-free errors if the destructor releases resources the kernel still needs.
facts.note: Example shows a managed pointer being freed by the host destructor while the kernel attempts to write to it.

facts.claim: User Responsibility: Developers must ensure that non-trivially copyable/destructible argument types do not rely on standard C++ lifecycle guarantees, as the CUDA execution model diverges from it.
facts.note: No automatic safety mechanisms are provided for these edge cases.

caveats: The guide explicitly warns that these behaviors may break programs where copy constructors or destructors have side effects. Developers should prefer trivially copyable/destructible types for kernel arguments or manage lifecycle explicitly.
The examples provided in the source use `cudaDeviceSynchronize()` to illustrate the timing, but the core issue is the asynchronous nature of the launch itself.

concept/gpu-memory-oversubscription.title: GPU Memory Oversubscription
concept/gpu-memory-oversubscription.summary: Explains how Unified Memory allows applications to allocate and share arrays larger than individual processor memory capacities, enabling out-of-core processing without significant model complexity.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence is a single paragraph under section 24.1.2.7. It is embedded within the broader Unified Memory chapter, immediately followed by subsections detailing performance optimization APIs (`cudaMemPrefetchAsync`, `cudaMemDiscardBatchAsync`, `cudaMemAdvise`). The surrounding context frames oversubscription as a core Unified Memory capability that triggers automatic fault-based migration and page eviction, requiring explicit performance hints to avoid redundant data transfers.

facts.claim: GPU memory oversubscription allows applications to allocate and share memory arrays that exceed the physical capacity of any individual processor in the system.
facts.note: Core definition.

facts.claim: This capability is enabled by CUDA Unified Memory, which abstracts physical memory boundaries and handles migration automatically.
facts.note: Enabling technology.

facts.claim: Primary use case is out-of-core processing of datasets that do not fit within a single GPU, without adding significant complexity to the programming model.
facts.note: Key benefit/use case.

caveats: Scope is strictly limited to the Unified Memory/Managed Memory paradigm; explicit host-device paging or third-party out-of-core frameworks are not covered.

Performance relies on automatic fault-based migration and eviction; the guide immediately follows this section with `cudaMemPrefetchAsync` and `cudaMemDiscardBatchAsync` to mitigate redundant transfers, indicating oversubscription requires active tuning for optimal performance.

concept/gpu-memory-oversubscription-pre6.title: GPU Memory Oversubscription on Pre-6.x
concept/gpu-memory-oversubscription-pre6.summary: States the limitation that devices with compute capability lower than 6.0 cannot allocate more managed memory than the physical size of GPU memory.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence falls under the section 'Unified memory on Windows or devices with compute capability 5.x', which details the limitations of CUDA Managed Memory v1.0 on older architectures. The specific subsection 'GPU Memory Oversubscription' explicitly states the hard limit on managed memory allocations.

facts.claim: Pre-6.x GPUs (compute capability < 6.0) do not support GPU memory oversubscription for managed memory.
facts.note: Managed memory allocations cannot exceed the physical size of the GPU memory on these architectures.

caveats: This limitation is specific to CUDA Managed Memory v1.0 on pre-6.x devices and Windows platforms. Later architectures (6.x+) introduced GPU page faulting and system-wide virtual address spaces to enable oversubscription and on-demand migration.
recommended_structure: State the hard limit: pre-6.x GPUs cannot allocate more managed memory than physical GPU memory.
Explain the architectural reason: lack of GPU page faulting and system-wide virtual address space present in 6.x+.
Note the historical context: this applies to CUDA Managed Memory v1.0 on older architectures and Windows platforms.

concept/graph-api-usage.title: Graph API Usage Guidelines
concept/graph-api-usage.summary: Covers thread-safety rules for `cudaGraph_t` (not thread-safe, user responsibility), concurrency limits for `cudaGraphExec_t` (cannot run concurrently with itself, ordered launches), and the role of streams for ordering without constraining internal graph parallelism.

research_reports.scope: Section 6.2.8.7.6 (Using Graph APIs) sits between node update/enable guidelines and device graph launch workflows, establishing core concurrency and execution model rules before diving into device-specific handling.
research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence outlines fundamental concurrency and execution constraints for CUDA Graph API objects (`cudaGraph_t` and `cudaGraphExec_t`), clarifying thread safety, launch ordering, and stream interaction semantics.

facts.claim: `cudaGraph_t` objects are not thread-safe; users must prevent concurrent access from multiple threads.
facts.note: Applies to graph capture/creation phase.

facts.claim: `cudaGraphExec_t` cannot run concurrently with itself; launches are strictly ordered after previous launches of the same executable graph.
facts.note: Applies to instantiated/executable graph phase.

facts.claim: Graph execution uses streams solely for ordering relative to other asynchronous work; the stream does not constrain internal graph parallelism or dictate node execution locations.
facts.note: Applies to launch/runtime phase.

concept/graph-execution-environments.title: Graph Execution Environments
concept/graph-execution-environments.summary: Covers the hierarchical execution environment model for device-side synchronization. Environments encapsulate graph work and generated fire-and-forget work. Host launches create a parent stream environment. Completion is marked when the graph and all child work finish.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence is a dedicated subsection (6.2.8.7.7.8) within the CUDA C Programming Guide's device graph documentation. It acts as a conceptual prerequisite for understanding device-side synchronization, directly preceding the detailed breakdowns of Tail Launch (L2998) and Sibling Launch (L3079).

facts.claim: An execution environment is a conceptual construct used to model device-side synchronization. When a graph is launched from the device, it is assigned its own execution environment.
facts.note: Encapsulation & Hierarchy: The environment encapsulates all work within the graph itself, plus any "fire and forget" work it spawns. Environments are hierarchical, supporting multiple nested levels of child environments from fire-and-forget launches.

facts.claim: A graph is considered complete only when its own execution finishes AND all generated child work (fire and forget) has also completed.
facts.note: Host Launch Context: When launched from the host, the graph's execution environment is nested inside a "stream environment." The stream environment acts as the parent, encapsulating all work from the overall launch. The stream launch is only complete when this parent stream environment is marked complete.

caveats: The source exclusively defines execution environments in the context of device-side graph launches and their synchronization model. It does not cover host-side graph execution environments outside of the parent stream environment relationship.

concept/graph-memory-footprint.title: Graph Memory Footprint
concept/graph-memory-footprint.summary: Management of physical memory footprint for graphs, including trimming via cudaDeviceGraphMemTrim and querying attributes.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence from Section 16.6 directly addresses the target concept by detailing CUDA's asynchronous pool management behavior for graph memory, the explicit trimming API, and the querying attributes used to monitor physical memory footprint.

facts.claim: Asynchronous allocation pool management retains physical memory even after graph destruction, preventing immediate return to the OS.
facts.note: Applies to graphs containing memory nodes, even if internal allocations are freed.

facts.claim: The cudaDeviceGraphMemTrim API explicitly unmasks and releases physical memory reserved by graph memory nodes that are not actively in use.
facts.note: Actively used memory (unfreed allocations, scheduled, or running graphs) is preserved.

facts.claim: Trimming releases memory to the OS but forces CUDA to reallocate and remap memory on subsequent graph launches.
facts.note: Represents a trade-off between OS-level memory availability and launch-time mapping overhead.

facts.claim: The graph memory pool is isolated from the stream-ordered memory allocator and is not exposed to cudaMemPoolTrimTo().
facts.note: cudaDeviceGraphMemTrim operates on a separate pool.

facts.claim: cudaDeviceGetGraphMemAttribute API is used to query graph memory footprint.
facts.note: Supports cudaGraphMemAttrReservedMemCurrent (total reserved physical memory) and cudaGraphMemAttrUsedMemCurrent (currently mapped physical memory).

facts.claim: These attributes help track new physical memory acquisition and measure savings from graph memory sharing mechanisms.
facts.note: Useful for performance and memory optimization analysis.

caveats: Source contains minor typos (e.g., 'diferent', 'steam ordered') which have been normalized in the findings for clarity.
No contradictions with target scope found; the section explicitly covers 'Physical Memory Footprint'.

concept/graph-memory-nodes.title: Graph Memory Nodes
concept/graph-memory-nodes.summary: Explains how graphs manage GPU-ordered memory allocations, their lifetimes, API fundamentals, stream capture, and cross-graph access/freeing procedures.

research_reports.scope: Chapter 16 covers Graph Memory Nodes, detailing their introduction, API fundamentals, stream capture mechanics, external access/freeing rules, auto-free flags, memory reuse strategies, performance considerations, and footprint management.
research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence provides the complete documentation for Chapter 16 of the CUDA C Programming Guide. It serves as the authoritative reference for how graphs manage memory lifetimes, enforce GPU-ordered dependencies, and optimize physical memory reuse across graph instantiations and launches.

facts.claim: Graph memory nodes enable graphs to create and own memory allocations with GPU-ordered lifetime semantics, matching the behavior of stream-ordered APIs like cudaMallocAsync and cudaFreeAsync.
facts.note: Legacy status: The guide explicitly warns that this document is legacy and no longer updated as of CUDA 13.0.

facts.claim: Support requires a CUDA driver version 11.4 or newer, along with GPU support for the stream-ordered allocator.
facts.note: Driver version checks are required before querying device attributes to avoid invalid value errors on older drivers.

facts.claim: Two node types exist: allocation nodes (cudaGraphAddMemAllocNode) and free nodes (cudaGraphAddMemFreeNode). Virtual addresses are fixed at node creation for the graph's lifetime, while contents are not persistent past the free operation.
facts.note: Graph destruction does not automatically free live graph-allocated memory; explicit freeing is required.

facts.claim: Lifetimes are GPU-ordered: they begin when GPU execution reaches the allocation node and end when execution reaches the free node, a cudaFreeAsync call, or a cudaFree call.
facts.note: Operations accessing the memory must be ordered after the alloc node and before the free node via dependency edges.

facts.claim: Graph memory nodes can be implicitly created by capturing cudaMallocAsync and cudaFreeAsync calls. Stream dependencies are automatically translated into graph dependencies.
facts.note: Capturing correctly written stream code guarantees proper ordering of graph memory nodes relative to captured operations.

facts.claim: Allocations can persist beyond the allocating graph's execution and be accessed by subsequent graphs or stream operations, provided ordering is established via CUDA events or stream mechanisms.
facts.note: Out-of-band synchronization (e.g., memory handshakes) is insufficient for ordering guarantees between device operations and the free operation.

facts.claim: The cudaGraphInstantiateFlagAutoFreeOnLaunch flag allows graphs with unfreed allocations to be relaunched by automatically inserting an asynchronous free, simplifying single-producer/multiple-consumer patterns.
facts.note: The flag does not change graph destruction behavior; applications must still explicitly free unfreed memory to avoid leaks.

facts.claim: CUDA optimizes memory reuse via virtual address assignment within graphs and virtual aliasing (mapping the same physical memory to different virtual addresses) across graphs launched in the same stream.
facts.note: Remapping costs occur when changing streams, trimming memory, or relaunching with overlapping lifetimes. cudaGraphUpload can pre-map memory to avoid launch-time remapping.

facts.claim: Destroying a graph does not immediately return physical memory to the OS. Applications should use cudaDeviceGraphMemTrim to release unused memory, and can query footprint via cudaDeviceGetGraphMemAttribute.
facts.note: cudaDeviceGraphMemTrim operates on a different pool than cudaMemPoolTrimTo.

facts.claim: Graph allocations support multi-GPU peer access, with CUDA automatically mapping allocations to peer GPUs as required.
facts.note: Relying on extra mappings beyond those requested during creation is an error.

caveats: The source document is explicitly marked as legacy (CUDA 13.0+) and replaced by the new CUDA Programming Guide.
Virtual addresses are fixed per graph lifetime, but physical backing memory may change or be aliased across graphs.
Out-of-band synchronization cannot replace explicit CUDA events or stream ordering for memory access/free guarantees.

concept/graph-memory-performance.title: Graph Memory Performance
concept/graph-memory-performance.summary: Performance considerations for graph memory, including remapping costs and stream consistency.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence falls under Section 16.5 (Performance Considerations) within a broader discussion on CUDA graph memory management, including virtual/physical reuse, address reuse, physical memory footprint, and peer access configurations.

facts.claim: CUDA optimizes graph memory performance by reusing the same physical memory for graphs launched sequentially in the same stream, retaining mappings between launches to avoid remapping overhead.
facts.note: Concurrent graphs require distinct memory to prevent data corruption, which forces remapping when execution contexts overlap.

facts.claim: Remapping is triggered by three primary operations: changing the launch stream, performing a graph memory pool trim, or relaunching a graph while an unfreed allocation from another graph shares the same physical memory.
facts.note: Remapping operations are OS calls with significant overhead and must strictly follow execution order, completing after any previous execution of the graph finishes to prevent unmapping live memory.

facts.claim: To minimize remapping costs, applications should consistently launch graphs containing allocation memory nodes into the same stream, avoiding stream switches and concurrent execution scenarios that force distinct memory allocation.
facts.note: Remapping operations are OS calls with significant overhead and must strictly follow execution order, completing after any previous execution of the graph finishes to prevent unmapping live memory.

caveats: Remapping must happen in execution order, but after any previous execution of that graph is complete (otherwise memory that is still in use could be unmapped). Due to this ordering dependency, as well as because mapping operations are OS calls, mapping operations can be relatively expensive. Applications can avoid this cost by launching graphs containing allocation memory nodes consistently into the same stream.

concept/graph-peer-access.title: Graph Peer Access
concept/graph-peer-access.summary: Configuration of graph allocations for multi-GPU peer access and virtual address reuse across GPUs.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence provides the high-level overview for Section 16.7 (Peer Access) within the CUDA Graphs memory management chapter. It establishes the baseline behavior for how graph allocations handle cross-GPU accessibility before diving into specific API implementations (node APIs vs. stream capture) in the subsequent subsections.

facts.claim: Graph allocations can be configured for access from multiple GPUs, causing CUDA to automatically map the allocations onto the required peer GPUs.
facts.note: Core behavior of graph peer access configuration.

facts.claim: CUDA permits graph allocations that require different peer mappings to reuse the same virtual address. When reused, the address range is mapped to all GPUs required by the distinct allocations.
facts.note: Explains the virtual aliasing mechanism applied to peer access mappings.

facts.claim: Due to virtual address reuse, an allocation may inadvertently acquire more peer access mappings than explicitly requested during creation. Relying on these extra, implicit mappings is an error.
facts.note: Critical caveat regarding implicit access expansion and developer responsibility.

caveats: The overview explicitly warns that developers must not rely on extra peer mappings that arise from virtual address reuse, even though CUDA may silently create them.

This high-level behavior is implemented differently depending on whether the graph is built via explicit node APIs (`cudaGraphAddMemAllocNode`) or stream capture, as detailed in the surrounding context (L16253-L16322).
recommended_structure: Implementation Context (Node APIs vs. Stream Capture)

concept/grid-synchronization.title: Grid Synchronization
concept/grid-synchronization.summary: Covers grid-level synchronization in CUDA, including the grid.sync() function, cooperative kernel launch requirements, occupancy calculation for maximizing parallelism, and device/platform compatibility constraints.

research_reports.context_summary: The evidence is extracted from Section 11.7 of the CUDA C Programming Guide, positioned between Cooperative Groups execution control primitives and the legacy Cluster Launch Control chapter. It specifically addresses inter-thread-block synchronization across the entire grid.

facts.claim: Grid synchronization replaces kernel-boundary synchronization, which implicitly invalidates state and carries performance overhead.
facts.note: Enables persistent thread blocks in pipeline stages to synchronize on-device when a stage completes.

facts.claim: Grid synchronization is invoked via the Cooperative Groups API using `this_grid().sync()`.
facts.note: Requires `grid_group` type from `cooperative_groups`.

facts.claim: Kernels using grid synchronization must be launched with `cudaLaunchCooperativeKernel` or the CUDA Driver API equivalent, bypassing the standard `<<<...>>>` syntax.
facts.note: Ensures all launched blocks co-reside on the GPU simultaneously.

facts.claim: Grid sizing examples demonstrate launching exactly `multiProcessorCount` blocks, or using `cudaOccupancyMaxActiveBlocksPerMultiprocessor` to calculate and launch a grid that maximally fills the GPU.
facts.note: Both approaches guarantee co-residency required for `grid.sync()` to function.

facts.claim: Hardware and platform constraints require Compute Capability 6.0+, verification via `cudaDevAttrCooperativeLaunch`, and execution on Linux (without MPS, or with MPS on CC 7.0+) or the latest Windows platform.
facts.note: None detected. The scope strictly covers grid-wide synchronization via Cooperative Groups and cooperative launch requirements. Matches target concept perfectly.

caveats: Standard `<<<...>>>` kernel launch syntax is incompatible and will fail to provide the necessary co-residency for `grid.sync()` to work correctly.
Platform restrictions apply: MPS support on Linux is limited to CC 7.0+, and older Windows versions may not support cooperative launches.

concept/hardware-multithreading.title: Hardware Multithreading
concept/hardware-multithreading.summary: Explains on-chip context maintenance for warps, enabling zero-cost context switching. Details partitioning of registers and shared memory among warps/blocks, limits on resident blocks/warps, and the formula for calculating total warps per block. Notes that insufficient resources cause kernel launch failures.

research_reports.context_summary: The evidence is extracted from Chapter 7 (Hardware Implementation) of the legacy CUDA C Programming Guide. It introduces the execution model of the Streaming Multiprocessor (SM), focusing on warp context management, scheduling, and resource partitioning before detailing specific compute capabilities.

facts.claim: Execution context (program counters, registers, etc.) for each warp is maintained on-chip for the warp's entire lifetime, making context switching costless.
facts.note: Directly supports the core definition of hardware multithreading via zero-overhead context switching.

facts.claim: At every instruction issue time, a warp scheduler selects a ready warp (with active threads) and issues the instruction to those threads.
facts.note: Describes the hardware scheduling mechanism enabled by multithreading.

facts.claim: Multiprocessors partition 32-bit registers among warps, while parallel data cache and shared memory are partitioned among thread blocks.
facts.note: Details the hardware resource allocation model supporting concurrent warp execution.

facts.claim: The number of resident blocks and warps per multiprocessor depends on the kernel's register/shared memory usage versus available hardware resources. These limits are compute-capability-dependent, and a kernel fails to launch if a block cannot fit.
facts.note: Explains occupancy constraints and launch failure conditions tied to multithreading capacity.

facts.claim: The total number of warps in a block is calculated as ceil(T / W_size, 1), where T is threads per block and W_size is fixed at 32.
facts.note: Provides the mathematical basis for warp granularity in the multithreaded execution model.

facts.claim: The CUDA Occupancy Calculator documents the total registers and shared memory allocated per block.
facts.note: References the official tooling for analyzing multithreading occupancy.

caveats: The source document is explicitly marked as legacy (replaced by the CUDA Programming Guide as of CUDA 13.0), but the architectural concepts remain consistent. No scope clashes or contradictions detected.
recommended_structure: Definition & Core Mechanism (On-chip context, zero-cost switching)
Resource Partitioning (Registers to warps, memory to blocks)
Occupancy & Limits (Resident block/warp constraints, launch failures)
Tooling & Calculation (Warp count formula, Occupancy Calculator)

concept/hardware-vs-software-coherency.title: Hardware vs. Software Coherency in Page Tables
concept/hardware-vs-software-coherency.summary: Contrasts hardware coherent systems (combined CPU/GPU page tables) with software coherent systems (separate page tables requiring page faults for coherency). Explains performance benefits of hardware coherency regarding TLB misses, cache-line granularity contention, and atomic updates.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence is located in the Unified Memory performance tuning section (24.2.2.1.2). It explicitly defines and contrasts hardware vs. software coherency architectures, detailing how page table sharing or separation dictates memory migration, TLB behavior, and synchronization overhead.

facts.claim: Hardware coherent systems feature a logically combined page table for both CPUs and GPUs (e.g., NVIDIA Grace Hopper). The GPU directly uses the CPU-created page table entries (PTEs) for System-Allocated Memory.
facts.note: If the CPU uses small default page sizes (4KiB or 64KiB), GPU accesses to large virtual memory areas can cause significant TLB misses and slowdowns.

facts.claim: Software coherent systems maintain separate logical page tables for the CPU and GPU.
facts.note: Coherency is emulated using page faults when a processor accesses memory mapped to the other processor's physical memory.

facts.claim: Page faults in software coherent systems trigger three steps: invalidating/updating the owning processor's PTE, creating/updating the requesting processor's PTE, and migrating the physical page to the requesting processor.
facts.note: Migration cost is proportional to the page size.

facts.claim: Hardware coherent systems offer significant performance benefits for frequent concurrent CPU/GPU accesses due to fewer page faults and reduced contention.
facts.note: Coherency operates at cache-line granularity instead of page-size granularity, meaning contention only occurs within a single cache line.

facts.claim: Architectural differences critically impact performance for concurrent atomic updates to the same address and CPU-GPU signaling scenarios.
facts.note: None

caveats: The source explicitly reserves the terms 'hardware coherent' and 'software coherent' for the remainder of the performance tuning documentation.

Hardware coherent systems may still suffer from TLB misses if small CPU default page sizes (4KiB/64KiB) are used for large virtual memory areas; configuring huge pages is recommended to mitigate this.
recommended_structure: Definition & Architecture (Combined vs. Separate Page Tables)
Performance Implications (TLB Misses, Cache-Line vs Page-Size Granularity)

concept/host-device-data-transfer.title: Host-Device Data Transfer
concept/host-device-data-transfer.summary: Details techniques to minimize host-device data transfers, including batching small transfers, using page-locked host memory, mapped memory, and handling integrated systems.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence falls under Section 8.3.1 within the broader 'Maximize Memory Throughput' chapter (8.3). It directly addresses the first optimization step: reducing low-bandwidth host-device transfers, preceding device memory access optimization (8.3.2).

facts.claim: Host-device data transfers should be minimized by moving computation to the device and keeping intermediate data structures in device memory.
facts.note: Batching small transfers into larger ones improves performance by amortizing transfer overhead.

facts.claim: Page-locked host memory improves transfer performance on systems with a front-side bus.
facts.note: Mapped page-locked memory enables implicit transfers on kernel access, avoiding explicit copy calls. Requires coalesced accesses and is most effective for single-read/write scenarios.

facts.claim: On integrated systems where host and device memory are physically shared, explicit copies are unnecessary. Use mapped page-locked memory and verify integration via the `integrated` device property (value 1).
facts.note: Fits under the 'Maximize Memory Throughput' chapter as the initial step to reduce low-bandwidth transfers before detailing device-side memory access patterns (Section 8.3.2).

caveats: Mapped memory performance is contingent on coalesced memory accesses and single-pass data usage.
contradictions: None detected.

concept/host-device-lambda-notes.title: Host Device Lambda Notes
concept/host-device-lambda-notes.summary: Behavioral notes on `__host__ __device__` lambdas, specifically regarding indirect function calls and host compiler inlining limitations.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence is Section 18.7.3, which isolates the host-side execution model and compiler behavior for `__host__ __device__` lambdas. It sits within a broader section detailing various device/extended lambda constraints, introspection limits, closure layout rules, and type trait discrepancies.

facts.claim: Unlike pure `__device__` lambdas, `__host__ __device__` lambdas are callable directly from host code.
facts.note: This distinguishes them from `__device__`-only lambdas, which are restricted to GPU execution.

facts.claim: The CUDA compiler transforms extended `__host__ __device__` lambdas defined in host code into instances of a named placeholder type.
facts.note: This placeholder acts as a bridge between host and device execution contexts.

facts.claim: The placeholder type invokes the original lambda's `operator()` using an indirect function call.
facts.note: This indirection is a deliberate compiler implementation choice to support dual-annotated execution.

facts.claim: The indirect function call can hinder host compiler optimization, specifically preventing easy inlining of the lambda body.
facts.note: As a result, `__host__ __device__` lambdas may execute slower on the host compared to explicitly or implicitly `__host__`-only lambdas, which the host compiler can inline directly.

caveats: The indirect call mechanism is a known optimization bottleneck on the host side. Developers should profile host-side execution of extended `__host__ __device__` lambdas if performance is critical.
No contradictions or scope clashes detected; the evidence cleanly addresses host-side compilation and execution notes.
