concept/host-functions-callbacks.title: Host Functions (Callbacks)
concept/host-functions-callbacks.summary: cudaLaunchHostFunc() inserts a CPU function call into a stream that executes after preceding commands complete. Commands issued after a callback wait for it, and callbacks must not make CUDA API calls to avoid deadlock.

research_reports.context_summary: Section 6.2.8.5.6 within the CUDA C Programming Guide's Streams chapter, detailing how to enqueue host-side callbacks into CUDA streams for synchronization and post-processing.

facts.claim: Host functions (callbacks) allow inserting a CPU function call into a CUDA stream at a specific point using `cudaLaunchHostFunc()`.
facts.note: The callback executes on the host once all preceding commands in the stream have completed.

facts.claim: Execution is strictly ordered: commands issued after the callback in the same stream are blocked until the callback finishes.
facts.note: This makes callbacks useful for triggering host-side processing (e.g., after a device-to-host memory copy) before proceeding with further stream work.

facts.claim: Callbacks must not make CUDA API calls (directly or indirectly) to avoid self-deadlock.
facts.note: Violating this constraint can cause the host thread to wait on itself, halting execution.

caveats: Callbacks are stream-specific and do not provide cross-stream synchronization guarantees.
The guide explicitly warns against CUDA API calls inside callbacks due to deadlock risk.

concept/host-native-atomics.title: Host Native Atomics
concept/host-native-atomics.summary: Describes hardware-accelerated atomic accesses to CPU-resident memory on NVLink-connected coherent systems, eliminating the need for page fault emulation.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence is located in Section 24.2.2.3, 'Host Native Atomics', which is part of the broader 'Direct Unified Memory Access from host' section. The surrounding context discusses hardware vs. software coherency, page table management, and the performance implications of page faults when CPUs and GPUs access the same memory. Host Native Atomics is presented as a hardware feature that optimizes concurrent atomic updates between the host and device.

facts.claim: Host Native Atomics are hardware-accelerated atomic accesses to CPU-resident memory.
facts.note: This defines the core concept of the target page.

facts.claim: Supporting hardware eliminates the need to emulate atomic accesses to host memory using page faults.
facts.note: This explains the primary performance benefit and mechanism of the feature.

facts.claim: Host Native Atomics are supported on NVLink-connected devices within hardware coherent systems.
facts.note: This specifies the hardware prerequisites for the feature.

facts.claim: The feature can be detected at runtime by checking if the device attribute cudaDevAttrHostNativeAtomicSupported is set to 1.
facts.note: This provides the programmatic method for verifying support.

caveats: The assigned evidence is brief and strictly defines the feature's existence, benefit, hardware requirements, and detection method. It does not provide code examples or deeper architectural details, which may need to be sourced from other documents if available.

concept/implicit-synchronization.title: Implicit Synchronization
concept/implicit-synchronization.summary: Operations from different streams cannot run concurrently if a CUDA operation on the NULL stream is submitted between them, unless non-blocking streams are used. Guidelines recommend issuing independent operations first and delaying synchronization.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence (L2243-L2252) falls under the 'Streams' section (6.2.8.5) of the CUDA C Programming Guide. It directly follows explanations of explicit synchronization methods and the NULL stream's role as the legacy default stream. The section outlines how the NULL stream acts as a synchronization barrier between independent streams.

facts.claim: Implicit synchronization is triggered when a CUDA operation is submitted to the NULL stream between two operations from different streams, preventing those operations from running concurrently.
facts.note: This behavior is specific to the NULL stream, which is the default stream in legacy compilation modes and enforces cross-thread/device ordering.

facts.claim: The implicit synchronization barrier does not apply if the streams involved are created with the cudaStreamNonBlocking flag.
facts.note: Non-blocking streams allow operations to proceed concurrently despite NULL stream submissions.

facts.claim: To maximize concurrency, applications should issue independent operations before dependent ones and delay synchronization as long as possible.
facts.note: These guidelines are provided to mitigate the performance impact of implicit synchronization and improve concurrent kernel execution potential.

caveats: The term 'implicit synchronization' here specifically refers to the NULL stream's cross-stream blocking behavior, not general device-host synchronization or explicit stream waits.
The behavior assumes legacy default stream compilation (`--default-stream legacy`), as per-thread default streams behave differently.

concept/implicitly-declared-functions.title: Implicitly-declared and non-virtual explicitly-defaulted functions
concept/implicitly-declared-functions.summary: Rules for determining execution space specifiers (__host__, __device__) for implicitly-declared or defaulted functions based on callers.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence is located in section 18.5.10.2 of the CUDA C++ Programming Guide, which falls under the broader discussion of language features and restrictions for functions. It is positioned between sections detailing external linkage rules and function parameter limitations. The text specifically addresses how the CUDA compiler resolves execution space specifiers (`__host__`, `__device__`) for functions that the programmer did not explicitly define with these specifiers.

facts.claim: For a function F that is either implicitly-declared or a non-virtual function explicitly defaulted on its first declaration, the execution space specifiers are determined by taking the union of the execution space specifiers of all functions that invoke it.
facts.note: A `__global__` caller is treated as a `__device__` caller for this specific analysis.

facts.claim: If F is an implicitly declared virtual function (e.g., a virtual destructor), the execution spaces of each virtual function D overridden by F are added to the set of execution spaces for F, provided D is not implicitly declared.
facts.note: This ensures that implicitly declared virtual destructors inherit the execution space requirements of their non-implicitly-declared overridden counterparts.

caveats: The compiler's behavior diverges from standard C++ in how it handles non-trivially copyable or destructible arguments during kernel launches (e.g., using `memcpy` instead of copy constructors), though this is detailed in the subsequent section 18.5.10.3.1.
The text contains a typo 'explicitlydefaulted' which should be read as 'explicitly defaulted'.
recommended_structure: Definition of the rule for determining execution space specifiers for implicitly-declared and non-virtual explicitly-defaulted functions.
Explanation of the 'union of callers' logic and the special handling of `__global__` callers.
Special rules for implicitly declared virtual functions (e.g., virtual destructors) inheriting execution spaces from overridden functions.
Code examples demonstrating non-virtual inheritance (Derived/Other classes) and virtual inheritance (Base1/Derived1 and Base2/Derived2 structs).

concept/inter-process-communication-unif.title: Inter-Process Communication with Unified Memory
concept/inter-process-communication-unif.summary: Explains that CUDA IPC does not support Managed Memory, but System-Allocated Memory on full-support systems is IPC-capable. Lists Linux mechanisms (mmap MAP_SHARED, POSIX IPC, memfd_create) for sharing memory across processes.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence is section 24.2.1.2, positioned between file-backed unified memory (24.2.1.1) and performance tuning guidelines (24.2.2). It specifically addresses cross-process memory sharing within the Unified Memory programming model, distinguishing between Managed Memory and System-Allocated Memory capabilities.

facts.claim: Using IPC with Unified Memory carries significant performance implications.
facts.note: Performance warning explicitly stated in the source.

facts.claim: Standard CUDA IPC does not support Managed Memory; handles cannot be shared via IPC mechanisms.
facts.note: Clarifies a hard limitation for Managed Memory.

facts.claim: On systems with full CUDA Unified Memory support, System-Allocated Memory is IPC-capable.
facts.note: Provides the viable alternative for cross-process sharing.

facts.claim: Once System-Allocated Memory is shared across processes, the programming model applies similarly to File-backed Unified Memory.
facts.note: Links IPC behavior to the previously discussed file-backed model.

facts.claim: IPC-capable System-Allocated Memory can be created on Linux using mmap with MAP_SHARED, POSIX IPC APIs, or memfd_create.
facts.note: Lists the three supported creation methods.

facts.claim: This technique cannot share memory between different hosts and their devices.
facts.note: Defines the hardware/network scope limitation.

caveats: The source explicitly warns of significant performance implications when using IPC with Unified Memory.
Managed Memory handles are explicitly excluded from IPC sharing; only System-Allocated Memory is supported under full Unified Memory support.

concept/interprocess-communication.title: Interprocess Communication
concept/interprocess-communication.summary: CUDA IPC API allows sharing device memory pointers and events across processes using cudaIpcGetMemHandle() and cudaIpcOpenMemHandle(). Supports 64-bit Linux and compute capability 2.0+. Memory sharing may expose sub-allocations, so 2MiB aligned allocations are recommended. L4T/Tegra devices have limited IPC support (events only for compute 7.x+).

research_reports.context_summary: Section 6.2.11 in the CUDA C Programming Guide's device memory management chapter. It follows Unified Virtual Address Space (L3598-L3609) and precedes Error Checking (L3626-L3637). It establishes the IPC API as the required mechanism for sharing device memory and events across process boundaries, which are otherwise process-local.

facts.claim: Device memory pointers and event handles are process-local and cannot be directly referenced by threads in different processes.
facts.note: Foundational constraint necessitating the IPC API.

facts.claim: The IPC API workflow involves calling cudaIpcGetMemHandle() to obtain a handle, transmitting it via standard IPC mechanisms (e.g., shared memory, files), and using cudaIpcOpenMemHandle() in the target process to retrieve a valid device pointer.
facts.note: Event handles use analogous entry points.

facts.claim: IPC is supported for 64-bit processes on Linux and devices with compute capability 2.0 or higher.
facts.note: Architectural and OS constraints.

facts.claim: cudaMallocManaged allocations are explicitly unsupported by the IPC API.
facts.note: Scope limitation.

facts.claim: Applications communicating via CUDA IPC must be compiled, linked, and run with the identical CUDA driver and runtime.
facts.note: Deployment requirement.

facts.claim: cudaMalloc() allocations may be sub-allocated from larger underlying memory blocks. Sharing a pointer shares the entire block, risking information disclosure between processes.
facts.note: Security caveat.

facts.claim: To prevent unintended sharing of underlying memory blocks, allocations should be 2MiB aligned.
facts.note: Mitigation recommendation.

facts.claim: A common use case is a primary process generating input data and sharing it with multiple secondary processes to avoid regeneration or copying.
facts.note: Typical workload pattern.

facts.claim: On L4T and embedded Linux Tegra devices (compute capability 7.x+), only event-sharing IPC APIs are supported as of CUDA 11.5; memory-sharing IPC remains unsupported.
facts.note: Platform-specific limitation.

caveats: The guide explicitly warns that sharing cudaMalloc pointers may expose unrelated sub-allocations due to internal memory pooling. Writers should highlight the 2MiB alignment recommendation as a security best practice.
Tegra/L4T platforms have a hard limitation on memory-sharing IPC, which may affect cross-platform compatibility claims.
recommended_structure: Platform & Version Constraints (Linux 64-bit, CC 2.0+, Tegra limits)
Security & Alignment (2MiB recommendation, cudaMalloc pooling caveat)

concept/ipc-memory-pools.title: IPC Memory Pools
concept/ipc-memory-pools.summary: Covers the creation, sharing, access control, and limitations of IPC-capable memory pools in CUDA, including handle export/import, setting access permissions, and sharing specific allocations across processes.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence covers the complete cross-process memory sharing workflow using CUDA memory pools. It sits within Chapter 15 (Memory Management), directly following the stream-ordered allocator and reuse policy documentation, and preceding synchronization API actions. The section details the two-phase sharing model, API usage, synchronization requirements, and strict limitations for both exporting and importing processes.

facts.claim: Purpose & Security Model
facts.note: IPC memory pools enable secure, efficient GPU memory sharing across processes, leveraging the same security guarantees as CUDA's virtual memory management APIs.

facts.claim: Two-Phase Sharing Model
facts.note: Sharing occurs in two distinct phases: (1) sharing pool access to establish/enforce security, and (2) sharing specific allocations to coordinate virtual addresses and mapping validity across processes.

facts.claim: Pool Export & Import
facts.note: Exporting processes create a pool via `cudaMemPoolCreate`, set `handleTypes` to a non-zero value in `cudaMemPoolProps` (e.g., `CU_MEM_HANDLE_TYPE_POSIX_FILE_DESCRIPTOR`), and call `cudaMemPoolExportToShareableHandle()`. The OS-native handle is transferred via standard IPC mechanisms and imported via `cudaMemPoolImportFromShareableHandle()`.

facts.claim: Access Control for Imported Pools
facts.note: Imported pools default to resident-device-only access and do not inherit the exporter's accessibility settings. The importing process must explicitly call `cudaMemPoolSetAccess` to grant access to any additional GPUs.

facts.claim: Allocation Sharing & Readiness Synchronization
facts.note: Allocations from `cudaMallocAsync()` are shared using `cudaMemPoolExportPointer()` to get an opaque `cudaMemPoolPtrExportData`. An IPC event (`cudaEventInterprocess`) coordinates readiness: the exporter records the event on the allocating stream, shares the data and event handle, and the importer calls `cudaMemPoolImportPointer()` followed by `cudaStreamWaitEvent` before accessing the memory.

facts.claim: Freeing Synchronization Protocol
facts.note: Allocations must be freed in the importing process *before* the exporting process. IPC events synchronize the `cudaFreeAsync` calls to ensure the exporter waits for the importer's final access before releasing the memory.

facts.claim: Export Pool Limitations
facts.note: IPC export pools cannot release physical memory back to the OS. Consequently, `cudaMemPoolTrimTo` acts as a no-op and `cudaMemPoolAttrReleaseThreshold` is ignored.

facts.claim: Import Pool Limitations
facts.note: Import pools cannot be used for new allocations (cannot be set current or used with `cudaMallocFromPoolAsync`), rendering reuse policy attributes meaningless. `cudaMemPoolTrimTo` is also a no-op, and resource usage stats only reflect imported allocations and their associated physical memory.

caveats: The documentation notes that IPC pool behavior regarding physical memory release is driver-controlled and subject to change in future updates.
Stream-ordered allocator reuse policies (`cudaMemPoolReuseFollowEventDependencies`, etc.) are explicitly noted as meaningless for import pools.
All cross-process synchronization relies on CUDA IPC events (`cudaEventInterprocess`) to maintain stream ordering semantics across process boundaries.

concept/just-in-time-compilation.title: Just-in-Time Compilation
concept/just-in-time-compilation.summary: Explains how PTX code is compiled to binary at runtime by the device driver, the benefits of JIT compilation, the compute cache mechanism, and its invalidation policy.

research_reports.context_summary: The concept is introduced in the CUDA C Programming Guide under the 'Compilation with NVCC' section (6.1.1.2), positioned between offline compilation workflows and binary/PTX compatibility discussions. It outlines the runtime compilation pipeline where intermediate representations are converted to device-specific binaries.

facts.claim: Definition and Mechanism
facts.note: JIT compilation occurs when the device driver compiles PTX code loaded by an application at runtime into executable binary code.

facts.claim: Performance and Compatibility Trade-offs
facts.note: JIT compilation increases application load time but allows applications to benefit from new compiler improvements in updated device drivers. It is also the only method to run applications on GPU architectures that did not exist at compile time.

facts.claim: Caching and Invalidation
facts.note: The device driver automatically caches the generated binary in a 'compute cache' to avoid recompilation on subsequent runs. This cache is automatically invalidated when the device driver is upgraded, ensuring applications use the new JIT compiler.

facts.claim: Configuration and Alternatives
facts.note: JIT compilation behavior can be controlled via environment variables. NVRTC is also available as an alternative runtime compilation library to compile CUDA C++ device code directly to PTX at runtime, bypassing offline `nvcc` compilation.

caveats: The guide notes that JIT compilation increases load time, which is a direct trade-off for its flexibility and forward compatibility.
contradictions: None detected within the assigned evidence.

concept/l2-cache-access-management.title: L2 Cache Access Management
concept/l2-cache-access-management.summary: Describes L2 cache set-aside for persisting accesses, access policy windows, hit ratios, access properties (streaming, persisting, normal), and API functions for querying and controlling L2 cache behavior.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence covers the complete subsection on L2 Cache Access Management within the CUDA C Programming Guide's Device Memory chapter. It details how developers can explicitly control data persistence in the L2 cache to optimize global memory bandwidth and latency, specifically for compute capability 8.0+ devices.

facts.claim: L2 Cache Access Management distinguishes between 'persisting' (repeatedly accessed) and 'streaming' (single-use) global memory accesses to optimize bandwidth and latency.
facts.note: Available starting with CUDA 11.0 on devices with compute capability 8.0 and above.

facts.claim: A configurable portion of the L2 cache can be reserved exclusively for persisting accesses. Persisting accesses get priority, while normal/streaming accesses only use this reserved portion when it is idle.
facts.note: The set-aside size is controlled via `cudaDeviceSetLimit(cudaLimitPersistingL2CacheSize, size)`, capped by `persistingL2CacheMaxSize` from device properties.

facts.claim: The feature is disabled in Multi-Instance GPU (MIG) mode. In Multi-Process Service (MPS) mode, the size must be set at server startup via the environment variable `CUDA_DEVICE_DEFAULT_PERSISTING_L2_CACHE_PERCENTAGE_LIMIT` rather than at runtime.
facts.note: An access policy window defines a contiguous global memory region and assigns persistence properties to accesses within it. It can be applied at the CUDA Stream level (`cudaStreamAttributeAccessPolicyWindow`) or at the CUDA Graph Kernel Node level (`cudaKernelNodeAttributeAccessPolicyWindow`).

facts.claim: The `hitRatio` parameter (0.0 to 1.0) dictates the probability that an access within the window receives the `hitProp` property (e.g., `cudaAccessPropertyPersisting`). This probabilistic assignment helps avoid cache thrashing when multiple concurrent streams compete for the limited set-aside cache.
facts.note: Three access properties are defined: `cudaAccessPropertyPersisting` (preferentially retained), `cudaAccessPropertyStreaming` (preferentially evicted), and `cudaAccessPropertyNormal` (resets previous persisting properties to normal status, freeing up set-aside cache).

facts.claim: Persisting cache lines can persist long after use, so explicit resets are recommended. Methods include applying `cudaAccessPropertyNormal` to a window, calling `cudaCtxResetPersistingL2Cache()` to clear all persisting lines, or relying on automatic reset (discouraged due to unpredictability).
facts.note: Developers can query relevant L2 properties (total size, max set-aside size, max window size) via the `cudaDeviceProp` struct using `cudaGetDeviceProperties`.

facts.claim: Managing set-aside utilization requires balancing the cache size against the number of concurrent kernels, their access policy window sizes, and reset timing to prevent resource starvation.
facts.note: The L2 set-aside cache size is queried using `cudaDeviceGetLimit` and set using `cudaDeviceSetLimit` as a `cudaLimit`. The maximum value is `cudaDeviceProp::persistingL2CacheMaxSize`.

caveats: The evidence is strictly scoped to compute capability 8.0+ and CUDA 11.0+. No backward compatibility notes are provided.
MIG mode completely disables the feature; MPS mode restricts runtime configuration.
Automatic cache reset is explicitly discouraged due to undetermined timing.
contradictions: None detected. The source is a single, cohesive documentation section.

concept/launch-bounds.title: 10.38. Launch Bounds
concept/launch-bounds.summary: Covers the __launch_bounds__() qualifier, its parameters, compiler heuristics for register usage optimization, and architecture-specific handling via __CUDA_ARCH__.

research_reports.scope: Section 10.38 sits between Execution Configuration (10.37) and Maximum Number of Registers per Thread (10.39), acting as the bridge between kernel launch syntax and low-level register tuning.
research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence provides the complete specification for the `__launch_bounds__()` qualifier, detailing its parameters, compiler optimization logic, launch constraints, and architecture-specific usage patterns.

facts.claim: Purpose & Mechanism
facts.note: Launch bounds aid compiler heuristics to minimize register usage, thereby increasing the number of resident threads/blocks per multiprocessor to improve performance.

facts.claim: Syntax & Parameters
facts.note: Qualifier: `__launch_bounds__(maxThreadsPerBlock, minBlocksPerMultiprocessor, maxBlocksPerCluster)`. `maxThreadsPerBlock` compiles to `.maxntid`. `minBlocksPerMultiprocessor` (optional) compiles to `.minnctapersm`. `maxBlocksPerCluster` (optional) compiles to `.maxclusterrank`.

facts.claim: Compiler Optimization Logic
facts.note: Compiler derives an upper register limit `L` to ensure `minBlocksPerMultiprocessor` blocks of `maxThreadsPerBlock` threads can reside on an SM. If initial usage > L, registers are reduced (often increasing local memory/instructions). If both bounds are specified, registers may be increased up to L to reduce instruction count and hide latency. If only `maxThreadsPerBlock` is given, it sets thresholds for n→n+1 block transitions.

facts.claim: Launch Constraints & Errors
facts.note: Kernel launch fails if executed with more threads per block than `maxThreadsPerBlock`, or more thread blocks per cluster than `maxBlocksPerCluster`.

facts.claim: Best Practices & Forward Compatibility
facts.note: Using single-argument `__launch_bounds__(maxThreadsPerBlock)` ensures at least one block can run on an SM and prevents 'too many resources requested for launch' errors. Two-argument form requires per-kernel analysis. Optimal bounds vary by architecture.

facts.claim: Architecture-Specific Handling
facts.note: Device code should use `__CUDA_ARCH__` macro to define architecture-specific bounds (e.g., different max threads/min blocks for compute capability >= 2.0 vs older).

facts.claim: Host Code Caveat
facts.note: `__CUDA_ARCH__` is undefined in host code. Using architecture-dependent macros directly in the execution configuration `<<<grid, block>>>` will fall back to the preprocessor default. Host code must use `__CUDA_ARCH__`-independent macros or query `cudaGetDeviceProperties` at runtime to match the device's compute capability.

facts.claim: Diagnostics
facts.note: Register usage is reported via `--ptxas-options=-v`. Resident block count is derived from occupancy metrics in the CUDA profiler.

caveats: The source text contains a truncated bullet point under compiler optimization logic ('▶ If the initial register usage is lower than L' at L11624), but the subsequent bullet clarifies the behavior when both bounds are specified.
Host code cannot use `__CUDA_ARCH__` macros for launch configuration; runtime property queries or architecture-agnostic compile-time macros are required.
related_terms: Maximum Number of Registers per Thread

concept/lazy-loading.title: Lazy Loading in CUDA
concept/lazy-loading.summary: Covers the entire Chapter 25 on Lazy Loading, including its purpose, version support (Driver/Toolkit/Compiler), triggering mechanisms, querying the loading mode, and potential issues like concurrent execution deadlocks, VRAM allocation conflicts, and autotuning warmup requirements.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence covers Chapter 25 of the CUDA C Programming Guide, detailing the Lazy Loading feature. It explains the mechanism, version requirements, triggering methods, and common pitfalls like deadlocks and memory allocation conflicts. The chapter is explicitly marked as legacy (replaced in CUDA 13.0+).

facts.claim: Lazy Loading delays module and kernel loading from program initialization to execution time, reducing startup latency and memory overhead (host & GPU) by only loading kernels that are actually invoked.
facts.note: Core definition and primary benefit.

facts.claim: Enabled globally by setting the CUDA_MODULE_LOADING environment variable to LAZY.
facts.note: Configuration method.

facts.claim: Driver requires R515+ user-mode library. Supports forward compatibility with older kernel-mode drivers. Without R515+, the feature is unavailable regardless of toolkit version.
facts.note: Driver compatibility constraint.

facts.claim: Toolkit: Introduced in 11.7, significantly upgraded in 11.8. Applications must be compiled with a 11.7+ toolkit and link 11.7+ libraries to benefit. Partial upgrades only apply lazy loading to the upgraded libraries.
facts.note: Toolkit & library versioning.

facts.claim: Compiler: No special compiler support required. Pre-11.7 SASS and PTX binaries are fully compatible.
facts.note: Compiler compatibility.

facts.claim: Runtime Optimization (11.8+): Skips loading all modules at init (except those containing managed variables). Modules load on first variable/kernel usage. Driver users using cuLibraryLoad can control this via CUDA_MODULE_DATA_LOADING.
facts.note: Runtime-specific behavior.

facts.claim: Module/Kernel Delay (11.7+): cuModuleLoad*() delays actual kernel loading until cuModuleGetFunction() is called. CUDA Runtime automatically triggers this on first kernel reference. Exceptions include kernels with pointers stored in global variables.
facts.note: Driver/Runtime shared behavior.

facts.claim: Explicit Preloading: To load a kernel without execution, call cuModuleGetFunction() (Driver API) or cudaFuncGetAttributes() (Runtime API).
facts.note: Manual triggering methods.

facts.claim: cuModuleGetLoadingMode() returns the current loading mode (CU_MODULE_LAZY_LOADING or CU_MODULE_EAGER_LOADING), but requires cuInit() to be called first.
facts.note: State querying API.

facts.claim: Concurrent Execution Deadlocks: Lazy loading may trigger context synchronization. If Kernel A spins waiting for Kernel B, launching B can deadlock while waiting for A to finish synchronization. Mitigation: preload concurrent kernels or set CUDA_MODULE_DATA_LOADING=EAGER.
facts.note: Concurrency caveat.

facts.claim: VRAM Allocator Conflicts: Delayed loading shifts memory allocation to launch time. Greedy VRAM allocation at startup can cause CUDA allocation failures. Mitigation: use cudaMallocAsync(), reserve a memory buffer, or preload kernels before allocator initialization.
facts.note: Memory management caveat.

facts.claim: Autotuning Skew: Benchmarking includes kernel load time, skewing results. Mitigation: run at least one warmup iteration or preload the benchmarked kernel before measurement.
facts.note: Performance measurement caveat.

facts.claim: The source document is marked as legacy and replaced by the new CUDA Programming Guide as of CUDA 13.0.
facts.note: Document status warning.

caveats: The assigned evidence is explicitly marked as legacy (CUDA 13.0+ replacement). The lead writer should verify if the new guide has superseded these specifics or if they remain accurate for historical context.

Lazy loading behavior differs slightly between CUDA Runtime and Driver APIs, particularly regarding `cuModuleLoad*()` vs `cuLibraryLoad()` and environment variable names (`CUDA_MODULE_LOADING` vs `CUDA_MODULE_DATA_LOADING`).
Deadlock and memory allocation issues are tied to non-standard programming patterns (e.g., spinning waits, greedy VRAM allocation). Standard CUDA programming model usage should remain unaffected.

concept/load-functions-using-cache-hints.title: Load Functions Using Cache Hints
concept/load-functions-using-cache-hints.summary: Reference for cache-hinted load intrinsics (__ldcg, __ldca, __ldcs, __ldlu, __ldcv) that control cache behavior for global memory reads, supporting various data types and requiring compute capability 5.0 or higher.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence is located in Section 10.11 of the CUDA C Programming Guide. It is positioned between the read-only data cache load function (`__ldg`) in Section 10.10 and the store functions using cache hints in Section 10.12.

facts.claim: Load functions using cache hints are supported only on devices with compute capability 5.0 and higher.
facts.note: This aligns with the hardware requirements for the read-only data cache load function (`__ldg`) in the adjacent section.

facts.claim: The available load functions using cache hints are `__ldcg`, `__ldca`, `__ldcs`, `__ldlu`, and `__ldcv`.
facts.note: All functions take a `const T* address` parameter and return a value of type `T`.

facts.claim: These functions return the data of type `T` located at the specified address.
facts.note: The operation utilizes the corresponding cache operator as defined in the PTX ISA.

facts.claim: Supported data types (`T`) include basic integer types (char, short, int, long, and their unsigned/signed variants), vector types (e.g., int2, float4, double2), and floating-point types (float, double).
facts.note: The source text lists specific vector types such as char2, char4, short2, short4, int2, int4, longlong2, uchar2, uchar4, ushort2, ushort4, uint2, uint4, ulonglong2, float, float2, float4, double, and double2.

facts.claim: Half-precision floating-point types (`__half`, `__half2`) are supported if the `cuda_fp16.h` header is included.
facts.note: Similarly, bfloat16 types (`__nv_bfloat16`, `__nv_bfloat162`) are supported if the `cuda_bf16.h` header is included.

caveats: The source text contains a typographical omission in the list of vector types, writing `longlong2uchar2` and `ulonglong2float` without spaces. The lead writer should ensure these are formatted correctly as `longlong2`, `uchar2`, `ulonglong2`, and `float` in the final page.
recommended_structure: Overview of cache hint load functions
Supported data types (primitives, vectors, half, bfloat16)

concept/managed-memory-direct-access.title: Managed Memory Direct Access & System Allocator vs Managed Memory Behavior
concept/managed-memory-direct-access.summary: Covers the difference between system-allocated memory and managed memory, and how the directManagedMemAccessFromHost hardware feature affects CPU/GPU memory migration and faulting behavior.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence (L21681-L21749) provides parallel code examples demonstrating that both System Allocator memory (via `malloc`) and CUDA Managed Memory (via `cudaMallocManaged`) exhibit identical direct access behaviors when the `cudaMemAdviseSetAccessedBy` hint is applied. The surrounding context (L21675-L21677, L21647-L21673) explains that this direct access is a feature of hardware-coherent systems (combined CPU/GPU page tables) and avoids the page faults required by software-coherent systems.

facts.claim: Direct Managed Memory Access from Host allows the host to read, write, and perform atomic operations on GPU-resident unified memory without triggering page faults or data migration.
facts.note: This capability is indicated by the device attribute `cudaDevAttrDirectManagedMemAccessFromHost` being set to 1, which is true for all hardware-coherent systems (e.g., NVLink-connected devices). To enable this direct access, the application must set the `cudaMemAdviseSetAccessedBy` hint with `cudaMemLocationTypeHost` on the memory pointer.

facts.claim: System Allocator memory (allocated via `malloc`) and CUDA Managed Memory (allocated via `cudaMallocManaged`) exhibit identical direct access behavior when the `cudaMemAdviseSetAccessedBy` hint is applied.
facts.note: Both allocation methods require the `cudaMemAdviseSetAccessedBy` hint with `cudaMemLocationTypeHost` to enable direct access on systems where it is not the default.

facts.claim: On systems with `directManagedMemAccessFromHost=1` (hardware-coherent): CPU accesses to the buffer do not trigger migrations; data remains resident in GPU memory. Subsequent GPU kernel accesses occur directly without faults or migrations.
facts.note: This avoids the performance penalties associated with page faults and data migration.

facts.claim: On systems with `directManagedMemAccessFromHost=0` (software-coherent): CPU accesses trigger a page fault and initiate device-to-host migration. Subsequent GPU kernel accesses will trigger host-to-device migration.
facts.note: This behavior is characteristic of systems with separate CPU and GPU page tables that must use page faults to emulate coherency.

caveats: The `cudaMemAdviseSetAccessedBy` hint is necessary to enable direct access without page faults on systems where it is not the default behavior.

Direct access without migration is a characteristic of hardware-coherent systems (combined CPU/GPU page tables), avoiding the page faults required by software-coherent systems (separate page tables) to emulate coherency.
recommended_structure: Direct Managed Memory Access from Host
System Allocator vs. Managed Memory Behavior
Code Examples (System Allocator and Managed Memory)

concept/managed-memory-space-specifier.title: __managed__ Memory Space Specifier
concept/managed-memory-space-specifier.summary: Restrictions and examples for __managed__ variables, including const/reference limitations, runtime state dependencies, and multi-GPU allocation behavior.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence covers Section 18.5.3.2, detailing the specific compiler restrictions, runtime safety rules, and linkage requirements for variables declared with the `__managed__` memory space specifier.

facts.claim: Managed variables share coherence and consistency behavior with dynamically allocated managed memory.
facts.note: On multi-GPU platforms, managed variables are allocated exactly once for the system, not per GPU.

facts.claim: Managed variables cannot have a `const` qualified type or a reference type.
facts.note: The address of a managed variable is not a constant expression.

facts.claim: Accessing the address or value of a managed variable is prohibited when the CUDA runtime may not be in a valid state.
facts.note: This includes static/dynamic initialization or destruction of objects with static or thread-local storage duration, code after `exit()` (e.g., `__attribute__((destructor))`), and code before CUDA runtime initialization (e.g., `__attribute__((constructor))`).

facts.claim: Managed variables cannot be used as an unparenthesized id-expression argument to `decltype()`.
facts.note: Parenthesizing the expression (e.g., `decltype((xxx))`) is permitted.

facts.claim: Linkage and scope restrictions apply to `__managed__` declarations.
facts.note: Declarations without `extern` linkage are forbidden inside host functions. Declarations without `extern` or `static` linkage are forbidden inside device functions.

caveats: The compiler does not guarantee diagnostics for all unsupported uses of `__managed__` in certain contexts, though explicit restrictions are listed.
Managed variables cannot be used in template non-type arguments requiring constant expressions (e.g., `template <int *addr> struct S3_t { }; S3_t<&xxx> temp;` is an error).

concept/managed-memory-v1-windows-cc5x.title: Unified Memory on Windows or Compute Capability 5.x
concept/managed-memory-v1-windows-cc5x.summary: Covers Managed Memory v1.0 support on Windows and devices with compute capability lower than 6.0, highlighting limited data migration, coherency, and oversubscription support.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence (L21826-L21828) is the introductory section (24.3.2) for a specific subset of Unified Memory support. It is categorized under the broader topic of 'Unified memory on devices without full CUDA Unified Memory support' (L21816). It explicitly contrasts the limited v1.0 support on Windows/CC5.x with the fully coherent Managed Memory available on CC 6.x+ devices (L21818).

facts.claim: Unified memory on Windows or devices with compute capability 5.x implements CUDA Managed Memory v1.0.
facts.note: This mode is active on Windows platforms and devices with a compute capability lower than 6.0.

facts.claim: It features limited support for data migration, coherency, and memory oversubscription.
facts.note: The subsequent subsections in the source document detail how to use and optimize Managed Memory on these platforms.

caveats: The source text uses the term 'compute capability 5.x' in the section header but clarifies in the body that it applies to 'compute capability lower than 6.0'.
recommended_structure: Introduction to Managed Memory v1.0 on Windows/CC5.x
Usage and Optimization Guidelines (referencing subsequent subsections)

concept/mathematical-functions.title: Mathematical Functions
concept/mathematical-functions.summary: Introductory reference to C/C++ standard library mathematical functions and CUDA device intrinsic mathematical functions supported in device code.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence is a two-sentence introductory paragraph for Section 10.7 'Mathematical Functions' within the CUDA C Programming Guide. It sits between the 'Synchronization Functions' section (covering `__syncthreads` and `__syncwarp`) and the 'Texture Functions' section.

facts.claim: The CUDA guide's Mathematical Functions section catalogs C/C++ standard library math functions supported in device code, plus CUDA-specific device intrinsics.
facts.note: Serves as a high-level reference header rather than a detailed mathematical definition page.

facts.claim: The documentation explicitly states that accuracy information is provided for these functions where relevant.
facts.note: Highlights that CUDA math functions may have device-specific precision characteristics compared to host CPU implementations.

caveats: The source text is purely a section header/intro; it does not list specific functions, parameters, or precision guarantees. The target page should reflect that CUDA's mathematical functions are a device-side subset of standard C/C++ libraries with added intrinsics.
recommended_structure: Overview of CUDA Math Functions (device-side C/C++ standard library subset)
Reference to full function list in CUDA C Programming Guide

concept/mathematical-functions-accuracy.title: Mathematical Functions Accuracy
concept/mathematical-functions-accuracy.summary: Accuracy bounds (ULP errors) for standard and intrinsic mathematical functions across single, double, and quad precision.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned document (CUDA C Programming Guide, legacy as of CUDA 13.0) details the accuracy guarantees for mathematical functions executed on NVIDIA GPUs. It quantifies accuracy using ULP (Unit in the Last Place) and provides extensive tables of maximum ULP errors for single, double, and quad-precision standard and intrinsic functions. It also covers compiler flags (`-use_fast_math`, `-prec-div`) that trade accuracy for performance, and notes that device functions do not set `errno` or report floating-point exceptions.

facts.claim: Accuracy is quantified using ULP (Unit in the Last Place). Error bounds are derived from extensive but non-exhaustive testing, meaning they are not mathematically guaranteed bounds.
facts.note: Device mathematical functions do not set `errno` or report floating-point exceptions. Users must implement custom input/output screening. Passing uninitialized parameters causes undefined behavior due to function inlining and compiler optimizations.

facts.claim: IEEE-compliant operations (`+`, `*`) have a max error of 0.5 ulp. Division and square root accuracy depend on compute capability and compiler flags (e.g., `-prec-div=true`, `-prec-sqrt=true`). Transcendental functions have bounded ULP errors (e.g., `expf` 2, `logf` 1, `sinf` 2, `powf` 4, `erf` 2). Functions using fast algorithms (e.g., `normf`, `rnormf`) have no provided error bound due to round-off loss.
facts.note: IEEE-compliant operations (`+`, `*`, `/`, `1/x`, `sqrt`) have 0 ulp error. Transcendental functions generally have low ULP bounds (e.g., `exp` 1, `sin` 2, `pow` 2, `erf` 2, `tgamma` 10).

facts.claim: Available for compute capability 10.0+. Basic operations and rounding functions are IEEE-compliant (0 ulp). Transcendental functions like `__nv_fp128_sin` and `__nv_fp128_exp` have a maximum ULP error of 1.
facts.note: Intrinsic functions (prefixed with `__`) are faster but less accurate, mapping to fewer native instructions. The `-use_fast_math` compiler flag forces standard functions to compile to their intrinsic counterparts, which may alter special case handling.

facts.claim: The `-prec-div` flag controls division accuracy. With `-prec-div=false`, `__fdividef` and `/` share accuracy for most ranges, but diverge for $2^{126} < |y| < 2^{128}$ (where `__fdividef` returns 0 or NaN incorrectly). Default is IEEE-compliant.
facts.note: `rintf()`/`rint()` are recommended over `roundf()`/`round()` for device code because they map to single instructions, whereas `round` maps to multi-instruction sequences.

facts.claim: Single-precision intrinsics like `__expf` have variable ULP errors (`2 + floor(abs(1.173 * x))`), while `__logf` and `__sinf` have specific absolute/ULP bounds depending on the input range. Double-precision intrinsics (`__dadd`, `__dsub`, `__dmul`, `__fma`, `__ddiv`, `__drcp`, `__dsqrt`) are IEEE-compliant on compute capability > 2.
facts.note: None

caveats: The source document is marked as legacy (replaced by the new CUDA Programming Guide as of CUDA 13.0). Accuracy tables and compiler flag behaviors may have evolved.
Error bounds are empirical, not theoretical guarantees. Actual accuracy may vary based on input ranges, compiler versions, and specific GPU architectures.
Intrinsic functions and `-use_fast_math` explicitly sacrifice accuracy and special-case handling for performance; they should be used selectively.
contradictions: None detected within the assigned context. The document consistently separates standard functions (higher accuracy, compiler-optimized) from intrinsics (lower accuracy, hardware-mapped) and clarifies IEEE compliance conditions.
recommended_structure: Overview of Accuracy Measurement (ULP, empirical bounds, lack of errno)
Standard Functions by Precision (Single, Double, Quad tables/summaries)
Compiler Flags & Architecture Dependencies (`-prec-div`, compute capability requirements)
Best Practices (e.g., `rint` vs `round`, selective intrinsic usage)

concept/maximize-instruction-throughput.title: Maximize Instruction Throughput
concept/maximize-instruction-throughput.summary: Brief section directing readers to the CUDA C++ Best Practices Guide for instruction throughput optimization details.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence is a standalone section header and a single cross-reference sentence. It appears immediately after detailed subsections explaining how memory access patterns (coalescing, bank conflicts, caching) across Global, Local, Shared, Constant, and Texture/Surface memory directly impact instruction throughput.

facts.claim: The document does not list specific instruction throughput optimizations here; instead, it explicitly directs readers to the CUDA C++ Best Practices Guide for those details.
facts.note: Serves as a redirect rather than a technical deep-dive. The preceding sections already established that memory distribution, alignment, and coalescing are the primary drivers of instruction throughput, making this section a logical conclusion to the memory access chapter.

facts.claim: The document is marked as legacy and superseded by the new CUDA Programming Guide as of CUDA 13.0.
facts.note: Suggests the guidance or cross-reference may be historical. The lead writer should verify if the CUDA C++ Best Practices Guide has also been updated or merged in newer documentation.

caveats: The section contains no technical content beyond the redirect. Any expansion of this wiki page will require external sources or the referenced Best Practices Guide.

Legacy status warning applies to the broader document structure; ensure the lead writer checks for superseded terminology or architecture-specific details (e.g., Compute Capability 5.x–12.0) in the surrounding context.
recommended_structure: Direct to CUDA C++ Best Practices Guide as the primary source
Briefly note that memory access patterns (coalescing, alignment, caching) are the foundational drivers of instruction throughput in this context
Flag legacy status of the source document

concept/maximize-memory-throughput.title: Maximize Memory Throughput
concept/maximize-memory-throughput.summary: Outlines the strategy for maximizing memory throughput by minimizing low-bandwidth transfers, leveraging on-chip memory (shared memory, L1/L2 caches), and optimizing global memory access patterns for coalescing.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence forms the introductory section for Chapter 8.3. It is preceded by occupancy calculator API documentation (L6251-L6370) and immediately followed by detailed subsections on host-device transfers (L6395-L6406) and device memory access patterns for specific memory types (L6407-L6514).

facts.claim: Maximizing memory throughput follows a two-step hierarchy: first, minimize low-bandwidth data transfers between the host and device; second, minimize transfers between global memory and the device by maximizing on-chip memory utilization (shared memory, L1/L2 caches, texture/constant caches).
facts.note: Shared memory acts as a user-managed cache. The standard pattern involves staging data from device memory into shared memory, synchronizing threads, processing data, synchronizing again if necessary, and writing results back to device memory.

facts.claim: For applications with data-dependent access patterns, hardware-managed caches are preferred to exploit data locality.
facts.note: On compute capability 7.x, 8.x, and 9.0 devices, the same on-chip memory pool is partitioned between L1 cache and shared memory, with the allocation ratio configurable per kernel launch.

facts.claim: Memory access throughput can vary by an order of magnitude depending on the access pattern employed for each memory type.
facts.note: Optimizing access patterns is critical, especially for global memory, because its bandwidth is significantly lower than on-chip bandwidths and arithmetic throughput. Non-optimal global memory accesses typically cause severe performance degradation.

caveats: The text explicitly notes that shared memory usage requires explicit synchronization (`__syncthreads()`) between load, process, and store phases to ensure data consistency across threads within a block.
recommended_structure: Overview of the two-step throughput maximization strategy
On-chip memory utilization (shared memory staging pattern, hardware cache fallback, L1/shared partitioning)
Access pattern optimization importance (global memory bandwidth bottleneck)
Cross-reference to detailed subsections on host-device transfers and specific device memory types

concept/maxnreg-qualifier.title: 10.39. Maximum Number of Registers per Thread
concept/maxnreg-qualifier.summary: Covers the __maxnreg__() qualifier for limiting register allocation per thread, its incompatibility with __launch_bounds__(), and the maxrregcount compiler option.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence falls within a series of compiler-level performance tuning directives in the CUDA C++ Programming Guide. It is positioned between Section 10.38 (Launch Bounds / `__launch_bounds__`) and Section 10.40 (`#pragma unroll`), indicating its role as a low-level register management tool alongside other optimization qualifiers.

facts.claim: The `__maxnreg__()` qualifier is a CUDA C++ function-level directive used for low-level performance tuning.
facts.note: Specifies the maximum number of registers to allocate to a single thread in a thread block.

facts.claim: Syntax and PTX mapping
facts.note: Applied to `__global__` functions. Compiles to the `.maxnreg` PTX directive.

facts.claim: Mutual exclusivity
facts.note: Cannot be applied to the same kernel as `__launch_bounds__()`.

facts.claim: Interaction with compiler options
facts.note: The `maxrregcount` compiler option controls register usage for all `__global__` functions in a file, but its value is ignored for functions marked with `__maxnreg__()`.

caveats: The documentation does not specify the valid range of values for `maxNumberRegistersPerThread` or hardware-specific register limits. Developers should consult hardware architecture guides for maximum register counts per thread.
contradictions: None detected. The scope aligns perfectly with the target page slug and title.

concept/memory-discarding-cuda.title: Memory Discarding in CUDA
concept/memory-discarding-cuda.summary: Details cudaMemDiscardBatchAsync and cudaMemDiscardAndPrefetchBatchAsync APIs. Explains how marking memory ranges as discarded prevents redundant migrations during faults/evictions, improving performance. Includes important considerations regarding indeterminate reads and concurrent access.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence falls under section 24.1.2.8.2 'Memory Discarding' within the broader 'Performance Hints' chapter for CUDA Unified Memory. It is positioned between Data Prefetching (24.1.2.8.1) and Data Usage Hints (21.2.8.3), framing memory discarding as a performance optimization technique rather than a semantic guarantee.

facts.claim: Purpose and Mechanism
facts.note: Informs the Unified Memory driver that specified memory ranges are no longer useful, preventing redundant automatic transfers (fault-based migration or evictions) that occur for oversubscription, thereby improving performance.

facts.claim: Primary API: cudaMemDiscardBatchAsync
facts.note: Performs a batch of memory discards. Requires `dptrs` and `sizes` arrays of equal length to `count`. Ranges must be managed memory (`cudaMallocManaged` or `__managed__`).

facts.claim: Combined API: cudaMemDiscardAndPrefetchBatchAsync
facts.note: Combines discard and prefetch in a single, more optimal call. Useful when memory must reside on a target location but its contents are irrelevant. Uses `prefetchLocs` and `prefetchLocIdxs` to map batch operations to destination locations.

facts.claim: Read/Write Semantics
facts.note: Reading a discarded page without a subsequent write/prefetch yields an indeterminate value. New writes after discard are guaranteed visible to subsequent reads. The discard can be undone by writing to the range or prefetching it.

facts.claim: Concurrency & Hardware Constraints
facts.note: Concurrent accesses/prefetches to ranges being discarded cause undefined behavior. All devices involved must report a non-zero value for `cudaDevAttrConcurrentManagedAccess`.

caveats: The feature is strictly a performance hint; removing it does not change application semantics [CUDA_C_Programming_Guide:L21195].
Undefined behavior is explicitly triggered if reads, writes, or prefetches occur simultaneously with the discard operation [CUDA_C_Programming_Guide:L21301].

concept/memory-fences.title: Memory Fences and Synchronization Example
concept/memory-fences.summary: Code example and explanation demonstrating the use of __threadfence(), atomic operations, and __syncthreads() to coordinate block completion and ensure memory visibility across threads.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence details a parallel reduction/summation kernel example to demonstrate memory fence usage. It highlights a race condition where an atomic completion counter might increment before partial sums are written to global memory, and shows how `__threadfence()` resolves the ordering issue while `volatile` and `__syncthreads()` handle visibility and intra-block synchronization.

facts.claim: The example solves a multi-block parallel summation problem where blocks store partial results in global memory and signal completion via an atomic counter.
facts.note: Without a fence, the atomic counter increment could precede the global memory store, allowing the final block to read stale partial sums.

facts.claim: `__threadfence()` is placed immediately after writing the partial sum and before the atomic counter increment to enforce store ordering.
facts.note: This ensures the counter only increments after the partial sum is guaranteed to be visible in the memory order.

facts.claim: Memory fences enforce operation ordering but do not guarantee cross-thread visibility; visibility is handled separately via `volatile` qualifiers and `__syncthreads()`.
facts.note: The `result` pointer is declared `volatile` to bypass L1 cache, and `__syncthreads()` synchronizes threads reading the completion flag.

facts.claim: The kernel execution flow strictly follows: store partial sum → `__threadfence()` → atomic counter increment → `__syncthreads()` → check completion flag → compute/finalize.
facts.note: Thread 0 of each block handles the fence/atomic logic, while all threads synchronize before checking the `isLastBlockDone` flag.

caveats: The example assumes `volatile` is used on the `result` pointer to force bypass of the L1 cache; omitting this could still cause visibility issues despite the fence.
The fence only orders operations for the calling thread; other threads must still rely on `__syncthreads()` or atomic operations to observe the changes.
recommended_structure: Problem Statement: Race condition between partial sum storage and completion counter.
Solution: Fence placement between store and atomic increment.
Visibility vs. Ordering: Distinction between fence scope and cache/visibility mechanisms.
Code Walkthrough: Step-by-step execution flow of the parallel summation kernel.

concept/memory-mapping.title: Mapping Memory
concept/memory-mapping.summary: Details cuMemMap to associate physical allocations with reserved virtual address ranges, and cuMemUnmap for decoupling.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence (Section 14.6) defines 'Mapping Memory' as the process of associating a reserved virtual address range with a physical memory allocation within CUDA's Virtual Memory Management (VMM) APIs. It details the `cuMemMap` function, its prerequisites, and its lifecycle management capabilities.

facts.claim: Mapping memory is the process of placing allocated physical memory into a reserved virtual address space to make it usable.
facts.note: This step bridges the distinction between physical memory and virtual address space introduced by VMM.

facts.claim: The association between a virtual address range and a physical allocation is performed using the `cuMemMap` function.
facts.note: This function takes a pointer from `cuMemAddressReserve` and a handle from `cuMemCreate` or `cuMemImportFromShareableHandle`.

facts.claim: Mappings can associate allocations from multiple devices to reside in contiguous virtual address ranges.
facts.note: This requires the application to have carved out sufficient virtual address space beforehand.

facts.claim: Physical allocations and address ranges can be decoupled using `cuMemUnmap`.
facts.note: Memory can be mapped and unmapped to the same address range multiple times, provided the virtual address range is not already mapped.

facts.claim: The standard usage signature is `CUresult result = cuMemMap(ptr, size, 0, allocHandle, 0);`.
facts.note: `ptr` is the address in the reserved range, and `allocHandle` is the physical allocation handle.

caveats: Mapping memory does not automatically make the address accessible to CUDA kernels; explicit access rights must be set using `cuMemSetAccess` (see Section 14.7 in context).
recommended_structure: Definition of Mapping Memory in VMM

concept/memory-paging-page-sizes.title: Memory Paging and Page Sizes
concept/memory-paging-page-sizes.summary: Defines virtual vs. physical pages, page sizes (4KiB, 2MiB, etc.), page tables, and TLBs. Explains how virtual addressing and paging mechanics impact Unified Memory performance tuning.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence is extracted from Section 24.2.2.1 'Memory Paging and Page Sizes', which serves as a foundational primer for Unified Memory performance tuning. It defines core virtual memory concepts, details hardware-specific page size defaults, and outlines how page table architecture and virtual page sizing impact performance.

facts.claim: Unified Memory systems rely on a virtual address space where application addresses map to physical locations.
facts.note: Foundational premise for paging.

facts.claim: Two distinct page types exist: Virtual pages (OS-tracked, fixed-size contiguous virtual memory chunks) and Physical pages (MMU-supported fixed-size contiguous memory chunks).
facts.note: Distinguishes OS-level tracking from hardware MMU capabilities.

facts.claim: Physical page size defaults vary by architecture: x86_64 CPUs use 4KiB; ARM CPUs support 4KiB, 16KiB, 32KiB, or 64KiB; NVIDIA GPUs prefer 2MiB or larger.
facts.note: Hardware-specific constraints subject to future changes.

facts.claim: Virtual page sizes typically match physical page sizes by default but can differ if supported by the OS and hardware, provided they are powers of 2 and multiples of the physical page size.
facts.note: Application-level tunability within hardware/OS limits.

facts.claim: Page tables track virtual-to-physical mappings via Page Table Entries (PTEs), while Translation Lookaside Buffers (TLBs) cache these mappings to accelerate address translation.
facts.note: Core mechanism for virtual address resolution.

facts.claim: Performance tuning focuses on two key factors: the chosen virtual page size and whether the system uses a combined CPU/GPU page table or separate per-processor page tables.
facts.note: Sets up subsequent subsections on page size selection and hardware/software coherency.

caveats: The document explicitly notes that physical page sizes are hardware-dependent and subject to change, advising developers to tune virtual page sizes rather than hardcoding against physical defaults.

This section is purely definitional; deeper performance implications (e.g., TLB miss costs, fragmentation trade-offs, hardware vs. software coherency) are deferred to subsequent subsections (24.2.2.1.1 and 24.2.2.1.2).
recommended_structure: Definition of Virtual vs. Physical Pages

concept/memory-synchronization-domains.title: Memory Synchronization Domains
concept/memory-synchronization-domains.summary: Hopper architecture (CUDA 12.0+) feature that isolates memory traffic into logical domains to reduce fence/flush interference. Uses launch attributes to map logical domains to physical domains and manage cross-domain system-scope fencing.

research_reports.context_summary: Section 6.2.7 of the CUDA C Programming Guide introduces Memory Synchronization Domains as a hardware/software feature to mitigate performance degradation from memory fence interference. It bridges the gap between the CUDA memory consistency model and asynchronous concurrent execution by allowing developers to isolate memory traffic via domain tagging.

facts.claim: Memory synchronization domains alleviate performance degradation caused by 'memory fence interference', where fence/flush operations wait on more transactions than the CUDA memory consistency model strictly requires.
facts.note: Interference often occurs at task boundaries or when a local compute kernel implicitly flushes writes, unnecessarily waiting on slower remote memory traffic (e.g., NVLink/PCIe) from parallel communication kernels.

facts.claim: The underlying issue stems from 'cumulativity': system-scope ordering must guarantee that writes from other threads (visible to the current thread) are also visible to downstream threads, forcing the GPU to conservatively track in-flight operations.
facts.note: Introduced in Hopper architecture GPUs with CUDA 12.0, domains tag writes and fences with an ID, restricting a fence's ordering guarantees to matching domain tags.

facts.claim: Cross-domain synchronization on the same GPU mandates system-scope fencing, while intra-domain synchronization only requires device-scope fencing.
facts.note: Cumulativity across domains is maintained by ensuring cross-domain traffic is flushed to system scope ahead of time.

facts.claim: The feature modifies the definition of thread_scope_device but maintains backward compatibility by defaulting all kernels to domain 0.
facts.note: Domains are configured via launch attributes: cudaLaunchAttributeMemSyncDomain (selects logical domains cudaLaunchMemSyncDomainDefault or cudaLaunchMemSyncDomainRemote) and cudaLaunchAttributeMemSyncDomainMap (maps logical to physical domains).

facts.claim: The 'remote' logical domain is intended for kernels performing remote memory access to isolate their traffic from local kernels.
facts.note: Physical domain count is queried via cudaDevAttrMemSyncDomainCount (Hopper supports 4). For portability, older architectures report 1.

facts.claim: Logical domains facilitate application composition; low-level libraries (e.g., NCCL) can use semantic logical domains while higher-level code handles physical mapping.
facts.note: Default mapping assigns logical default to physical 0 and logical remote to physical 1.

facts.claim: Attributes apply uniformly to CUDA streams, cudaLaunchKernelEx launches, and kernel nodes in CUDA graphs.
facts.note: During graph execution, graphs inherit attributes from the node itself; stream-level domain attributes are ignored.

caveats: The feature is hardware-dependent (Hopper+) and CUDA version-dependent (12.0+).
Cross-domain synchronization strictly requires system-scope fencing, which may impact performance if overused.
Graph execution ignores stream-level domain attributes, relying solely on node-captured attributes.

concept/memory-type.title: Memory Type (CUDA 10.2+)
concept/memory-type.summary: Introduction to user-controlled memory allocation via cuMemCreate and CUmemAllocationProp::allocFlags.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence (L15097-L15100) introduces the 'Memory Type' section within Chapter 14 (Virtual Memory Management). It establishes that CUDA 10.2 added explicit user-controlled memory type allocation via the VMM APIs, moving away from the implicit, fixed memory behavior of pre-10.2 CUDA.

facts.claim: Memory Type allocation features were introduced in CUDA 10.2 as part of the Virtual Memory Management APIs.
facts.note: Replaces legacy implicit memory handling with explicit application control.

facts.claim: Applications specify memory type requirements at allocation time using the `CUmemAllocationProp::allocFlags` field within the `cuMemCreate` API.
facts.note: Allows opting into specific hardware-supported memory features.

facts.claim: Developers must explicitly verify that the target device supports the requested memory type before allocation.
facts.note: Prevents allocation failures on unsupported hardware configurations.

caveats: The assigned evidence is a high-level introduction. Detailed memory type variants (e.g., Compressible Memory) are covered in subsequent subsections (L15101-L15127) but fall outside the assigned evidence range.
Memory type specification is tightly coupled with the Virtual Memory Management workflow (cuMemCreate -> cuMemAddressReserve -> cuMemMap), rather than standalone legacy allocation calls.
recommended_structure: Overview of Memory Type introduction in CUDA 10.2
Relationship to Virtual Memory Management workflow

concept/minimize-memory-thrashing.title: Minimize Memory Thrashing
concept/minimize-memory-thrashing.summary: Provides recommendations for managing device memory allocation to avoid performance degradation, including sizing allocations, batching cudaMalloc/cudaFree calls, and using cudaMallocManaged for oversubscription.

research_reports.context_summary: Section 8.5 sits between device memory access optimization (8.3.2) and instruction throughput guidance (8.4), framing memory thrashing as a host-side allocation lifecycle issue rather than a device-side data movement problem.

facts.claim: Frequent allocation and deallocation causes progressively slower allocation calls due to OS memory reclamation overhead.
facts.note: Defines the core problem: thrashing stems from repeated `cudaMalloc`/`cudaFree` cycles, not device memory access patterns.

facts.claim: Allocations should be sized precisely to the workload; avoiding full-device allocation prevents forced immediate residency, reduces OS scheduler pressure, and avoids blocking other GPU applications.
facts.note: Applies to `cudaMalloc`, `cudaMallocHost`, and `cuMemCreate`.

facts.claim: Memory should be allocated early in the application lifecycle when the GPU is idle, and `cudaMalloc`/`cudaFree` calls should be minimized, especially in performance-critical regions.
facts.note: Direct lifecycle management recommendation to avoid runtime fragmentation/overhead.

facts.claim: If device memory is exhausted, fall back to `cudaMallocHost` or `cudaMallocManaged` to maintain progress, acknowledging potential performance trade-offs.
facts.note: Fallback strategy when strict device memory limits are hit.

facts.claim: `cudaMallocManaged` supports oversubscription; with appropriate `cudaMemAdvise` policies, it retains near-`cudaMalloc` performance, defers residency until access/prefetch, reduces OS scheduler pressure, and improves multi-tenancy.
facts.note: Highlights managed memory as a modern mitigation for thrashing, emphasizing policy-driven paging over forced residency.

caveats: The guide treats memory thrashing strictly as a host-side allocation frequency/residency issue, distinct from device-side bank conflicts or uncoalesced global memory accesses covered in section 8.3.2.
Performance trade-offs are explicitly acknowledged when falling back to host/managed memory; thrashing mitigation here prioritizes stability and scheduler efficiency over peak device bandwidth.
recommended_structure: Problem Definition: Host-side allocation overhead and OS reclamation latency
Best Practices: Early allocation, workload-sized pools, minimizing free/alloc cycles
Fallback Strategies: Host/managed memory when device memory is exhausted
Managed Memory Optimization: Oversubscription, `cudaMemAdvise`, deferred residency, multi-tenancy benefits

concept/multi-gpu-managed-memory-pre6x.title: Multi-GPU Managed Memory on Pre-6.x
concept/multi-gpu-managed-memory-pre6x.summary: Details managed memory visibility across GPUs via peer-to-peer, Linux driver migration behavior, Windows zero-copy fallback, and the CUDA_MANAGED_FORCE_DEVICE_ALLOC environment variable.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence covers section 24.3.2.3 Multi-GPU, which details how managed memory behaves across multiple GPUs on pre-6.x architectures. It is framed by section 24.3.2.2 (GPU Memory Oversubscription limits) and 24.3.2.4 (Coherency and Concurrency constraints), highlighting the legacy, non-page-fault-driven memory model.

facts.claim: On pre-6.x systems, managed allocations are automatically visible to all GPUs via peer-to-peer (P2P) capabilities, behaving like `cudaMalloc()` where the active device is the physical home and other GPUs access data over PCIe at reduced bandwidth.
facts.note: Pre-6.x architectures do not support GPU memory oversubscription for managed memory; allocations cannot exceed physical GPU memory size.

facts.claim: Linux: Allocations remain in GPU memory only if all actively used GPUs support P2P. Introducing a GPU without P2P support triggers a driver migration of all managed allocations to system memory, imposing PCIe bandwidth restrictions across all GPUs.
facts.note: Windows: Lacks of P2P mappings (e.g., mixed architectures) force an automatic fallback to zero-copy memory, even if only one GPU is utilized. To force GPU memory allocation in single-GPU scenarios, `CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES` must be set prior to launch.

facts.claim: Windows Environment Variable: `CUDA_MANAGED_FORCE_DEVICE_ALLOC` can be set to a non-zero value to force device memory storage. This requires all devices used within the process to be P2P-compatible. Using an incompatible device returns `cudaErrorInvalidDevice`, even if `cudaDeviceReset` was previously called. Note that this variable has no effect on Linux starting from CUDA 8.0.
facts.note: The assigned evidence directly addresses the target page's scope, detailing the legacy multi-GPU memory management model before the introduction of full Unified Memory page faulting and hardware coherency in compute capability 6.x.

facts.claim: It sits between the oversubscription limitation (pre-6.x cannot oversubscribe) and the strict concurrency/coherency rules (pre-6.x requires explicit synchronization and lacks concurrent CPU/GPU access). The multi-GPU behavior is fundamentally tied to these older architectural constraints, where memory residency and migration are heavily dependent on P2P availability and explicit environment variables rather than on-demand page faulting.
facts.note: No contradictions or scope clashes detected. The evidence aligns perfectly with the target page's historical/pre-6.x focus.

caveats: Pre-6.x multi-GPU managed memory relies entirely on P2P availability and driver-level migration rather than hardware coherency or page faulting. Any P2P mismatch forces system memory fallback, degrading performance.
contradictions: None detected.

concept/multicast-support.title: Multicast Support
concept/multicast-support.summary: Details NVLINK SHARP support via Multicast Objects, cuMulticastCreate, cuMulticastAddDevice, and multimem PTX instructions for broadcast/reductions.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned document covers Multicast Support (Section 14.9) as part of the Virtual Memory Management APIs. It details the hardware prerequisites (NVLINK SHARP/NVSWITCH), the step-by-step API workflow for creating and binding Multicast Objects, and the required inline PTX assembly (`multimem` instructions) for compute capability 9.0+. It also notes that the guide is legacy as of CUDA 13.0.

facts.claim: Multicast Support leverages NVLINK SHARP on NVLINK-connected GPUs linked via NVSWITCH to accelerate fabric operations like broadcasts and reductions.
facts.note: Multiple GPUs form a 'Multicast Team,' where each GPU maintains a local physical replica of a Multicast Object. PTX `multimem` instructions operate across all replicas.

facts.claim: Applications must query device support using `cuDeviceGetAttribute` with `CU_DEVICE_ATTRIBUTE_MULTICAST_SUPPORTED` before use.
facts.note: Creation requires `cuMulticastCreate` with a `CUmemAllocationProp` specifying `numDevices`, `handleTypes` (e.g., `CU_MEM_HANDLE_TYPE_FABRIC`), and a size padded to the granularity from `cuMulticastGetGranularity`.

facts.claim: Participating GPUs must be registered via `cuMulticastAddDevice` on all processes *before* any physical memory is bound to the object.
facts.note: Memory binding is performed per device using `cuMulticastBindMem`, linking `cuMemCreate` allocations to the multicast handle.

facts.claim: Multicast mappings require reserving an address range, mapping the handle, and setting access rights via Virtual Memory Management APIs. Unicast and Multicast mappings to the same physical memory are supported.
facts.note: When accessing the same physical memory through both Multicast and Unicast proxies, a `fence.proxy.alias` instruction is mandatory to ensure memory consistency.

facts.claim: Multicast operations require Compute Capability 9.0 or higher (`__CUDA_ARCH__ >= 900`) and are implemented via inline PTX assembly using `multimem` instructions.
facts.note: Examples include `multimem.red.release.sys.global.add.u32` for atomic reductions and `multimem.ld_reduce.relaxed.sys.global.add.f32` for atomic loads.

facts.claim: The official `multi_node_p2p` example demonstrates this API but is intended for library developers (e.g., NCCL, NVSHMEM) to understand internal fabric operations. General application developers should rely on higher-level interfaces like MPI, NCCL, or NVSHMEM.
facts.note: The source document is marked as legacy (replaced by the CUDA Programming Guide as of CUDA 13.0), but the API concepts remain valid.

caveats: The source document is explicitly marked as legacy (CUDA 13.0+), though the Multicast API concepts remain accurate for the target concept.
Strict ordering is required: devices must be added to the team before memory binding, and `fence.proxy.alias` is required when mixing Unicast and Multicast accesses to the same physical memory.

concept/namespace-reservations.title: Namespace Reservations
concept/namespace-reservations.summary: Rules prohibiting additions to reserved CUDA namespaces, with examples of valid/invalid usage.

research_reports.context_summary: Section 18.5.9 of the CUDA C++ Programming Guide, located within the 'C++ Language Features' chapter (Section 18.5). This section details restrictions on adding user-defined code to namespaces reserved by the CUDA runtime and libraries.

facts.claim: The namespaces cuda::, nv::, cooperative_groups::, and any namespaces nested within them are reserved.
facts.note: Adding declarations or definitions to these reserved namespaces is undefined behavior.

facts.claim: It is allowed to nest a reserved namespace inside a non-reserved (user-defined) namespace.
facts.note: Example: namespace utils { namespace cuda { ... } } is valid.

facts.claim: Using a using namespace directive that brings a reserved namespace into global scope is equivalent to adding symbols to the reserved namespace at global scope.
facts.note: This is considered undefined behavior.

caveats: The documentation explicitly states that adding declarations or definitions to reserved namespaces is undefined behavior.
The examples provided show that while direct modification of cuda:: is forbidden, nesting cuda:: inside a user namespace is permitted.

concept/nanosleep-function.title: Nanosleep Function
concept/nanosleep-function.summary: Verbatim source-backed fallback page for Nanosleep Function.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence contains the complete documentation section (10.23) for the `__nanosleep` function, including its synopsis, behavioral description, hardware requirements, and a practical device-side mutex example demonstrating exponential back-off.

facts.claim: Function signature is `void __nanosleep(unsigned ns);`
facts.note: Directly from the Synopsis subsection.

facts.claim: Suspends the thread for approximately `ns` nanoseconds, with a maximum sleep duration of ~1 millisecond.
facts.note: From the Description subsection.

facts.claim: Supported on devices with compute capability 7.0 or higher.
facts.note: From the Description subsection.

facts.claim: Commonly used for implementing exponential back-off in device-side synchronization primitives (e.g., mutex locks) to reduce contention after failed atomic operations.
facts.note: Demonstrated in the Example subsection with a `mutex_lock` function that doubles the sleep duration up to 256 ns on repeated `atomicCAS` failures.

caveats: The function is a device-side intrinsic (`__device__` implied by context and example).
Sleep duration is approximate; hardware scheduling may introduce variance.
No contradictions or scope clashes detected with the target page meaning.

concept/nvsci-interoperability.title: NVIDIA Software Communication Interface (NVSCI) Interoperability
concept/nvsci-interoperability.summary: Allocates, configures, and imports NvSciBuf memory objects into CUDA, including cache and compression attribute handling.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence covers the allocation, attribute configuration, import, and mapping of NvSciBuf memory objects, as well as the generation, reconciliation, import, and signaling/waiting of NvSciSync synchronization objects. The broader context window extends slightly beyond the assigned evidence to cover the full sync workflow.

facts.claim: NVSCI provides two primary interfaces: NvSciBuf for allocating/exchanging memory buffers, and NvSciSync for managing synchronization objects at operation boundaries.
facts.note: Purpose of NvSciBuf and NvSciSync

facts.claim: Allocating an NvSciBuf object compatible with a CUDA device requires setting the GPU ID via NvSciBufGeneralAttrKey_GpuId. Optional attributes include CPU access needs, buffer alignment, access permissions, GPU L2 cache control, and GPU compression.
facts.note: Memory object allocation attributes

facts.claim: Access permissions can be restricted by creating a duplicate NvSciBuf object with reduced permissions (e.g., read-only) using NvSciBufObjDupWithReducePerm() before importing into CUDA.
facts.note: Permission reduction workflow

facts.claim: NvSciBuf memory objects are imported into CUDA via cudaImportExternalMemory() using the cudaExternalMemoryHandleTypeNvSciBuf handle type. Applications must query attributes like buffer size and software cache coherency (NvSciBufGeneralAttrKey_GpuSwNeedCacheCoherency).
facts.note: Importing memory objects

facts.claim: Mapped device pointers are obtained via cudaExternalMemoryGetMappedBuffer() and must be freed with cudaFree(). Mipmapped arrays are mapped via cudaExternalMemoryGetMappedMipmappedArray() and freed with cudaFreeMipmappedArray().
facts.note: Mapping buffers and mipmapped arrays
