facts.claim: NvSciSync objects are created by generating device-compatible attributes via cudaDeviceGetNvSciSyncAttributes(), reconciling signaler and waiter attribute lists, and allocating the object. They are imported into CUDA via cudaImportExternalSemaphore() using cudaExternalSemaphoreHandleTypeNvSciSync.
facts.note: Synchronization object lifecycle

facts.claim: Signaling and waiting on imported NvSciSync semaphores are handled by cudaSignalExternalSemaphoresAsync() and cudaWaitExternalSemaphoresAsync(). The cudaExternalSemaphoreSignalSkipNvSciBufMemSync flag can bypass default memory synchronization operations if software cache coherency is disabled.
facts.note: Signaling and waiting workflow

facts.claim: Applications are responsible for maintaining attribute lists and NvSciBuf/NvSciSync objects. If an NvSciBuf object is mapped by other drivers, NvSciSync objects must be used as barriers to maintain coherence based on the GpuSwNeedCacheCoherency attribute.
facts.note: Coherence and ownership caveats

caveats: Memory and sync objects must be managed by the application; ownership of NvSciSyncObj handles remains with the app even after CUDA import.
Cache coherency handling is explicit: if GpuSwNeedCacheCoherency is FALSE, developers should use the skip flag or manage barriers manually.
Mipmapped array mapping requires exactly one mip level.

concept/occupancy-api-and-calculator.title: Occupancy API and Calculator
concept/occupancy-api-and-calculator.summary: Covers the occupancy-based kernel launch API (cudaOccupancyMaxPotentialBlockSize), cluster occupancy API (cudaOccupancyMaxPotentialClusterSize, cudaOccupancyMaxActiveClusters), and the standalone Nsight Compute occupancy calculator tool.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence covers code samples and documentation for occupancy-based launch configurators and cluster occupancy APIs. It details how to calculate optimal block/grid sizes for maximum occupancy, query maximum active clusters, handle cluster size compatibility for compute capability 9.0+, and utilize a standalone Nsight Compute calculator header for environments without the full CUDA stack.

facts.claim: cudaOccupancyMaxPotentialBlockSize calculates an execution configuration (block size and minimum grid size) to achieve maximum multiprocessor-level occupancy based on input size and dynamic shared memory.
facts.note: Used to configure occupancy-based kernel launches.

facts.claim: cudaOccupancyMaxActiveClusters predicts the maximum number of active clusters for a given cluster size, block size, and shared memory usage.
facts.note: Reports occupancy in terms of max active clusters on the GPU.

facts.claim: A cluster size of 8 is forward compatible starting at compute capability 9.0, but may be reduced on GPU hardware or MIG configurations that cannot support 8 multiprocessors.
facts.note: Users should query the maximum cluster size before launching a cluster kernel.

facts.claim: The maximum cluster size can be queried using the cudaOccupancyMaxPotentialClusterSize API.
facts.note: Used alongside cudaLaunchConfig_t to validate cluster dimensions.

facts.claim: A standalone occupancy calculator and launch configurator implementation is provided in <CUDA_Toolkit_Path>/include/cuda_occupancy.h for environments that cannot depend on the CUDA software stack.
facts.note: Serves as a learning tool that visualizes how changes to block size, registers per thread, and shared memory per thread affect occupancy.

caveats: Cluster size of 8 is not universally supported on all compute capability 9.0+ hardware or MIG configurations; dynamic reduction may occur.
The standalone calculator header is intended for environments without the full CUDA software stack or as a visualization/learning tool.

concept/optimized-memory-reuse.title: Optimized Memory Reuse
concept/optimized-memory-reuse.summary: CUDA reuses memory via virtual address assignment within graphs and physical memory aliasing between graphs.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence (Section 16.4) bridges the preceding discussion on graph capture, event ordering, and `cudaGraphInstantiateFlagAutoFreeOnLaunch` (L16071-L16190) with the subsequent performance and memory management sections covering `cudaGraphUpload` and `cudaDeviceGraphMemTrim` (L16223-L16247). It establishes the foundational rules for how CUDA handles memory allocation nodes inside graphs.

facts.claim: CUDA optimizes memory via two mechanisms: intra-graph reuse based on virtual address assignment, and inter-graph physical memory reuse via virtual aliasing (different graphs map the same physical memory to unique virtual addresses).
facts.note: Intra-graph reuse assigns identical virtual address ranges to allocations with non-overlapping lifetimes, meaning pointers to disjoint allocations are not guaranteed unique. Reuse is strictly dependency-bound: an allocation node can only reuse a freed address if it depends on the corresponding free node.

facts.claim: Multiple graphs may share the same physical memory for distinct allocations provided they do not execute concurrently. Physical pages are blocked from reuse if bound to multiple simultaneous graphs or if an allocation remains unfreed.
facts.note: CUDA updates physical mappings dynamically during instantiation, launch, or execution. To prevent live allocations from colliding on the same physical memory, the driver may inject synchronization between future graph launches.

facts.claim: Accessing a pointer outside an allocation's lifetime can cause silent data corruption (reading/writing live data from another allocation), even if the virtual address is unique. Compute sanitizer is recommended to catch these errors.
facts.note: Graphs launched sequentially in the same stream never run concurrently, allowing CUDA to safely reuse the same physical memory across them.

caveats: No contradictions or scope clashes detected. The evidence strictly defines the memory reuse semantics for graph nodes and aligns with the surrounding performance/trimming context.
recommended_structure: Overview of intra-graph vs inter-graph reuse
Virtual address assignment rules & dependency constraints
Stream execution impact on memory reuse

concept/page-locked-host-memory.title: Page-Locked Host Memory
concept/page-locked-host-memory.summary: Host memory allocated with cudaHostAlloc or cudaHostRegister that prevents paging, enabling concurrent transfers, device mapping, and higher bandwidth. Includes portable, write-combining, and mapped memory variants.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence covers Section 6.2.6 and its subsections, detailing the APIs, performance benefits, allocation flags, and hardware constraints of page-locked host memory in CUDA.

facts.claim: Page-locked (pinned) host memory is allocated via cudaHostAlloc()/cudaFreeHost() or registered via cudaHostRegister() to replace standard pageable malloc memory.
facts.note: Enables concurrent memory transfers and kernel execution on supported devices.

facts.claim: Page-locked memory can be mapped directly into the device address space, removing explicit copy overhead.
facts.note: Increases host-to-device bandwidth, especially on front-side bus systems.

facts.claim: Portable memory flags (cudaHostAllocPortable, cudaHostRegisterPortable) extend page-locked benefits across all system devices, not just the current device at allocation time.
facts.note: Write-Combining (cudaHostAllocWriteCombined) bypasses host L1/L2 caches and PCIe snooping, improving transfer speeds by up to 40%. Not recommended for host reads or CPU atomics.

facts.claim: Mapped memory (cudaHostAllocMapped, cudaHostRegisterMapped) exposes dual host/device addresses. Device pointers are retrieved via cudaHostGetDevicePointer() after enabling mapping with cudaSetDeviceFlags(cudaDeviceMapHost).
facts.note: Kernel-originated transfers automatically overlap with execution, but shared access requires explicit stream/event synchronization to prevent hazards.

facts.claim: Atomics on mapped memory are not atomic from the host or other device perspectives. Naturally aligned 1-16 byte accesses are preserved as single operations; PCIe bridges splitting 8-byte ops are unsupported.
facts.note: Non-I/O coherent Tegra devices do not cache page-locked memory and do not support cudaHostRegister().

caveats: Mapped memory bandwidth is lower than native device memory.
cudaHostGetDevicePointer() fails if the device lacks mapped memory support (check canMapHostMemory property).
Explicit synchronization is mandatory for mapped memory due to shared host/device visibility.

concept/peer-to-peer-memory-access.title: Peer-to-Peer Memory Access
concept/peer-to-peer-memory-access.summary: Devices can directly address each other's memory based on PCIe/NVLINK topology. Enabled via cudaDeviceEnablePeerAccess() and verified with cudaDeviceCanAccessPeer(). Requires 64-bit applications and unified address space. Linux bare-metal systems require IOMMU to be disabled for PCIe P2P, while VM pass-through with VFIO is supported.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence falls under section 6.2.9.4 'Peer-to-Peer Memory Access' within the broader 'Multi-Device System' section of the CUDA C Programming Guide. It details how devices can directly access each other's memory, prerequisites, limits, and specific Linux IOMMU constraints.

facts.claim: Definition and Topology Dependency
facts.note: Peer-to-peer memory access allows a kernel executing on one device to dereference a pointer to the memory of another device. This capability depends on the system's PCIe and/or NVLink topology.

facts.claim: Capability Check
facts.note: Support for peer-to-peer access between two specific devices can be verified by checking if `cudaDeviceCanAccessPeer()` returns true.

facts.claim: Requirements and Enablement
facts.note: Peer-to-peer memory access is restricted to 64-bit applications and must be explicitly enabled between two devices using `cudaDeviceEnablePeerAccess()`. It relies on a Unified Virtual Address Space (UVA), allowing the same pointer to address memory from both devices.

facts.claim: Connection Limits
facts.note: On systems without NVSwitch, each device supports a system-wide maximum of eight peer connections.

facts.claim: Linux IOMMU Constraints
facts.note: On Linux, CUDA and the display driver do not support IOMMU-enabled bare-metal PCIe peer-to-peer memory copy. Users running on bare-metal Linux should disable IOMMU. IOMMU is supported via VM pass-through using the VFIO driver. Windows does not have this limitation.

caveats: Linux bare-metal systems require IOMMU to be disabled for PCIe peer-to-peer memory copy to function correctly.

concept/peer-to-peer-memory-copy.title: Peer-to-Peer Memory Copy
concept/peer-to-peer-memory-copy.summary: Memory can be copied directly between devices using cudaMemcpyPeer() or similar functions. Copies in the implicit NULL stream synchronize with all previous commands. Asynchronous copies can overlap with other operations. P2P access via cudaDeviceEnablePeerAccess() avoids staging through host memory, improving performance.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence (Sec 6.2.9.5) sits between multi-device stream/event behavior (6.2.9.3), peer-to-peer memory access (6.2.9.4), and unified virtual address space (6.2.10). It bridges kernel-level pointer dereferencing (P2P access) and bulk data transfer, clarifying that P2P access enables direct GPU reads but explicit copy APIs are required for memory movement. The surrounding IOMMU caveat (6.2.9.4.1) directly applies to this operation.

facts.claim: Direct memory transfers between two distinct GPU devices without staging through host memory.
facts.note: Requires 64-bit application context.

facts.claim: APIs: cudaMemcpyPeer(), cudaMemcpyPeerAsync(), cudaMemcpy3DPeer(), cudaMemcpy3DPeerAsync(). If Unified Virtual Address Space (UVA) is enabled, standard cudaMemcpy() variants can be used.
facts.note: UVA simplifies pointer management across devices.

facts.claim: Implicit NULL stream copies are synchronous across devices: they block until all prior commands on either device complete, and prevent subsequent commands on either device from starting until completion.
facts.note: Asynchronous copies in named streams can overlap with other operations.

facts.claim: Requires explicit enablement via cudaDeviceEnablePeerAccess(). When enabled, transfers bypass host staging, significantly improving performance.
facts.note: Max 8 peer connections per device on non-NVSwitch systems.

facts.claim: Linux bare-metal systems with IOMMU enabled do not support P2P memory copy (must disable IOMMU or use VM passthrough/VFIO). Windows has no IOMMU limitation.
facts.note: Critical deployment caveat for Linux servers.

caveats: No contradictions found. The source clearly distinguishes P2P memory access (kernel dereferencing) from P2P memory copy (data transfer), but notes they are functionally linked: enabling P2P access removes host staging overhead for copies.
Target page should explicitly note that P2P copy is distinct from CUDA IPC, which handles cross-process sharing rather than cross-device transfers.

concept/performance-tuning-unified-memor.title: Unified Memory Performance Tuning
concept/performance-tuning-unified-memor.summary: Provides general advice for tuning Unified Memory performance: understanding paging, keeping data local, tuning for transfer granularity, and carefully using performance hints to avoid overhead.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence is the introductory section (24.2.2) for Unified Memory performance tuning. It sits immediately before detailed subsections on memory paging/page sizes (L21615+), hardware vs. software coherency (L21647+), and direct host access (L21676+). The evidence provides the high-level strategic guidelines that the subsequent technical sections implement.

facts.claim: Achieving good Unified Memory performance requires three core actions: understanding system paging to avoid unnecessary page faults, using mechanisms to keep data local to the accessing processor, and tuning applications to match the system's memory transfer granularity.
facts.note: Performance hints can improve performance but may degrade it if used incorrectly. They also incur a host-side execution cost, meaning their benefits must strictly outweigh this overhead to be considered useful.

caveats: No contradictions or scope clashes detected. The assigned evidence aligns perfectly with the target page slug and title.

The evidence is purely conceptual/strategic; the surrounding context (L21615-L21734) contains the technical implementation details (page sizes, coherency models, `cudaMemAdvise` usage) that the lead writer may need to reference separately.
recommended_structure: Overview of core tuning principles (paging, locality, granularity)
Guidance on performance hints and their host-side cost
Cross-reference to detailed subsections on paging/page sizes, coherency models, and direct access hints.

concept/pointers.title: Pointers
concept/pointers.summary: Rules for pointer dereferencing across host/device boundaries and usage of addresses for device/shared/constant variables.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence (L16847-L16852) defines the strict separation between host and device memory pointer usage, including restrictions on dereferencing and address-taking. The context window (L16727-L16972) provides additional framing regarding managed variables, volatile semantics, built-in variables, and namespace reservations.

facts.claim: Host code dereferencing pointers to global or shared memory results in undefined behavior (typically segmentation fault).
facts.note: This applies to pointers obtained via standard address-of operators or other means in host code.

facts.claim: Device code dereferencing pointers to host memory results in undefined behavior (typically segmentation fault).
facts.note: This prevents device kernels from accessing host memory directly via pointers.

facts.claim: Taking the address of __device__, __shared__, or __constant__ variables restricts the resulting pointer's usage to device code.
facts.note: This applies to addresses obtained via the address-of operator (&).

facts.claim: Using cudaGetSymbolAddress() to obtain the address of __device__ or __constant__ variables restricts the resulting pointer's usage to host code.
facts.note: This is the inverse of the standard address-of operator rule, allowing host code to access device symbols.

caveats: Built-in variables cannot have their address taken (L16865-L16865).
__managed__ variables allow cross-space access but have restrictions on initialization and type qualifiers (e.g., no const, no references) (L16729-L16761).
volatile is not suitable for inter-thread synchronization or MMIO (L16774-L16845).

concept/pragma-unroll.title: 10.40. #pragma unroll
concept/pragma-unroll.summary: Covers the #pragma unroll directive for controlling loop unrolling behavior, including ICE arguments and compiler fallback rules.

research_reports.context_summary: The target section 10.40 sits within a broader chapter on compiler directives and pragmas (sections 10.38–10.43), specifically following register management qualifiers and preceding SIMD/assembly instructions.

facts.claim: By default, the CUDA compiler automatically unrolls small loops that have a known trip count.
facts.note: The #pragma unroll directive is used to explicitly control the unrolling behavior of any given loop, overriding or reinforcing the compiler's default heuristics.

facts.claim: The #pragma unroll directive must be placed immediately before the target loop and applies exclusively to that specific loop.
facts.note: It can optionally be followed by an Integral Constant Expression (ICE).

facts.claim: If no ICE is provided and the loop's trip count is constant, the compiler will completely unroll the loop.
facts.note: If the ICE evaluates to 1, the compiler will explicitly disable loop unrolling.

facts.claim: The pragma is ignored if the ICE evaluates to a non-positive integer or an integer greater than the maximum value representable by the int data type.
facts.note: Valid ICE examples include template parameters (e.g., X+1) or static const members (e.g., T2::value).

caveats: The ICE must be a compile-time constant expression; runtime variables cannot be used.

concept/programmatic-dependent-launch.title: Programmatic Dependent Launch
concept/programmatic-dependent-launch.summary: A mechanism (compute capability 9.0+) allowing a secondary kernel to launch before its primary kernel finishes in the same stream. Uses cudaTriggerProgrammaticLaunchCompletion() and cudaGridDependencySynchronize() for safe concurrent execution. Supported in CUDA Graphs via edge data.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence (Section 6.2.8.6) sits between stream synchronization mechanics (6.2.8.5) and CUDA Graphs (6.2.8.7), serving as a bridge that introduces stream-level concurrency optimizations and maps them directly to graph edge dependencies.

facts.claim: Definition & Purpose
facts.note: Allows a dependent secondary kernel to launch before its primary kernel (in the same stream) finishes. Hides launch latency and enables concurrent execution of independent preamble work, improving performance on compute capability 9.0+ devices.

facts.claim: API & Execution Model
facts.note: Primary kernel signals readiness via `cudaTriggerProgrammaticLaunchCompletion()`. Secondary kernel uses `cudaLaunchKernelEx` with `cudaLaunchAttributeProgrammaticStreamSerialization` enabled. Secondary kernel must call `cudaGridDependencySynchronize()` to block until primary results are flushed to global memory. Implicit trigger occurs if omitted.

facts.claim: CUDA Graph Integration
facts.note: Maps to graphs using `cudaGraphDependencyTypeProgrammatic` on kernel-to-kernel edges. Connects to `cudaGraphKernelNodePortProgrammatic` or `cudaGraphKernelNodePortLaunchCompletion`. Stream capture automatically translates launch attributes to these graph edges.

facts.claim: Caveats & Guarantees
facts.note: Execution overlap is opportunistic, not guaranteed. Relying on it can cause deadlocks. Secondary blocks may launch before primary data is visible, making explicit synchronization mandatory.

facts.claim: Legacy Warning
facts.note: Document marked as legacy; replaced by the new CUDA Programming Guide as of CUDA 13.0. Information is no longer actively updated.

caveats: The source explicitly flags this documentation as legacy (CUDA 13.0+). Any implementation details should be cross-referenced with the current CUDA Programming Guide to avoid relying on deprecated APIs or behaviors.
The mechanism is strictly opportunistic; developers must not assume guaranteed concurrency and must implement fallback synchronization to prevent deadlocks.

concept/querying-data-usage-attributes.title: Querying Data Usage Attributes
concept/querying-data-usage-attributes.summary: Describes cudaMemRangeGetAttribute and cudaMemRangeGetAttributes APIs used to query attributes set via cudaMemAdvise or cudaMemPrefetchAsync. Lists queryable attributes like ReadMostly, PreferredLocation, AccessedBy, and LastPrefetchLocation.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence directly maps to Section 24.1.2.8.4 of the guide, which follows the 'Data Usage Hints' section. It provides the official API and attribute enumerations for retrieving memory usage hints previously applied to CUDA Managed Memory via `cudaMemAdvise` or `cudaMemPrefetchAsync`.

facts.claim: The primary API for querying a single memory range attribute is `cudaMemRangeGetAttribute`, which takes parameters for the data pointer, data size, attribute enum, target device pointer, and count.
facts.note: Multiple attributes can be queried simultaneously using the corresponding `cudaMemRangeGetAttributes` function.

facts.claim: The memory range queried must be CUDA Managed Memory, allocated via `cudaMallocManaged` or declared with `__managed__`.
facts.note: Attributes queried are those previously assigned via `cudaMemAdvise` or `cudaMemPrefetchAsync`.

facts.claim: cudaMemRangeAttributeReadMostly returns 1 if the entire range has cudaMemAdviseSetReadMostly set, otherwise 0.
facts.note: None

facts.claim: cudaMemRangeAttributePreferredLocation returns the preferred processor's device ID or cudaCpuDeviceId. Returns cudaInvalidDeviceId if the range lacks a uniform preferred location.
facts.note: The actual physical location of the memory at query time may differ from the preferred location.

facts.claim: cudaMemRangeAttributeAccessedBy returns the list of devices that have the AccessedBy advice set for the memory range.
facts.note: None

facts.claim: cudaMemRangeAttributeLastPrefetchLocation returns the last location explicitly requested via cudaMemPrefetchAsync.
facts.note: This only reflects the application's request; it does not indicate whether the prefetch operation has completed or begun.

facts.claim: cudaMemRangeAttributePreferredLocationType and cudaMemRangeAttributePreferredLocationId return the type (Device, Host, HostNuma, or Invalid) and corresponding ID (GPU ordinal or NUMA node) of the preferred location.
facts.note: Invalid is returned if pages lack a uniform preferred location or have none.

facts.claim: cudaMemRangeAttributeLastPrefetchLocationType and cudaMemRangeAttributeLastPrefetchLocationId return the type and ID of the last explicitly prefetched location.
facts.note: Invalid is returned if pages were not prefetched to the same location or were never prefetched.

caveats: Querying preferred or prefetch location attributes reflects application hints or explicit requests, not necessarily the physical memory location at the time of the query.
All pages in the queried range must share the same attribute value/type for the query to return a valid non-Invalid/non-0 result; otherwise, it indicates a mixed state.
recommended_structure: Important Caveats (Hints vs. Actual Location, Uniformity Requirements)

concept/relaxed-constexpr.title: Relaxed Constexpr
concept/relaxed-constexpr.summary: Experimental `-expt-relaxed-constexpr` flag enabling cross-execution space constexpr calls (host-to-device and device-to-host) when evaluated in constant-evaluation contexts.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence isolates the core definition, default restrictions, and enabling mechanism for relaxed constexpr in CUDA. It sits between sections on lambda capture rules/ADL (previously) and detailed runtime/ODR restrictions when the flag is active (subsequently).

facts.claim: Relaxed constexpr enables cross-execution space calls between __host__ and __device__ constexpr functions, which are otherwise prohibited.
facts.note: Default behavior blocks these calls during their respective code generation phases.

facts.claim: Default restrictions cause compilation errors for: (1) calling a __device__-only constexpr from host code (when __CUDA_ARCH__ is undefined), and (2) calling a __host__-only constexpr from __device__/__global__ code (when __CUDA_ARCH__ is defined).
facts.note: These restrictions apply strictly during the host or device code generation phases.

facts.claim: The feature is experimental and must be enabled using the -expt-relaxed-constexpr compiler flag.
facts.note: None

facts.claim: When enabled, cross-execution space calls are permitted only in contexts that require constant evaluation, such as initializing a constexpr variable.
facts.note: Examples show valid usage in both host-to-device and device-to-host directions when used as constexpr initializers.

caveats: The assigned evidence strictly covers the enabling mechanism and constant-evaluation context requirement. Broader implications (e.g., ODR-use of host variables, exception handling, or platform-specific stdlib behavior) are documented in the surrounding source text but fall outside the assigned evidence range.
contradictions: None detected within the assigned evidence.

concept/run-time-type-information.title: Run Time Type Information (RTTI)
concept/run-time-type-information.summary: RTTI features supported only in host code, not device code.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence is located in Section 18.5.6 of the CUDA C++ Programming Guide. This section is part of a broader section (18.5) that outlines C++ language features supported by CUDA C++. It is positioned between sections on Operators (18.5.5) and Exception Handling (18.5.7), categorizing it alongside other language-level feature restrictions and limitations.

facts.claim: CUDA C++ supports specific RTTI-related features exclusively in host code, and they are explicitly unsupported in device code.
facts.note: This establishes the primary execution space constraint for RTTI in CUDA.

facts.claim: The specific RTTI features supported in host code are the typeid operator, std::type_info, and the dynamic_cast operator.
facts.note: Lists the exact language features permitted under the host-only constraint.

caveats: The source does not provide workarounds or alternative mechanisms for performing type identification or casting within device code.

concept/runtime-detection-unified-memory.title: Runtime Detection of Unified Memory Support Level
concept/runtime-detection-unified-memory.summary: Provides a code example demonstrating how to query device attributes (cudaDevAttrPageableMemoryAccess, cudaDevAttrConcurrentManagedAccess) at runtime to determine the level of Unified Memory support available on the system.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence is extracted from section 24.1.2.6, which sits between pointer attribute detection (24.1.2.5) and GPU memory oversubscription/performance hints (24.1.2.7–24.1.2.8). It provides a direct code example for querying hardware capabilities before utilizing unified memory features.

facts.claim: Runtime detection is performed by querying device attributes via cudaDeviceGetAttribute after obtaining the device ID with cudaGetDevice.
facts.note: Framing: The example initializes an integer, calls cudaGetDevice, then queries two specific attributes to determine support levels.

facts.claim: cudaDevAttrPageableMemoryAccess returning 1 indicates 'Full Unified Memory Support'.
facts.note: Framing: This attribute is the primary gate for basic unified memory functionality.

facts.claim: cudaDevAttrConcurrentManagedAccess returning 1 indicates 'CUDA Managed Memory with full support'.
facts.note: Framing: This attribute specifically governs concurrent CPU+GPU access capabilities.

facts.claim: The guide prints 'YES' or 'NO' based on whether each queried attribute equals 1.
facts.note: Framing: Demonstrates a straightforward conditional check pattern for application logic.

caveats: Detection must occur after a valid CUDA context is established (implied by cudaGetDevice usage).
Values other than 0 or 1 are not handled in the example; applications should treat non-1 as unsupported.
recommended_structure: Integration with unified memory workflows (prefetching, concurrent access)

concept/shared-memory.title: Shared Memory
concept/shared-memory.summary: Explains shared memory allocation using __shared__, its performance benefits over global memory, and provides detailed matrix multiplication examples demonstrating shared memory usage for tiling and synchronization.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence covers Section 6.2.4 (Shared Memory) and Section 6.2.5 (Distributed Shared Memory). It transitions from standard block-scoped shared memory usage and synchronization to the newer cluster-scoped distributed shared memory introduced in Compute Capability 9.0, including API usage and launch configuration.

facts.claim: Shared memory is allocated using the __shared__ memory space specifier within kernels.
facts.note: Can also be declared dynamically using extern __shared__.

facts.claim: Shared memory is significantly faster than global memory and serves as scratchpad memory or a software-managed cache to minimize global memory accesses.
facts.note: Used to tile data and reduce redundant global reads.

facts.claim: In tiled algorithms like matrix multiplication, loading sub-matrices into shared memory reduces global memory reads by a factor equal to the block size.
facts.note: Demonstrates bandwidth savings by reusing data across threads within a block.

facts.claim: Explicit synchronization via __syncthreads() is mandatory to ensure data visibility after loading and before computation, and to synchronize before loading new tiles.
facts.note: Prevents race conditions and ensures correct tiled computation.

facts.claim: Distributed Shared Memory (DSM) was introduced in Compute Capability 9.0 alongside thread block clusters.
facts.note: Extends shared memory scope from a single block to a cluster of blocks.

facts.claim: Threads in a cluster can read, write, or perform atomics on the shared memory of any participating block via the distributed address space.
facts.note: Enables cross-block communication without global memory round-trips.

facts.claim: Shared memory size specifications (static or dynamic) remain per-thread-block. Total DSM size equals the number of blocks per cluster multiplied by the per-block shared memory size.
facts.note: Allocation model does not change, only the accessibility scope expands.

facts.claim: Accessing DSM data requires all participating thread blocks to exist concurrently, guaranteed using cluster.sync() from the Cooperative Groups API.
facts.note: Ensures all blocks have started before cross-block accesses begin.

facts.claim: Developers must ensure all DSM operations complete before any thread block exits to prevent invalid accesses or race conditions.
facts.note: Critical for correctness when remote blocks read local block memory.

facts.claim: Remote block shared memory is accessed via cluster.map_shared_rank(), and cross-block updates use standard atomics on the mapped pointer.
facts.note: Example shows histogram bin updates across blocks.

facts.claim: DSM kernels are launched using extensible launch APIs (cudaLaunchKernelEx) with cudaLaunchAttributeClusterDimension to define cluster size, while dynamic shared memory size is still specified per block.
facts.note: Cluster dimension is a launch attribute, not a kernel parameter.

caveats: Standard shared memory is strictly scoped to a single thread block. DSM is a newer feature (CC 9.0+) that extends this scope to clusters, requiring explicit cluster synchronization.
Dynamic shared memory size is always specified per block, even when computing across a cluster. The total accessible DSM is an implicit product of cluster size and per-block size.

concept/simd-video-instructions.title: 10.41. SIMD Video Instructions
concept/simd-video-instructions.summary: Covers PTX ISA 3.0 SIMD video instructions, their availability on CC 3.0+, and the syntax for inline assembly using the asm() statement.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence is a standalone section (10.41) within the CUDA C++ Programming Guide. It sits between compiler optimization directives (`#pragma unroll`, `__maxnreg__`) and diagnostic/ABI pragmas, functioning as a dedicated reference for low-level PTX assembly integration.

facts.claim: SIMD video instructions are part of PTX ISA version 3.0, operating on pairs of 16-bit values and quads of 8-bit values. They are available on devices with compute capability 3.0.
facts.note: The documentation references older architecture standards (PTX 3.0, Compute Capability 3.0), suggesting legacy or historical scope in the documentation.

facts.claim: The guide enumerates 14 specific instructions: vadd2, vadd4, vsub2, vsub4, vavrg2, vavrg4, vabsdif2, vabsdif4, vmin2, vmin4, vmax2, vmax4, vset2, and vset4.
facts.note: None

facts.claim: Instructions are integrated into CUDA code via the inline assembly asm() statement. Basic syntax: asm("template-string" : "constraint"(output) : "constraint"(input));
facts.note: The source contains a minor typographical error in the syntax example (an extra quote mark), which should be corrected in the final page.

facts.claim: An example demonstrates vabsdiff4.u32.u32.u32.add to compute a sum of absolute differences across four bytes of unsigned integers A and B. The .add suffix is noted as an optional accumulate operation that sums the differences.
facts.note: None

caveats: The section references PTX 3.0 and Compute Capability 3.0, which are historically significant but may be outdated for modern hardware. The writer should verify if newer PTX versions supersede or extend these instructions.
The provided syntax example contains a typo (extra quotation mark) that should be fixed before publication.
contradictions: None detected within the assigned evidence.
recommended_structure: References (Inline PTX Assembly guide, PTX ISA docs)

concept/simt-architecture.title: SIMT Architecture
concept/simt-architecture.summary: Details the Single-Instruction, Multiple-Thread (SIMT) execution model. Explains warps (32 threads), warp scheduling, branch divergence handling, and the shift from pre-Volta shared program counters to Volta's Independent Thread Scheduling (per-thread state, sub-warp divergence/reconvergence). Covers active/inactive thread states and memory write/atomic behavior within warps.

research_reports.scope: Legacy hardware implementation chapter (Chapter 7), specifically Section 7.1. Sits between general multiprocessor overview and hardware multithreading/performance guidelines.
research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence details the SIMT execution model within NVIDIA Streaming Multiprocessors (SMs). It covers warp composition, scheduling, divergence handling, the distinction from SIMD, architectural shifts introduced by Independent Thread Scheduling (Volta+), and memory write semantics for divergent threads. The document is explicitly marked as legacy (CUDA 13.0+).

facts.claim: SIMT Core Mechanism
facts.note: SMs use SIMT to execute hundreds of threads concurrently. Instructions are pipelined, leveraging both instruction-level parallelism (ILP) within a thread and thread-level parallelism (TLP) via hardware multithreading. Unlike CPUs, GPU instructions are issued in order with no branch prediction or speculative execution.

facts.claim: Warp Composition & Scheduling
facts.note: Threads are grouped into warps of 32 parallel threads. All threads in a warp start at the same program address but maintain independent program counters and register states. A warp scheduler partitions blocks into warps (consecutive thread IDs) and schedules them. Divergence is strictly intra-warp; different warps execute independently.

facts.claim: Divergence Handling
facts.note: When threads in a warp diverge via data-dependent conditional branches, the warp serially executes each branch path, disabling threads not on that path. Full efficiency is achieved only when all 32 threads follow the same execution path.

facts.claim: SIMT vs. SIMD Paradigm
facts.note: SIMT differs from SIMD by not exposing the vector width to software; instead, instructions specify execution/branching per thread. This allows programmers to write thread-level parallel code for independent scalar threads as well as data-parallel code. SIMT behavior can be ignored for correctness but must be managed (minimizing divergence) for peak performance.

facts.claim: Architectural Evolution: Pre-Volta vs. Volta+
facts.note: Pre-Volta warps shared a single program counter and active mask, preventing divergent threads from easily signaling/exchanging data and risking deadlocks with fine-grained locks. Starting with Volta, Independent Thread Scheduling (ITS) maintains per-thread state (PC, call stack) and yields at a per-thread granularity. A schedule optimizer groups active threads into SIMT units, enabling divergence/reconvergence at sub-warp granularity. This breaks assumptions of warp-synchronous code.

facts.claim: Active/Inactive Threads & Memory Semantics
facts.note: Threads participating in the current instruction are 'active'; others are 'inactive' (disabled) due to early exit, different branch paths, or block sizes not being multiples of 32. Non-atomic writes to the same location by multiple threads result in an undefined number of serialized writes and an undefined final writer. Atomic instructions serialize all read/modify/write operations, but their order remains undefined.

facts.claim: Document Status
facts.note: The source document is explicitly marked as legacy, replaced by the new CUDA Programming Guide as of CUDA 13.0, and is no longer being updated.

caveats: The source is legacy (CUDA 13.0+). The lead writer should verify if the new CUDA Programming Guide has superseded these specifics or if they remain accurate for current architectures.

Independent Thread Scheduling (Volta+) fundamentally changes warp-synchronous behavior. Any existing wiki content assuming strict warp-level synchronization should be flagged for review against this model.
Memory write semantics for divergent non-atomic/atomic instructions are hardware-dependent and explicitly undefined; code should not rely on them.
recommended_structure: Memory Write Semantics for Divergent Threads

concept/sli-interoperability.title: SLI Interoperability
concept/sli-interoperability.summary: Explains special considerations for CUDA interoperability in SLI configurations, including memory allocation impacts across GPUs, the recommendation to create one CUDA context per GPU, and using cudaD3D[9|10|11]GetDevices() or cudaGLGetDevices() to map resources to the correct device per frame.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence (Section 6.2.15.3) sits between a code example demonstrating D3D11/CUDA vertex buffer registration and a section on External Resource Interoperability (Vulkan). The SLI section specifically addresses constraints and best practices for CUDA interoperability with Direct3D and OpenGL in multi-GPU SLI configurations.

facts.claim: Memory Allocation Impact in SLI
facts.note: In an SLI configuration, a CUDA memory allocation on one device consumes memory across all GPUs in the SLI setup of the associated Direct3D or OpenGL device. This can cause allocations to fail earlier than expected.

facts.claim: Context Management Strategy
facts.note: Applications should create multiple CUDA contexts—one for each GPU in the SLI configuration—to avoid unnecessary data transfers between devices.

facts.claim: Device Identification APIs
facts.note: Use `cudaD3D[9|10|11]GetDevices()` for Direct3D and `cudaGLGetDevices()` for OpenGL to identify the CUDA device handle(s) performing rendering for the current or next frame.

facts.claim: Resource Mapping Configuration
facts.note: Map Direct3D or OpenGL resources to the identified CUDA device by setting the `deviceList` parameter to `cudaD3D[9|10|11]DeviceListCurrentFrame` or `cudaGLDeviceListCurrentFrame`.

facts.claim: Registration Constraints
facts.note: Resources returned from `cudaGraphicsD3D[9|10|11]RegisterResource` and `cudaGraphicsGLRegister[Buffer|Image]` must only be used on the device where registration occurred. In SLI configurations where data for different frames is computed on different CUDA devices, resources must be registered separately for each device.

facts.claim: Related Documentation
facts.note: Detailed integration steps for Direct3D and OpenGL are located in the 'Direct3D Interoperability' and 'OpenGL Interoperability' sections.

caveats: The SLI interoperability rules described here are specific to Direct3D and OpenGL. The surrounding context (Section 6.2.16) introduces a different paradigm for 'External Resource Interoperability' (e.g., Vulkan) that does not mention SLI-specific memory or context constraints.
recommended_structure: Overview of SLI mode in CUDA (multiple GPUs as separate devices)
Memory allocation behavior across SLI GPUs
Best practices for CUDA contexts in SLI

concept/standard-library.title: Standard Library
concept/standard-library.summary: Standard libraries supported only in host code unless specified otherwise.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence is located within a section detailing CUDA C++ language support restrictions (Section 18.5). It is grouped alongside other host-only C++ features, such as Run Time Type Information (RTTI) and Exception Handling, establishing a pattern where features requiring significant host-side runtime or OS interaction are disabled on the GPU.

facts.claim: Standard libraries are supported in host code.
facts.note: Explicitly stated as the primary supported environment.

facts.claim: Standard libraries are not supported in device code.
facts.note: General restriction applies to __device__ code.

facts.claim: Exceptions to the device code restriction may exist.
facts.note: The text qualifies the restriction with 'unless specified otherwise', implying specific standard library features or headers may be supported on the device in other sections.

caveats: The provided text does not list specific standard library headers or classes that are supported or unsupported; it only provides the general rule of host-only support.
recommended_structure: Exceptions (Refer to specific CUDA C++ features documentation for device-supported std:: components)

concept/static-variables-within-function.title: Static Variables within Function
concept/static-variables-within-function.summary: Rules for memory space specifiers of static variables in device/host functions, including initialization restrictions and __CUDA_ARCH__ conditions.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence falls under section 18.5.10.4 of the CUDA C Programming Guide, which details C++ language extensions and restrictions for functions. It specifically addresses the declaration, memory space assignment, and initialization rules for static variables defined within the scope of device or dual-execution functions.

facts.claim: Static variables can be declared within the immediate or nested block scope of __global__, __device__-only, or __host__ __device__ functions.
facts.note: For __host__ __device__ functions, device-specific memory space specifiers are only valid when __CUDA_ARCH__ is defined.

facts.claim: Variable memory space specifiers (e.g., __device__, __managed__, __constant__, __shared__) are allowed on function-scope static variables.
facts.note: If no explicit specifier is provided, an implicit __device__ specifier is assumed during device compilation.

facts.claim: Initialization of function-scope static variables is restricted to compile-time constants or aggregate initialization.
facts.note: Dynamic initialization is prohibited; for example, initializing a variable with a runtime value or an object with a non-empty constructor will cause a compilation error.

facts.claim: In __host__ __device__ functions, device-specific memory space specifiers like __device__ or __shared__ must be guarded by #ifdef __CUDA_ARCH__ to avoid host compilation errors.
facts.note: Using these specifiers unconditionally in a __host__ __device__ function results in an error when the host compiler processes the code.

caveats: The initialization rules for function-scope static variables mirror those of namespace-scope variables with the same memory space specifiers (e.g., a __device__ variable cannot have a 'non-empty' constructor).

concept/store-functions-using-cache-hint.title: Store Functions Using Cache Hints
concept/store-functions-using-cache-hint.summary: Reference for cache-hinted store intrinsics (__stwb, __stcg, __stcs, __stwt) that control cache behavior for global memory writes, supporting various data types and requiring compute capability 5.0 or higher.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence (Section 10.12) details the store functions that utilize cache hints. It is positioned immediately after Section 10.11 (Load Functions Using Cache Hints) and before Section 10.13 (Time Function).

facts.claim: The CUDA C Programming Guide defines four store functions that use cache hints: __stwb, __stcg, __stcs, and __stwt.
facts.note: These functions are device functions that store a value to a specified memory address using the corresponding cache operator defined in the PTX ISA.

facts.claim: These store functions require a GPU device with compute capability 5.0 or higher.
facts.note: This hardware requirement is identical to the load functions using cache hints found in the preceding section (10.11).

facts.claim: The generic type T supported by these functions includes standard integers (char, short, int, long, long long, and their unsigned variants) and vector types (e.g., int2, float4, double2).
facts.note: The source text lists specific vector types such as char2, char4, short2, short4, int2, int4, longlong2, uchar2, uchar4, ushort2, ushort4, uint2, uint4, and ulonglong2.

facts.claim: Floating-point types float, float2, float4, double, and double2 are supported.
facts.note: Additionally, if the cuda_fp16.h header is included, T can be __half or __half2. If the cuda_bf16.h header is included, T can be __nv_bfloat16 or __nv_bfloat162.

caveats: The source text contains minor typographical spacing issues in the type list (e.g., 'long longunsigned', 'longlong2uchar2'), but the intent is clearly a list of standard and vector integer types.
recommended_structure: Overview of store functions using cache hints
Supported data types (Standard, Vector, FP16, BF16)

concept/stream-and-event-behavior.title: Stream and Event Behavior
concept/stream-and-event-behavior.summary: Details the behavior of streams and events across different devices. Kernel launches fail if issued to a stream not associated with the current device, while memory copies may succeed. Events and streams on different devices have specific synchronization and error-checking rules. Default streams on different devices may execute concurrently.

research_reports.context_summary: The evidence is extracted from Section 6.2.9.3 'Stream and Event Behavior' within the 'Multi-Device System' chapter. It follows device enumeration and selection, establishing how stream and event APIs behave when the active device changes or across multiple GPUs.

facts.claim: Kernel launches fail if issued to a stream not associated with the currently set device.
facts.note: Demonstrated via code sample where switching devices causes a subsequent launch on the old stream to fail.

facts.claim: Memory copies succeed even if issued to a stream not associated with the current device.
facts.note: Contrasts with kernel launch behavior, showing relaxed constraints for memory operations.

facts.claim: cudaEventRecord() fails if the input event and input stream are associated with different devices.
facts.note: Enforces device locality for event recording.

facts.claim: cudaEventElapsedTime() fails if the two input events are associated with different devices.
facts.note: Prevents cross-device timing measurements via this API.

facts.claim: cudaEventSynchronize() and cudaEventQuery() succeed even if the input event is associated with a different device than the current one.
facts.note: Allows host threads to monitor or wait on events regardless of the currently active device.

facts.claim: cudaStreamWaitEvent() succeeds even if the input stream and input event are associated with different devices, enabling multi-device synchronization.
facts.note: Provides a mechanism to order execution across separate GPU devices.

facts.claim: Each device has its own default stream, so commands in one device's default stream may execute out of order or concurrently with commands in another device's default stream.
facts.note: Highlights that default stream serialization is strictly intra-device.

caveats: Behavior is specific to multi-device environments where the current device context may change. Single-device code will not encounter these cross-device failure modes.

concept/stream-associated-memcpy-memset.title: Memcpy()/Memset() Behavior With Stream-associated Unified Memory
concept/stream-associated-memcpy-memset.summary: Details the rules for cudaMemcpy*() and cudaMemset*() operations on stream-associated unified memory, including coherency checks, stream activity constraints, and error conditions.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence (Section 24.3.2.4.7) provides the specific operational rules for `cudaMemcpy*` and `cudaMemset*` on Unified Memory. It logically follows the preceding context (lines 21937–22056), which explains the `cudaStreamAttachMemAsync` function, stream attachment, and how stream association dictates data visibility between the CPU and GPU.

facts.claim: The rules in this section apply specifically to devices where the `concurrentManagedAccess` device attribute is not set (zero).
facts.note: For devices where `concurrentManagedAccess` is set, the guide defers to the general 'Memcpy()/Memset() Behavior With Unified Memory' page.

facts.claim: Coherent accessibility from the host in a given stream means the memory is neither globally visible nor associated with that stream.
facts.note: This definition is critical for determining the source/destination of `cudaMemcpyHostTo*` and `cudaMemcpy*ToHost` operations.

facts.claim: Coherent accessibility from the device in a given stream means the memory is either globally visible or associated with that stream.
facts.note: This definition is critical for determining the source/destination of `cudaMemcpyDeviceTo*` and `cudaMemcpy*ToDevice` operations, as well as `cudaMemset*`.

facts.claim: If `cudaMemcpyHostTo*` is specified and the source is Unified Memory, the data is accessed from the host if it is coherently accessible from the host in the copy stream; otherwise, it is accessed from the device.
facts.note: Similar rules apply to the destination when `cudaMemcpy*ToHost` is specified.

facts.claim: If `cudaMemcpyDeviceTo*` is specified and the source is Unified Memory, the data is accessed from the device. The source must be coherently accessible from the device in the copy stream, otherwise an error is returned.
facts.note: Similar rules apply to the destination when `cudaMemcpy*ToDevice` is specified.

facts.claim: If `cudaMemcpyDefault` is specified, Unified Memory is accessed from the host if it cannot be coherently accessed from the device in the copy stream, OR if the preferred location is `cudaCpuDeviceId` and it can be coherently accessed from the host; otherwise, it is accessed from the device.
facts.note: This rule handles the default behavior when the direction is not explicitly forced by the programmer.

facts.claim: When using `cudaMemset*()` with Unified Memory, the data must be coherently accessible from the device in the stream being used for the operation; otherwise, an error is returned.
facts.note: Unlike `cudaMemcpy`, `cudaMemset` does not have a fallback to host access if device coherence fails.

facts.claim: When data is accessed from the device by `cudaMemcpy*` or `cudaMemset*`, the stream of operation is considered active on the GPU.
facts.note: This active state triggers concurrency restrictions on older architectures.

facts.claim: If the GPU has a zero value for `concurrentManagedAccess`, any CPU access to data associated with that stream (or data with global visibility) while the stream is active on the GPU will result in a segmentation fault.
facts.note: Programs must synchronize appropriately to ensure the `cudaMemcpy*` or `cudaMemset*` operation has completed before accessing associated data from the CPU.

caveats: The assigned text explicitly defers to the 'Memcpy()/Memset() Behavior With Unified Memory' page for a general overview and for devices where `concurrentManagedAccess` is set.
The segmentation fault warning is strictly tied to devices where `concurrentManagedAccess` is zero; devices with this attribute set handle the concurrency differently (as per the deferred page).
recommended_structure: Overview of stream-associated UM memcpy/memset behavior
Definitions of Coherent Accessibility (Host vs. Device)

concept/stream-attach-mem-async.title: Managing Data Visibility with cudaStreamAttachMemAsync
concept/stream-attach-mem-async.summary: Explains how cudaStreamAttachMemAsync() associates managed allocations with specific streams to enable finer-grained CPU/GPU concurrency and data transfer optimizations.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence (Section 24.3.2.4.3) is situated within the broader documentation on Unified Memory coherency and concurrency limitations for pre-Compute Capability 6.x devices (where `concurrentManagedAccess` is 0). While the surrounding context establishes that the GPU generally holds exclusive access to all managed memory while *any* kernel is active, this specific section introduces `cudaStreamAttachMemAsync` as the mechanism to relax this restriction on a per-stream basis, enabling finer-grained concurrency.

facts.claim: Purpose and Scope
facts.note: Designed to provide finer-grained control over managed memory visibility and concurrent CPU/GPU access. It is intended to work on all devices supporting managed memory, including older architectures where `concurrentManagedAccess` is 0.

facts.claim: Function Signature
facts.note: cudaError_t cudaStreamAttachMemAsync(cudaStream_t stream, void *ptr, size_t length=0, unsigned int flags=0);

facts.claim: Attachment Mechanics
facts.note: Associates `length` bytes of memory starting at `ptr` with the specified stream. The `length` parameter must currently be 0 to indicate that the entire region should be attached.

facts.claim: Visibility Rules (Default vs. Attached)
facts.note: If an allocation is not associated with a specific stream, it is visible to all running kernels regardless of their stream (the default for `cudaMallocManaged()` or `__managed__` variables). Consequently, the CPU may not touch the data while any kernel is running.

facts.claim: Visibility Rules (Attached Behavior)
facts.note: By associating an allocation with a specific stream, the Unified Memory system allows CPU access to that memory region as long as all operations in that specific stream have completed, regardless of whether other streams are active. This constrains exclusive ownership by the GPU to per-stream activity instead of whole-GPU activity.

facts.claim: Programmer Responsibility and Error Checking
facts.note: The system performs no error checking. By associating an allocation with a stream, the program makes a guarantee that only kernels launched into that stream will touch the data; it is the programmer's responsibility to ensure this guarantee is honored.

facts.claim: Performance Implications
facts.note: In addition to allowing greater concurrency, using `cudaStreamAttachMemAsync()` can (and typically does) enable data transfer optimizations within the Unified Memory system that may affect latencies and other overhead.

caveats: The documentation is from the legacy `CUDA_C_Programming_Guide` (replaced as of CUDA 13.0), though the concepts apply to the historical context of pre-Compute Capability 6.x Unified Memory behavior.
recommended_structure: Overview of cudaStreamAttachMemAsync and its role in managing data visibility.
Function signature and parameters (specifically the requirement for `length=0`).
Default visibility rules (global visibility to all streams) vs. attached visibility (per-stream visibility).
How it enables concurrent CPU/GPU access on pre-6.x architectures.
Programmer guarantees and the lack of system error checking.
Impact on Unified Memory data transfer optimizations.

concept/stream-attach-multithreading.title: Stream Attach With Multithreaded Host Programs
concept/stream-attach-multithreading.summary: Describes using cudaStreamAttachMemAsync() for independent task parallelism in multi-threaded programs, the role of the cudaMemAttachHost flag, and modular program constraints.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence sits within the Unified Memory section of the CUDA C Programming Guide. It follows the general introduction to `cudaStreamAttachMemAsync()` and basic stream-association examples, and precedes detailed `Memcpy`/`Memset` behavior rules. The surrounding text establishes that pre-6.x GPUs require explicit synchronization to avoid CPU/GPU data races, and that stream attachment restricts memory visibility from whole-GPU scope to per-stream scope.

facts.claim: Primary purpose of cudaStreamAttachMemAsync() in multithreaded contexts is to enable independent task parallelism across CPU threads by binding each thread's managed allocations to a private CUDA stream, avoiding dependencies inherent to the default NULL stream.
facts.note: The default global visibility of managed memory complicates multi-threaded CPU programs. Attaching memory to a specific stream restricts CPU access permissions to when that specific stream's operations complete, rather than requiring the entire GPU to be idle.

facts.claim: Typical multi-threaded usage involves creating a stream, allocating managed memory with the cudaMemAttachHost flag, calling cudaStreamAttachMemAsync() to link them, synchronizing the stream, and then alternating host/device work on the data within that stream's lifecycle.
facts.note: The allocation-stream association is established once per thread and reused for repeated host/device iterations, simplifying code compared to explicit host-device copies.

facts.claim: Using the cudaMemAttachHost flag during cudaMallocManaged() ensures the allocation is initially invisible to device execution, preventing race conditions where another thread's running kernel might claim the newly allocated memory before the current thread attaches it to its private stream.
facts.note: Without this flag, a new allocation could be considered in-use on the GPU if another thread's kernel is running, blocking CPU access before attachment.

facts.claim: Destroying a stream causes attached allocations to revert to default global visibility. If global barriers are impractical, relying on cudaMemAttachHost is recommended to safely manage thread independence without needing explicit synchronization barriers around stream attachment or destruction.
facts.note: An alternative to cudaMemAttachHost is placing process-wide barriers after attachment and before stream destruction, but this is often infeasible in modular programs.

caveats: Stream attachment does not automatically restrict other variables; each managed allocation must be explicitly attached to the desired stream.
The cudaMemAttachHost flag is critical for modular programs to avoid accidental cross-thread GPU interactions during the window between allocation and attachment.
Stream destruction reverts visibility to global, requiring careful lifecycle management in multi-threaded environments.
recommended_structure: Overview: Purpose of stream attachment in multithreaded host programs
Workflow: Creating private streams, attaching memory, and iterating
Lifecycle & Caveats: Stream destruction, visibility reversion, and barrier alternatives

concept/stream-attachment-examples.title: Stream Association Examples and Visibility Rules
concept/stream-attachment-examples.summary: Provides code examples demonstrating stream attachment, host-attach flags, and explains the conservative default global visibility behavior when allocations are not stream-attached.

research_reports.context_summary: Section 24.3.2.4.4 provides concrete code examples demonstrating how `cudaStreamAttachMemAsync` modifies the default global visibility of managed memory, enabling fine-grained CPU/GPU concurrency control. It highlights the independent nature of stream attachments per variable and the mandatory synchronization step required after attachment.

facts.claim: cudaStreamAttachMemAsync overrides the default global visibility of managed allocations by binding them to a specific stream, allowing the programmer to control which streams can access the data.
facts.note: Default visibility is global; unattached variables remain visible to all streams, triggering CPU access errors if any kernel is active.

facts.claim: Attaching memory with the cudaMemcpyAttachHost flag makes the region continuously accessible to the CPU, but GPU access to that same region becomes undefined.
facts.note: Example shows `y` attached with `cudaMemcpyAttachHost`, allowing CPU write `y = 20` during kernel execution, but warning that GPU reads/writes to `y` yield undefined results.

facts.claim: Stream attachment is variable-specific; attaching one variable does not restrict or alter the visibility of other managed variables.
facts.note: Example shows `x` attached to `stream1`, but `y` remains globally visible. CPU access to `y` during kernel execution still causes an error because the system conservatively assumes the kernel might touch it.

facts.claim: cudaDeviceSynchronize() must be called immediately after cudaStreamAttachMemAsync to guarantee the attachment is established before kernel launches.
facts.note: Synchronization ensures the host attachment or stream association completes before the kernel is launched into the stream.

facts.claim: These visibility constraints and attachment rules are primarily critical for devices with compute capability < 6.0 (where concurrentManagedAccess is 0), though the API is available for all devices.
facts.note: Pre-6.x architectures lack GPU page-faulting capabilities for concurrent access, making explicit stream attachment necessary to avoid segmentation faults.

caveats: The examples assume the programmer strictly honors the visibility contract; the Unified Memory system performs no runtime error checking to verify that only the attached stream accesses the data.
While the API exists for all architectures, the concurrency benefits and segmentation fault avoidance are most relevant for pre-6.x GPUs where concurrent CPU/GPU access is otherwise blocked.

concept/stream-capture-peer-access.title: Stream Capture Peer Access
concept/stream-capture-peer-access.summary: Behavior of peer accessibility recording during stream capture, where pool access state at capture time is fixed.

research_reports.context_summary: Section 16.7.2 details how peer access settings are handled for memory allocations captured via CUDA stream capture, contrasting it with explicit graph node APIs.

facts.claim: Peer accessibility for stream-captured allocations is recorded at the exact moment the allocation call (e.g., cudaMallocAsync) executes within the capture session.
facts.note: Captures the pool's accessibility state at capture time.

facts.claim: Modifying the memory pool's peer accessibility after an allocation has been captured does not retroactively change the mappings applied by the resulting graph.
facts.note: Ensures graph execution consistency regardless of later pool configuration changes.

facts.claim: Subsequent capture sessions will reflect updated pool accessibility settings, allowing different graph nodes to inherit different peer access configurations based on when they were captured.
facts.note: Demonstrated by two separate capture blocks around cudaMemPoolSetAccess.

caveats: Peer access mappings are strictly bound to the pool state at capture time; relying on post-capture pool changes to alter graph behavior will fail.
Only ReadWrite and Device access flags are supported for peer access in this context.
recommended_structure: Overview of Stream Capture Peer Access

concept/stream-ordered-memory-allocator.title: Stream Ordered Memory Allocator
concept/stream-ordered-memory-allocator.summary: Covers the Stream Ordered Memory Allocator (CUDA 11.2+), including cudaMallocAsync/cudaFreeAsync, memory pools, caching behavior, reuse policies, and multi-GPU accessibility.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned document covers the Stream Ordered Memory Allocator as a replacement for synchronous `cudaMalloc`/`cudaFree`, detailing its core async APIs, memory pool management, caching/reuse policies, and multi-GPU accessibility controls. A legacy warning notes the guide was superseded in CUDA 13.0.

facts.claim: Purpose & Benefits
facts.note: Replaces synchronous `cudaMalloc`/`cudaFree` which block all streams. Enables stream-ordered allocation/deallocation, improves memory reuse, allows caching control via release thresholds, and supports secure IPC sharing. Reduces custom memory management overhead and enables driver optimizations.

facts.claim: Feature Querying
facts.note: Support queried via `cudaDeviceGetAttribute` with `cudaDevAttrMemoryPoolsSupported`. IPC pool support queried via `cudaDevAttrMemoryPoolSupportedHandleTypes` (CUDA 11.3+). Driver version checks recommended before querying to avoid `cudaErrorInvalidValue` on older drivers.

facts.claim: Core APIs & Stream Ordering
facts.note: Core functions are `cudaMallocAsync` and `cudaFreeAsync`, which accept a stream to define allocation availability. Pointer resolution is synchronous. Cross-stream usage requires explicit synchronization (events/streams) to guarantee access occurs after allocation. `cudaFreeAsync` can free legacy `cudaMalloc` allocations. Freeing `cudaMallocAsync` with `cudaFree` requires explicit stream/device synchronization to ensure GPU completion.

facts.claim: Memory Pool Management
facts.note: Managed via `cudaMemPool_t`. Default (implicit) pools are non-migratable device allocations without IPC support. Explicit pools created via `cudaMemPoolCreate` allow custom properties (IPC, NUMA placement). Current device pool set via `cudaDeviceSetMempool`. Allocations can target specific pools via `cudaMallocFromPoolAsync` or C++ overloads of `cudaMallocAsync`.

facts.claim: Caching & Resource Tracking
facts.note: Physical memory caching controlled by `cudaMemPoolAttrReleaseThreshold` (bytes held before releasing to OS on sync). `UINT64_MAX` disables shrinking. `cudaMemPoolTrimTo` explicitly shrinks footprint. Resource stats tracked via `cudaMemPoolAttrReservedMemCurrent/High` and `cudaMemPoolAttrUsedMemCurrent/High` (watermarks resettable via `cudaMemPoolSetAttribute`).

facts.claim: Memory Reuse Policies
facts.note: Driver reuses `cudaFreeAsync`-released memory before OS allocation. Policies: `cudaMemPoolReuseFollowEventDependencies` (uses event dependencies across streams), `cudaMemPoolReuseAllowOpportunistic` (reuses based on stream progress semantics), and `cudaMemPoolReuseAllowInternalDependencies` (inserts stream dependencies on OS allocation failure). Policies can be disabled to prevent run-to-run variance or non-deterministic serialization.

facts.claim: Multi-GPU Accessibility
facts.note: Accessibility controlled via `cudaMemPoolSetAccess`, independent of `cudaDeviceEnablePeerAccess`. Default access is restricted to the resident device (cannot be revoked). Requires peer capability (`cudaDeviceCanAccessPeer`). Settings apply to all current and future allocations from the pool; frequent changes are discouraged.

facts.claim: Legacy Status
facts.note: Document marked as legacy as of CUDA 13.0, replaced by the new CUDA Programming Guide.

caveats: The assigned evidence range (L15392-L15704) covers core APIs, pools, caching, reuse, and multi-GPU access, but cuts off before the detailed IPC memory pool section (L15705-L15824). IPC sharing mechanics (pool/export/import handles, pointer sharing, and free-ordering between processes) should be sourced from the broader context window if needed.

Code snippets in the source contain minor typos (e.g., `Cuuid64_t` instead of `uint64_t`, `cudaMemcpyAllocationTypePinned` instead of `cudaMemAllocationTypePinned`), which the lead writer should correct when drafting examples.

concept/stream-overlap.title: Stream Overlap Behavior
concept/stream-overlap.summary: Execution overlap between streams depends on command order and device support for overlapping data transfers and kernel execution. Proper ordering can hide launch latency and enable concurrent execution.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence (Section 6.2.8.5.5) sits within a broader discussion on CUDA concurrency, following subsections that define asynchronous operations, device capability queries (`asyncEngineCount`, `concurrentKernels`), stream creation, and synchronization. It serves as the practical execution-timeline guide that translates hardware capabilities and stream ordering into actual overlap behavior.

facts.claim: The degree of execution overlap between two streams is determined by the issuance order of commands across streams and specific device capabilities.
facts.note: Capabilities include overlap of data transfer and kernel execution, concurrent kernel execution, and concurrent data transfers.

facts.claim: On devices lacking concurrent data transfer support, stream operations may serialize if issued sequentially across streams.
facts.note: Example: A host-to-device copy issued to stream[1] after a device-to-host copy issued to stream[0] will wait for the latter to complete before starting.

facts.claim: Reordering commands to batch operations by type (all host-to-device, then all kernels, then all device-to-host) enables cross-stream overlap.
facts.note: With this reordering, stream[1]'s host-to-device copy overlaps with stream[0]'s kernel launch, provided the device supports data transfer/kernel overlap.

facts.claim: On devices that support concurrent data transfers, stream overlap is maximized.
facts.note: Stream[1]'s host-to-device copy can overlap with both stream[0]'s device-to-host copy and its kernel execution simultaneously.

caveats: Overlap requires page-locked host memory when host memory is involved in transfers (noted in surrounding context, but relevant to the overlap examples).
Overlap behavior is not guaranteed for correctness; inter-kernel communication between streams remains undefined.
related_terms: Overlap of Data Transfer and Kernel Execution
recommended_structure: Impact of Hardware Features (concurrent transfers vs. kernel overlap)
Command Scheduling Strategies (batching/reordering for max overlap)
Practical Examples (timeline comparison of serialized vs. overlapped streams)

concept/stream-priorities.title: Stream Priorities
concept/stream-priorities.summary: Stream priorities can be set at creation using cudaStreamCreateWithPriority(). The GPU scheduler uses these as hints to prioritize higher-priority tasks, but they do not preempt running tasks or guarantee strict ordering.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence covers section 6.2.8.5.7 Stream Priorities, detailing how relative stream priorities are defined, queried, and utilized by the GPU scheduler. It sits within the broader stream management and synchronization context (preceded by explicit/implicit synchronization and followed by programmatic dependent launch).

facts.claim: Stream priorities are assigned at creation time using cudaStreamCreateWithPriority().
facts.note: The valid priority range for a device is queried via cudaDeviceGetStreamPriorityRange(), which returns values ordered from greatest to least priority.

facts.claim: GPU scheduler behavior: Priorities serve as hints to determine task execution order, not strict guarantees.
facts.note: Pending tasks in higher-priority streams take precedence over those in lower-priority streams.

facts.claim: Preemption and dynamic updates: Higher-priority tasks do not preempt already running lower-priority tasks, and the GPU does not reassess work queues during execution.
facts.note: Dynamically increasing a stream's priority will not interrupt ongoing work.

facts.claim: Usage pattern: Typical implementation queries the priority range, then creates streams (often with the cudaStreamNonBlocking flag) using the retrieved greatest and least priority values.
facts.note: The guide emphasizes leveraging priorities to influence execution without relying on strict ordering guarantees.

caveats: Priorities are explicitly defined as non-guaranteed hints; applications should not depend on them for strict ordering.
No preemption occurs; ongoing work in lower-priority streams will not be interrupted by higher-priority tasks.

concept/surface-functions.title: Surface Functions
concept/surface-functions.summary: Reference for CUDA surface read/write functions (surf1Dread, surf1Dwrite, surf2Dread, surf2Dwrite, surf3Dread, surf3Dwrite, and their layered and cubemap variants), including boundary mode handling and coordinate specifications.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence covers the introduction to Surface Functions and the complete list of Surface Object API functions (read/write variants for 1D, 2D, 3D, layered, cubemap, and cubemap layered surfaces).

facts.claim: Surface functions provide read and write access to CUDA arrays through surface objects and require a minimum compute capability of 2.0.
facts.note: Coordinates for all surface functions are specified in byte coordinates.

facts.claim: The `boundaryMode` parameter controls how out-of-range surface coordinates are handled, with three supported modes: clamp to valid range, return zero/ignore writes, or trap (fail kernel execution).
facts.note: The default boundary mode for all listed functions is `cudaBoundaryModeTrap`.

facts.claim: The Surface Object API provides templated read and write functions for various surface types: 1D, 2D, 3D, layered (1D/2D), cubemap, and cubemap layered.
facts.note: Functions follow a consistent naming convention: `surf<Dimension><Type><read/write>()`.

caveats: The source text contains minor typographical errors in the function descriptions (e.g., describing 2D layered writes as 'one-dimensional layered surface object' in L7531), which should be corrected in the final page.
The evidence only covers the API function signatures and basic descriptions; it does not detail the underlying memory layout or performance characteristics of surface objects.

concept/synchronization-api-actions.title: Synchronization API Actions
concept/synchronization-api-actions.summary: Details how the CUDA driver synchronizes and releases memory based on stream-ordered allocation policies, including checking release thresholds and making freed allocations available.

research_reports.context_summary: Section 15.12 sits between IPC memory pool sharing (15.11.x) and allocator limitations/addendums (15.13.x) in the CUDA C Programming Guide's memory management chapter. It provides a concise conceptual bridge explaining how synchronization calls trigger deterministic memory reclamation and allocator optimization.

facts.claim: Synchronization API Actions describe the integration between CUDA sync calls and the driver-managed memory allocator.
facts.note: Directly matches target title and section heading.

facts.claim: When a sync API is invoked, the driver waits for asynchronous work to complete before returning.
facts.note: Core operational behavior.

facts.claim: Before returning, the driver determines which memory frees are guaranteed complete and makes those allocations available for reuse.
facts.note: Explains the 'actions' taken during sync.

facts.claim: Freed allocations bypass standard stream ordering and disabled allocation policies to become immediately available.
facts.note: Key allocator optimization detail.

facts.claim: The driver checks cudaMemPoolAttrReleaseThreshold during sync and releases excess physical memory back to the OS.
facts.note: Covers physical memory reclamation behavior.

caveats: Scope is narrow: focuses exclusively on driver-level allocator optimization triggered by sync calls, not general CUDA stream/device synchronization semantics.
No contradictions or scope clashes detected with the target page meaning.

concept/synchronization-functions.title: Synchronization Functions
concept/synchronization-functions.summary: Documentation for CUDA synchronization primitives including __syncthreads(), __syncthreads_count(), __syncthreads_and(), __syncthreads_or(), and __syncwarp(), detailing their behavior, usage constraints, and memory ordering guarantees.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence covers Section 10.6, detailing CUDA's block-level and warp-level synchronization intrinsics. It contrasts these visibility-guaranteeing barriers with the preceding memory fence functions (which only enforce ordering) and precedes the Mathematical Functions section.

facts.claim: __syncthreads() blocks execution until all threads in the thread block reach the barrier, ensuring all prior global and shared memory accesses by these threads are visible to the entire block. It is used to coordinate communication and prevent data hazards (read-after-write, write-after-read, write-after-write). Conditional execution is only permitted if the condition evaluates identically across the entire block; otherwise, it may cause hangs or unintended side effects.
facts.note: I will format the output as a clean markdown list as requested.

caveats: Warp-level sync `__syncwarp()` requires strict mask convergence on architectures targeting `sm_6x` or below; the union of all masks must equal the active mask, or behavior is undefined.
Block-level sync functions (`__syncthreads*`) are only supported on devices with compute capability 2.x and higher.

concept/synchronous-calls.title: Synchronous Calls
concept/synchronous-calls.summary: Synchronous functions block the host thread until the device completes the requested task. Host thread behavior (yield, block, or spin) can be configured using cudaSetDeviceFlags() before any CUDA calls.

research_reports.context_summary: The evidence is a standalone conceptual subsection (6.2.8.9) in the CUDA C Programming Guide, positioned between event-based synchronization/timing (6.2.8.8) and multi-device management (6.2.9).
