It serves as a high-level API behavior note defining the default blocking contract of the CUDA runtime.

facts.claim: Synchronous calls block the host thread until the GPU device completes the requested task, at which point control returns to the host.
facts.note: Post-completion host thread behavior (yield, block, or spin) is configurable via `cudaSetDeviceFlags()` with specific flags.

facts.claim: `cudaSetDeviceFlags()` must be invoked before any other CUDA API call on the host thread to take effect.
facts.note: The guide treats this as a brief API behavior note rather than an in-depth architectural explanation. Specific flag values and deeper synchronization mechanics are deferred to the reference manual, as noted in the source.

caveats: No contradictions with standard CUDA terminology. The source provides a concise, canonical definition suitable for a foundational concept page. Further implementation details (e.g., specific flags like `cudaDeviceScheduleSpin`) would require additional sources.

concept/system-allocated-memory-examples.title: System-Allocated Memory Examples
concept/system-allocated-memory-examples.summary: Demonstrates advanced use-cases for System-Allocated Memory on devices with full Unified Memory support. Includes examples for malloc, cudaMallocManaged, stack variables, file-scope/static variables, global-scope variables, extern variables, and file-backed memory (mmap).

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence covers Section 24.2.1 ("System-Allocated Memory: in-depth examples") and 24.2.1.1 ("File-backed Unified Memory"). It demonstrates how the GPU can access host-process-owned memory on systems with full CUDA Unified Memory support, providing code snippets for various allocation strategies and highlighting compiler/access constraints.

facts.claim: System-Allocated Memory examples are presented under the premise that devices with full CUDA Unified Memory support can access any memory owned by the host process.
facts.note: A shared `__global__` kernel (`kernel`) is used across all examples to print the first 8 characters of an input character array.

facts.claim: Examples demonstrate six distinct memory allocation/access patterns: heap allocation via `malloc`/`free`, managed allocation via `cudaMallocManaged`/`cudaFree`, stack variables, file-scope static variables, global-scope variables, and global-scope extern variables (e.g., from third-party libraries initialized via constructor attributes).
facts.note: Stack, file-scope, and global-scope variables cannot be accessed directly in `__device__`/`__global__` code; passing them as pointers to the kernel is required to avoid compilation errors.

facts.claim: Extern variables may be declared and managed by third-party libraries that do not interact with CUDA.
facts.note: File-backed memory can be accessed directly by mapping a physical file into memory using `mmap` and passing the mapped pointer to the kernel.

facts.claim: Atomic accesses to file-backed memory are unsupported on systems lacking the `hostNativeAtomicSupported` property, including systems with Linux HMM enabled.
facts.note: All examples use `cudaDeviceSynchronize()` and an `ASSERT` macro for error checking before freeing or unmapping memory.

caveats: Direct access to global variables without the `__managed__` specifier in device code causes compilation errors; the address must be passed as a kernel argument instead.
File-backed memory examples apply to both physical files and memory-backed files, but atomic operations are restricted on certain architectures (Linux HMM).
recommended_structure: Overview of System-Allocated Memory on full Unified Memory support systems
Core kernel example used across demonstrations
Memory allocation patterns (malloc, managed, stack, static, global, extern)
Access constraints & compiler behavior (pointer passing requirement)
Hardware/OS limitations (atomic operations on file-backed memory)

concept/tensor-map-alignment.title: Tensor Map Alignment and Properties
concept/tensor-map-alignment.summary: Covers negative indices, out-of-bounds handling, size/stride definitions, and alignment requirements for multi-dimensional bulk tensor asynchronous copy operations.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence sits within the TMA usage guidelines, specifically detailing parameter constraints for multi-dimensional bulk tensor asynchronous copy operations. It covers boundary handling, dimension sizing/striding, and hardware-enforced alignment rules required for Compute Capability 9.0.

facts.claim: Out-of-bounds and negative index handling differs by direction.
facts.note: When reading global-to-shared, out-of-bounds shared memory is zero-filled and top-left corner indices may be negative. When writing shared-to-global, out-of-bounds parts are allowed but top-left corner indices cannot be negative.

facts.claim: Tensor size and stride definitions include padding requirements.
facts.note: Size is the element count per dimension (must be > 1). Stride is the byte distance between same-dimension elements. Row-major matrices may require padding to satisfy stride alignment (e.g., a 4x3 int matrix uses strides of 4 and 16 bytes).

facts.claim: Strict alignment constraints apply to addresses, strides, and transfer sizes.
facts.note: Global memory address: 16-byte aligned. Global memory strides: multiples of 16 bytes. Shared memory address: 128-byte aligned. Shared memory barrier address: 8-byte aligned. Transfer size: multiple of 16 bytes. Global memory sizes only require >= 1.

caveats: Alignment rules are specific to multi-dimensional bulk tensor async copy operations on Compute Capability 9.0.
Shared memory barrier alignment is explicitly noted as guaranteed by the `cuda::barrier` type.

concept/tensor-memory-accelerator.title: Asynchronous Data Copies using the Tensor Memory Accelerator (TMA)
concept/tensor-memory-accelerator.summary: The Tensor Memory Accelerator (TMA) is a hardware feature introduced in Compute Capability 9.0 that provides an efficient mechanism for asynchronous data transfers between global memory, shared memory, and distributed shared memory. It supports both one-dimensional contiguous arrays and multi-dimensional arrays (up to 5D) via tensor maps, reducing the need for error-prone address calculations on the device. TMA operations are asynchronous, allowing the CPU/GPU to continue computation while data is transferred, and utilize specific completion mechanisms like shared memory barriers (mbarrier) and bulk async-groups.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned context covers Section 10.29 of the CUDA C Programming Guide, detailing the Tensor Memory Accelerator (TMA) introduced in Compute Capability 9.0. It explains the purpose of TMA, the distinction between 1D and multi-dimensional bulk asynchronous copies, tensor map creation and transfer, asynchronous completion mechanisms, synchronization primitives, alignment requirements, and shared memory swizzling.

facts.claim: TMA is a hardware feature introduced in Compute Capability 9.0 designed to efficiently transfer large amounts of data between global and shared memory, offloading complex address calculations for multi-dimensional arrays.
facts.note: TMA operations are formally termed 'bulk-asynchronous copies' or 'bulk tensor asynchronous copies' to distinguish them from standard async memory ops.

facts.claim: Multi-dimensional arrays (up to 5D) require a tensor map (`CUtensorMap`) created on the host via `cuTensorMapEncodeTiled` and passed to the device, typically as a `const __grid_constant__` kernel parameter.
facts.note: 1D contiguous arrays do not require a tensor map and can be transferred using simple pointer and size parameters.

facts.claim: TMA supports transfers between Global, Shared::cta, and Shared::cluster memory spaces. Multicast transfers from global memory to multiple blocks' shared memory are supported, optimized for the `sm_90a` architecture.
facts.note: Empty cells in the source table indicate unsupported source-destination pairs.

facts.claim: Completion mechanisms differ by direction: Global-to-Shared transfers use a Shared Memory Barrier (`mbarrier`), allowing any block thread to wait. Shared-to-Global/Shared::cluster transfers use a thread-local 'bulk async-group' mechanism, where only the initiating thread can wait.
facts.note: This asynchronous nature allows the initiating thread to continue computation while the hardware copies data.

facts.claim: 1D array transfers use `cuda::memcpy_async` for reads and `ptx::cp_async_bulk` for writes. Synchronization requires initializing a `cuda::barrier`, using `fence_proxy_async` to make it visible to the async proxy, and waiting via `bar.wait()`.
facts.note: Shared memory writes must be ordered before subsequent async reads using `fence_proxy_async` and `__syncthreads()`.

facts.claim: Multi-dimensional transfers use `cde::cp_async_bulk_tensor_*_global_to_shared` and `cde::cp_async_bulk_tensor_*_shared_to_global` wrappers. Out-of-bounds reads zero-fill shared memory, and negative tile indices are supported for reads.
facts.note: Tensor maps can also be passed via `__constant__` memory or global memory (requiring a `fence_proxy_tensorsmap_generic`).

facts.claim: Alignment requirements for 1D bulk operations: Global/Shared addresses must be 16-byte aligned, barrier addresses 8-byte aligned, and transfer sizes must be multiples of 16 bytes.
facts.note: Table 9 in the source details these constraints.

facts.claim: Alignment requirements for multi-dimensional bulk tensor operations: Global address 16-byte aligned, global strides multiples of 16 bytes, shared address 128-byte aligned, barrier address 8-byte aligned, and transfer sizes multiples of 16 bytes.
facts.note: Table 10 in the source details these constraints.

facts.claim: TMA supports a 'swizzle' mode (e.g., 128-byte swizzle) that rearranges data in shared memory upon load to avoid shared memory bank conflicts during subsequent kernel accesses.
facts.note: The swizzle pattern is encoded in the tensor map and automatically reversed when writing back to global memory.

caveats: The source explicitly notes that whether data transfers occur asynchronously in practice depends on the hardware implementation and may change in future architectures.
Multicast features are highly optimized for `sm_90a` and may suffer significant performance penalties on other architectures.
GCC C++ compilers may emit ABI alignment warnings when passing 64-byte aligned tensor maps as `__grid_constant__` parameters; these can be safely ignored.

concept/texture-and-surface-memory.title: Texture and Surface Memory
concept/texture-and-surface-memory.summary: CUDA provides access to texture and surface memory for performance benefits over global memory. Texture objects specify texture dimensions, texel types, read modes, coordinate normalization, addressing modes, and filtering modes (point or linear). Textures support 1D, 2D, and 3D addressing with various coordinate transformations and interpolation methods.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence constitutes the core conceptual definition of Texture and Surface Memory within Section 6.2.14. It is positioned between the 'Call Stack' and 'Texture Object API' sections, establishing the hardware abstraction, performance rationale, and configuration parameters for texture fetching before detailing the runtime API.

facts.claim: CUDA exposes a subset of GPU graphics texturing hardware to compute kernels. Reading from texture/surface memory instead of global memory can provide performance benefits.
facts.note: Surface memory is mentioned alongside texture memory in the intro but lacks detailed parameter breakdown in this snippet.

facts.claim: Texture data is read via device functions that perform a 'texture fetch'. Each fetch requires a runtime-created texture object to define behavior.
facts.note: None

facts.claim: A texture object defines dimensionality (1D, 2D, or 3D arrays), where individual elements are called 'texels'.
facts.note: None

facts.claim: Texel types are restricted to basic integers, single-precision floats, and 1/2/4-component vector types derived from them.
facts.note: None

facts.claim: Read modes include cudaReadModeElementType (raw values) and cudaReadModeNormalizedFloat (maps 8/16-bit integers to [0.0, 1.0] or [-1.0, 1.0] as floats).
facts.note: None

facts.claim: Coordinates default to non-normalized ranges [0, N-1]. Normalized coordinates use [0.0, 1.0-1/N], decoupling coordinate logic from texture dimensions.
facts.note: None

facts.claim: Addressing modes define out-of-bounds behavior. Default is clamping. Alternatives include Border (returns zero), Wrap (fractional wrapping), and Mirror (mirrored wrapping). Wrap/Mirror require normalized coordinates.
facts.note: None

facts.claim: Filtering modes are cudaFilterModePoint (nearest texel) and cudaFilterModeLinear (interpolation). Linear filtering performs 1D linear, 2D bilinear, or 3D trilinear interpolation and is restricted to floating-point texel types.
facts.note: None

caveats: The assigned evidence covers Texture Memory extensively but only references Surface Memory in the section header and intro. Surface-specific APIs or behaviors are not detailed here.
The source contains a typo 'cudaReadModeElement-Type' which should be interpreted as cudaReadModeElementType based on standard CUDA naming conventions.
recommended_structure: Configuration Parameters (Dimensionality, Types, Read Mode, Coordinates, Addressing, Filtering)

concept/texture-functions.title: Texture Functions
concept/texture-functions.summary: Comprehensive reference for CUDA texture fetching functions (tex1Dfetch, tex1D, tex2D, tex3D, texCubemap, and their layered, gradient, LOD, and sparse array variants), detailing parameters, coordinate systems, and addressing modes.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence covers Section 10.8 (Texture Functions) and Section 10.8.1 (Texture Object API) of the CUDA C Programming Guide. It is positioned between Synchronization Functions (10.6) and Surface Functions (10.9), serving as the definitive reference for device-side texture fetching operations using the modern texture object API.

facts.claim: Texture functions are templated device functions that fetch data from a cudaTextureObject_t, abstracting memory access with hardware-accelerated filtering and coordinate handling.
facts.note: The evidence lists function signatures and brief descriptions for all variants.

facts.claim: Functions are categorized by dimensionality: 1D, 2D, 3D, Layered (array textures), and Cubemap.
facts.note: Layered variants take an int layer parameter; Cubemap variants use 3D direction coordinates (x, y, z).

facts.claim: Coordinate and LOD handling is split into three variants: standard (implicit LOD), Lod (explicit float level), and Grad (LOD derived from dx/dy gradients).
facts.note: Grad variants are useful for explicit LOD control in shaders.

facts.claim: tex1Dfetch uses integer coordinates, supports only border and clamp addressing modes, performs no filtering, and may promote integer types to single-precision float.
facts.note: Specific to L7073-L7078.

facts.claim: Sparse CUDA array variants of 2D, 3D, and layered functions include an additional bool* isResident output parameter.
facts.note: If the texel is not resident, the function returns zeros and sets isResident to false.

facts.claim: tex2Dgather fetches multiple components using an optional comp parameter for component selection.
facts.note: Specific to L7125-L7133.

caveats: The provided text is strictly an API reference listing function signatures and one-line descriptions. It does not cover texture object creation, state configuration, or performance tuning, which are likely in adjacent sections (e.g., Texture Object API setup or Texture Fetching).
No contradictions found with standard CUDA texture semantics.
recommended_structure: Overview of Texture Functions in CUDA
Coordinate & LOD Variants (Standard, Lod, Grad, fetch)

concept/texture-object-api.title: Texture Object API
concept/texture-object-api.summary: Texture objects are created using cudaCreateTextureObject() with resource and texture descriptions. The API supports advanced features like anisotropic filtering, mipmapping, and sRGB. The section provides a complete code example demonstrating texture allocation, configuration, kernel execution, and cleanup.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence is Section 6.2.14.1.1, which defines the Texture Object API. It sits within the broader 'Texture and Surface Memory' chapter (6.2.14), following the conceptual introduction to texture memory (dimensionality, texels, addressing/filtering modes) and preceding the Surface Object API. The section provides the exact C struct definitions, API function signatures, and a complete host/device code example demonstrating the lifecycle of creating, using, and destroying a texture object.

facts.claim: Texture objects are created at runtime by binding a resource description and a texture description using cudaCreateTextureObject(), and must be explicitly destroyed with cudaDestroyTextureObject().
facts.note: The API decouples data binding from fetch configuration, allowing the same underlying texture to be sampled with different parameters.

facts.claim: The cudaResourceDesc struct specifies the underlying data source, such as a CUDA array (cudaResourceTypeArray).
facts.note: In the example, it is initialized to point to a cudaArray_t created via cudaMallocArray().

facts.claim: The cudaTextureDesc struct configures sampling behavior, including addressMode[3], filterMode, readMode, and normalizedCoords.
facts.note: addressMode handles out-of-bounds coordinates (clamp, wrap, border, mirror). filterMode selects point or linear interpolation. readMode controls integer-to-float conversion. normalizedCoords switches coordinate space from [0, N-1] to [0.0, 1.0).

facts.claim: Additional cudaTextureDesc fields support advanced features like sRGB conversion, maxAnisotropy, mipmapFilterMode, mipmapLevelBias, and mipmap level clamping.
facts.note: These are referenced in the struct definition but detailed in the reference manual.

facts.claim: Device kernels consume texture objects via texture functions (e.g., tex2D<float>(texObj, tu, tv)) to perform hardware-accelerated fetches.
facts.note: The example demonstrates a 2D coordinate transformation kernel that uses normalized coordinates and linear filtering to rotate/scale texture data.

caveats: The example uses the legacy tex2D<float>() syntax. Modern CUDA typically uses tex2D<float>() or the newer tex2D() template, but the API object creation remains the same.
The code sample uses C-style structs and memset for initialization, which is standard for CUDA C but may differ in CUDA C++ idioms.
recommended_structure: Key Structures & Functions (cudaResourceDesc, cudaTextureDesc, cudaCreateTextureObject, cudaDestroyTextureObject)
Configuration Parameters (addressing, filtering, read modes, normalization)
Usage Lifecycle (Host setup -> Object creation -> Device sampling -> Cleanup)

concept/this-capture-by-value.title: This Capture By Value
concept/this-capture-by-value.summary: C++17 `*this` capture mode for lambdas in CUDA, solving host memory access runtime errors by copying the object instead of capturing the pointer. Covers supported contexts and dialect requirements.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence corresponds exactly to section 18.7.4, which introduces the `*this` capture mode as a solution to the host-memory pointer capture problem in C++11/14. It details CUDA's support boundaries, memory copy behavior, and language dialect restrictions, providing complete source material for the target concept page.

facts.claim: In C++11/C++14, capturing `this` by value inside a lambda defined within a non-static member function captures the pointer, not the object. If an extended `__device__` or `__host__ __device__` lambda executes on the GPU with a `this` pointer referencing host memory, accessing member variables causes a runtime error.
facts.note: C++17 `*this` Capture Mode: C++17 introduces the `*this` capture mode, which instructs the compiler to copy the object denoted by `*this` by value instead of capturing the pointer.

facts.claim: The CUDA compiler supports `*this` capture for lambdas defined within `__device__` and `__global__` functions, as well as for extended `__device__` lambdas defined in host code (requires the `--extended-lambda` flag).
facts.note: Memory Access Behavior: When `*this` is captured by value, the GPU code accesses a copy of the object (e.g., `copy_of_star_this->xxx`), successfully avoiding host memory access issues.

facts.claim: Using `*this` capture in unannotated lambdas defined in host code, or in extended `__host__ __device__` lambdas, is an error unless explicitly enabled by the selected language dialect.
facts.note: Context Fit: The assigned evidence constitutes the exact subsection 18.7.4 within the CUDA C++ Programming Guide's lambda expressions chapter. It directly addresses the target concept by contrasting legacy C++ pointer capture behavior with the C++17 object-copy capture mode, detailing CUDA's implementation support, and providing usage examples and dialect restrictions. All facts are strictly bounded within this section.

caveats: The `--extended-lambda` nvcc flag is required for host-defined extended `__device__` lambdas.
Dialect-specific flags may be required to enable `*this` capture in `__host__ __device__` or unannotated host lambdas.
No contradictions or scope clashes detected.

concept/tma-swizzle.title: TMA Swizzle Patterns
concept/tma-swizzle.summary: Explains the use of swizzle patterns (32B, 64B, 128B, NONE) to rearrange data in shared memory, avoiding bank conflicts during matrix transpose and other access patterns. Includes offset formulas and alignment requirements.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence covers the TMA Swizzle feature (Section 10.29.3), explaining its purpose to eliminate shared memory bank conflicts, the available swizzle modes, alignment/offset requirements, index mapping formulas, and a matrix transpose example.

facts.claim: TMA swizzle modifies the shared memory layout during data transfer to eliminate bank conflicts caused by the default 32-bank, 32-bit word mapping.
facts.note: The TMA engine automatically 'swizzles' data on load from global to shared memory and 'unswizzles' it on store back to global memory. The mode is encoded in the tensor map via `CUtensorMapSwizzle`.

facts.claim: Four swizzle modes exist: `SWIZZLE_NONE` (default), 32 bytes, 64 bytes, and 128 bytes.
facts.note: Table 12 (L10830-L10834) contains OCR artifacts (e.g., `B288B`, `B464B`, `B232B`, `E_NONE`) but contextually maps to 128B, 64B, 32B, and NONE modes.

facts.claim: Swizzle granularity is fixed at 16 bytes, organizing data into 16-byte chunks mapped to subgroups of four banks.
facts.note: The inner dimension of the shared memory block must be ≤ the swizzle width. If the swizzle width exceeds the inner dimension, shared memory must be allocated to accommodate the full swizzle width.

facts.claim: Global memory must be aligned to 128 bytes when using swizzle. Shared memory should be aligned to the swizzle pattern's repeat size (128 bytes) to avoid offset issues.
facts.note: If shared memory is misaligned relative to the swizzle repeat size, an offset occurs between the pattern and the buffer.

facts.claim: Offset calculation depends on the swizzle mode and shared memory pointer alignment: `offset = (reinterpret_cast<uintptr_t>(smem_ptr)/128)%N`, where N is 8 (128B), 4 (64B), or 2 (32B).
facts.note: The logical index relation follows `smem[y][x] <-> smem[y][((y+offset)%N)^x]`, where `^` is bitwise XOR. This formula adjusts access patterns to match the swizzled layout.

facts.claim: Swizzle is particularly useful for operations like matrix transposes, where row-major global data is loaded and accessed column-wise in shared memory.
facts.note: Using `CU_TENSOR_MAP_SWIZZLE_128B` with an 8x8 `int4` matrix eliminates the 8-way bank conflicts that would otherwise occur during column-wise storage and access.

caveats: Table 12 in the source text contains significant OCR/formatting errors (e.g., `B288B bytes` instead of `SWIZZLE_128B`). The lead writer should reconstruct the table using the corrected values inferred from the context and standard CUDA naming conventions.
The provided example kernel (L10666-L10793) is explicitly noted as illustrative rather than performant or scalable beyond the given dimensions.

concept/toolkit-and-driver-compatibility.title: Toolkit and Driver Compatibility
concept/toolkit-and-driver-compatibility.summary: Requirements for 12.1 Toolkit/r530 driver+ for kernels with >4KB parameters, and link compatibility rules across toolkit revisions.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence falls under the broader topic of __global__ function argument processing (Section 18.5.10.3), specifically detailing version requirements and linking constraints for kernels with large parameter sizes.

facts.claim: Developers must use CUDA Toolkit 12.1 and Driver r530 (or higher) to compile, launch, and debug kernels that accept parameters larger than 4KB.
facts.note: This is a hard requirement for any kernel parameter exceeding the 4KB threshold.

facts.claim: Launching kernels with parameters larger than 4KB on drivers older than r530 will result in the CUDA_ERROR_NOT_SUPPORTED error.
facts.note: This applies to the runtime execution phase on older driver versions.

facts.claim: When linking device objects, if at least one object contains a kernel with a parameter larger than 4KB, all device objects must be recompiled from their respective sources using Toolkit 12.1 or higher before linking.
facts.note: Failure to recompile all objects with the required toolkit version will result in a linker error.

caveats: These compatibility rules are specifically triggered by the 4KB parameter size threshold for __global__ functions. Smaller parameters do not have these specific version/linking constraints according to this text.

concept/unified-memory-atomics.title: Unified Memory Atomic Accesses & Synchronization Primitives
concept/unified-memory-atomics.summary: Covers CUDA Unified Memory support for atomic operations, heterogeneous synchronization primitives, and page fault behavior on systems with/without CPU and GPU page tables.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence corresponds to section 24.2.2.4 of the CUDA C++ Programming Guide. It details how Unified Memory handles concurrent host-device interactions, specifically focusing on atomic operations and synchronization primitives, while contrasting the performance and support implications between hardware-coherent (combined page tables) and software-coherent (separate page tables) system architectures.

facts.claim: Unified Memory supports all atomic operations for both host and device threads, enabling concurrent access to the same shared memory location.
facts.note: The CUDA C++ standard library provides heterogeneous synchronization primitives tuned for concurrent host-device use, including cuda::atomic, cuda::atomic_ref, cuda::barrier, and cuda::semaphore.

facts.claim: On systems with combined CPU/GPU page tables (hardware coherent), host-device atomics do not require page faults, though they remain subject to other standard memory access faults.
facts.note: On systems with separate CPU/GPU page tables (software coherent), atomic accesses from the device to file-backed host memory are unsupported and exhibit undefined behavior.

facts.claim: On software coherent systems, atomic accesses to unified memory may incur page faults, leading to significant latencies.
facts.note: Some GPU atomics to CPU memory on software coherent systems may avoid page faults; developers can verify supported operations using the command nvidia-smi -q | grep "Atomic Caps Outbound".

facts.claim: The guide provides an example using cuda::atomic_ref to atomically store and load a value in file-backed memory (mapped via mmap), demonstrating concurrent host-device access.
facts.note: None

caveats: Hardware coherency drastically changes the latency profile of atomics; software coherent systems must account for page fault overhead when using Unified Memory atomics.
contradictions: None identified.
recommended_structure: Overview of Unified Memory atomic support
Hardware vs. Software Coherency Impacts on Atomics
Code Example: Using cuda::atomic_ref with Unified Memory

concept/unified-memory-compute-capabilit.title: Unified Memory on Devices with Compute Capability 6.x+
concept/unified-memory-compute-capabilit.summary: Describes full support for CUDA Managed Memory on devices with compute capability 6.x or higher but without pageable memory access, noting the exclusion of system allocators.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence (L21816-L21825) specifically addresses Compute Capability 6.x+ devices that lack pageable memory access, categorizing them under 'CUDA Managed Memory support' rather than full 'Unified Memory support'. The surrounding context (L21826-L21856) contrasts this with pre-6.x architectures and Windows platforms, which rely on Managed Memory v1.0 with limited migration, no oversubscription, and strict concurrency rules.

facts.claim: For Compute Capability 6.x or higher devices that lack pageable memory access, CUDA Managed Memory is fully supported and coherent.
facts.note: This defines the specific hardware subset covered by the assigned evidence.

facts.claim: The programming model and performance tuning for 6.x+ devices without pageable memory access are largely similar to devices with full CUDA Unified Memory support.
facts.note: Establishes the baseline behavior for developers targeting these devices.

facts.claim: A key constraint for 6.x+ devices without pageable memory access is that system allocators cannot be used to allocate memory.
facts.note: Explicitly restricts memory allocation methods for this hardware subset.

facts.claim: The 'System-Allocated Memory: in-depth examples' and 'Hardware/Software Coherency' sections of the CUDA C Programming Guide do not apply to 6.x+ devices without pageable memory access.
facts.note: Clarifies which documentation sections are irrelevant for this specific compute capability tier.

facts.claim: Contextual Contrast: Unlike pre-6.x architectures or Windows platforms (which use Managed Memory v1.0), 6.x+ devices introduce GPU page faulting. This allows fine-grained on-demand data movement and generally lifts the strict concurrency restrictions where CPU access to managed memory was previously forbidden while the GPU was active.
facts.note: Framing context from the assigned document showing how 6.x+ fundamentally changed the Unified Memory landscape compared to older hardware.

caveats: The assigned evidence strictly applies to Compute Capability 6.x+ devices *without* pageable memory access. Devices with full Unified Memory support (which do support pageable memory) follow a slightly different model regarding system allocators and coherency.
recommended_structure: 1. Overview of Compute Capability 6.x+ Unified Memory (distinguishing between full support and Managed Memory only).
2. Devices with Only CUDA Managed Memory Support (6.x+ without pageable memory access).
3. Programming Model and Constraints (allocator limitations, excluded guide sections).
4. Comparison to Pre-6.x/Windows (page faulting, concurrency, and migration improvements).

concept/unified-memory-memcpy-memset.title: Memcpy()/Memset() Behavior With Unified Memory
concept/unified-memory-memcpy-memset.summary: Explains how cudaMemcpy*() and cudaMemset*() accept unified memory pointers, the role of cudaMemcpyKind as a performance hint, and best practices for performance optimization.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence falls under section 24.2.2.5, which details how standard memory transfer and initialization APIs interact with the Unified Memory programming model. The surrounding context covers hardware/software coherency, page faulting, and migration behaviors, establishing that memory residency is dynamic and architecture-dependent.

facts.claim: cudaMemcpy*() and cudaMemset*() natively accept unified memory pointers as arguments.
facts.note: The direction parameter (cudaMemcpyKind) in cudaMemcpy*() serves as a performance hint rather than a strict operational constraint. Providing an inaccurate hint can trigger unnecessary page faults or data migrations, significantly impacting performance.

facts.claim: When the physical location of the unified memory is known, developers should specify an accurate cudaMemcpyKind hint.
facts.note: If the memory location is unknown or the provided hint is inaccurate, cudaMemcpyDefault is the preferred fallback to avoid performance penalties.

facts.claim: These APIs should not be used for memory initialization; buffers must always be populated (initialized) beforehand.
facts.note: For System-Allocated Memory specifically, cudaMemcpy*() should be avoided entirely. Instead, developers should launch a GPU kernel or use a standard CPU memory copy algorithm like std::memcpy.

caveats: The performance impact of incorrect cudaMemcpyKind hints is directly tied to the underlying system's coherency model (hardware vs. software) and whether CPU/GPU page tables are present, as migration costs vary by architecture.
recommended_structure: API Compatibility (Acceptance of Unified Memory Pointers)

concept/unified-memory-performance-hints.title: Unified Memory Performance Hints
concept/unified-memory-performance-hints.summary: Introduces performance hints (cudaMemPrefetchAsync, cudaMemAdvise, etc.) that allow applications to provide CUDA with additional information to optimize data placement and migration without altering application semantics.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence (L21193-L21200) is the introductory section (24.1.2.8) for Unified Memory Performance Hints. It sits between GPU Memory Oversubscription (24.1.2.7) and specific hint APIs like Data Prefetching (24.1.2.8.1), Memory Discarding (24.1.2.8.2), and Data Usage Hints (24.1.2.8.3). The text establishes the foundational rules for these APIs before detailing their syntax and use cases.

facts.claim: Unified Memory Performance Hints are APIs that allow applications to provide additional context to the CUDA runtime, enabling better performance decisions for memory management when the subsystem lacks sufficient information.
facts.note: Scope & Applicability

facts.claim: The hints apply to all Unified Memory (e.g., CUDA Managed memory, __managed__ variables). On systems with full CUDA Unified Memory support, they also extend to System-Allocated Memory.
facts.note: Semantic Impact

facts.claim: These APIs are strictly performance-oriented. They do not alter application semantics, correctness, or results, and can be added or removed anywhere in the codebase without side effects.
facts.note: Usage Guideline

facts.claim: Developers should only utilize these hints when they demonstrably improve application performance.
facts.note: Context Fit

facts.claim: The assigned evidence serves as the conceptual foundation for Section 24.1.2.8, framing the subsequent subsections (Data Prefetching, Memory Discarding, Data Usage Hints) by clarifying their non-semantic nature and cross-platform applicability. No scope clashes or contradictions with the target page concept were detected.
facts.note: Framing & Clash Detection

caveats: The evidence explicitly states these are hints, not guarantees. Performance improvements are workload-dependent.
Applicability to System-Allocated Memory is conditional on full CUDA Unified Memory support at runtime.
contradictions: None detected. The target slug/title aligns perfectly with the source section 24.1.2.8.

concept/unified-memory-vs-mapped-memory.title: Difference between Unified Memory and Mapped Memory
concept/unified-memory-vs-mapped-memory.summary: Explains the core difference: Mapped Memory lacks guaranteed support for all memory operations (e.g., atomics) across systems, while Unified Memory guarantees portability for these operations.

research_reports.context_summary: The surrounding context covers the broader Unified Memory ecosystem, including `cudaMallocManaged`, `__managed__` variables, pointer attribute detection, runtime capability checks, oversubscription, and prefetching APIs. Section 24.1.2.4 sits within this feature set as a clarifying comparison to older or alternative memory mapping approaches.

facts.claim: Unified Memory guarantees full memory access semantics (including atomics) across all supported systems, whereas CUDA Mapped Memory does not.
facts.note: Mapped Memory supports a limited, guaranteed subset of operations, but this subset is available on a wider range of systems than Unified Memory.

facts.claim: The evidence serves as a direct comparison note within the Unified Memory documentation, highlighting the trade-off between full hardware feature support and broader legacy/limited hardware compatibility.
facts.note: No contradictions with surrounding context. The distinction aligns with the guide's broader theme of balancing portability against feature completeness.

facts.claim: Source contains minor typos ("Diference", "diference", "memory memory") that should be corrected during page generation.
facts.note: Framing/terminology: The guide uses "CUDA Mapped Memory" to distinguish it from the newer, fully-featured "Unified Memory" abstraction.

caveats: The source text contains minor typos ("Diference", "diference", "memory memory") that should be corrected during page generation.
Framing/terminology: The guide uses "CUDA Mapped Memory" to distinguish it from the newer, fully-featured "Unified Memory" abstraction.

concept/unified-virtual-address-space.title: Unified Virtual Address Space
concept/unified-virtual-address-space.summary: In 64-bit processes, a single address space covers the host and all devices (compute capability 2.0+). Pointers can be queried for location using cudaPointerGetAttributes(), and cudaMemcpyDefault can be used for transfers. cudaHostAlloc() creates portable memory accessible directly by kernels without explicit device pointer conversion.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence falls under Section 6.2.10, which details the Unified Virtual Address Space (UVAS). The surrounding context (L3535, L3569) highlights how UVAS simplifies Peer-to-Peer (P2P) memory access and inter-device copies by allowing a single pointer to reference memory across devices.

facts.claim: In 64-bit processes, a single virtual address space encompasses the host and all devices of compute capability 2.0 or higher, covering all host and device memory allocations made via the CUDA API.
facts.note: UVAS is strictly limited to 64-bit processes and CC 2.0+ devices.

facts.claim: Memory location (host or device) can be determined directly from the pointer value using cudaPointerGetAttributes().
facts.note: Eliminates the need for separate host/device pointer tracking.

facts.claim: The cudaMemcpyKind parameter in cudaMemcpy*() functions can be set to cudaMemcpyDefault to auto-detect source and destination locations from the pointers. This also applies to host pointers not allocated via CUDA, provided the current device uses unified addressing.
facts.note: Simplifies memory copy calls by removing explicit cudaMemcpyHostToDevice, etc.

facts.claim: Host memory allocated with cudaHostAlloc() is automatically portable across all devices using the unified address space. Pointers can be used directly in device kernels without needing cudaHostGetDevicePointer().
facts.note: Removes the explicit mapping step required in older CUDA versions.

facts.claim: Applications can verify if a specific device uses the unified address space by checking if the unifiedAddressing device property equals 1.
facts.note: Standard runtime query method for capability detection.

facts.claim: The unified address space allows the same pointer to address memory across different devices, enabling direct P2P memory access and inter-device memory copies without staging through the host.
facts.note: Context framing: UVAS is the foundational requirement for simplified P2P pointer usage.

caveats: UVAS is only available for 64-bit processes.
Requires compute capability 2.0 or higher.
No contradictions or scope clashes detected in the assigned evidence.
recommended_structure: Pointer & Memory Management Benefits (cudaPointerGetAttributes, cudaMemcpyDefault, cudaHostAlloc portability)

concept/virtual-address-reservation.title: Reserving a Virtual Address Range
concept/virtual-address-reservation.summary: Explains cuMemAddressReserve for carving out virtual address space independent of physical memory, analogous to mmap/VirtualAlloc.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence (Section 14.4) details the process of reserving a virtual address range within CUDA's Virtual Memory Management (VMM) APIs. It explains the separation of address space and physical memory, the `cuMemAddressReserve` API, size constraints, cross-device mapping capabilities, and the release process via `cuMemAddressFree`.

facts.claim: In CUDA Virtual Memory Management (VMM), the notions of virtual address and physical memory are distinct. Applications must carve out an address range to hold physical memory allocations (such as those made by `cuMemCreate`).
facts.note: This establishes the foundational concept for why reservation is necessary.

facts.claim: The reserved virtual address range must be at least as large as the sum of the sizes of all physical memory allocations the user plans to place within it.
facts.note: Defines the sizing constraint for the reservation.

facts.claim: Applications reserve a virtual address range using the `cuMemAddressReserve` function. The function returns the starting virtual address of the reserved range in a `CUdeviceptr` variable.
facts.note: Details the primary API and its return value.

facts.claim: A newly reserved virtual address range initially has no device or host physical memory associated with it.
facts.note: Describes the initial state of the reserved range before mapping.

facts.claim: The reserved range can be mapped to memory chunks belonging to any device in the system, providing the application with a continuous virtual address range backed by memory from different devices.
facts.note: Highlights the cross-device mapping capability of the reserved range.

facts.claim: Applications return the virtual address range to CUDA using `cuMemAddressFree`. The entire VA range must be unmapped before calling `cuMemAddressFree`.
facts.note: Details the release process and the unmapping prerequisite.

facts.claim: These reservation and release functions are conceptually similar to `mmap`/`munmap` on Linux or `VirtualAlloc`/`VirtualFree` on Windows.
facts.note: Provides an OS-level analogy for developers familiar with standard memory management.

facts.claim: Code example: `CUresult result = cuMemAddressReserve(&ptr, size, 0, 0, 0);` where alignment is 0 for default alignment.
facts.note: Provides the exact function signature and parameter usage from the source.

caveats: The source text contains a typo ('diferent devices'), which has been corrected to 'different' in the findings.

The evidence strictly covers the reservation step (Section 14.4) and does not detail the subsequent mapping (`cuMemMap`) or access control (`cuMemSetAccess`) steps, though it notes that physical memory must be mapped to the reserved range.

concept/virtual-aliasing.title: Virtual Aliasing Support
concept/virtual-aliasing.summary: Describes creating multiple virtual memory mappings (proxies) to the same allocation via cuMemMap, and handling coherence/fencing with fence.proxy.alias.

research_reports.context_summary: The evidence sits within the CUDA Virtual Memory Management (VMM) API workflow. It follows physical memory allocation (Sec 14.3) and virtual address reservation (Sec 14.4), and precedes memory mapping (Sec 14.6) and access control (Sec 14.7). It specifically addresses the memory consistency implications of mapping a single physical allocation to multiple virtual addresses.

facts.claim: Virtual aliasing allows creating multiple virtual memory mappings or "proxies" to the same physical allocation by calling cuMemMap with different virtual addresses.
facts.note: Incoherence Rule: Writes to one proxy are inconsistent and incoherent with other proxies until the writing device operation (grid launch, memcpy, memset, etc.) completes. This also applies to grids launched before the write but reading after it completes.

facts.claim: Undefined Behavior: Accessing aliases within the same kernel without synchronization is undefined. Example: writing to pointer A and immediately reading from alias B in the same __global__ function yields unpredictable results.
facts.note: Defined Behavior (Host/Stream Sync): Behavior is defined if accesses are ordered monotonically via streams or events. Read operations must wait for the preceding write operation to complete.

facts.claim: In-Kernel Synchronization: To safely access the same allocation through different proxies within a single kernel, the fence.proxy.alias PTX instruction must be used between accesses (e.g., asm volatile("fence.proxy.alias;" ::: "memory");).
facts.note: Scope/Clash Check: No contradictions. The documentation strictly defines the memory model rules for VMM proxies and provides explicit synchronization mechanisms (stream/event ordering or PTX fences) to resolve incoherence.

caveats: The incoherence rule applies unless explicitly overridden by the PTX ISA.
Aliases are only allowed at operation boundaries (e.g., stream/event synchronization or memcpy/memset boundaries).

concept/virtual-memory-management.title: Virtual Memory Management
concept/virtual-memory-management.summary: CUDA Virtual Memory Management (VMM) APIs for fine-grained control over the unified virtual address space, including physical memory allocation, VA range reservation, mapping, and interprocess communication.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence constitutes Chapter 14 of the guide, directly following Chapter 13 (Dynamic Parallelism) and preceding the remainder of the document. It introduces the Virtual Memory Management (VMM) APIs, detailing the shift from pointer-based allocation to handle-based physical memory management, VA range reservation, and platform-specific IPC/graphics interop mechanisms.

facts.claim: VMM APIs decouple virtual addresses from physical memory, enabling dynamic mapping/unmapping, multi-device contiguous VA placement, fine-grained peer access control, and legacy `cudaMalloc`-style resizing limitations.
facts.note: Introduced in CUDA 10.2; requires UVA support.

facts.claim: Device support is queried via `cuDeviceGetAttribute` using `CU_DEVICE_ATTRIBUTE_VIRTUAL_MEMORY_MANAGEMENT_SUPPORTED`.
facts.note: Must be checked before API usage.

facts.claim: Physical memory is allocated via `cuMemCreate`, returning a `CUmemGenericAllocationHandle` rather than a device pointer. Allocations must be aligned to device-specific granularity (`cuMemGetAllocationGranularity`) and freed with `cuMemRelease`.
facts.note: Initial allocations lack host/device mappings until explicitly mapped.

facts.claim: The VMM workflow requires reserving a VA range (`cuMemAddressReserve`), mapping physical allocations to it (`cuMemMap`), and explicitly granting device access rights (`cuMemSetAccess`).
facts.note: Decouples address reservation from physical backing.

facts.claim: IPC and graphics interop are handled via platform-specific shareable handles (`cuMemExportToShareableHandle`, `cuMemImportFromShareableHandle`) rather than legacy IPC functions.
facts.note: Requires setting `requestedHandleTypes` in `CUmemAllocationProp` and querying device attributes for POSIX file descriptor or Win32 handle support.

facts.claim: The document is marked as legacy as of CUDA 13.0 and has been replaced by the new CUDA Programming Guide.
facts.note: Applies to the entire chapter.

caveats: The evidence covers only the introduction, support querying, physical allocation, and shareable allocation sections of Chapter 14. It does not cover VA range reservation details, mapping/access control specifics, or virtual aliasing, which appear in the surrounding context window (L15097-L15216) but fall outside the assigned evidence range.
All VMM APIs require a system that supports Unified Virtual Addressing (UVA).
contradictions: None detected within the assigned evidence. The guide explicitly notes the deprecation of legacy `cudaMalloc` resizing workflows and legacy IPC functions in favor of the new handle-based VMM model.

concept/volatile-qualifier.title: Volatile Qualifier
concept/volatile-qualifier.summary: Limitations of the volatile keyword in CUDA, emphasizing its unsuitability for inter-thread synchronization and MMIO, with alternatives like cuda::atomic_ref and inline PTX.

research_reports.context_summary: The section follows discussions on memory space specifiers (`__managed__`, etc.) and precedes sections on pointers and operators. It specifically addresses the `volatile` qualifier's behavior and limitations in CUDA C++.

facts.claim: The `volatile` keyword is supported primarily for ISO C++ compatibility, with few non-deprecated uses remaining relevant for GPUs.
facts.note: Reads and writes to volatile qualified objects are not atomic and compile to one or more `.volatile` PTX instructions.

facts.claim: Volatile operations do not guarantee memory operation ordering or that the hardware execution count matches the PTX instruction count.
facts.note: Inter-thread synchronization is unsupported; developers must use `cuda::atomic_ref`, `cuda::atomic`, or atomic functions (e.g., `atomicAdd`, `atomicExch`) which provide synchronization guarantees and better performance.

facts.claim: Memory-Mapped I/O (MMIO) is unsupported; developers must use inline PTX MMIO operations (e.g., `st.relaxed.mmio.sys.u32`, `ld.relaxed.mmio.sys.u32`) because PTX MMIO strictly preserves access counts, whereas volatile operations may perform a non-deterministic number of accesses.
facts.note: The provided code examples demonstrate correct usage of `cuda::atomic_ref`, `cuda::atomic`, atomic functions, and inline PTX for MMIO as alternatives to `volatile`.

caveats: The guide explicitly marks `volatile` as deprecated for typical GPU concurrency and hardware I/O patterns.
No standard C++ synchronization or MMIO semantics apply to CUDA's `volatile` implementation.

concept/vulkan-interoperability.title: Vulkan Interoperability
concept/vulkan-interoperability.summary: Details Vulkan-specific interoperability, including matching CUDA devices to Vulkan physical devices via UUIDs, importing memory objects via file descriptors, NT handles, named handles, and KMT handles, and mapping them as device pointers or mipmapped arrays.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence covers the CUDA external memory interoperability subsystem, specifically detailing how to bridge Vulkan memory and synchronization objects into the CUDA runtime. It provides the complete pipeline: device matching via UUIDs, handle-type-specific import routines, and buffer/mipmapped-array mapping workflows with corresponding cleanup and conversion helpers.

facts.claim: Vulkan objects must be imported and mapped on the exact same CUDA device they were created on. Device matching is done by comparing the CUDA device UUID with the Vulkan physical device UUID.
facts.note: Vulkan physical device must not be part of a device group with >1 physical device.

facts.claim: OS support varies: Linux and Windows 10 support both dedicated and non-dedicated memory imports; Windows 7 only supports dedicated memory.
facts.note: Dedicated imports require the `cudaExternalMemoryDedicated` flag.

facts.claim: Handle type `VK_EXTERNAL_MEMORY_HANDLE_TYPE_OPAQUE_FD_BIT` maps to `cudaExternalMemoryHandleTypeOpaqueFd` (Linux). CUDA assumes ownership of the file descriptor; post-import usage is undefined.
facts.note: Imported via `cudaImportExternalMemory()`.

facts.claim: Handle type `VK_EXTERNAL_MEMORY_HANDLE_TYPE_OPAQUE_WIN32_BIT` maps to `cudaExternalMemoryHandleTypeOpaqueWin32` (Windows). CUDA does not assume ownership; the application must manually close the handle.
facts.note: Named NT handles use the same type but populate the `name` field instead of `handle`.

facts.claim: Handle type `VK_EXTERNAL_MEMORY_HANDLE_TYPE_OPAQUE_WIN32_KMT_BIT` maps to `cudaExternalMemoryHandleTypeOpaqueWin32Kmt`. These do not hold a reference to the memory and auto-destroy when other references drop.
facts.note: None.

facts.claim: Buffers are mapped to imported memory using `cudaExternalMemoryGetMappedBuffer()`. Offset and size must exactly match the Vulkan mapping. Mapped pointers are freed via `cudaFree()`.
facts.note: None.

facts.claim: Mipmapped arrays are mapped using `cudaExternalMemoryGetMappedMipmappedArray()`. Offset, dimensions, format, and mip levels must match Vulkan. If the Vulkan image is a color target, set `cudaArrayColorAttachment`. Freed via `cudaFreeMipmappedArray()`.
facts.note: None.

facts.claim: Helper functions are provided to translate Vulkan formats, extents, and image view types to CUDA equivalents (`getCudaChannelFormatDescForVulkanFormat`, `getCudaExtentForVulkanExtent`, `getCudaMipmappedArrayFlagsForVulkanImage`).
facts.note: Source cuts off mid-switch statement, but the conversion pattern is established.

caveats: The source evidence ends abruptly at L4737 during the Vulkan-to-CUDA format conversion switch statement. The lead writer should note that the full enum mapping for `VkFormat` to `cudaChannelFormatKind` is truncated in this window.
Synchronization object import (semaphores) is mentioned in the broader section header (L4524) but the detailed code/examples are cut off in this specific window.
recommended_structure: Memory Object Import (Handle types, OS limits, ownership semantics)
Resource Mapping (Buffers vs. Mipmapped Arrays, flag requirements)

concept/warp-match-functions.title: 10.20 Warp Match Functions
concept/warp-match-functions.summary: Warp match functions perform broadcast-and-compare operations of a variable between threads within a warp. Supported by compute capability 7.x+. Includes __match_any_sync and __match_all_sync. Requires mask parameter. Do not imply memory barriers.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence covers section 10.20 Warp Match Functions, detailing the `__match_any_sync` and `__match_all_sync` intrinsics, their signatures, supported types, behavioral descriptions, mask/convergence requirements, and memory ordering guarantees.

facts.claim: Purpose and Functionality
facts.note: `__match_any_sync` and `__match_all_sync` perform a broadcast-and-compare operation of a variable between threads within a warp after synchronizing threads named in a mask.

facts.claim: Hardware Support
facts.note: Supported by devices of compute capability 7.x or higher.

facts.claim: Function Signatures
facts.note: `unsigned int __match_any_sync(unsigned mask, T value);` and `unsigned int __match_all_sync(unsigned mask, T value, int *pred);`

facts.claim: Supported Data Types (T)
facts.note: `int`, `unsigned int`, `long`, `unsigned long`, `long long`, `unsigned long long`, `float`, or `double`.

facts.claim: `__match_any_sync` Behavior
facts.note: Returns a mask of threads that have the same value as the calling thread's `value` within the specified mask.

facts.claim: `__match_all_sync` Behavior
facts.note: Returns the mask if all threads in the mask have the same value for `value`; otherwise returns `0`. Sets the predicate `pred` to `true` if all values match, or `false` otherwise.

facts.claim: Convergence and Mask Requirements
facts.note: The mask indicates participating threads. A bit representing each thread's lane ID must be set to ensure hardware convergence. Each calling thread must have its own bit set in the mask, and all non-exited threads named in the mask must execute the same intrinsic with the same mask, or the result is undefined.

facts.claim: Memory Ordering
facts.note: These intrinsics do not imply a memory barrier and do not guarantee any memory ordering.

caveats: No contradictions or scope clashes detected. The evidence is self-contained and directly addresses the target page slug and title.

concept/warp-matrix-functions.title: Warp Matrix Functions
concept/warp-matrix-functions.summary: Verbatim source-backed fallback page for Warp Matrix Functions.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence covers Section 10.24 of the CUDA C Programming Guide, which documents Warp Matrix Functions (WMMA). It is positioned between the Nanosleep Function (10.23) and DPX functions (10.25), serving as the definitive reference for Tensor Core matrix multiply-accumulate operations. The text provides API specifications, precision variants, architectural constraints, and a complete usage example.

facts.claim: WMMA operations leverage Tensor Cores to accelerate D=A*B+C computations and require compute capability 7.0 or higher.
facts.note: Requires full warp cooperation and identical conditional evaluation across the warp to prevent hangs.

facts.claim: All WMMA types and functions reside in the nvcuda::wmma namespace, with sub-byte operations in nvcuda::wmma::experimental.
facts.note: The core data structure is the fragment class, which holds a matrix section distributed across warp threads. Template parameters define the role (matrix_a, matrix_b, accumulator), dimensions (m, n, k), data type (T), and layout (row_major, col_major, or void).

facts.claim: Core synchronization functions include load_matrix_sync, store_matrix_sync, fill_fragment, and mma_sync.
facts.note: load_matrix_sync and store_matrix_sync require 256-bit aligned pointers and specific ldm stride multiples. mma_sync performs D=A*B+C, supports in-place accumulation, and includes an optional satf flag to clamp infinities and NaNs. Direct element access is possible via the fragment's num_elements and x[num_elements] members.

facts.claim: Precision support includes standard mixed-precision (__half, char), alternate floating point (CC 8.0+), double precision (CC 8.0+), and sub-byte operations.
facts.note: Alternate FP includes __nv_bfloat16 (f32 range, 7-bit) and tf32 (>=10-bit, requires __float_to_tf32 conversion, limited to 16x16x8). Double precision uses .rn rounding. Sub-byte operations (u4, s4, b1) are preview features with packed storage.

facts.claim: Bitwise matrix operations are handled by bmma_sync, which performs logical operations (XOR on CC 7.5+, AND on CC 8.0+) followed by a population count (POPC) accumulation.
facts.note: Support for u4, s4, and b1 with XOR is deprecated and will be removed in sm_90.

facts.claim: Fragments are opaque, architecture-specific ABI structures. Passing them between functions compiled for different link-compatible architectures (e.g., sm_70 and sm_75) is unsafe.
facts.note: To safely transit data across compilation units, matrices must be stored to memory first. Supported matrix sizes vary by precision, commonly including 16x16x16, 32x8x16, 8x32x16, 16x16x8 (tf32), 8x8x4 (double), and 8x8x32/128 (sub-byte).

facts.claim: A typical WMMA workflow involves declaring fragments, initializing the accumulator, loading inputs, computing with mma_sync, and storing results.
facts.note: The guide provides a complete 16x16x16 matrix multiplication example demonstrating this exact sequence.

caveats: Sub-byte and experimental APIs are subject to change and may not be forward-compatible.
tf32 precision requires manual conversion and is restricted to 16x16x8 matrix dimensions.
Cross-architecture fragment passing is strictly prohibited due to opaque ABI differences.

concept/warp-reduce-functions.title: Warp Reduce Functions
concept/warp-reduce-functions.summary: Verbatim source-backed fallback page for Warp Reduce Functions.

research_reports.context_summary: Section 10.21 of the CUDA C Programming Guide details the Warp Reduce Functions, which are hardware-accelerated intrinsics for performing reduction operations across threads in a warp after synchronization.

facts.claim: The __reduce_sync intrinsics perform a reduction operation on data provided by each thread after synchronizing the threads specified in a mask, and are supported on devices with compute capability 8.x or higher.
facts.note: Type constraints: T can be unsigned or signed for arithmetic operations (add, min, max), but unsigned only for logical operations (and, or, xor).

facts.claim: Arithmetic variants (__reduce_add_sync, __reduce_min_sync, __reduce_max_sync) exist for both unsigned and int types.
facts.note: Logical variants (__reduce_and_sync, __reduce_or_sync, __reduce_xor_sync) are available only for the unsigned type.

facts.claim: Arithmetic functions return the result of applying add, min, or max across the value provided by each participating thread.
facts.note: Logical functions return the result of applying AND, OR, or XOR across the value provided by each participating thread.

facts.claim: The mask parameter specifies participating threads via lane ID bits. All participating threads must have their corresponding bit set in the mask and must execute the same intrinsic with the same mask to ensure proper hardware convergence.
facts.note: Failure to meet convergence requirements results in undefined behavior.

facts.claim: These intrinsics do not imply a memory barrier and do not guarantee any memory ordering.
facts.note: None

caveats: The evidence strictly covers compute capability 8.x+ hardware. Older devices may require manual warp-level reduction or deprecated pre-sync intrinsics (not covered in this specific source window).
Logical operations are restricted to unsigned types; signed types are only supported for arithmetic operations.

concept/warp-shuffle-functions.title: Warp Shuffle Functions
concept/warp-shuffle-functions.summary: Verbatim source-backed fallback page for Warp Shuffle Functions.

research_reports.context_summary: Section 10.22 of the CUDA C Programming Guide details warp shuffle intrinsics, their signatures, supported types, addressing modes, convergence requirements, and example algorithms (broadcast, scan, reduction).

facts.claim: Warp shuffle functions exchange variables between threads within a warp without using shared memory, moving 4 or 8 bytes per thread depending on the data type.
facts.note: The source explicitly states the exchange occurs simultaneously for all active threads within the warp and named in the mask.

facts.claim: The sync variants (__shfl_sync, __shfl_up_sync, __shfl_down_sync, __shfl_xor_sync) are supported on devices with compute capability 5.0 or higher. The non-sync variants were deprecated in CUDA 9.0 and removed for compute capability 7.x+.
facts.note: None

facts.claim: Supported data types (T) include int, unsigned int, long, unsigned long, long long, unsigned long long, float, and double. With cuda_fp16.h, __half and __half2 are supported. With cuda_bf16.h, __nv_bfloat16 and __nv_bfloat162 are supported.
facts.note: None

facts.claim: Four addressing modes are available: __shfl_sync (direct indexed lane), __shfl_up_sync (lower ID), __shfl_down_sync (higher ID), and __shfl_xor_sync (bitwise XOR of caller's lane ID).
facts.note: None

facts.claim: The 'width' parameter divides the warp into independent subsections. It must be a power of two in the range [1, warpSize]. Logical lane IDs reset to 0 at the start of each subsection.
facts.note: None

facts.claim: Threads may only read data from actively participating threads. Reading from an inactive target thread results in undefined values.
facts.note: None

facts.claim: All participating threads must execute the same intrinsic with the same mask. Each thread must set its own lane bit in the mask. Failure to converge results in undefined behavior.
facts.note: None

facts.claim: These intrinsics do not imply a memory barrier and do not guarantee any memory ordering.
facts.note: None

facts.claim: Common use cases include broadcasting a single value across a warp, performing inclusive prefix scans across sub-partitions, and executing warp-level reductions (e.g., butterfly reduction using XOR mode).
facts.note: None

caveats: The source explicitly notes that the non-sync variants are deprecated and removed on CC 7.x+, so the page should emphasize the _sync variants.
Convergence is strictly required: all non-exited threads in the mask must execute the same intrinsic with the same mask, otherwise results are undefined.

concept/warp-vote-functions.title: 10.19 Warp Vote Functions
concept/warp-vote-functions.summary: Warp vote functions allow threads in a warp to perform reduction-and-broadcast operations on integer predicates. Includes __all_sync, __any_sync, __ballot_sync, and __activemask. Requires mask parameter specifying participating threads. Do not imply memory barriers.

research_reports.context_summary: Section 10.19 details the Warp Vote Functions, which perform reduction-and-broadcast operations across warp threads. It lists four sync-based intrinsics, notes the deprecation/removal of their non-sync predecessors, and specifies mask requirements and memory ordering guarantees.

facts.claim: Warp vote functions enable threads in a warp to perform a reduction-and-broadcast operation by evaluating an integer predicate from each thread against zero.
facts.note: The results are combined across active threads and a single value is broadcast back to each participating thread.

facts.claim: The four current warp vote intrinsics are __all_sync, __any_sync, __ballot_sync, and __activemask.
facts.note: These functions require a mask parameter (except __activemask) to specify participating threads.

facts.claim: __all_sync(mask, predicate) returns non-zero if and only if the predicate evaluates to non-zero for all non-exited threads specified in the mask.
facts.note: None

facts.claim: __any_sync(mask, predicate) returns non-zero if and only if the predicate evaluates to non-zero for any non-exited thread specified in the mask.
facts.note: None

facts.claim: __ballot_sync(mask, predicate) returns an integer bitmask where the Nth bit is set if and only if the predicate is non-zero for the Nth thread and that thread is active.
facts.note: None

facts.claim: __activemask() returns a 32-bit integer mask of all currently active threads in the calling warp, with the Nth bit set for the Nth active lane.
facts.note: Exited threads are always marked inactive. Threads convergent at the call are not guaranteed to remain convergent at subsequent instructions unless those instructions are synchronizing warp-builtin functions.

facts.claim: The legacy functions __any, __all, and __ballot were deprecated in CUDA 9.0 and are completely removed when targeting compute capability 7.x or higher.
facts.note: Developers must use the _sync variants for modern architectures.

facts.claim: For __all_sync, __any_sync, and __ballot_sync, the mask must have the bit corresponding to each participating thread's lane ID set to ensure hardware convergence.
facts.note: Each calling thread must set its own bit in the mask, and all non-exited threads in the mask must execute the same intrinsic with the identical mask; otherwise, the result is undefined.

facts.claim: These warp vote intrinsics do not imply a memory barrier and do not guarantee any memory ordering.
facts.note: None

caveats: The source explicitly warns that convergence at an __activemask() call is not guaranteed to persist for subsequent instructions unless those instructions are synchronizing warp-builtin functions.
Undefined behavior occurs if non-exited threads named in the mask do not execute the exact same intrinsic with the exact same mask.

dynamic-global-memory-allocation.title: Dynamic Global Memory Allocation and Operations
dynamic-global-memory-allocation.summary: Dynamic global memory allocation is supported on compute capability 2.x and higher. Functions include __device__ malloc(), __nv_aligned_device_malloc(), free(), memcpy(), and memset(). Memory is allocated from a fixed-size device heap (default 8 MB, configurable via cudaDeviceSetLimit). Allocated memory persists for the context lifetime and can be shared across threads and kernel launches. It cannot be freed via host-side runtime APIs, nor can host-allocated memory be freed via device free(). Examples cover per-thread, per-block, and cross-kernel-persistence allocation patterns.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned section (10.36) details the CUDA device-side API for dynamic global memory allocation, covering function signatures, heap configuration, lifetime rules, interoperability limits, and usage examples.

facts.claim: Dynamic global memory allocation is supported on devices with compute capability 2.x and higher.
facts.note: Hardware requirement.

facts.claim: The device-side API provides malloc, __nv_aligned_device_malloc, free, memcpy, and memset functions for in-kernel memory management.
facts.note: Function signatures and basic descriptions.

facts.claim: malloc guarantees 16-byte alignment; __nv_aligned_device_malloc allows custom alignment (must be a non-zero power of 2). Both return NULL on failure.
facts.note: Alignment guarantees and failure handling.

facts.claim: free() deallocates memory; passing NULL is ignored, and repeated calls with the same pointer result in undefined behavior.
facts.note: Deallocation rules.

facts.claim: Allocated memory persists for the lifetime of the CUDA context or until explicitly freed, and is accessible across threads and subsequent kernel launches.
facts.note: Lifetime and cross-kernel scope.

facts.claim: The device heap has a fixed size that must be set before module load. The default is 8 MB, configurable via cudaDeviceSetLimit(cudaLimitMallocHeapSize, size). It cannot be changed after module load and does not resize dynamically.
facts.note: Heap configuration and constraints.

facts.claim: Device heap memory is separate from host-side allocations (e.g., cudaMalloc).
facts.note: Memory space separation.

facts.claim: Strict interoperability boundaries exist: device malloc memory cannot be freed by host runtime functions, host memory cannot be freed by device free(), and device malloc memory cannot be used in host/runtime API calls like cudaMemcpy.
facts.note: API interoperability constraints.

facts.claim: Common usage patterns include per-thread allocation, per-block allocation (using shared memory to share the pointer and __syncthreads() for synchronization), and cross-kernel persistence where allocations are stored in __device__ arrays and freed in a dedicated cleanup kernel.
facts.note: Practical implementation patterns from examples.

caveats: The documentation explicitly warns that heap size is locked after module load, requiring careful pre-launch configuration.
Cross-kernel usage requires careful synchronization and ownership management to avoid double-freeing or use-after-free, as memory persists across launches.
recommended_structure: Core API Functions (malloc, aligned malloc, free, memcpy, memset)

entity/__assume.title: 10.18.3 __assume()
entity/__assume.summary: Compiler optimization hint. Allows compiler to assume Boolean argument is true. Undefined behavior if false at runtime. Unspecified behavior if argument has side effects.

research_reports.context_summary: The target function `__assume()` is documented in Section 10.18.3 as a compiler optimization hint. It sits alongside related intrinsics like `__builtin_assume()` and `__builtin_expect()` within the 'Compiler Optimization Hint Functions' section.

facts.claim: Signature and Purpose
facts.note: Declared as `void __assume(bool exp)`. It serves as a compiler optimization hint that allows the compiler to assume the Boolean argument is true.

facts.claim: Runtime Behavior & Side Effects
facts.note: If the argument is false at runtime, behavior is undefined. If the argument contains side effects, behavior is unspecified.

facts.claim: Example Usage
facts.note: Demonstrates assuming an index is within bounds to aid optimization: `__device__ int get(int *ptr, int idx) { __assume(idx <= 2); return ptr[idx]; }`

facts.claim: Platform Restrictions
facts.note: Explicitly restricted to the `cl.exe` host compiler. Other compiler hint functions in the same section are supported on all platforms.

caveats: The documentation restricts `__assume()` to the `cl.exe` host compiler, which is atypical for general CUDA device code and may indicate a platform-specific or legacy constraint. Verify against current nvcc behavior if cross-platform compatibility is required.

facts.claim: Signature

facts.claim: Purpose
facts.note: Serves as a compiler optimization hint that asserts the Boolean argument `exp` is true, enabling the optimizer to make assumptions about control flow or data properties.

facts.claim: Runtime Behavior & Caveats
facts.note: If `exp` evaluates to false at runtime, the program exhibits undefined behavior. If `exp` contains side effects, the behavior is unspecified.

facts.claim: Usage Example
facts.note: Demonstrates device-side usage to hint index bounds before array access: `__builtin_assume(idx <= 2); return ptr[idx];`.

facts.claim: Platform Support & Restrictions
facts.note: Unlike `__assume()` (restricted to `cl.exe`), `__builtin_assume()` is supported on all platforms. If the host compiler lacks native support, it must be called inside a `__device__`/`__global__` function body or guarded by the `__CUDA_ARCH__` macro.

caveats: The documentation uses the term "side efects" (typo in source); behavior is unspecified if present.

entity/__builtin-expect.title: 10.18.4 __builtin_expect()
entity/__builtin-expect.summary: Compiler optimization hint. Indicates expected value of expression for branch prediction. Returns value of exp.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence falls under Section 10.18 'Compiler Optimization Hint Functions', which groups `__builtin_expect()` with other compiler hint intrinsics like `__builtin_assume_aligned()`, `__builtin_assume()`, `__assume()`, and `__builtin_unreachable()`.

facts.claim: Function signature
facts.note: Declared as `long __builtin_expect(long exp, long c)`.

facts.claim: Purpose
facts.note: Indicates to the compiler that `exp == c` is expected, primarily used to provide branch prediction information. The function returns the value of `exp`.

facts.claim: Usage example
facts.note: `if (__builtin_expect(var, 0)) doit();` tells the compiler that `var == 0` is likely, marking the `if` block body as unlikely to execute at runtime.

facts.claim: Section context
facts.note: Categorized under 'Compiler Optimization Hint Functions' (Section 10.18), alongside `__builtin_assume_aligned()`, `__builtin_assume()`, `__assume()`, and `__builtin_unreachable()`.

entity/__builtin-unreachable.title: 10.18.5 __builtin_unreachable()
entity/__builtin-unreachable.summary: Compiler optimization hint. Indicates control flow never reaches this point. Undefined behavior if reached at runtime.

It shares the platform and invocation restrictions detailed in section 10.18.6 with other compiler hints like `__builtin_expect()` and `__builtin_assume()`.

facts.claim: Signature

facts.claim: Purpose
facts.note: Informs the compiler that control flow will never reach the point of invocation, serving as an optimization hint.

facts.claim: Runtime Behavior
facts.note: If execution actually reaches this point at runtime, the program exhibits undefined behavior.

facts.claim: Example Usage
facts.note: Typically placed in a `switch` statement's `default` case to signal that the default path is unreachable.

facts.claim: Platform Support & Invocation Restrictions
facts.note: Supported on all platforms (unlike `__assume()`, which is restricted to `cl.exe`). If the host compiler does not natively support it, it must be invoked within a `__device__`/`__global__` function body, or guarded by the `__CUDA_ARCH__` macro.

caveats: None detected. The documentation is straightforward and aligns with standard compiler intrinsic behavior.
contradictions: None.

entity/__nv-atomic-compare-exchange.title: 10.14.1.11 __nv_atomic_compare_exchange() (CUDA 12.8)
entity/__nv-atomic-compare-exchange.summary: Generic atomic compare-and-exchange function introduced in CUDA 12.8. Compares value at ptr with value at expected. If equal, returns true and stores desired to ptr. Otherwise, returns false and stores ptr value to expected. Ignores weak parameter, uses stronger of success/failure orders. Supports 2, 4, 8, or 16-byte types. Requires sm_60+ for memory order/scope, sm_90+ for 16-byte types, sm_70+ for 2-byte types, and sm_90+ for cluster scope. Arguments order and scope must be integer literals.

research_reports.context_summary: Falls within Section 10.14.1 (Atomic Functions) of the CUDA C Programming Guide. It is grouped with other CUDA 12.8 generic atomic functions that introduce memory ordering and thread scope support, positioned between `__nv_atomic_exchange_n()` and `__nv_atomic_compare_exchange_n()`.

facts.claim: Function signature
facts.note: __device__ bool __nv_atomic_compare_exchange(T* ptr, T* expected, T* desired, bool weak, int success_order, int failure_order, int scope = __NV_THREAD_SCOPE_SYSTEM)

facts.claim: Operational semantics
facts.note: Reads value at ptr and compares with value at expected. On equality, returns true and writes desired to ptr. On inequality, returns false and writes original ptr value to expected. The weak parameter is ignored; the stronger of success_order and failure_order is used.

facts.claim: Supported data types
facts.note: Generic atomic compare-and-exchange; T can be any data type of size 2, 4, 8, or 16 bytes.

facts.claim: Compute capability requirements
facts.note: Memory order/thread scope: sm_60+. 2-byte types: sm_70+. 16-byte types and cluster thread scope: sm_90+.

facts.claim: Argument constraints
facts.note: success_order, failure_order, and scope must be integer literals; variables are not permitted.

facts.claim: Version introduction
facts.note: Introduced in CUDA 12.8.

caveats: The source text contains a rendering artifact in the signature (`bool ,<sub>→</sub>weak`), but the semantic description confirms `weak` is a standard boolean parameter that is explicitly ignored by the implementation.

Supports integral types of 2, 4, 8, or 16 bytes. Requires sm_60+ for memory order/scope, sm_90+ for 16-byte types, sm_70+ for 2-byte types, and sm_90+ for cluster scope. Arguments order and scope must be integer literals.

facts.note: Returns `bool`. Parameters: `T* ptr`, `T* expected`, `T desired`, `bool weak`, `int success_order`, `int failure_order`, `int scope = __NV_THREAD_SCOPE_SYSTEM`.

facts.claim: Core atomic operation
facts.note: Compares value at `ptr` with value at `expected`. On match: returns `true`, stores `desired` to `ptr`. On mismatch: returns `false`, stores current `ptr` value to `expected`. The `weak` parameter is ignored; the stronger of `success_order` and `failure_order` is used.

facts.claim: Type constraints
facts.note: Non-generic API; `T` must be an integral type of size 2, 4, 8, or 16 bytes.
