Floating-point subtraction (`float`/`double`) is supported, but atomic floating-point operations can exhibit platform-specific precision or rounding behaviors not detailed here.

entity/__nv-atomic-fetch-xor.title: 10.14.2.5 __nv_atomic_fetch_xor() and __nv_atomic_xor() (CUDA 12.8)
entity/__nv-atomic-fetch-xor.summary: Atomic bitwise XOR functions introduced in CUDA 12.8. Reads value at ptr, XORs with val, stores result back to ptr. __nv_atomic_fetch_xor returns old value; __nv_atomic_xor returns void. Supports integral types of 4 or 8 bytes. Requires sm_60+ for memory order/scope, sm_90+ for cluster scope. Arguments order and scope must be integer literals.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence corresponds to section 10.14.2.5 within the 'Bitwise Functions' chapter of the CUDA C Programming Guide. It is grouped with other new CUDA 12.8 atomic bitwise operations like `__nv_atomic_fetch_or` and `__nv_atomic_fetch_and`.

facts.claim: Function signatures for `__nv_atomic_fetch_xor` and `__nv_atomic_xor`
facts.note: Both functions take a pointer `ptr`, a value `val`, memory order `order`, and thread scope `scope` (defaulting to `__NV_THREAD_SCOPE_SYSTEM`).

facts.claim: Behavior and return values
facts.note: Introduced in CUDA 12.8. Reads the value at `ptr`, XORs it with `val`, and stores the result back to `ptr`. `__nv_atomic_fetch_xor` returns the old value; `__nv_atomic_xor` returns void.

facts.claim: Type constraints
facts.note: Template parameter `T` must be an integral type of exactly 4 or 8 bytes.

facts.claim: Architecture and scope support
facts.note: Memory order and thread scope operations require sm_60 or higher. The cluster thread scope is supported on sm_90 or higher.

facts.claim: Argument constraints
facts.note: The `order` and `scope` arguments must be integer literals; variables are not permitted.

caveats: The `order` and `scope` parameters are strictly enforced as compile-time integer literals, limiting dynamic runtime configuration for memory ordering or thread scoping.

entity/__nv-atomic-load.title: 10.14.3.1 __nv_atomic_load() (CUDA 12.8)
entity/__nv-atomic-load.summary: Generic atomic load function introduced in CUDA 12.8. Loads value at ptr and writes to ret. Supports 1, 2, 4, 8, or 16-byte types. Requires sm_60+ for memory order/scope, sm_70+ for 16-byte types, sm_90+ for cluster scope. Arguments order and scope must be integer literals. order cannot be __NV_ATOMIC_RELEASE or __NV_ATOMIC_ACQ_REL.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence falls under section 10.14.3 'Other atomic functions', which groups newer CUDA 12.8 atomic primitives alongside `__nv_atomic_load_n()`, `__nv_atomic_store()`, `__nv_atomic_store_n()`, and `__nv_atomic_thread_fence()`. This section follows the traditional bitwise atomic functions (10.14.2) and the max/fetch-max operations (10.14.1.16).

facts.claim: Function signature and parameters
facts.note: Signature: `__device__ void __nv_atomic_load(T* ptr, T* ret, int order, int scope = __NV_THREAD_SCOPE_SYSTEM);`

facts.claim: Introduction version
facts.note: Introduced in CUDA 12.8.

facts.claim: Core behavior
facts.note: Performs an atomic load from the memory location pointed to by `ptr` and writes the retrieved value to the memory location pointed to by `ret`.

facts.claim: Type constraints
facts.note: Generic atomic load; `T` can be any data type with sizes 1, 2, 4, 8, or 16 bytes.

facts.claim: Architecture requirements
facts.note: Base atomic operation with memory order/scope requires sm_60+. 16-byte data types require sm_70+. Cluster thread scope requires sm_90+.

facts.claim: Argument constraints
facts.note: `order` and `scope` must be integer literals (variables are not allowed). `order` cannot be `__NV_ATOMIC_RELEASE` or `__NV_ATOMIC_ACQ_REL`.

caveats: Unlike `__nv_atomic_load_n()` (which returns the loaded value directly), this function uses an output pointer `ret` and has a `void` return type.
Memory ordering restrictions explicitly forbid release/acq-rel semantics, consistent with a load-only operation.

entity/__nv-atomic-load-n.title: 10.14.3.2 __nv_atomic_load_n() (CUDA 12.8)
entity/__nv-atomic-load-n.summary: Non-generic atomic load function introduced in CUDA 12.8. Loads value at ptr and returns it. Supports integral types of 1, 2, 4, 8, or 16 bytes. Requires sm_60+ for memory order/scope, sm_70+ for 16-byte types, sm_90+ for cluster scope. Arguments order and scope must be integer literals. order cannot be __NV_ATOMIC_RELEASE or __NV_ATOMIC_ACQ_REL.

research_reports.context_summary: Falls under section 10.14.3 'Other atomic functions', positioned between `__nv_atomic_load()` and `__nv_atomic_store()`. Follows the same structural pattern and constraint rules as neighboring atomic functions in the CUDA 12.8 release.

facts.claim: Function signature
facts.note: __device__ T __nv_atomic_load_n(T* ptr, int order, int scope = __NV_THREAD_SCOPE_SYSTEM);

facts.claim: Purpose
facts.note: Introduced in CUDA 12.8. Atomically loads the value at the memory location pointed to by ptr and returns it.

facts.claim: Type constraints
facts.note: Non-generic atomic load. T is restricted to integral types of size 1, 2, 4, 8, or 16 bytes.

facts.claim: Architecture support
facts.note: Atomic operation with memory order and thread scope: sm_60+. 16-byte data types: sm_70+. Cluster thread scope: sm_90+.

facts.claim: Argument constraints
facts.note: order and scope must be integer literals (variables are invalid). order cannot be __NV_ATOMIC_RELEASE or __NV_ATOMIC_ACQ_REL.

caveats: order parameter is restricted from using release/acquire-release semantics, likely because atomic loads inherently act as acquire operations or to prevent redundant memory ordering.
No contradictions found with surrounding atomic functions in the assigned context.

entity/__nv-atomic-store.title: 10.14.3.3 __nv_atomic_store() (CUDA 12.8)
entity/__nv-atomic-store.summary: Generic atomic store function introduced in CUDA 12.8. Reads value at val and stores to ptr. Supports 1, 2, 4, 8, or 16-byte types. Requires sm_60+ for memory order/scope, sm_70+ for 16-byte types, sm_90+ for cluster scope. Arguments order and scope must be integer literals. order cannot be __NV_ATOMIC_CONSUME, __NV_ATOMIC_ACQUIRE, or __NV_ATOMIC_ACQ_REL.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence falls under section 10.14.3 'Other atomic functions', which introduces a suite of new atomic operations in CUDA 12.8 that support explicit memory ordering and thread scopes. It is grouped alongside `__nv_atomic_load`, `__nv_atomic_load_n`, `__nv_atomic_store_n`, and `__nv_atomic_thread_fence`.

facts.claim: Function signature and version
facts.note: Introduced in CUDA 12.8. Signature is `__device__ void __nv_atomic_store(T* ptr, T* val, int order, int scope = __NV_THREAD_SCOPE_SYSTEM);` (source contains a typo: `__NV_THREAD_ , SCOPE_SYSTEM`).

facts.claim: Behavior
facts.note: Atomically reads the value pointed to by `val` and stores it to the memory location pointed to by `ptr`.

facts.claim: Type support
facts.note: Generic atomic store: `T` can be any data type of size 1, 2, 4, 8, or 16 bytes.

facts.claim: Architecture support
facts.note: General memory order/scope: sm_60+. 16-byte types: sm_70+. Cluster thread scope: sm_90+.

facts.claim: Parameter constraints
facts.note: `order` and `scope` must be integer literals (not variables). `order` cannot be `__NV_ATOMIC_CONSUME`, `__NV_ATOMIC_ACQUIRE`, or `__NV_ATOMIC_ACQ_REL`.

caveats: Source text incorrectly describes the function as a 'generic atomic load' in the description paragraph, despite the function name and behavior clearly indicating a store operation.
Source signature contains a formatting/typo error for the default scope parameter (`__NV_THREAD_ , SCOPE_SYSTEM` instead of `__NV_THREAD_SCOPE_SYSTEM`).

entity/__nv-atomic-store-n.title: 10.14.3.4 __nv_atomic_store_n() (CUDA 12.8)
entity/__nv-atomic-store-n.summary: Non-generic atomic store function introduced in CUDA 12.8. Stores val to ptr. Supports integral types of 1, 2, 4, 8, or 16 bytes. Requires sm_60+ for memory order/scope, sm_70+ for 16-byte types, sm_90+ for cluster scope. Arguments order and scope must be integer literals. order cannot be __NV_ATOMIC_CONSUME, __NV_ATOMIC_ACQUIRE, or __NV_ATOMIC_ACQ_REL.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence is the exact documentation block for `__nv_atomic_store_n()` within the CUDA C++ Programming Guide's atomic functions section. It is grouped with other CUDA 12.8 additions (`__nv_atomic_load_n`, `__nv_atomic_store`, etc.) that introduce memory ordering and thread scope parameters. The surrounding text confirms the function's role as a store operation, despite a typo in the source calling it a 'load'.

facts.claim: Function signature is `__device__ void __nv_atomic_store_n(T* ptr, T val, int order, int scope = __NV_THREAD_SCOPE_SYSTEM);`
facts.note: Introduced in CUDA 12.8, performs an atomic store by writing `val` to the memory location pointed to by `ptr`.

facts.claim: Type `T` is restricted to integral types of size 1, 2, 4, 8, or 16 bytes.
facts.note: Source text incorrectly labels this as a 'non-generic atomic load'; context confirms it is a store operation.

facts.claim: Memory order and thread scope operations require compute capability sm_60 or higher.
facts.note: 16-byte data types require sm_70 or higher. Cluster thread scope requires sm_90 or higher.

facts.claim: Arguments `order` and `scope` must be integer literals; variables are not permitted.
facts.note: `order` cannot be `__NV_ATOMIC_CONSUME`, `__NV_ATOMIC_ACQUIRE`, or `__NV_ATOMIC_ACQ_REL`.

facts.claim: Function is part of the CUDA 12.8 `__nv_atomic_*` family that adds memory ordering and thread scope to standard atomics.
facts.note: Differs from `__nv_atomic_store()` only in accepting the value directly (`T val`) rather than via pointer (`T* val`).

entity/__nv-atomic-thread-fence.title: 10.14.3.5 __nv_atomic_thread_fence() (CUDA 12.8)
entity/__nv-atomic-thread-fence.summary: Atomic thread fence function introduced in CUDA 12.8. Establishes ordering between memory accesses based on specified memory order. Thread scope parameter specifies observing threads. Requires sm_90+ for cluster scope. Arguments order and scope must be integer literals.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence covers section 10.14.3.5 of the CUDA C Programming Guide, detailing the `__nv_atomic_thread_fence()` function. It sits within a broader section (10.14.3) introducing new atomic primitives in CUDA 12.8, alongside load/store variants. The function signature, purpose, argument constraints, and architecture support are explicitly defined.

facts.claim: Function signature
facts.note: Declares `__device__ void __nv_atomic_thread_fence (int order, int scope = __NV_THREAD_SCOPE_SYSTEM);`

facts.claim: Purpose
facts.note: Establishes an ordering between memory accesses requested by the calling thread based on the specified `order`. The `scope` parameter defines the set of threads that may observe the ordering effect.

facts.claim: Argument constraints
facts.note: Both `order` and `scope` must be integer literals; variables cannot be passed.

facts.claim: Architecture & scope support
facts.note: The cluster thread scope is supported on architectures `sm_90` and higher.

caveats: The function returns `void` and does not perform a memory read/write operation itself, unlike the fetch/store variants in the surrounding context.

entity/alloca.title: 10.17 Alloca Function
entity/alloca.summary: Allocates size bytes of memory in the stack frame of the caller. Returns 16-byte aligned pointer in device code. Automatically freed on function return. Requires compute capability 5.2+. On Windows, <malloc.h> must be included. May cause stack overflow.

research_reports.context_summary: The `alloca` function is documented in Section 10.17, situated between address space conversion utilities (Section 10.16) and compiler optimization hint functions (Section 10.18). It serves as a low-level stack allocation utility within the CUDA C++ device/host programming model.

facts.claim: Function signature is `__host__ __device__ void * alloca(size_t size);`
facts.note: Allocates `size` bytes of memory directly in the caller's stack frame.

facts.claim: The returned pointer is automatically freed when the caller function returns.
facts.note: When invoked from device code, the returned pointer is guaranteed to be 16-byte aligned.

facts.claim: On Windows platforms, the `<malloc.h>` header must be included before use.
facts.note: Supported for compute capability 5.2 or higher.

facts.claim: Dynamic stack allocation may cause stack overflow.
facts.note: Users must adjust the stack size configuration accordingly to prevent overflow.

caveats: No contradictions or scope clashes detected. The documentation aligns with standard C `alloca` semantics adapted for CUDA's device/host execution model.

entity/atomicand.title: 10.14.2.1 atomicAnd()
entity/atomicand.summary: Atomic bitwise AND function. Reads 32-bit or 64-bit word at address, computes (old & val), stores result back. Returns old value. 64-bit version requires compute capability 5.0 or higher.

research_reports.context_summary: Falls under section 10.14.2 (Bitwise Functions), positioned between legacy bitwise atomics (atomicOr, atomicXor) and newer CUDA 12.8 `__nv_atomic_fetch_and()` variants that support memory ordering and cluster scopes.

facts.claim: Function signatures are overloaded for 32-bit and 64-bit integral types: int, unsigned int, and unsigned long long int.
facts.note: Reads a 32-bit or 64-bit word `old` from global or shared memory at `address`, computes `(old & val)`, and stores the result back to the same address in one atomic transaction.

facts.claim: Returns the original `old` value read from memory before the operation.
facts.note: The 64-bit version requires compute capability 5.0 or higher.

facts.claim: Documented as a legacy bitwise atomic operation, distinct from the newer CUDA 12.8 `__nv_atomic_fetch_and()` and `__nv_atomic_and()` functions which support memory ordering and thread scopes.
facts.note: Contextual framing only; core functionality and signatures are fully covered by the assigned evidence.

caveats: No memory order or thread scope parameters are supported by this legacy function, unlike the `__nv_atomic_*` variants introduced in CUDA 12.8.
contradictions: None detected.

entity/built-in-vector-types.title: Built-in Vector Types
entity/built-in-vector-types.summary: Describes built-in vector types (char, short, int, long, float, double, dim3) derived from basic types, their component accessors (x, y, z, w), constructors, and alignment requirements.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence covers Section 10.3 (Built-in Vector Types) and the beginning of Section 10.4 (Built-in Variables). It defines the structure, naming conventions, constructors, and alignment rules for CUDA's vector types, and introduces the specialized `dim3` type used for dimension specifications.

facts.claim: Built-in vector types are derived from basic integer and floating-point types (char, short, int, long, longlong, float, double) and are implemented as structures.
facts.note: Components are accessed via x, y, z, w fields.

facts.claim: Each vector type provides a constructor function named make_<type_name> (e.g., make_int2(int x, int y)) to initialize components.
facts.note: None

facts.claim: Alignment requirements vary by base type and component count. Examples: int4/float4 require 16-byte alignment, int3/float3 require 4-byte alignment, and specialized variants like long4_32a require 32-byte alignment.
facts.note: Full alignment rules are detailed in Table 7, which includes standard variants and _16a/_32a aligned variants.

facts.claim: dim3 is a specialized integer vector type based on uint3 used to specify dimensions. Unspecified components in a dim3 variable are automatically initialized to 1.
facts.note: None

facts.claim: Built-in vector types underpin CUDA's built-in variables: gridDim and blockDim use dim3, while blockIdx and threadIdx use uint3.
facts.note: These variables are only valid within device-executed functions.

caveats: The source text contains OCR artifacts in Table 7 (e.g., `long $4^3$ `), but the alignment values for standard and _16a/_32a variants are clearly listed. The lead writer should verify the exact table formatting when rendering.

Section 10.4 (Built-in Variables) is included in the evidence window but is conceptually distinct from the vector type definitions themselves. The lead writer should keep the focus on the types, using the variables only as usage examples.

entity/conditional-handles.title: Conditional Handles
entity/conditional-handles.summary: Covers `cudaGraphConditionalHandle` creation and lifecycle. Handles are tied to a single node, cannot be destroyed, and can be initialized with a default value using `cudaGraphCondAssignDefault`. Defaults update during whole-graph updates.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence covers the definition, lifecycle, initialization behavior, and update mechanics of `cudaGraphConditionalHandle`, which is used to drive conditional graph nodes (IF, WHILE, SWITCH).

facts.claim: Conditional handles are represented by the `cudaGraphConditionalHandle` type and created via `cudaGraphConditionalHandleCreate()`.
facts.note: Must be associated with a single conditional node and cannot be destroyed.

facts.claim: If the `cudaGraphCondAssignDefault` flag is specified during creation, the condition value is initialized to the specified default at the start of each graph execution.
facts.note: Without this flag, the condition value is undefined at execution start and does not persist across executions.

facts.claim: The default value and flags associated with a handle are updated during a whole graph update.
facts.note: Used to access condition values for conditional nodes (IF, WHILE, SWITCH), which can be set by device code using `cudaGraphSetConditional()`.

caveats: Handles are strictly tied to a single conditional node and lack a destroy API, implying a static lifecycle within the graph's scope.

entity/conditional-if-nodes.title: Conditional IF Nodes
entity/conditional-if-nodes.summary: Covers IF conditional nodes that execute a body graph once if the condition is non-zero. Supports an optional second body graph (else branch) executed if zero (added in CUDA 12.8). Includes creation and population examples.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence covers section 6.2.8.7.8.3, which details the syntax, API parameters, and code examples for Conditional IF Nodes. It is nested within the broader 'Conditional Graph Nodes' section (6.2.8.7.8) that introduces the concept alongside WHILE and SWITCH nodes, and the 'Conditional Node Body Graph Requirements' section (6.2.8.7.8.2), which lists universal constraints applied to all conditional body graphs.

facts.claim: The body graph of an IF node executes exactly once if the condition value is non-zero upon node evaluation.
facts.note: Core execution behavior.

facts.claim: Starting in CUDA 12.8, an IF node supports an optional second body graph that executes once if the condition value is zero.
facts.note: Introduced 'else' branch capability.

facts.claim: A condition value is represented by a cudaGraphConditionalHandle, created via cudaGraphConditionalHandleCreate() before the node is added.
facts.note: Handle lifecycle requirement.

facts.claim: The condition value is set by device code using cudaGraphSetConditional().
facts.note: Device-side condition assignment.

facts.claim: The node is added using cudaGraphAddNode() with cudaGraphNodeTypeConditional, setting cParams.conditional.type = cudaGraphCondTypeIf.
facts.note: Node registration API.

facts.claim: The size parameter in cParams.conditional dictates the number of body graphs (1 or 2).
facts.note: Controls single vs. dual body graph allocation.

facts.claim: Body graphs are accessed via cParams.conditional.phGraph_out[0] (and [1] for the optional graph) and populated using graph APIs or cudaStreamBeginCaptureToGraph().
facts.note: Graph population mechanism.

facts.claim: Body graphs must reside on a single device and may only contain kernel, empty, memcpy, memset, child graph, and conditional nodes.
facts.note: General conditional node constraint.

facts.claim: Kernels within the body cannot use CUDA Dynamic Parallelism or Device Graph Launch.
facts.note: Execution restriction.

facts.claim: Memcpy/Memset operations are restricted to device and pinned device-mapped host memory; CUDA arrays are not permitted.
facts.note: Memory access restriction.

caveats: The condition value is undefined at the start of each graph execution unless cudaGraphCondAssignDefault is specified during handle creation (L3139).
Handles cannot be destroyed and must be associated with a single conditional node (L3137).
recommended_structure: API & Setup (Handle creation, Node addition, Body graph population)

entity/conditional-while-nodes.title: Conditional WHILE Nodes
entity/conditional-while-nodes.summary: Covers WHILE conditional nodes that execute their body graph repeatedly as long as the condition remains non-zero. Condition is evaluated at execution and after each body completion. Includes creation and loop example.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence covers section 6.2.8.7.8.4 on Conditional WHILE Nodes, detailing their execution behavior, API setup, and a code example demonstrating a loop kernel that updates the condition handle.

facts.claim: A Conditional WHILE node executes its body graph repeatedly as long as the associated condition evaluates to non-zero.
facts.note: Condition evaluation occurs at node execution and after each body graph completion.

facts.claim: WHILE nodes are configured by setting `cParams.conditional.type` to `cudaGraphCondTypeWhile` and `cParams.conditional.size` to 1.
facts.note: The body graph is accessed via `cParams.conditional.phGraph_out[0]`.

facts.claim: The conditional handle can be initialized with `cudaGraphCondAssignDefault` to bypass the need for an upstream kernel solely for condition assignment.
facts.note: This simplifies graph setup by allowing the body graph's kernel to manage the condition directly.

facts.claim: Condition state is updated inside the body graph's kernel using `cudaGraphSetConditional(handle, value)`.
facts.note: Example shows a static counter decrementing from 10, returning 1 to continue looping and 0 to terminate.

caveats: The provided code snippet contains minor typos (e.g., `cuGraphCreate` instead of `cudaGraphCreate`, `kernelsArgs` instead of `kernelArgs`), which should be corrected in the final page.
WHILE nodes are distinct from IF nodes (which execute once if non-zero) and SWITCH nodes (which select one of n graphs).

entity/cu_getprocaddress_guidelines.title: Guidelines for cuGetProcAddress
entity/cu_getprocaddress_guidelines.summary: Provides guidelines for using cuGetProcAddress, including version matching and driver version checks, and explains how to determine failure reasons using CUdriverProcAddressQueryResult.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence covers Section 21.5.4 (Guidelines for cuGetProcAddress), 21.5.4.1 (Guidelines for Runtime API Usage), and 21.5.5 (Determining cuGetProcAddress Failure Reasons). It details best practices for version handling, pre-checks, and interpreting the `CUdriverProcAddressQueryResult` status codes.

facts.claim: The cudaVersion argument passed to cuGetProcAddress must be hardcoded to match the specific function's typedef version (e.g., _v11020). Using compile-time macros like CUDA_VERSION or dynamic values from cuDriverGetVersion is discouraged.
facts.note: Guideline: Version Hardcoding

facts.claim: Developers should verify the installed driver version using cuDriverGetVersion before invoking cuGetProcAddress to prevent errors or unexpected symbol resolution.
facts.note: Guideline: Pre-flight Driver Check

facts.claim: The runtime counterpart cudaGetDriverEntryPointByVersion adheres to the same guidelines, as it also allows requesting a specific CUDA driver version.
facts.note: Runtime API Parity

facts.claim: Failures are split into two categories: (1) API/usage errors (e.g., NULL pointers, invalid flags) returned via the standard CUresult return value, and (2) symbol lookup failures encoded in the CUdriverProcAddressQueryResult *symbolStatus output parameter.
facts.note: Error Classification

facts.claim: CU_GET_PROC_ADDRESS_VERSION_NOT_SUFFICIENT is returned when the symbol exists in the current driver but was introduced in a version higher than the cudaVersion argument provided.
facts.note: Query Result: VERSION_NOT_SUFFICIENT

facts.claim: CU_GET_PROC_ADDRESS_SYMBOL_NOT_FOUND is returned when the symbol cannot be located in the driver, often due to an outdated driver or a typo in the function name (e.g., case sensitivity).
facts.note: Query Result: SYMBOL_NOT_FOUND

facts.claim: CU_GET_PROC_ADDRESS_SUCCESS indicates the symbol was successfully found and the function pointer was populated.
facts.note: Query Result: SUCCESS

caveats: The guidelines explicitly warn against using dynamic version queries (cuDriverGetVersion) as the version argument to cuGetProcAddress, as this can lead to mismatched typedefs and undefined behavior.
Symbol lookup failures (SYMBOL_NOT_FOUND) can occur during deployment if an application developed against a newer driver is run on an older driver, even if development-time calls succeeded.

entity/cuda-atomicadd.title: atomicAdd()
entity/cuda-atomicadd.summary: Performs an atomic addition operation on 16-bit, 32-bit, 64-bit, and vector types (float2, float4, __half, __nv_bfloat16) in global or shared memory. Returns the old value.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence is the exact documentation section (10.14.1.1) for the `atomicAdd()` device function. It sits within a broader chapter on atomic operations (10.14), following general atomic operation notes and preceding other arithmetic atomic functions like `atomicSub()` and `atomicCAS()`.

facts.claim: Functionality: Reads a 16-bit, 32-bit, or 64-bit value (`old`) from global or shared memory, computes `old + val`, and stores the result back in a single atomic transaction. Returns the original `old` value.
facts.note: Supported Types & Compute Capability Requirements: `float` (CC 2.x+), `double` (CC 6.x+), `__half` (CC 7.x+), `__nv_bfloat16` (CC 8.x+), `__half2`/`__nv_bfloat162` (CC 6.x+), `float2`/`float4` (CC 9.x+). Integral types are also supported.

facts.claim: Atomicity Guarantees: For vector types (`__half2`, `__nv_bfloat162`, `float2`, `float4`), atomicity is guaranteed per element, not as a single 32-bit, 64-bit, or 128-bit access.
facts.note: Memory Scope: General variants operate on global or shared memory. The `float2` and `float4` variants are restricted to global memory addresses only.

caveats: The documentation explicitly restricts `float2` and `float4` to global memory only, unlike other variants which support shared memory.
Vector type atomicity is element-wise, which is a critical distinction for multi-element atomic operations.

entity/cuda-atomiccas.title: atomicCAS()
entity/cuda-atomiccas.summary: Performs an atomic compare-and-swap operation on 16-bit, 32-bit, 64-bit, and 128-bit types in global or shared memory. Returns the old value.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence covers Section 10.14.1.8 `atomicCAS()`, situated within the CUDA C Programming Guide's atomic functions reference. It sits between counter operations (`atomicInc`/`atomicDec`) and newer CUDA 12.8 memory-order scoped atomic functions (`__nv_atomic_*`), serving as the foundational compare-and-swap primitive.

facts.claim: Core Operation
facts.note: Reads a 16-bit, 32-bit, 64-bit, or 128-bit word `old` from global or shared memory at `address`. Computes `(old == compare ? val : old)` and stores the result back to the same address in a single atomic transaction. Returns the original `old` value.

facts.claim: Signatures
facts.note: Supports `int`, `unsigned int`, `unsigned long long int`, and `unsigned short int` via explicit overloads. Also provides a C++ template `atomicCAS(T* address, T compare, T val)` for 128-bit types.

facts.claim: 128-bit Template Constraints
facts.note: The generic 128-bit template requires `T` to satisfy `sizeof(T) == 16`, `alignof(T) >= 16`, and `std::is_trivially_copyable<T>::value == true`. For C++03 and older, `T` must also be `std::is_default_constructible<T>::value == true`.

facts.claim: Hardware Support
facts.note: The 128-bit `atomicCAS()` variant is exclusively supported on devices with compute capability 9.x and higher.

caveats: The source does not specify compute capability requirements for the 16-bit, 32-bit, or 64-bit variants, only the 128-bit template.

entity/cuda-atomicdec.title: atomicDec()
entity/cuda-atomicdec.summary: Performs an atomic decrement operation on a 32-bit unsigned integer in global or shared memory. Returns the old value.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence (L7905-L7914) is the exact definition block for `atomicDec()` within the 'Arithmetic Functions' section (10.14.1). It sits between `atomicInc()` and `atomicCAS()`, following the standard pattern of other atomic arithmetic operations in the guide.

facts.claim: Function signature is `unsigned int atomicDec(unsigned int* address, unsigned int val);`
facts.note: Only 32-bit `unsigned int` version is documented in this section.

facts.claim: Reads a 32-bit word `old` from global or shared memory, computes `(((old == 0) || (old > val)) ? val : (old-1))`, and stores it back.
facts.note: The logic resets the value to `val` if `old` is 0 or exceeds `val`; otherwise, it decrements by 1.

facts.claim: The read, compute, and store operations are performed in a single atomic transaction.
facts.note: Ensures thread-safety without external synchronization.

facts.claim: Returns the original `old` value located at the address before the operation.
facts.note: Consistent with other atomic arithmetic functions in the guide.

entity/cuda-atomicexch.title: atomicExch()

facts.note: None

entity/cuda-compute-capability-10-0.title: Compute Capability 10.0
entity/cuda-compute-capability-10-0.summary: Architecture specifications for Compute Capability 10.0 (Blackwell) including fifth-gen Tensor Cores, 256KB unified cache/shared memory, and extended MMA acceleration features.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence corresponds to Section 20.9 of the CUDA C Programming Guide, which formally defines the architecture, memory hierarchy, and specialized computation features for Compute Capability 10.0 (Blackwell architecture). It sits between CC 9.0 (Hopper) and CC 12.0 in the guide's versioning sequence.

facts.claim: Streaming Multiprocessor (SM) composition includes 128 FP32 cores, 64 FP64 cores, 64 INT32 cores, 16 special function units, and 4 warp schedulers.
facts.note: Warp distribution is static across schedulers, with each scheduler issuing one instruction per ready warp per issue cycle.

facts.claim: Tensor Cores: 4 mixed-precision fifth-generation Tensor Cores per SM, supporting FP8 (E4M3/E5M2), fp16, __nv_bfloat16, tf32, INT8, and fp64 matrix arithmetic with sparsity support.
facts.note: Explicitly notes FP8 exponent/mantissa format options.

facts.claim: Memory hierarchy features a unified data cache and shared memory pool totaling 256 KB, plus a read-only constant cache shared across functional units.
facts.note: Global memory behavior is inherited from CC 5.x specifications.

facts.claim: Shared memory carveout is configurable per kernel, with supported capacities: 0, 8, 16, 32, 64, 100, 132, 164, 196, or 228 KB.
facts.note: Maximum addressable shared memory per thread block is 227 KB (1 KB reserved for system overhead).

facts.claim: Dynamic shared memory allocations exceeding 48 KB per block require explicit opt-in via cudaFuncSetAttribute() using cudaFuncAttributeMaxDynamicSharedMemorySize.
facts.note: Kernels using >48 KB are architecture-specific and cannot rely on static shared memory arrays.

facts.claim: Specialized computation features extend Hopper's MMA acceleration (asynchronous execution, warp-group spanning matrices, dynamic register reassignment, shared memory operand access) and are exposed via inline PTX.
facts.note: Guide strongly recommends leveraging CUDA-X libraries (cuBLAS, cuDNN, cuFFT) or CUTLASS for GEMM workloads rather than raw PTX.

caveats: The guide treats CC 10.0 as a direct architectural successor to CC 9.0 with identical unified cache/shared memory sizing (256 KB), differing primarily in Tensor Core generation and Blackwell-specific MMA extensions.
No explicit device names or release dates are provided in this section; the architecture is solely identified as 'NVIDIA Blackwell GPU architecture'.

entity/cuda-compute-capability-12-0.title: Compute Capability 12.0
entity/cuda-compute-capability-12-0.summary: Architecture specifications for Compute Capability 12.0 (Blackwell variant) including fifth-gen Tensor Cores, 100KB unified cache/shared memory, and specialized MMA acceleration features.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence covers Section 20.10 of the CUDA C Programming Guide, detailing the architecture, memory hierarchy, and specialized computation features for Compute Capability 12.0 (NVIDIA Blackwell GPU architecture).

facts.claim: Compute Capability 12.0 corresponds to the NVIDIA Blackwell GPU architecture.
facts.note: SM architecture consists of 128 FP32 cores, 2 FP64 cores, 64 INT32 cores, 16 special function units, and 4 warp schedulers. Warps are statically distributed among schedulers.

facts.claim: Tensor Cores are fifth-generation, supporting mixed-precision matrix arithmetic (FP8 E4M3/E5M2, fp16, __nv_bfloat16, tf32, INT8, fp64) with sparsity support.
facts.note: Global memory behavior is identical to compute capability 5.x.

facts.claim: The SM features a unified data cache and shared memory with a total capacity of 100 KB.
facts.note: Shared memory is partitioned from the unified cache and can be configured on a per-kernel basis to 0, 8, 16, 32, 64, or 100 KB. A single thread block can address up to 99 KB, with 1 KB reserved for system use.

facts.claim: Kernels requiring more than 48 KB of shared memory per block must use dynamic shared memory and explicitly opt-in via cudaFuncAttributeMaxDynamicSharedMemorySize.
facts.note: Specialized computation features (MMA acceleration) extend those from the Hopper architecture and are accessible via inline PTX. NVIDIA strongly recommends utilizing these features through CUDA-X libraries (cuBLAS, cuDNN, cuFFT) and CUTLASS.

caveats: FP64 core count is drastically reduced to 2 per SM compared to 64 in CC 9.0/10.0, indicating a shift in architectural focus.
Unified cache/shared memory size is reduced to 100 KB from 256 KB in CC 9.0/10.0.

entity/cuda-compute-capability-5-x.title: Compute Capability 5.x
entity/cuda-compute-capability-5-x.summary: Architecture specifications for Compute Capability 5.x including SM components (CUDA cores, SFUs, schedulers), cache hierarchy (L1/texture, L2), global memory caching via __ldg(), and shared memory bank organization.

research_reports.context_summary: Section 20.4 details the architecture, global memory, and shared memory specifications for Compute Capability 5.x (Pascal architecture). It covers SM composition, cache hierarchy, L1/L2 caching behaviors for read-only vs. mutable data, and shared memory bank organization.

facts.claim: An SM consists of 128 CUDA cores, 32 special function units (SFUs) for single-precision transcendental functions, and 4 warp schedulers.
facts.note: Warp distribution is handled by the 4 schedulers, each issuing one instruction per ready warp at every instruction issue time.

facts.claim: Shared memory capacity is 64 KB for CC 5.0 and 96 KB for CC 5.2.
facts.note: The unified L1/texture cache is fixed at 24 KB. A shared L2 cache handles local/global memory and register spills.

facts.claim: Global memory accesses are always cached in L2. Read-only data can be cached in L1 using the __ldg() function.
facts.note: Using const and __restrict__ qualifiers increases the compiler's ability to detect read-only conditions for automatic __ldg() usage.

facts.claim: CC 5.0 does not support L1 caching for non-read-only data.
facts.note: CC 5.2 enables L1 caching for non-read-only data via inline assembly, -Xptxas -dlcm=ca, or -Xptxas -fscm=ca compiler flags, except when register usage exceeds SM limits (reported by profiler).

facts.claim: Shared memory is organized into 32 banks, each 32 bits wide, with 32 bits/clock/bank bandwidth.
facts.note: Successive 32-bit words map to successive banks. Accessing the same word within a warp avoids bank conflicts: reads broadcast the word, while writes have an undefined winning thread.

caveats: The source document is marked as legacy (replaced by the new CUDA Programming Guide as of CUDA 13.0), but the technical specifications remain valid for historical/reference purposes.

entity/cuda-compute-capability-6-x.title: Compute Capability 6.x
entity/cuda-compute-capability-6-x.summary: Architecture specifications for Compute Capability 6.x (Pascal) including updated core counts, scheduler configurations, cache sizes, and memory behavior inheriting from 5.x.

research_reports.scope: Section 20.5 covers Compute Capability 6.x (Pascal architecture), positioned between the 5.x specifications and the 7.x (Volta/Turing) features in the guide's architecture reference chapter.
research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence details the SM composition, memory hierarchy, warp scheduling behavior, and memory access patterns for Compute Capability 6.x, explicitly differentiating specifications across sub-versions 6.0, 6.1, and 6.2.

facts.claim: SM hardware composition varies by sub-version: 6.0 has 64 CUDA cores, 16 SFUs, and 2 warp schedulers; 6.1 and 6.2 have 128 CUDA cores, 32 SFUs, and 4 warp schedulers.
facts.note: Warp schedulers distribute warps and issue one instruction per ready warp at each issue time.

facts.claim: Cache and memory sizes differ by sub-version: 6.0 and 6.2 use 24 KB unified L1/texture cache and 64 KB shared memory; 6.1 uses 48 KB L1/texture cache and 96 KB shared memory.
facts.note: A shared L2 cache handles local/global memory and register spills, with behavior configurable per-access via load modifiers.

facts.claim: Global memory and shared memory behavior for 6.x devices are identical to Compute Capability 5.x devices.
facts.note: Shared memory operates in 32-bit bank size mode, with documentation referencing strided and irregular access patterns (broadcast, random permutation).

caveats: The source treats 6.0, 6.1, and 6.2 as a single architectural family (6.x) with minor hardware scaling differences rather than distinct feature sets.
No new memory features or scheduling paradigms are introduced in 6.x compared to 5.x; the guide explicitly defers to the 5.x sections for global and shared memory behavior.

entity/cuda-compute-capability-7-x.title: Compute Capability 7.x
entity/cuda-compute-capability-7-x.summary: Architecture specifications for Compute Capability 7.x (Volta/Turing) including Tensor Cores, unified data cache/shared memory partitioning, independent thread scheduling changes, __syncwarp() usage, and dynamic shared memory carveout via cudaFuncSetAttribute().

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence covers the official NVIDIA CUDA C++ Programming Guide section on Compute Capability 7.x (Volta and Turing architectures). It details the SM architecture, the introduction of independent thread scheduling, unified cache/shared memory partitioning, and shared memory carveout configuration APIs.

facts.claim: SM Architecture & Execution
facts.note: Each SM contains 64 FP32 cores, 32 FP64 cores, 64 INT32 cores, 8 mixed-precision Tensor Cores, 16 special function units, and 4 warp schedulers. Warps are statically distributed across schedulers, which issue one instruction per ready warp at each cycle.

facts.claim: Memory Hierarchy
facts.note: The SM features a read-only constant cache and a unified data cache/shared memory pool. The total pool size is 128 KB for Volta (Compute Capability 7.0) and 96 KB for Turing (Compute Capability 7.5). Shared memory is carved out of this pool, with the remainder functioning as an L1 cache and serving the texture unit.

facts.claim: Independent Thread Scheduling (Volta)
facts.note: Volta introduces independent thread scheduling, allowing threads within a warp to diverge and execute asynchronously. This breaks legacy warp-synchronous assumptions and requires: using `*_sync` warp intrinsics (e.g., `__shfl_xor_sync`, `__ballot_sync`) with explicit thread masks; inserting `__syncwarp()` barriers between intra-warp shared/global memory data exchanges; and `__syncthreads()` is now strictly enforced per-thread (not per-warp), preventing deadlocks from partial barrier hits. Developers can opt into the legacy Pascal scheduling model for backward compatibility.

facts.claim: Shared Memory Configuration
facts.note: Shared memory capacity is configurable per-kernel via the `cudaFuncSetAttribute()` API using `cudaFuncAttributePreferredSharedMemoryCarveout`. The carveout is a hint (not a hard requirement) and is rounded up to the next supported capacity. Volta supports shared memory allocations of 0, 8, 16, 32, 64, or 96 KB. Turing supports 32 or 64 KB. Allocations exceeding 48 KB per block require dynamic shared memory and explicit opt-in via `cudaFuncAttributeMaxDynamicSharedMemorySize`.

facts.claim: Global Memory
facts.note: Global memory access and caching behavior remain identical to Compute Capability 5.x devices.

caveats: The source explicitly distinguishes between Volta (7.0) and Turing (7.5) for unified cache size and supported shared memory carveout steps. Turing does not support the full range of Volta carveouts (e.g., 0, 8, 16 KB) and caps at 32/64 KB.

Independent thread scheduling is a Volta-specific architectural change that fundamentally alters warp-synchronous programming models. Legacy code relying on implicit warp lockstep or partial `__syncthreads()` hits will deadlock unless updated with `*_sync` intrinsics, `__syncwarp()`, or Pascal compatibility mode.
recommended_structure: Overview & Architecture (SM components, Tensor Cores, Schedulers)
Memory Hierarchy (Unified Cache, Shared Memory partitioning)
Independent Thread Scheduling (Volta changes, `*_sync` intrinsics, `__syncwarp()`, `__syncthreads()` enforcement)
Shared Memory Configuration (Carveout API, Volta vs Turing limits, dynamic shared memory opt-in)

entity/cuda-compute-capability-8-x.title: Compute Capability 8.x
entity/cuda-compute-capability-8-x.summary: Architecture specifications for Compute Capability 8.x (Ampere) including third/fourth-gen Tensor Cores, unified cache/shared memory sizes (192KB/128KB), and shared memory carveout configurations.

The text explicitly differentiates between the 8.0/8.7 variants and the 8.6/8.9 variants across multiple hardware specifications.

facts.claim: Compute Capability 8.x SMs contain 64 INT32 cores, 16 special function units, and 4 warp schedulers across all variants.
facts.note: Warp schedulers statically distribute warps and issue one instruction per scheduler per issue time.

facts.claim: FP32 core counts vary: 64 cores for compute capability 8.0, and 128 cores for 8.6, 8.7, and 8.9.
facts.note: None

facts.claim: FP64 core counts vary: 32 cores for compute capability 8.0, and 2 cores for 8.6, 8.7, and 8.9.
facts.note: None

facts.claim: Tensor Cores differ by generation: 8.0, 8.6, and 8.7 use 4 mixed-precision Third-Generation Tensor Cores, while 8.9 uses 4 mixed-precision Fourth-Generation Tensor Cores.
facts.note: 4th Gen (8.9) adds explicit support for fp8 input types alongside fp16, bfloat16, tf32, sub-byte, and fp64.

facts.claim: Unified data cache and shared memory total capacity is 192 KB for 8.0 and 8.7, and 128 KB for 8.6 and 8.9.
facts.note: Shared memory is partitioned out of this unified pool; the remainder functions as the L1 cache and texture unit cache.

facts.claim: Shared memory carveout options differ by variant: 8.0 and 8.7 support capacities of 0, 8, 16, 32, 64, 100, 132, or 164 KB. 8.6 and 8.9 support 0, 8, 16, 32, 64, or 100 KB.
facts.note: Carveout is configured via `cudaFuncSetAttribute` with `cudaFuncAttributePreferredSharedMemoryCarveout`, accepting integer percentages or specific enums. The driver treats this as a hint and may round up to the nearest supported capacity.

facts.claim: Maximum shared memory per thread block is 163 KB for 8.0/8.7 and 99 KB for 8.6/8.9, with 1 KB reserved for system use.
facts.note: Kernels requiring >48 KB per block must use dynamic shared memory and explicitly opt-in via `cudaFuncAttributeMaxDynamicSharedMemorySize`.

facts.claim: Global memory behavior for Compute Capability 8.x is identical to that of compute capability 5.x devices.
facts.note: None

caveats: The source text groups 8.0 and 8.7 together for cache size, shared memory options, and max block size, while grouping 8.6 and 8.9 together for FP32/FP64 counts, tensor core generation, and cache size. Ensure the target page reflects these architectural splits clearly.
Shared memory carveout percentages are rounded up to the nearest supported capacity (e.g., 50% of 164 KB maps to 100 KB, not 82 KB).
Dynamic shared memory allocations exceeding 48 KB per block are architecture-specific and require explicit API opt-in.
recommended_structure: Overview & Architecture (SM composition, core counts split by variant)
Tensor Cores (3rd Gen vs 4th Gen support)

entity/cuda-compute-capability-9-0.title: Compute Capability 9.0
entity/cuda-compute-capability-9-0.summary: Architecture specifications for Compute Capability 9.0 (Hopper) including fourth-gen Tensor Cores with FP8 support, 256KB unified cache/shared memory, and specialized MMA acceleration features.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence covers the complete section for Compute Capability 9.0 (Section 20.8), detailing its SM architecture, memory hierarchy, shared memory configuration, and specialized MMA acceleration features introduced in the Hopper/H100 architecture.

facts.claim: SM Architecture Components
facts.note: Each SM contains 128 FP32 cores, 64 FP64 cores, 64 INT32 cores, 4 fourth-generation Tensor Cores, 16 special function units, and 4 warp schedulers. Warps are statically distributed among schedulers.

facts.claim: Tensor Core Capabilities
facts.note: 4th-gen Tensor Cores support FP8 (E4M3/E5M2), fp16, __nv_bfloat16, tf32, INT8, and fp64 matrix arithmetic, with sparsity support.

facts.claim: Memory Hierarchy & Cache
facts.note: The unified data cache and shared memory total 256 KB. Global memory behavior is identical to compute capability 5.x. The remaining cache space acts as L1 and serves the texture unit.

facts.note: Shared memory carveout supports 0, 8, 16, 32, 64, 100, 132, 164, 196, or 228 KB. Maximum per thread block is 227 KB. Allocations >48 KB require dynamic shared memory and an explicit `cudaFuncSetAttribute()` opt-in. 1 KB is reserved for system use.

facts.note: Hopper architecture introduces async MMA execution, warp-group spanning matrices, dynamic register reassignment, and shared memory operand access. These are exposed via inline PTX and strongly recommended for use through cuBLAS, cuDNN, cuFFT, and CUTLASS.

entity/cuda-graph-add-mem-alloc-node.title: cudaGraphAddMemAllocNode
entity/cuda-graph-add-mem-alloc-node.summary: API for adding memory allocation nodes to graphs with explicit peer access descriptors and location properties.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence covers section 16.7.1 'Peer Access with Graph Node APIs', detailing how cudaGraphAddMemAllocNode handles peer access mappings and resident device configuration via its parameter structure.

facts.claim: cudaGraphAddMemAllocNode accepts peer access mapping requests through the accessDescs array field within the cudaMemAllocNodeParams structure.
facts.note: The resident device is specified via poolProps.location, and access from that device is implicitly assumed, eliminating the need for an explicit accessDescs entry for it.

facts.note: Example code shows configuring device 1 as the resident device and explicitly requesting ReadWrite access for devices 0 and 2.

facts.note: In the example, device 1 (resident) plus devices 0 and 2 (explicitly requested) all gain access.
