entity/cuda-graph-instantiate-flag-auto-.title: cudaGraphInstantiateFlagAutoFreeOnLaunch
entity/cuda-graph-instantiate-flag-auto-.summary: Flag allowing graph relaunch with unfreed allocations by automatically inserting asynchronous frees.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence (Section 16.3.4) explicitly defines the `cudaGraphInstantiateFlagAutoFreeOnLaunch` flag, detailing its mechanism for allowing graph relaunches with unfreed memory, its primary use case in single-producer/multiple-consumer algorithms, and the critical caveat that explicit memory freeing is still required before graph destruction.

facts.claim: The `cudaGraphInstantiateFlagAutoFreeOnLaunch` flag allows a CUDA graph containing unfreed memory allocations to be relaunched.
facts.note: Under normal circumstances, CUDA prevents a graph from being relaunched if it has unfreed memory allocations to avoid memory leaks caused by multiple allocations at the same address.

facts.claim: When instantiated with this flag, the graph launch automatically inserts an asynchronous free operation for any unfreed allocations from previous launches.
facts.note: This behavior is useful for single-producer, multiple-consumer algorithms where a producer graph creates allocations accessed by a varying set of consumers, and consumers cannot free the allocations because subsequent consumers may require access.

facts.claim: The flag does not change the behavior of graph destruction; the application must explicitly free the unfreed memory to avoid memory leaks.
facts.note: Even with the flag enabled, explicit calls to free functions (e.g., `cudaFreeAsync`) are required before destroying the graph executable (`cudaGraphExecDestroy`).

caveats: The auto-free mechanism only applies to the graph's launch loop; it does not replace the need for explicit memory management during the application's shutdown or graph destruction phase.

entity/cuda-graph-upload.title: cudaGraphUpload
entity/cuda-graph-upload.summary: API to separate allocation cost from launch by performing mappings immediately and associating with an upload stream.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence appears in Section 16.5.1, titled "First Launch / cudaGraphUpload", which is nested under the broader "16.5. Performance Considerations" section for CUDA graphs. This section discusses how CUDA handles memory reuse, remapping costs, and physical memory footprint management when graphs are launched.

facts.claim: Default mapping behavior occurs at launch, not instantiation.
facts.note: Physical memory mapping is deferred to launch time because the execution stream is unknown during graph instantiation.

facts.claim: cudaGraphUpload separates allocation/mapping costs from launch.
facts.note: Calling cudaGraphUpload performs all necessary memory mappings immediately and binds the graph to a specified upload stream, decoupling this overhead from the actual launch.

facts.claim: Stream consistency affects remapping overhead.
facts.note: Launching the graph in the same stream used for upload avoids additional remapping. Using different streams for upload and launch triggers remapping operations similar to switching streams.

facts.claim: Memory pool management can interfere with upload benefits.
facts.note: Unrelated memory pool management operations may reclaim memory from the idle upload stream, which can negate the performance impact of the upload.

caveats: The documentation notes that using different streams for upload and launch behaves like switching streams, likely causing remaps. This implies cudaGraphUpload is an optimization specifically for single-stream or consistent-stream launch patterns.
The evidence does not specify the exact API signature or return codes, only its functional role and performance implications within the graph execution pipeline.

entity/cuda_environment_variables.title: CUDA Environment Variables
entity/cuda_environment_variables.summary: Lists and describes various CUDA environment variables such as CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES, CUDA_MANAGED_FORCE_DEVICE, CUDA_DEVICE_ORDER, CUDA_CACHE_DISABLE, CUDA_FORCE_PTX_JIT, CUDA_LAUNCH_BLOCKING, and CUDA_LOG_FILE.

research_reports.context_summary: Chapter 22 of the CUDA C Programming Guide (marked legacy as of CUDA 13.0) contains Table 30, which catalogs CUDA environment variables across categories: Device Enumeration, Compilation, Execution, Debugging/MPS, Module Loading, Pre-loading, CUDA Graphs, and Error Logging.

facts.claim: Document is explicitly marked as legacy as of CUDA 13.0, replaced by the new CUDA Programming Guide.
facts.note: Table 30 lists environment variables categorized by function.

facts.claim: CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES: Comma-separated GPU indices or UUIDs (supports abbreviated UUIDs and MIG format MIG-UUID/...). Controls visibility and enumeration order. Invalid index stops visibility for subsequent devices.
facts.note: Table 30 lists environment variables categorized by function.

facts.claim: CUDA_MANAGED_FORCE_DEVICE: 0 or 1 (default 0). Forces driver to place all managed allocations in device memory.
facts.note: Table 30 lists environment variables categorized by function.

facts.claim: CUDA_DEVICE_ORDER: FASTEST_FIRST or PCI_BUS_ID (default FASTEST_FIRST). Controls device enumeration heuristic/order.
facts.note: Table 30 lists environment variables categorized by function.

facts.claim: CUDA_CACHE_DISABLE: 0 or 1 (default 0). Enables/disables JIT compilation caching.
facts.note: Table 30 lists environment variables categorized by function.

facts.claim: CUDA_CACHE_PATH: filepath. Directory for JIT cache. Defaults: Windows %APPDATA%\NVIDIA\ComputeCache, Linux ~/.nv/ComputeCache.
facts.note: Table 30 lists environment variables categorized by function.

facts.claim: CUDA_CACHE_MAXSIZE: integer (default 1 GiB desktop/server, 256 MiB embedded, max 4 GiB). Sets cache size limit; older binaries evicted as needed.
facts.note: Table 30 lists environment variables categorized by function.

facts.claim: CUDA_FORCE_PTX_JIT / CUDA_FORCE_JIT: 0 or 1 (default 0). Forces driver to ignore embedded binaries and JIT compile embedded PTX. CUDA_FORCE_JIT can be overridden by CUDA_FORCE_PTX_JIT=0.
facts.note: Table 30 lists environment variables categorized by function.

facts.claim: CUDA_DISABLE_PTX_JIT / CUDA_DISABLE_JIT: 0 or 1 (default 0). Disables JIT of embedded PTX, forcing use of embedded binaries. CUDA_DISABLE_JIT can be overridden by CUDA_DISABLE_PTX_JIT=0.
facts.note: Table 30 lists environment variables categorized by function.

facts.claim: CUDA_LAUNCH_BLOCKING: 0 or 1 (default 0). Enables/disables asynchronous kernel launches.
facts.note: Table 30 lists environment variables categorized by function.

facts.claim: CUDA_DEVICE_MAX_CONNECTIONS: 0-32 (default 8). Sets concurrent compute/copy engine work queues per device (CC 3.5+). (Note: Source text contains OCR typo 'So 32')
facts.note: Table 30 lists environment variables categorized by function.

facts.claim: CUDA_DEVICE_MAX_COPY_CONNECTIONS: 0-32 (default 8). Sets concurrent async copy engine work queues per device (CC 8.0+). Overwritten by CUDA_DEVICE_MAX_CONNECTIONS if both are set. (Note: Source text contains OCR typo 'No')
facts.note: Table 30 lists environment variables categorized by function.

facts.claim: CUDA_AUTO_BOOST: 0 or 1. Overrides nvidia-smi autoboost behavior; honored only if no conflicting requests from other apps on the same GPU.
facts.note: Table 30 lists environment variables categorized by function.

facts.claim: CUDA_SCALE_LAUNCH_QUEUE: '0.25x', '0.5x', '2x', or '4x'. Scales launch queue size by a fixed multiplier. (Note: Source contains trailing '$' typo)
facts.note: Table 30 lists environment variables categorized by function.

facts.claim: CUDA_DEVICE_WAITS_ON_EXCEPTION: 0 or 1 (default 0). Halts application on device exception to allow debugger attachment (Linux/cuda-gdb).
facts.note: Table 30 lists environment variables categorized by function.

facts.claim: CUDA_DEVICE_DEFAULT_PERSISTING: 0-100% (default 0). Sets aside L2 cache for persisting data accesses (CC 8.x). Must be set before starting CUDA MPS control daemon.
facts.note: Table 30 lists environment variables categorized by function.

facts.claim: CUDA_MODULE_LOADING: DEFAULT, LAZY, EAGER (default LAZY). Controls when kernels/data from cubin/fatbin/PTX are loaded (cuModuleLoad*/cuLibraryLoad*).
facts.note: Table 30 lists environment variables categorized by function.

facts.claim: CUDA_MODULE_DATA_LOADING: DEFAULT, LAZY, EAGER (default LAZY). Controls data loading mode for cuLibraryLoad*. Inherited from CUDA_MODULE_LOADING if unset.
facts.note: Table 30 lists environment variables categorized by function.

facts.claim: CUDA_BINARY_LOADER_THREAD: COUNT (default 0). Sets CPU threads for loading device binaries. 0 defaults to 1 thread. Overrides CU_JIT_BINARY_LOADER_THREAD.
facts.note: Table 30 lists environment variables categorized by function.

facts.claim: CUDA_FORCE_PRELOAD_LIBRARIES: 0 or 1 (default 0). Forces preloading of NVVM/PTX JIT libraries during driver init to avoid deadlocks with multiple CUDA threads. (Note: Source text contains OCR typo 'CR 1')
facts.note: Table 30 lists environment variables categorized by function.

facts.claim: CUDA_GRAPHS_USE_NODE_PRIORITY: 0 or 1. Overrides cudaGraphInstantiateFlagUseNodePriority flag for all graphs. (Note: Source text contains OCR typo 'PHY1')
facts.note: Table 30 lists environment variables categorized by function.

facts.claim: CUDA_LOG_FILE: stdout, stderr, or valid file path. Directs error logs to the specified location.
facts.note: Table 30 lists environment variables categorized by function.

caveats: Source document is legacy (CUDA 13.0+).

Table contains several OCR/typographical errors in the 'Values' column (e.g., 'So 32' -> 0-32, 'No' -> 0-32, 'CR 1' -> 0 or 1, 'PHY1' -> 0 or 1, trailing '$' on CUDA_SCALE_LAUNCH_QUEUE). Writer should verify/clean these before publication.

entity/device-graph-creation.title: Device Graph Creation
entity/device-graph-creation.summary: Covers the explicit instantiation requirement for device graphs using the `cudaGraphInstantiateFlagDeviceLaunch` flag. Structure is fixed at instantiation, and instantiation must occur on the host.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence is the dedicated subsection (6.2.8.7.7.1) defining the instantiation step for device-executable graphs. It bridges the general overview of device graph launching with the subsequent sections detailing requirements, upload procedures, and execution modes.

facts.claim: Device graphs require explicit instantiation to be launched from the device.
facts.note: Standard graphs default to host execution; device launch capability must be opt-in.

facts.claim: The cudaGraphInstantiateFlagDeviceLaunch flag must be passed to cudaGraphInstantiate().
facts.note: This flag signals the runtime to prepare the graph structure for device-side execution.

facts.claim: Instantiation must be performed on the host.
facts.note: Even though the resulting graph runs on the GPU, the creation/instantiation API call is host-bound.

facts.claim: Device graph structure is fixed at instantiation and cannot be updated without re-instantiation.
facts.note: This mirrors host graph behavior, enforcing immutability post-instantiation.

facts.claim: Instantiation is contingent on adhering to specific structural and operational requirements.
facts.note: The guide notes that failure to meet these prerequisites will block successful device graph creation.

caveats: The assigned evidence explicitly states that instantiation is host-only, which contrasts with the device-side launch capability. This is a critical architectural constraint to note.
No scope clashes detected; the evidence cleanly defines the creation step within the broader device graph workflow.

entity/device-graph-update.title: Device Graph Update
entity/device-graph-update.summary: Covers that device graphs can only be updated from the host and require re-upload to the device for changes to take effect. Launching from the device during an update causes undefined behavior.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence (L2923-L2926) falls under section 6.2.8.7.7.4 'Device Graph Update', which follows the 'Device Graph Upload' section. It outlines the constraints and procedures for modifying instantiated device graphs after they have been prepared for execution.

facts.claim: Device graphs can only be updated from the host side.
facts.note: Directly stated in the first sentence of the assigned evidence.

facts.claim: Changes to a device graph require re-uploading it to the device after an executable graph update to take effect.
facts.note: Explicitly required for the update to propagate to the device.

facts.claim: Re-upload can be achieved using the same methods previously outlined for device graph upload (explicit upload or implicit upload via host launch).
facts.note: References the methods detailed in the preceding section (6.2.8.7.7.3).

facts.claim: Launching a device graph from the device while an update is being applied results in undefined behavior.
facts.note: Highlights a strict concurrency caveat that differs from host graph behavior.

caveats: Unlike host graphs, device graphs cannot be safely launched from the device during an active update phase, which may impact dynamic control-flow workflows that mix device-side launches with host-side parameter modifications.
recommended_structure: Concurrency limitations and undefined behavior on device launch during update

entity/device-graph-upload.title: Device Graph Upload
entity/device-graph-upload.summary: Covers methods to upload device graphs to the GPU to populate device resources: explicit upload via `cudaGraphUpload()`, upload during instantiation via `cudaGraphInstantiateWithParams()`, or implicit upload via an initial host launch.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence (L2888-L2922) directly defines the 'Device Graph Upload' concept within section 6.2.8.7.7.3. It sits logically between device graph creation/requirements and update workflows, establishing the mandatory resource-population step required before any device-side execution can occur.

facts.claim: Device graph upload is a mandatory prerequisite step that populates necessary device resources before a graph can be launched on the GPU.
facts.note: This is the core definition and purpose.

facts.claim: Explicit upload can be performed immediately after instantiation by calling `cudaGraphUpload()`.
facts.note: Method 1 from the source.

facts.claim: Explicit upload can be requested as part of instantiation by passing `cudaGraphInstantiateFlagUpload` and an `uploadStream` via `cudaGraphInstantiateWithParams()`.
facts.note: Method 2 from the source.

facts.claim: Implicit upload occurs automatically when the graph is first launched from the host via `cudaGraphLaunch()`, removing the need for a separate upload call.
facts.note: Method 3 from the source.

caveats: The source explicitly notes that device graphs must be uploaded before device-side execution, and that updates to device graphs require re-uploading to take effect (L2925-L2926).
contradictions: None detected.
recommended_structure: Upload Methods (Explicit Post-Instantiation, Explicit During Instantiation, Implicit via Host Launch)
Relationship to Updates (Re-upload required after changes)

entity/device-launch.title: Device Launch Execution
entity/device-launch.summary: Covers launching device graphs from host or device using `cudaGraphLaunch()`. Notes that device-side launch is per-thread, requiring users to select a single thread for a given graph to avoid conflicts.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence sits within the CUDA Graphs section (6.2.8.7.7), specifically detailing the runtime execution phase for device graphs. It follows subsections covering device graph creation, requirements, upload, and update, and precedes subsections detailing specific device launch modes (fire-and-forget, tail launch) and execution environments.

facts.claim: Execution is performed via cudaGraphLaunch(), which uses identical function signatures and executable graph handles (cudaGraphExec_t) on both the host and the device.
facts.note: Establishes API parity between host and device execution contexts.

facts.claim: Device-side execution requires the graph to be launched from within another graph context.
facts.note: Defines the mandatory parent-child graph relationship for device-initiated launches.

facts.claim: Device-side launch execution is strictly per-thread. While multiple threads may launch graphs concurrently, a single graph instance must be assigned to one thread.
facts.note: Clarifies threading constraints and prevents race conditions during device-side graph execution.

caveats: The evidence does not cover synchronization primitives or stream ordering for device graphs, which are detailed in the subsequent 'Device Launch Modes' and 'Graph Execution Environments' subsections.
No contradictions or scope clashes detected with the target page meaning.

entity/dpx.title: DPX (Dynamic Programming eXtensions)
entity/dpx.summary: DPX is a set of hardware-accelerated functions (compute capability 9+) for min/max, fused add/min/max, and optional ReLU operations on 16/32-bit signed/unsigned integers. Widely used in dynamic programming algorithms like Smith-Waterman, Needleman-Wunsch, and Floyd-Warshall.

research_reports.context_summary: Section 10.25 introduces DPX (Dynamic Programming eXtensions) as a suite of CUDA Math API functions for optimized min/max, fused addition, and ReLU operations on 16/32-bit integers. Section 10.25.1 provides concrete usage examples.

facts.claim: DPX provides functions for computing min/max values, fused addition with min/max, and optional ReLU (clamping to zero) on up to three 16-bit or 32-bit signed/unsigned integer parameters.
facts.note: DPX is specifically designed to accelerate dynamic programming workloads, including Smith-Waterman, Needleman-Wunsch (genomics), and Floyd-Warshall (route optimization).

caveats: Hardware acceleration is exclusively available on devices with compute capability 9.0 and higher. Older architectures rely on software emulation.
Full API documentation is located in the CUDA Math API reference.

entity/error_log_management.title: Error Log Management
entity/error_log_management.summary: Describes the Error Log Management mechanism for reporting CUDA API errors in plain-English format, including activation via CUDA_LOG_FILE, log output format, and APIs like cuLogsRegisterCallback and cuLogsDumpToMemory.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence comprises Chapter 23 of the CUDA C Programming Guide, which is sandwiched between Chapter 22 (Environment Variables, where `CUDA_LOG_FILE` is briefly tabled) and Chapter 24 (Unified Memory Programming). The chapter serves as a standalone reference for the Error Log Management feature, detailing its purpose, activation, API surface, buffer behavior, and known limitations.

facts.claim: Purpose & Background
facts.note: Replaces generic non-zero return codes with plain-English error descriptions. Introduced in CUDA Toolkit 12.9 to aid debugging across 100+ existing error codes.

facts.claim: Activation
facts.note: Controlled via the `CUDA_LOG_FILE` environment variable (`stdout`, `stderr`, or file path). Programmatic dumping is possible regardless of the env var. Error-free runs produce no output.

facts.claim: Output Format
facts.note: Structured as `[Time][TID][Source][Severity][API Entry Point] Message`. Example: `[22:21:32.099][25642][CUDA][E][cuLogsDumpToMemory] buffer cannot be NULL`.

facts.claim: Callback APIs
facts.note: Developers register callbacks via `cuLogsRegisterCallback` with signature `void callbackFunc(void *data, CUlogLevel logLevel, char *message, size_t length)`. Unregistered via `cuLogsUnregisterCallback`.

facts.claim: Iteration & Dumping APIs
facts.note: `cuLogsCurrent` tracks the buffer end. `cuLogsDumpToFile` and `cuLogsDumpToMemory` export logs. Passing `NULL` for the iterator dumps the entire buffer and updates the iterator to the current end.

facts.claim: Memory Dump Constraints
facts.note: Buffers are null-terminated with newline-separated entries, capped at 25,600 bytes. If the provided buffer is too small, a note is prepended and oldest entries are dropped. `size` is updated with actual bytes written.

facts.claim: Limitations
facts.note: Hard limit of 100 entries (oldest overwritten, rollover noted). Incomplete API coverage (ongoing). Log path validity deferred until a log is generated. Driver-only at time of writing (Runtime equivalents planned). Messages hardcoded in US English.

facts.claim: Legacy Status
facts.note: Explicitly marked as legacy documentation as of CUDA 13.0, superseded by the new CUDA Programming Guide.

caveats: The feature is explicitly deprecated/legacy in CUDA 13.0+ per the source warning.
APIs are Driver-only in this version; Runtime equivalents were noted as future work.
All line numbers and API signatures are specific to the legacy CUDA C Programming Guide and may differ in the modern CUDA Programming Guide.

entity/fire-and-forget-launch.title: Fire and Forget Launch
entity/fire-and-forget-launch.summary: Covers the fire-and-forget mode where the launched graph runs independently as a child of the parent graph's execution environment. Limits up to 120 fire-and-forget graphs per parent execution, resetting between launches.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence is section 6.2.8.7.7.7, a subsection of the broader Device Graph Launch documentation. It details one of three device-only launch modes (alongside Tail and Sibling launches) used for dynamic, device-side control flow.

facts.claim: A fire-and-forget launch is submitted to the GPU immediately and executes independently of the launching (parent) graph, with the launched graph acting as a child.
facts.note: Definition & Execution Model

facts.claim: Implemented via cudaGraphLaunch() using the cudaStreamGraphFireAndForget stream parameter. The target graph must be a device graph that has been instantiated and uploaded.
facts.note: API & Implementation

facts.claim: A single graph execution can spawn up to 120 fire-and-forget graphs concurrently.
facts.note: Concurrency Limits

facts.claim: The 120-graph limit counter resets each time the parent graph is re-launched.
facts.note: Lifecycle Reset

caveats: The assigned evidence does not cover execution environment encapsulation or synchronization behavior, which are documented in adjacent sections (6.2.8.7.7.8).
No contradictions or scope clashes detected with the target page meaning.

entity/function-execution-space-specifie.title: Function Execution Space Specifiers
entity/function-execution-space-specifie.summary: Defines __global__, __device__, __host__, __noinline__, __forceinline__, and __inline_hint__ specifiers, their usage rules, cross-execution space call restrictions, and undefined behavior conditions.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence covers Section 10.1 of the CUDA C Programming Guide, detailing function execution space specifiers. It defines the core specifiers (`__global__`, `__device__`, `__host__`), their execution and callability rules, mutual exclusivity constraints, undefined behavior regarding cross-execution space calls, and function inlining qualifiers (`__noinline__`, `__forceinline__`, `__inline_hint__`).

facts.claim: Function execution space specifiers denote whether a function executes on the host or device and whether it is callable from the host or device.
facts.note: General definition of the specifiers.

facts.claim: The `__global__` specifier declares a function as a kernel that executes on the device.
facts.note: Core definition of `__global__`.

facts.claim: A `__global__` function is callable from the host, and callable from the device for compute capability 5.0 or higher.
facts.note: Callability rules for `__global__`.

facts.claim: A `__global__` function must have a `void` return type and cannot be a member of a class.
facts.note: Constraints on `__global__` functions.

facts.claim: Any call to a `__global__` function must specify its execution configuration and is asynchronous.
facts.note: Execution and asynchronous nature of `__global__`.

facts.claim: The `__device__` specifier declares a function executed on the device that is callable from the device only.
facts.note: Core definition of `__device__`.

facts.claim: The `__global__` and `__device__` execution space specifiers cannot be used together.
facts.note: Mutual exclusivity between `__global__` and `__device__`.

facts.claim: The `__host__` specifier declares a function executed on the host that is callable from the host only.
facts.note: Core definition of `__host__`.

facts.claim: Declaring a function with only the `__host__` specifier, or with no execution space specifier, compiles the function for the host only.
facts.note: Default behavior when no specifier is used.

facts.claim: The `__global__` and `__host__` execution space specifiers cannot be used together.
facts.note: Mutual exclusivity between `__global__` and `__host__`.

facts.claim: The `__device__` and `__host__` execution space specifiers can be used together to compile a function for both the host and the device.
facts.note: Combining `__device__` and `__host__`.

facts.claim: The `__CUDA_ARCH__` macro can be used to differentiate code paths between host and device when a function is compiled for both.
facts.note: Usage of `__CUDA_ARCH__` macro.

facts.claim: A 'cross-execution space' call has undefined behavior if `__CUDA_ARCH__` is defined and a `__global__`, `__device__`, or `__host__ __device__` function calls a `__host__` function.
facts.note: Undefined behavior rule 1.

facts.claim: A 'cross-execution space' call has undefined behavior if `__CUDA_ARCH__` is undefined and a `__host__` function calls a `__device__` function.
facts.note: Undefined behavior rule 2.

facts.claim: The compiler inlines any `__device__` function when deemed appropriate.
facts.note: Default inlining behavior for `__device__`.

facts.claim: The `__noinline__` qualifier can be used as a hint for the compiler not to inline a function.
facts.note: Definition of `__noinline__`.

facts.claim: The `__forceinline__` qualifier can be used to force the compiler to inline a function.
facts.note: Definition of `__forceinline__`.

facts.claim: The `__noinline__` and `__forceinline__` function qualifiers cannot be used together, and neither can be applied to an `inline` function.
facts.note: Constraints on `__noinline__` and `__forceinline__`.

facts.claim: The `__inline_hint__` qualifier enables more aggressive inlining in the compiler and can be used to improve inlining across modules when using LTO.
facts.note: Definition of `__inline_hint__`.

facts.claim: Neither the `__noinline__` nor the `__forceinline__` function qualifier can be used with the `__inline_hint__` function qualifier.
facts.note: Constraints on `__inline_hint__`.

caveats: The source document is marked as legacy (replaced by the CUDA Programming Guide as of CUDA 13.0), though the specific section on function execution space specifiers remains relevant for understanding the syntax.
recommended_structure: Overview of Function Execution Space Specifiers

entity/individual-node-enable.title: Individual Node Enable
entity/individual-node-enable.summary: Covers enabling or disabling specific kernel, memset, and memcpy nodes within an instantiated graph using `cudaGraphNodeSetEnabled()`. Disabled nodes act as empty nodes until re-enabled, and parameter updates while disabled apply upon re-enablement.

research_reports.context_summary: Falls under the graph execution update/optimization hierarchy (following Whole Graph Update and Individual Node Update). Provides a lightweight alternative to re-instantiation or parameter swapping for runtime control flow.

facts.claim: Enables/disables execution of specific nodes in an instantiated graph without re-instantiation.
facts.note: Core purpose: avoids instantiation overhead by toggling nodes.

facts.claim: Supported node types are limited to Kernel, memset, and memcpy nodes.
facts.note: Explicitly excludes other node types like host, event, or child graph nodes.

facts.claim: APIs provided: cudaGraphNodeSetEnabled() and cudaGraphNodeGetEnabled().
facts.note: Standard getter/setter pattern for state management.

facts.claim: Disabled nodes behave as functional no-ops (empty nodes) until re-enabled.
facts.note: Does not break graph topology or dependencies.

facts.claim: Enable/disable cycles do not modify underlying node parameters.
facts.note: State and parameters are decoupled.

facts.claim: Enable state is independent of parameter updates (both individual and whole-graph via cudaGraphExecUpdate()).
facts.note: Toggling does not interfere with other update mechanisms.

facts.claim: Parameters modified while a node is disabled are applied automatically upon re-enable.
facts.note: Deferred parameter application ensures consistency.

facts.claim: Use case: Create a single graph superset and customize execution per launch.
facts.note: Optimizes for scenarios with conditional or variable workloads.

caveats: Limited to kernel, memset, and memcpy nodes only.
Refer to Graph API docs for current limitations and usage details.
No contradictions or scope clashes detected with surrounding graph update sections.

entity/individual-node-update.title: Individual Node Update
entity/individual-node-update.summary: Covers updating parameters of instantiated graph nodes directly using specific setter APIs to avoid instantiation overhead. Applicable when the number of nodes to update is small relative to the total graph size.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence is section 6.2.8.7.5.3, which sits between 'Whole Graph Update' and 'Individual Node Enable'. It introduces direct parameter modification for instantiated executable graphs, contrasting it with the less efficient alternative of updating the base `cudaGraph_t` and re-updating the executable handle.

facts.claim: Directly updates parameters of nodes in an instantiated executable graph (`cudaGraphExec_t`), avoiding the overhead of full graph instantiation or creating a new `cudaGraph_t`.
facts.note: Recommended when the number of nodes requiring update is small relative to the total graph size.

facts.claim: Enumerates nine specific setter functions tailored to different node types, including kernel, memcpy, memset, host, child graph, event record/wait, and external semaphore signal/wait nodes.
facts.note: Points readers to the Graph API for detailed usage and current limitations.

caveats: The guide explicitly contrasts this with updating `cudaGraph_t` nodes directly (e.g., `cudaGraphKernelNodeSetParams()`) and subsequently updating the executable graph, noting that the explicit `cudaGraphExec_t` node update APIs are more efficient.

entity/ldg.title: Read-Only Data Cache Load Function (__ldg)
entity/ldg.summary: Documentation for the __ldg() intrinsic, which loads data from global memory into the read-only data cache, supporting various primitive and vector types, and requiring compute capability 5.0 or higher.

research_reports.context_summary: Section 10.10 of the CUDA C Programming Guide, situated between surface object read/write functions (10.9.1.x) and cache-hint load/store functions (10.11-10.12).

facts.claim: Function signature is `T __ldg(const T* address);`
facts.note: The `__ldg` intrinsic loads data from device memory into the read-only data cache.

facts.claim: Supported data types include standard scalars (`char`, `short`, `int`, `long`, `long long`, `unsigned char`, `unsigned short`, `unsigned int`, `unsigned long`, `unsigned long long`, `float`, `double`) and their vector variants (`char2`, `char4`, `short2`, `short4`, `int2`, `int4`, `longlong2`, `uchar2`, `uchar4`, `ushort2`, `ushort4`, `uint2`, `uint4`, `ulonglong2`, `float2`, `float4`, `double2`).
facts.note: Markdown extraction contains minor typographical spacing issues (e.g., `longlong2uchar2`), but the intended types are clearly scalar and vector pairs.

facts.claim: Extended half-precision types `__half`/`__half2` and `__nv_bfloat16`/`__nv_bfloat162` are supported when `cuda_fp16.h` and `cuda_bf16.h` are included, respectively.
facts.note: None

facts.claim: The operation is cached in the read-only data cache.
facts.note: None

facts.claim: Hardware support requires compute capability 5.0 or higher.
facts.note: None

caveats: The source text lists types in a single continuous string with missing spaces/newlines (e.g., `long, long longunsigned char...`), which is an artifact of the markdown extraction rather than a language specification.

entity/memory-fence-functions.title: Memory Fence Functions
entity/memory-fence-functions.summary: Explains __threadfence_block(), __threadfence(), and __threadfence_system() for enforcing sequentially-consistent memory ordering across different scopes (block, device, system) to prevent data races.

research_reports.context_summary: Section 10.5 covers Memory Fence Functions, explaining CUDA's weakly-ordered memory model and how fences enforce sequential consistency across different thread scopes without guaranteeing visibility.

facts.claim: CUDA uses a weakly-ordered memory model where write order is not guaranteed to match observation order across threads and memory spaces (shared, global, host, peer). Memory fences enforce sequentially-consistent ordering but do not guarantee visibility, which requires `__syncthreads()` or `volatile`.
facts.note: Fences order operations relative to the fence call and are independent of the accessed memory space.

facts.claim: `__threadfence_block()` is equivalent to `cuda::atomic_thread_fence(cuda::memory_order_seq_cst, cuda::thread_scope_block)` and ensures ordering of reads/writes for all threads within the calling thread's block.
facts.note: Sufficient when communicating threads belong to the same block.

facts.claim: `__threadfence()` is equivalent to `cuda::atomic_thread_fence(cuda::memory_order_seq_cst, cuda::thread_scope_device)` and ensures ordering across all threads on the device.
facts.note: Required when communicating threads are in different blocks on the same device.

facts.claim: `__threadfence_system()` is equivalent to `cuda::atomic_thread_fence(cuda::memory_order_seq_cst, cuda::thread_scope_system)` and ensures ordering across the device, host threads, and peer devices. Requires compute capability 2.x or higher.
facts.note: Required for cross-device or host-CUDA communication.

caveats: Fences only affect operation ordering, not visibility. Visibility still requires explicit synchronization (e.g., `__syncthreads()`) or `volatile` qualifiers.
Using fences incorrectly (e.g., missing them when crossing block boundaries) leads to undefined behavior due to data races.
recommended_structure: Overview of CUDA's weakly-ordered memory model
Purpose and scope of memory fences
Usage guidelines and visibility vs. ordering distinction

entity/sibling-launch.title: Sibling Launch
entity/sibling-launch.summary: Covers sibling launch mode, where the graph is launched as a child of the parent environment rather than the launching graph's environment. Does not gate tail launches enqueued by the launching graph.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence falls within the CUDA C Programming Guide's section on device graph execution environments. It specifically addresses section 6.2.8.7.7.11, which details Sibling Launch, positioned between discussions on Tail Self-launch and Conditional Graph Nodes.

facts.claim: Sibling launch is a variation of fire-and-forget launch where the target graph is attached as a child of the launching graph's parent environment, rather than the launching graph's own execution environment.
facts.note: Functionally equivalent to a fire-and-forget launch executed from the parent environment.

facts.claim: The feature is invoked using `cudaGraphLaunch()` with the flag `cudaStreamGraphFireAndForgetAsSibling` inside a device kernel.
facts.note: Code example demonstrates instantiation, upload, and device-side capture/launch workflow.

facts.claim: Sibling launches do not gate tail launches enqueued by the launching graph.
facts.note: This is a direct consequence of bypassing the launching graph's execution environment.

caveats: The source does not specify limits on concurrent sibling launches or explicit synchronization primitives for them, unlike tail launches (max 255) or fire-and-forget launches (max 120).
No mention of host-side sibling launch capabilities; the example is strictly device-captured.

entity/tail-launch.title: Tail Launch
entity/tail-launch.summary: Covers the tail launch mode for serial work dependencies on the device. Executes when the parent graph's environment completes. Supports multiple enqueued graphs, strict ordering, and up to 255 pending tail launches per graph.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence is extracted from Section 6.2.8.7.7.9, which details device graph launch modes. It contrasts tail launch with host-side synchronization and other device modes (Fire and Forget, Sibling Launch) in the surrounding sections.

facts.claim: Purpose & Context
facts.note: Tail launch is a device-only graph execution mode designed to enable serial work dependencies on the GPU, as traditional synchronization (`cudaDeviceSynchronize`, `cudaStreamSynchronize`) is not supported for device graphs. It operates alongside Fire and Forget and Sibling Launch modes.

facts.claim: Execution Trigger
facts.note: A tail launch executes only when the launching graph's execution environment is fully complete—meaning the graph itself and all its child work (including any fire-and-forget launches) have finished.

facts.claim: Environment Replacement
facts.note: Upon completion, the environment of the next queued graph replaces the completed environment as a child of the parent environment.

facts.claim: Ordering Rules
facts.note: Executes in strict FIFO order based on enqueue time. If a tail graph enqueues additional tail launches, those new launches execute before any tail launches enqueued by earlier graphs in the chain.

facts.claim: Limits
facts.note: A single graph can have up to 255 pending tail launches.

facts.claim: API Usage
facts.note: Implemented via `cudaGraphLaunch(graph, cudaStreamGraphTailLaunch);`. Requires the target graph to be instantiated and uploaded to the device beforehand.

caveats: Tail launch is strictly a device-side mechanism; host-side graphs cannot use it.
The environment replacement model means downstream dependencies must account for chained graph completions rather than traditional stream synchronization.

entity/tail-self-launch.title: Tail Self-Launch
entity/tail-self-launch.summary: Covers the ability of a device graph to enqueue itself for a tail launch using `cudaGetCurrentGraphExec()`. Allows device-side looping/recursion. Only one self-launch can be enqueued at a time.

research_reports.context_summary: The section falls under Device Launch Modes (6.2.8.7.7.6) and builds upon the Tail Launch concept (6.2.8.7.7.9), which executes a graph only after the launching graph's environment is fully complete. Tail Self-Launch (6.2.8.7.7.10) is a specialized case where a graph enqueues itself for the next tail launch slot.

facts.claim: A device graph can enqueue itself for a subsequent tail launch, enabling GPU-side iterative loops without host CPU involvement.
facts.note: Only one self-launch can be enqueued at a time per graph.

facts.claim: The device-side function `cudaGraphExec_t cudaGetCurrentGraphExec()` retrieves the handle of the currently executing device graph.
facts.note: Returns `NULL` if the kernel is not part of a device graph.

facts.claim: Self-launches execute sequentially as tail launches, meaning the self-launched instance runs only after the current graph's execution environment (and all its children) is complete.
facts.note: Leverages the standard tail launch ordering and environment completion rules.

facts.claim: Implementation typically involves a device-side counter to limit iterations, calling `cudaGraphLaunch()` with `cudaStreamGraphTailLaunch` and the handle from `cudaGetCurrentGraphExec()`.
facts.note: Sample code demonstrates a loop controlled by `relaunchCount`.

caveats: The document notes that only one self-launch can be enqueued at a time, preventing recursive or branching self-launch chains within a single execution pass.
This feature is strictly for device graphs; host graphs cannot use this mechanism.
contradictions: None detected. Aligns with standard device graph launch semantics.

entity/tma-ptx-wrappers.title: Multi-dimensional TMA PTX Wrappers
entity/tma-ptx-wrappers.summary: Lists the CUDA C++ wrapper functions for `cp.async.bulk.tensor` PTX instructions, supporting 1D to 5D bulk tensor asynchronous copies between global and shared memory.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence is section 10.29.2.1, which documents C++ wrapper functions for the PTX `cp.async.bulk.tensor` instructions. These wrappers facilitate bulk tensor asynchronous memory transfers between global and shared memory on compute capability 9.0+ architectures.

facts.claim: The wrappers are C++ `inline __device__` functions located in the `cuda::device::experimental` namespace.
facts.note: Wrappers bridge the gap between high-level C++ and the underlying `cp.async.bulk.tensor` PTX instructions for bulk tensor asynchronous copies.

facts.claim: Two directional sets of wrappers exist for each tensor rank (1D through 5D): `*_global_to_shared` and `*_shared_to_global`.
facts.note: Global-to-shared wrappers require a destination pointer, tensor map, coordinate indices, and a block-scoped barrier reference. Shared-to-global wrappers require a tensor map, coordinate indices, and a source pointer, but do not take a barrier argument in their signature.

facts.claim: Global-to-Shared signature pattern: `void cuda::device::experimental::cp_async_bulk_tensor_<rank>_global_to_shared(void *dest, const CUtensorMap *tensor_map, int c0, ..., int c<rank-1>, cuda::barrier<cuda::thread_scope_block> &bar)`.
facts.note: Coordinates `c0` through `c<rank-1>` specify the starting position in global memory.

facts.claim: Shared-to-Global signature pattern: `void cuda::device::experimental::cp_async_bulk_tensor_<rank>_shared_to_global(const CUtensorMap *tensor_map, int c0, ..., int c<rank-1>, const void *src)`.
facts.note: Coordinates specify the starting position in global memory, while `src` points to the shared memory buffer.

facts.claim: The wrappers support tensor ranks from 1D to 5D, with function names explicitly encoding the dimensionality (e.g., `cp_async_bulk_tensor_1d_global_to_shared`, `cp_async_bulk_tensor_5d_shared_to_global`).
facts.note: Each rank adds one additional `int` coordinate parameter to the function signature.

caveats: The provided source text contains truncated function signatures (missing closing parentheses and semicolons in the raw markdown extraction). The lead writer should verify the exact syntax against the official CUDA headers or PTX documentation.

The evidence does not detail error handling, return values (all are `void`), or specific alignment constraints, which are covered in the surrounding TMA documentation (e.g., 16-byte global alignment, 128-byte shared alignment).
recommended_structure: Usage Notes & Alignment Constraints (referencing surrounding TMA section)

entity/unified_memory_programming.title: Unified Memory Programming
entity/unified_memory_programming.summary: Introduces CUDA Unified Memory, its system requirements, programming model, allocation APIs (cudaMallocManaged, __managed__), and examples demonstrating memory allocation and usage across host and device.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence corresponds to Chapter 24 of the CUDA C Programming Guide, situated between Chapter 23 (Error Log Management) and the continuation of Chapter 24's performance hint sections. The chapter is explicitly marked as legacy documentation as of CUDA 13.0.

facts.claim: Legacy Status & Scope
facts.note: Chapter 24 is deprecated as of CUDA 13.0 and superseded by the new CUDA Programming Guide.

facts.claim: Core Concept & Benefits
facts.note: Unified Memory provides a single pool accessible by all CPUs/GPUs via one pointer, supporting concurrent access and native operations. It boosts productivity by removing manual `cudaMalloc`/`cudaMemcpy` cycles, improves performance via data migration, reduces duplication, and enables oversubscription.

facts.claim: Allocation Methods
facts.note: Two primary methods: System-Allocated Memory (standard host APIs like `malloc`, `mmap`, stack, globals) and CUDA APIs (`cudaMallocManaged()`).

facts.claim: System-Allocated Memory Behavior
facts.note: On full-support systems, all host allocations are unified. Physical memory is populated on first-touch near the accessing processor (CPU or GPU).

facts.claim: CUDA Managed Memory API
facts.note: `cudaMallocManaged()` allocates managed memory syntactically identical to `cudaMalloc()`, freed via `cudaFree()`. Compute capability 5.x allocates directly on GPU, while 6.x+ uses first-touch.

facts.claim: Managed Variables (`__managed__`)
facts.note: Global/file-scope variables declared with `__managed__` behave like `cudaMallocManaged()` allocations. `__managed__` implies `__device__`. Accessing them triggers CUDA context creation if none exists.

facts.claim: System Requirements & Detection
facts.note: Support levels are determined by device properties `pageableMemoryAccess` and `concurrentManagedAccess`. Full support requires both to be 1. Runtime detection uses `cudaGetDeviceProperties()` or `cudaDeviceGetAttribute()`.

facts.claim: Pointer Attributes & Type Checking
facts.note: `cudaPointerGetAttributes()` identifies allocation type (`cudaMemoryTypeManaged`, `cudaMemoryTypeHost`, `cudaMemoryTypeUnregistered`). Attributes reflect allocation method, not physical location.

facts.claim: Unified vs. Mapped Memory
facts.note: Unified Memory guarantees portability for all memory operations (e.g., atomics), whereas Mapped Memory only guarantees a limited set across systems.

facts.claim: Performance Hints & Prefetching
facts.note: Applications can use `cudaMemAdvise` and `cudaMemPrefetchAsync` to hint at data usage or manually migrate data closer to the accessing processor. These are optional optimizations that do not affect program correctness.

caveats: The documentation is explicitly marked as legacy (CUDA 13.0+).
Behavior is undefined if Unified Memory is used on unsupported systems.
Pointer attributes indicate allocation/registration method, not physical memory location.
`__managed__` variables cannot be marked as `__constant__`.

entity/variable-memory-space-specifiers.title: Variable Memory Space Specifiers
entity/variable-memory-space-specifiers.summary: Covers __device__, __constant__, __shared__, __grid_constant__, __managed__, and __restrict__ specifiers. Details their lifetime, scope, alignment, aliasing implications, and compiler optimization effects.

research_reports.context_summary: Falls under Chapter 10 (C++ Language Extensions), Section 10.2. Directly follows function execution space specifiers and precedes built-in vector types. The guide is marked as legacy (CUDA 13.0+), but the C++ extension syntax remains documented.

facts.claim: Purpose and default behavior
facts.note: Specifiers denote device memory location. Unspecified automatic variables usually reside in registers but may be placed in local memory by the compiler, causing performance penalties.

facts.claim: __device__ specifier
facts.note: Declares a variable on the device. Can be combined with other specifiers. Defaults to global memory, lifetime matches the CUDA context, distinct per device, and accessible from all grid threads and host via runtime APIs.

facts.claim: __constant__ specifier
facts.note: Places variable in constant memory. Shares CUDA context lifetime and per-device distinctness. Accessible from all grid threads and host. Modifying from host during concurrent grid access is undefined behavior.

facts.claim: __shared__ specifier
facts.note: Places variable in thread block shared memory. Lifetime matches the block, distinct per block, accessible only within the block, and lacks a constant address. Supports dynamic sizing via `extern __shared__`, requiring manual offset management and strict alignment.

facts.claim: __grid_constant__ specifier
facts.note: Available for compute architecture ≥ 7.0. Annotates `const`-qualified `__global__` parameters. Lifetime is the grid, private to the grid, distinct per grid, and read-only. Prevents compiler from creating per-thread local copies when addresses are taken, improving performance. Requires signature matching across templates.

facts.claim: __managed__ specifier
facts.note: Declares a variable accessible from both host and device code. Lifetime is tied to the application.

facts.claim: __restrict__ specifier
facts.note: Implements C99 restricted pointers to resolve aliasing. Asserts non-overlapping pointers, enabling compiler optimizations like code reordering and common sub-expression elimination. Increases register pressure, which may reduce occupancy and negatively impact performance.

caveats: The source document is explicitly marked as legacy (replaced by the new CUDA Programming Guide as of CUDA 13.0).
__grid_constant__ has strict C++ template matching requirements and compute architecture constraints (≥ 7.0).
__restrict__ optimization benefits are balanced against increased register pressure, which can lower occupancy.

formatted-output.title: Formatted Output
formatted-output.summary: Formatted output is supported on compute capability 2.x and higher via the in-kernel printf() function. It behaves similarly to the standard C library printf() but outputs to a host-side stream. Each thread executes printf independently. The function returns the number of arguments parsed. Limitations include host-dependent formatting, a 32-argument limit, potential output corruption on win64 with %ld, and a fixed-size circular buffer that flushes on kernel launch, synchronization, memory copies, module loading, context destruction, or stream callbacks. Thread execution order may be affected.

research_reports.context_summary: Section 10.35 (Formatted Output) sits between device-side debugging utilities (profiler, assertions, traps, breakpoints) and dynamic global memory allocation. It details the device-side printf() implementation, its execution model, format specifier support, buffer management, and host-side API integration.

facts.claim: Device support and signature
facts.note: Requires compute capability 2.x or higher. Signature: int printf(const char *format[, arg, ...]);

facts.claim: Execution model
facts.note: Executes per-thread. Multi-threaded kernels produce one output line per thread unless restricted by the programmer (e.g., threadIdx.x == 0).

facts.claim: Return value behavior
facts.note: Returns number of arguments parsed (0 if none, -1 if NULL format, -2 on error). Differs from standard C printf() which returns characters printed.

facts.claim: Format specifier syntax and types
facts.note: Follows %[flags][width][.precision][size]type. Supports flags (#, space, 0, +, -), width (*, digits), precision (digits), size (h, l, ll), and types (c, d, i, o, u, x, X, p, e, E, f, g, G, a, A, s). Accepts any combination, even invalid ones, but may yield undefined host output.

facts.claim: Host-side formatting and OS dependency
facts.note: Final formatting occurs on the host, making behavior host-OS dependent.

facts.claim: Argument limit
facts.note: Maximum of 32 arguments plus format string. Extra arguments are ignored.

facts.claim: Cross-platform long type warning
facts.note: %ld may corrupt output on 64-bit Windows due to long size differences (4 vs 8 bytes). Match compile/run platforms.

facts.claim: Circular output buffer and flush triggers
facts.note: Fixed-size circular buffer (default 1 MB). Older output overwritten if full. Flushes on: kernel launch, sync calls, blocking memcpy, module load/unload, context destruction, and prior to stream callbacks. Not flushed automatically on exit; requires cudaDeviceReset() or cuCtxDestroy().

facts.claim: Thread execution order impact
facts.note: Uses a shared data structure, which may alter thread execution order. CUDA guarantees order only at __syncthreads() barriers.

facts.claim: Host-side API for buffer management
facts.note: cudaDeviceGetLimit() and cudaDeviceSetLimit() with cudaLimitPrintfFifoSize manage buffer size.

facts.claim: Usage examples
facts.note: Demonstrates per-thread output producing multiple lines, and conditional output restricting to a single thread.

caveats: The guide explicitly warns that invalid format specifier combinations are accepted by the device but may produce undefined output on the host due to final formatting happening there.
Buffer overflow behavior is destructive (circular overwrite), which can cause loss of early kernel logs if not flushed frequently.
Execution order is non-deterministic when printf() is used due to shared data structure contention.

profiler-counter-function.title: Profiler Counter Function
profiler-counter-function.summary: Each multiprocessor has sixteen hardware counters that applications can increment via the __prof_trigger() function. Counters 0-7 are available for applications, while 8-15 are reserved. Values can be retrieved using nvprof --events prof_trigger_0x. Counters reset before each kernel launch.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence is part of a sequence of device-side utility and debugging functions in the CUDA C Programming Guide. It sits between Tensor Map operations (Section 10.30) and other runtime control/debugging functions like assertions, traps, breakpoints, and formatted output (Sections 10.32–10.35).

facts.claim: The function signature is `void __prof_trigger(int counter);`
facts.note: Increments a per-multiprocessor hardware counter by one for every warp that executes the instruction.

facts.claim: Multiprocessors provide 16 hardware counters. Indices 0–7 are available for application use, while 8–15 are reserved.
facts.note: Counter values (0–7) are collected using the `nvprof` tool with the `--events prof_trigger_0x` flag (where `x` corresponds to the counter index).

facts.claim: Counters are automatically reset prior to each kernel launch. Collecting these counters forces kernel launches to be synchronous.
facts.note: No contradictions or scope clashes found. The documentation clearly delineates the function's purpose, usage limits, and profiling tool integration.

caveats: The function increments the counter *per warp*, not per thread. Developers must account for warp-level execution when interpreting profiler data.

trap-function.title: Trap Function
trap-function.summary: The __trap() function can be called from any device thread to initiate a trap operation. Execution of the kernel is aborted and an interrupt is raised in the host program.

research_reports.context_summary: The assigned evidence falls within Section 10.33 of the CUDA C Programming Guide, situated between the 'Assertion' (10.32) and 'Breakpoint Function' (10.34) device-side utility functions.

facts.claim: The trap function is invoked via `void __trap();` and can be called from any device thread.
facts.note: Aborts kernel execution and raises a host-side interrupt.

facts.claim: The function is documented as a standalone device-side utility for halting execution, distinct from assertions or breakpoints.
facts.note: Positioned between `assert` and `__brkpt()` in the guide's device function reference.

caveats: The source text is brief and does not specify error codes returned to the host, required compute capabilities, or interaction with streams/contexts beyond raising an interrupt.

facts.claim: ## Group Algorithms Support for parallel patterns in SYCL device code is provided by a separate library of group algorithms.
